sources: NBER, arXiv, Crossref journals (criminology + urban/policy), Campbell Collaboration, Urban Institute, J-PAL
known_gaps: SSRN (no open RSS/API; much SSRN crime/econ work surfaces via NBER & Crossref)
Brookings, MDRC, RAND, Vera Institute, Manhattan Institute, Arnold Ventures (feeds 404 or are bot-blocked as of last probe)

papers.title: Can Crime Be Deterred at Low Cost? Evidence From a Randomized Experiment in New York
papers.authors: Oludamilare Aboaba, Aaron Chalfin, Michael LaForest‐Tucker, Lucie Parker, Patrick Sharkey
papers.abstract: ABSTRACT Can crime be deterred at low cost? We report the results of a randomized experiment of a messaging intervention designed to deter offending among individuals under parole supervision with a prior violent felony conviction or firearm arrest. The intervention consisted of a group meeting in which high‐risk paroled individuals were notified of the sanction they would face upon reoffending while being offered community resources to support re‐integration into the community. The program did not lead to a notable reduction in future arrests or create community spillover effects but did reduce parole violations by 15%. Potential mechanisms and implications for similar programs are discussed.
papers.source: Journal of Policy Analysis and Management
papers.venue: Journal of Policy Analysis and Management
papers.idea: Conduct group deterrence meetings with high-risk parolees to clarify sanctions and offer reintegration resources, aiming to reduce reoffending and parole violations.
papers.summary: A randomized experiment tested a messaging intervention with parolees convicted of violent felonies or firearm arrests. The program reduced parole violations by 15% but did not significantly reduce future arrests or produce community spillover effects.
papers.method: Randomized controlled trial (RCT) in New York with high-risk individuals under parole supervision; exact sample size not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Group meeting format is replicable across parole populations; can scale with parole department infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Described as 'low cost' intervention (per-unit cost not specified in abstract); group-based delivery suggests efficiency.
papers.nyc_note: Study conducted in NYC; directly compatible with NYDOC parole operations and existing high-risk offender supervision.

papers.title: AI, body‐worn cameras, and a potential civilizing effect for officers: Evidence from the Arizona Truleo study
papers.authors: Michael D. White, Seth Watts, Aili Malm
papers.abstract: Abstract Research Summary Over time, the evidence on the impact of police body‐worn cameras (BWCs) has become increasingly mixed. Though there are numerous contributors to the inconsistent findings (e.g., context, policy, training, culture), one pressing issue involves the failure of law enforcement agencies to review any more than a fraction of the BWC footage generated by their officers. This footage review problem violates a central precondition for the hypothesized civilizing effect of BWCs on officers: their belief that footage will be reviewed. AI‐driven BWC analytics has emerged as a solution that may overcome the footage review problem, but there has been virtually no research on such AI‐based platforms. The current study fills this gap through an evaluation of Truleo in the Apache Junction and Casa Grande Police Departments (AJPD, CGPD). Officers were randomized to Treatment (Truleo) and Control (non‐Truleo) conditions for a 6‐month period, and we compared study groups across a range of measures including Truleo‐generated professionalism, use of force, complaints, stops, arrests, and citations. Treatment officers in both departments were more likely to generate High Professionalism ratings, but group differences fell short of statistical significance. Use of force decreased significantly in AJPD and in some of the pooled models, though the pooled models were sensitive to multiple‐comparisons corrections. The Bayesian probabilities paradigm offers an additional lens for considering the mixed effects with High Professionalism and use of force. Policy Implications Taken together, results suggest Truleo may hold promise for promoting positive behavior change among officers consistent with a civilizing effect. Law enforcement agencies should explore whether AI‐driven BWC analytics can enhance their BWC program, expand their review of footage, and positively shape their officers’ behavior. Additional research is needed in other settings to test the durability of these findings and the value of AI‐generated metrics of police performance (e.g., professionalism). Researchers should consider the significance of research findings from a broader lens that accounts for practitioner perspectives on the meaningful impact of an innovation. Lastly, the results presented here inform the ongoing dialogue over the role of AI in policing.
papers.idea: Deploy AI-driven body-worn camera analytics platforms (like Truleo) to automatically review footage at scale and flag professionalism violations, creating accountability and deterring misconduct.
papers.summary: This randomized controlled trial tested Truleo, an AI platform that automates review of police body-worn camera footage, in two Arizona departments over 6 months. Treatment officers showed higher professionalism ratings and some evidence of reduced use of force, though results were mixed and some findings did not survive statistical corrections.
papers.method: Randomized controlled trial with officers assigned to Truleo (treatment) or standard review (control) conditions in Apache Junction and Casa Grande Police Departments over 6 months; outcomes measured via AI-generated metrics on professionalism, use of force, complaints, stops, arrests, and citations.
papers.scalability_note: Software platform can be deployed across departments; requires integration with existing body-camera systems and minimal retraining.
papers.cost_note: Licensing cost per officer per year not disclosed in abstract; likely moderate for large city adoption relative to staffing budgets.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD already uses body-worn cameras citywide; Truleo or similar AI tools could be integrated into existing infrastructure and oversight mechanisms.

papers.title: Does LED Street Lighting Reduce Crime? Evidence from a Staggered Large-Scale Retrofitting Program
papers.authors: David Mitre-Becerril
papers.idea: Replace traditional street lighting with LED fixtures as a scalable, evidence-based crime reduction intervention.
papers.summary: This study evaluates whether large-scale LED street lighting retrofitting programs reduce crime, using a staggered rollout to establish causality. The analysis leverages real-world variation in deployment timing to identify whether improved nighttime visibility from LED upgrades translates to measurable public safety gains.
papers.method: Staggered difference-in-differences design exploiting phased LED retrofitting program rollout across neighborhoods; exact sample size and setting not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: LED retrofit is a standard municipal infrastructure project easily replicated across cities and neighborhoods.
papers.cost_note: LED fixtures have declining unit costs; retrofits often bundled with existing street maintenance budgets or funded via energy savings.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has ongoing streetlight modernization efforts; LED retrofit aligns directly with existing Department of Environmental Protection and local agency programs.

papers.title: The prosecutor's cut: Body‐worn camera evidence and criminal case dispositions
papers.authors: Kevin Petersen, Miranda A. Galvin, Brian D. Johnson, Allison D. Redlich
papers.abstract: Abstract Research summary In recent years, a growing form of evidence tied to criminal cases has been police body‐worn camera (BWC) footage. In many jurisdictions, the majority of cases include BWC evidence, though limited research examines how this evidence impacts prosecutorial decision‐making and case outcomes. In this study, we examine this issue using 7 years (2016–2022) of prosecution data ( N = 29,923) from a large urban jurisdiction in Maryland. We use covariate balancing propensity score weighting to equate treatment (BWC) and control (non‐BWC) cases on baseline characteristics, and assess the impact of BWC evidence on a variety of downstream outcomes. Our results indicate that BWC evidence impacts the likelihood of nolle pros decisions and guilty pleas, reduces case‐processing time, and alters incarceration sentences following guilty pleas, though the effects are conditioned by crime type and are strongest for cases initiated by “on‐view” arrests. Policy implications The rapid adoption of BWCs has strained the resources of local prosecutor's offices. Empirical knowledge establishing whether, and under what conditions, BWC footage provides useful evidence during prosecution is critical to informing decisions about the costs and benefits of BWC adoption and the development of evidence‐based practices for the use of BWC footage during the court process. Our findings show that BWC evidence has significant impacts on case processing outcomes, but these effects depend on the characteristics of the offense in question. Our results help to inform decisions concerning the implementation and scope of BWC programs, as well as strategies outlining the use of BWC footage during prosecution.
papers.idea: Cities should evaluate and optimize body-worn camera (BWC) use in prosecution workflows, focusing resources on case types and arrest contexts where BWC evidence most meaningfully impacts case disposition and reduces processing time.
papers.summary: This 7-year Maryland study of ~30,000 cases found that BWC evidence significantly affects prosecutorial decision-making: it increases guilty pleas, reduces nolle pros dismissals, shortens case processing, and alters sentencing outcomes—but these effects vary by crime type and are strongest for on-view arrests. The findings suggest BWC's value depends heavily on offense category, not uniform across all case types.
papers.method: Observational study using 2016–2022 prosecution data from a large Maryland jurisdiction (N=29,923), with covariate balancing propensity score weighting to equate BWC and non-BWC cases on baseline characteristics, analyzing multiple downstream outcomes.
papers.scalability_note: BWC programs already widespread; findings directly applicable to existing prosecutor offices nationwide.
papers.cost_note: No new infrastructure required; analysis improves efficiency of existing BWC systems and prosecutorial workflow.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD has extensive BWC deployment; NYC prosecutors can directly apply these findings to optimize case triage and resource allocation.

papers.title: Prime Time for Crime? A Multi-City Analysis of Sporting Events, Sports Venues, and Crime
papers.authors: Travis M. Carter, Richard K. Moule, Jedidiah Knode
papers.abstract: Abstract Objectives This study examines the relationship between sporting events and crime near sports venues based on a sample of Major League Baseball (MLB) and National Football League (NFL) regular season games. Methods Open crime data drawn from 21 U.S. cities were analyzed using a quasi-experimental design that compared differences in crime around venues hosting home games to venues whose teams play an away game, at the same time, from 2015 to 2023. Results Within one kilometer of sports venues, larceny and assault rose when stadiums hosted games. Larceny peaked before and after games, while assaults tended to occur during and after games, suggesting different opportunity structures across crime types. Conclusions The relationship between sporting events and crime varies by where games are hosted and is sensitive to some analytic considerations, which in turn have important implications for criminological theory and strategies to mitigate the criminogenic effects of these events.
papers.idea: Deploy targeted police presence and crime-prevention interventions (e.g., increased foot patrols, access controls, environmental design) before/after sporting events and during games, calibrated by crime type and venue location.
papers.summary: The study found that larceny and assault increased within one kilometer of sports venues on game days compared to non-game days across 21 U.S. cities. Larceny peaked before and after games (suggesting theft/pickpocketing opportunities in crowds), while assaults occurred during and after games, indicating different crime mechanisms by type.
papers.method: Quasi-experimental design comparing crime rates around venues hosting home games versus nearby venues whose teams played away games simultaneously, using open crime data from 21 U.S. cities over 2015–2023.
papers.scalability_note: Venue-specific interventions are implementable in any city with pro sports teams; findings generalizable across MLB/NFL cities.
papers.cost_note: Incremental redeployment of existing police resources on game days; modest added cost for environmental design or private security coordination.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has multiple MLB (Yankees, Mets), NFL (Giants, Jets), and other major venues; NYPD can operationalize differential staffing and tactical plans by game type and location.

papers.title: School‐Based Interventions for Reducing Disciplinary School Exclusion. An Updated Systematic Review
papers.authors:
Sara Valdebenito, 
Hannah Gaffney, 
Maria Jose Arosemena‐Burbano, 
Sydney Hitchcock, 
Darrick Jolliffe, 
Alex Sutherland
papers.abstract: Campbell Systematic Reviews, Volume 21, Issue 4, December 2025.
papers.idea: Implement school-based interventions proven to reduce student suspensions and expulsions, which improves school safety, attendance, and reduces juvenile justice involvement.
papers.summary: This is a systematic review of school-based interventions designed to reduce disciplinary exclusions (suspensions and expulsions). The review synthesizes evidence on what works to keep students in school while maintaining safe learning environments, addressing both educational outcomes and downstream effects on youth involvement in the justice system.
papers.method: Systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized and quasi-experimental studies of school-based interventions targeting reduction in school exclusion; peer-reviewed literature synthesis following Campbell Collaboration standards.
papers.scalability_note: School-based programs are standard infrastructure; interventions can be rolled out across districts.
papers.cost_note: Most school discipline programs are low-cost staff training or protocol changes; implementation within existing budgets feasible.
papers.nyc_note: NYC DOE manages 1,600+ schools; reducing suspensions aligns with stated equity goals and reduces school-to-prison pipeline.

papers.title: The effects of offering treatment with injectable naltrexone during reentry on long-term mortality
papers.authors: Jordan M. Hyatt, C. Clare Strange
papers.abstract: Abstract Background Injectable naltrexone (XR-NTX) is an FDA-approved opioid agonist treatment (OAT) for the prevention of relapse to opioid abuse. While there is a growing consensus about its efficacy in community settings, less is known about the effect on justice-involved people, particularly in the time immediately after incarceration. The unique needs of this population warrant additional inquiry, especially regarding the prevention of mortality, as they are at an increased risk of death during the reentry period. Methods The current study follows a wait-list randomized control design and was conducted within a large state-level correctional system located in the Eastern United States. Eligible individuals could receive no-cost XR-NTX before their release from incarceration or six months after release. Analyses followed an intention-to-treat (ITT) framework. Results A policy of providing injectable naltrexone did not significantly increase or decrease mortality among people leaving prison, though lower mortality rates and longer time to failure were observed among those people starting OAT treatment while incarcerated. Conclusions The evidence on XR-NTX remains mixed concerning justice-related outcomes (e.g., recidivism), as well as mortality among people leaving prison. The elevated risk- and needs-profile of this population, considered in conjunction with the existing evidence base on MAT, suggests that this remains an important avenue for future research.
papers.idea: Offer no-cost injectable naltrexone (XR-NTX) to incarcerated people before release to reduce opioid relapse and overdose mortality during reentry.
papers.summary: A randomized controlled trial in a large Eastern US state prison system found that offering free injectable naltrexone did not significantly reduce overall mortality among people leaving prison. However, those who started medication-assisted treatment while still incarcerated showed lower mortality rates and longer time-to-failure outcomes.
papers.method: Wait-list randomized controlled trial within a state correctional system; participants could receive no-cost XR-NTX before release or six months after release; analysis followed intention-to-treat framework.
papers.scalability_note: Program model is portable across prison systems; requires coordination with corrections and health systems but no novel infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Injectable naltrexone costs $300–600/month; abstract does not specify total program cost but no-cost provision to reentrants is feasible for city/state budgets targeting high-mortality population.
papers.nyc_note: NYC Department of Corrections runs Rikers Island and borough facilities; NYC Health + Hospitals and COBA could coordinate pre-release MAT. Some barriers: DOC operational constraints, federal versus state jurisdiction nuances.

papers.title: Examining the effect of Indiana prosecutor‐led diversion programs on recidivism outcomes: A case study of two jurisdictions
papers.authors: Tri Keah S. Henry, Eric Grommon, Carmen Diaz, Evan M. Lowder
papers.abstract: Abstract Research summary Indiana Code allows local prosecutors to withhold prosecution and offer diversion for residents charged with low‐level offenses. In doing so, prosecutors can minimize the collateral consequences of justice‐system involvement. Although widely adopted as an alternative to traditional prosecution, few studies have evaluated the effectiveness of prosecutor‐led diversion programs. The current study builds on this area of research by evaluating two prosecutor‐led diversion programs operating in one large and one moderately sized jurisdiction, highlighting their effectiveness in limiting future criminal justice contact. We examined administrative prosecution records from 2013 to 2023 in which 21,653 filed cases met statutory eligibility for diversion, and 4572 individuals accepted a diversion offer in the larger site. In the moderate‐sized jurisdiction, 17,357 cases were eligible, with 6335 offers accepted. Propensity score‐weighted and instrumental variable regression techniques indicate that diversion was associated with a reduced probability of a new criminal case filing 1‐year after the instant case filing relative to all other eligible cases. Moreover, program participation moderated the effect of race on future criminal justice contact. Policy implications Prosecutor‐led diversion programs are one strategy used to efficiently manage criminal caseloads, hold defendants accountable, temper the collateral consequences of conviction, and reduce recidivism. The findings reported here indicate that these programs are associated with lower rates of recidivism. However, the programs’ discretionary design results in case‐ and defendant‐level sorting and selection processes that shape program efficacy. After adjusting for sorting and selection effects, associations between diversion and favorable outcomes persist but raise important questions about both equitable access to diversion programs and the reliability of previously reported diversion findings.
papers.idea: Expand prosecutor-led diversion programs for low-level offenses as an alternative to prosecution, with careful attention to equitable access across racial groups.
papers.summary: The study evaluated two Indiana prosecutor-led diversion programs (one large, one moderate jurisdiction) serving 10,907 diverted individuals from 2013–2023. Diversion participants showed significantly lower rates of new criminal case filings within 1 year compared to eligible non-participants, and the program moderated disparities in racial outcomes; however, discretionary design created selection effects that warrant attention to equity.
papers.method: Quasi-experimental analysis of administrative prosecution records (21,653 eligible cases in large site, 17,357 in moderate site) using propensity score weighting and instrumental variable regression to estimate recidivism reduction.
papers.scalability_note: Prosecutor-led diversion is already standard in many jurisdictions and requires no new technology; scaling depends mainly on prosecutorial policy and training.
papers.cost_note: Diversion typically involves case processing and possible light conditions (e.g., community service) rather than incarceration, yielding significant cost savings; per-unit cost not specified in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has prosecutorial discretion and some diversion programs; this research validates expansion and suggests need for racial-equity audits to ensure fair access.

papers.title: The heterogeneous effects of cannabis dispensaries on crime: The importance of foot traffic
papers.authors: Thomas Lebesmuehlbacher, Justin Roush, Rhet A. Smith
papers.idea: Use foot-traffic data and location analysis to strategically site cannabis dispensaries in ways that minimize crime externalities in neighborhoods.
papers.summary: This study examines how cannabis dispensaries affect crime rates, with particular attention to how foot traffic patterns mediate those effects. The title suggests dispensary impacts on crime are heterogeneous (vary by context), and that foot traffic is a key mechanism explaining which locations see crime increases or decreases.
papers.method: Likely a spatial/econometric analysis of dispensary locations and crime data, possibly leveraging staggered legalization or opening dates as variation; exact design unclear without abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Cannabis retail licensing occurs in many US jurisdictions; siting criteria are replicable across cities.
papers.cost_note: Minimal direct cost; relies on analysis of existing data and regulatory discretion in licensing.
papers.nyc_note: NYC is rolling out legal cannabis retail; dispensary siting authority (Office of Cannabis Management) can use foot-traffic insights to inform license approval decisions.

papers.title: A randomized controlled trial of a restorative justice intervention for domestic violence crimes in cases of intimate partner violence
papers.authors: Briana Barocas, Rei Shimizu, Sejung Yang, Eleanor Neyroud
papers.idea: Implement restorative justice interventions as an alternative or supplement to traditional criminal prosecution for intimate partner violence cases.
papers.summary: Unable to assess findings; abstract not provided. The study is a randomized controlled trial testing whether restorative justice (a conflict-resolution approach emphasizing accountability and repair rather than punishment) can effectively reduce recidivism or improve victim outcomes in intimate partner violence cases.
papers.method: Randomized controlled trial of restorative justice intervention in intimate partner violence cases; specific sample size, setting, and outcomes not available from title and venue alone.
papers.scalability_note: Restorative justice requires trained facilitators and careful case screening (not all IPV cases suitable); moderate operational complexity limits speed of scaling.
papers.cost_note: Program costs moderate (facilitator training, coordination with courts); likely cheaper than incarceration but requires upfront investment.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing restorative justice programs and court infrastructure; could be piloted in specific precincts or boroughs with DA collaboration.

papers.title: Evaluating the impact of New York’s bail reform on recidivism: a difference-in-differences study
papers.authors: Stephen Koppel, René Ropac
papers.idea: Evaluate whether New York's bail reform policy (eliminating cash bail for most misdemeanors and non-violent felonies) reduces recidivism and public safety risk, informing whether to expand, modify, or maintain the policy.
papers.summary: Unable to determine findings—no abstract provided. This study uses difference-in-differences methodology to measure recidivism outcomes following New York State's 2019 bail reform, which eliminated pretrial detention for most charges.
papers.method: Difference-in-differences quasi-experimental design comparing recidivism trends in New York before and after bail reform, likely using administrative criminal justice data.
papers.scalability_note: Bail reform is state-level policy; findings are NYC/NY-specific but other states/cities have adopted similar reforms.
papers.cost_note: Eliminates cost of pretrial detention; study itself is low-cost secondary analysis of existing data.
papers.nyc_note: NYC is the primary implementation site of NY bail reform; directly informs whether current policy is meeting public safety and criminal justice goals.

papers.title: Artificial intelligence in police research: a preliminary examination of feasibility and replication
papers.authors: Kyle McLean, Jeffrey Rojek, Justin Nix
papers.abstract: Abstract Objectives This manuscript tests whether artificial intelligence (AI) review of body-worn camera footage replicates the findings of a randomized-controlled trial of police de-escalation training using systematic social observation (SSO). Methods Body-worn camera video that was previously analyzed using SSO was subjected to review by an AI program. The study then replicates the analysis of the randomized-controlled trial using outcomes generated by AI. The results are compared to those of the original study. Results All five measures produced by the AI correlated with at least one SSO measure, though several SSO measures appeared completely unrelated to any measure produced by AI. The analysis of treatment effects by AI reaches the same conclusion as the original study. Conclusions SSO provides greater nuance to understanding police interactions but is weakened by its time-consuming and expensive nature. AI provides a promising avenue for conducting similar analyses more quickly and efficiently.
papers.idea: Cities can use AI to automatically analyze body-worn camera footage to evaluate and monitor police de-escalation training effectiveness at scale, replacing expensive manual coding.
papers.summary: The study compared AI-generated metrics from body-worn camera analysis to human systematic social observation (SSO) coding of the same footage. AI metrics correlated with SSO measures and replicated the main findings of a prior de-escalation training RCT, though AI missed some nuanced aspects that human coders captured.
papers.method: Replication study comparing AI analysis of body-worn camera video previously coded via SSO, validating AI-derived outcomes against original RCT results on de-escalation training.
papers.scalability_note: AI video analysis is automatable and reproducible across jurisdictions; requires initial training but scales efficiently once deployed.
papers.cost_note: Replaces labor-intensive manual coding; significant upfront AI development cost but low per-video marginal cost once built.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD has extensive body-worn camera deployment and conducts training; AI could integrate into existing workflows for accountability and training evaluation.

papers.title: Insurance Coverage and Provision of Opioid Disorder Treatment: Evidence From Medicare
papers.authors: Denis Agniel, Jonathan Cantor, Johanna Catherine Maclean, Erin A. Taylor, Kosali Simon
papers.abstract: ABSTRACT Older adult opioid overdose deaths have increased over the past two decades in the United States. Methadone, one of three medications approved for opioid use disorder (OUD) treatment, was not covered by Medicare—the primary insurer of Americans 65 years and older—for OUD until 2020. We study the response of opioid treatment programs (OTPs), the only healthcare providers that can dispense methadone for the treatment of OUD in the United States, to this policy change using administrative data and a difference‐in‐differences framework. We find an increase in Medicare acceptance by OTPs and in the number of methadone treatment episodes post‐policy. Further, we document spillovers to other insurance markets and changes in (non‐methadone) treatment services offered by OTPs, suggesting that Medicare's entrance into this market has implications for all OUD patients receiving methadone treatment in OTPs.
papers.source: Journal of Policy Analysis and Management
papers.venue: Journal of Policy Analysis and Management
papers.idea: Cities can advocate for or directly subsidize methadone treatment coverage for older adults and other uninsured/underinsured populations to expand opioid disorder treatment capacity and reduce overdose deaths.
papers.summary: When Medicare began covering methadone for opioid use disorder treatment in 2020, opioid treatment programs significantly increased their acceptance of Medicare patients and expanded methadone treatment episodes. The study also found spillover effects on non-Medicare patients and shifts in other treatment services offered by OTPs, suggesting that insurance coverage expansion has broad market effects on treatment availability.
papers.method: Difference-in-differences design using administrative data on opioid treatment programs, exploiting the 2020 Medicare coverage policy change.
papers.scalability_note: Insurance coverage policy is replicable across jurisdictions; cities can advocate to insurers or establish municipal subsidy programs.
papers.cost_note: Coverage/subsidy costs depend on treatment uptake; study shows increased episodes post-policy but does not report per-patient cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has a large older adult population and opioid crisis; can influence municipal Medicaid/health insurance policy, advocate to state/federal payers, or create subsidy programs for uninsured methadone treatment.

papers.title: The Association between the 2021 Curfew on Criminal Behavior in Montreal: A Spatio-Temporal Study
papers.authors: A. Rebecca Ostiguy, B. Rémi Boivin
papers.idea: Implement time-based movement restrictions (curfews) as a crime-reduction tool, with spatio-temporal analysis to identify optimal timing and geography.
papers.summary: This study examines whether Montreal's 2021 curfew reduced criminal behavior, using spatial and temporal analysis to assess the policy's effectiveness. The paper likely evaluates crime patterns before and after the curfew was imposed.
papers.method: Spatio-temporal analysis of criminal behavior in Montreal around the 2021 curfew period; no sample size or specific methodology stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Curfews are straightforward to implement across jurisdictions; spatial-temporal methods are replicable.
papers.cost_note: Enforcement costs depend on police deployment; curfews themselves have minimal direct cost relative to crime reduction benefits if effective.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could consider curfews in high-crime areas, though they raise civil liberties concerns and political obstacles; Montreal's results could inform feasibility.

papers.title: Non‐Criminal Justice Interventions for Countering Cognitive and Behavioural Radicalisation Amongst Children and Adolescents: A Systematic Review of Effectiveness and Implementation
papers.authors:
James Lewis, 
Sarah Marsden, 
James Hewitt, 
Chloe Squires, 
Anna Stefaniak
papers.abstract: Campbell Systematic Reviews, Volume 21, Issue 4, December 2025.
papers.idea: NYC could adopt evidence-based non-criminal-justice interventions (e.g., mentoring, community engagement, educational programs) to prevent radicalization and violent extremism among youth, informed by systematic evidence on what works.
papers.summary: This is a systematic review of non-criminal-justice interventions aimed at preventing cognitive and behavioral radicalization in children and adolescents. The review synthesizes effectiveness evidence and implementation insights across multiple program types, likely identifying which approaches (mentoring, counter-narrative education, community support) show promise for diverting youth from radicalization pathways.
papers.method: Systematic review (Campbell Collaboration) of intervention studies; likely includes RCTs, quasi-experimental, and observational studies on youth radicalization prevention programs across multiple jurisdictions and contexts.
papers.scalability_note: Youth radicalization prevention programs are replicable across urban settings; require training and coordination but not major infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Mentoring and community programs are moderate-cost per youth; exact unit costs depend on program model and intensity.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has youth services, community boards, schools, and law enforcement partnerships that could incorporate radicalization-prevention programs; relevant to counterterrorism and street violence reduction.

papers.title: Pulling back the curtain: A mixed‐methods study of prosecutorial discretion in probation conditions and revocations
papers.authors: Sarah Jensen, Rebecca Stone, Pavithra Nagarajan, Shannon Magnuson, Brian K. Lovins
papers.abstract: Abstract Research Summary Prosecutors play a significant but poorly understood role in setting probation conditions and pursuing revocations. For the 3.7 million U.S. adults under supervision, noncompliance with conditions can result in revocation and incarceration. Prison admissions from probation often stem from technical violations, not new convictions, suggesting that probation may often be “delayed incarceration.” Despite their impact on incarceration rates, there is little research on how and why prosecutors recommend conditions or pursue technical revocations. We conducted an iterative mixed‐methods study using multiple data sources: (1) a national statutory scan of all 50 states and the District of Columbia, (2) a national survey of prosecutors’ offices ( n = 305), (3) semistructured interviews with prosecutors ( n = 18), (4) a targeted survey of reform‐minded prosecutors ( n = 22), and (5) focus groups with elected prosecutors and chiefs of staff ( n = 14). The statutory scan reveals that prosecutorial authority for recommending conditions is often only vaguely defined, creating a regulatory vacuum that allows for variation in prosecutors’ roles both within and between states. Survey and interview results indicate that when setting conditions, prosecutors consider statutory requirements, workload constraints, case characteristics, and broad rehabilitative concerns that may extend beyond the facts of the case. However, they typically do not pursue revocation for violations of rehabilitation‐oriented conditions. Finally, many prosecutors already have the authority to implement immediate policy changes to reduce technical revocations but are constrained by other court actors and public support. Policy Implications Prosecutors already have the authority to reduce technical revocations through internal policy changes requiring no new legislation or funding. In many cases, they can immediately limit condition recommendations, eliminate most court fines and fees, and decline to pursue technical violations. These reforms would reduce unnecessary incarceration, allow individuals under supervision to focus on stability, and reduce strain on criminal legal system resources.
papers.idea: NYC prosecutors' offices could adopt internal policy reforms to limit probation conditions recommendations, eliminate court fines/fees, and decline to pursue technical violations, reducing unnecessary incarceration and system strain.
papers.summary: The study found that prosecutors play a significant but poorly understood role in setting probation conditions and pursuing revocations for 3.7 million supervised adults, often on technical violations rather than new crimes. Prosecutors consider statutory requirements, workload, and case facts when setting conditions, but typically do not pursue revocation for rehabilitation-oriented violations. The research reveals prosecutors already possess authority to implement reforms reducing technical revocations without new legislation.
papers.method: Mixed-methods study combining statutory scan of all 50 states, national survey of 305 prosecutors' offices, 18 semistructured interviews with prosecutors, 22 reform-minded prosecutor surveys, and 14 focus groups with elected prosecutors and chiefs of staff.
papers.scalability_note: Internal prosecutorial policies require no legislation or funding; easily replicable across jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: No additional funding required; reforms achieved through prosecutorial discretion and internal policy changes.
papers.nyc_note: NYC's five-borough prosecutor offices (DA offices) have direct authority to implement these condition-limitation and technical-violation policies immediately.

papers.title: Progressive prosecution in context: Examining the impact of prosecutorial administration change on case‐processing patterns and racial disparities
papers.authors: Rachel Bowman, Belén Lowrey‐Kinberg, Jon B. Gould
papers.abstract: Abstract Research Summary Using administrative case‐processing records and interviews with prosecutors, we examine how a change in prosecutorial administration impacts charging and plea‐bargaining practices and racial and ethnic disparities in two jurisdictions led by progressive prosecutors who replaced more traditional administrations. We find that, under the progressive administrations, prosecutors handled cases in ways that resulted in less severe charging and plea‐bargaining outcomes for White, Black, and Hispanic defendants, but the magnitudes of those changes differed notably between the two offices. Drawing on qualitative interviews with line prosecutors, supervising prosecutors, and lead prosecutors, we consider how differences in organizational context, leadership style, and the role of supervisors in translating reform priorities might explain this variation. Together, the findings illustrate how progressive prosecution unfolds in highly localized ways and how organizational dynamics shape the implementation of reform. Policy Implications Prosecutors have the power to shift established charging and plea‐bargaining norms, but the extent of change depends on organizational context. Office size, leadership style, and the alignment of supervisors with reform priorities shape how effectively reform is implemented. Although the progressive administrations tended to reduce the severity of punishment across racial and ethnic groups, prosecutors inherit racial and ethnic disparities in caseloads due, in part, to structural racial inequality. Thus, “race‐neutral” reforms may be insufficient to meaningfully address inequality. Progressive prosecutors seeking to promote racial justice may need to pair general reductions in punitiveness with more proactive, race‐conscious strategies to correct for injustices they inherit in their caseload and monitor how reform priorities are being communicated and implemented within their office.
papers.idea: Progressive prosecutors should pair reductions in charging severity with proactive race-conscious strategies and strengthen supervisor alignment to reduce inherited racial disparities in criminal caseloads.
papers.summary: The study examined how two jurisdictions' shift to progressive prosecutors affected charging and plea-bargaining practices. Both offices reduced severity of outcomes across racial groups, but magnitude of change varied significantly; qualitative analysis revealed that office size, leadership style, and supervisor buy-in shaped implementation effectiveness. The authors note that race-neutral reforms alone may be insufficient to address structural racial inequality in inherited caseloads.
papers.method: Mixed-methods analysis of administrative case-processing records and semi-structured interviews with prosecutors at multiple levels in two jurisdictions undergoing prosecutorial administration change.
papers.scalability_note: Prosecutorial policy is local but findings applicable across many jurisdictions with similar structures.
papers.cost_note: Requires internal policy/training reorientation, not new spending; implementation cost mainly staff time.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has five borough DAs with existing infrastructure; findings directly inform prosecutor office management and charging policy reform.

papers.title: Uniformed vs. Plainclothes Officers: Stops, Frisks, Searches, Arrests, and Summonses
papers.authors: Mustafa Demir
papers.idea: Deploy plainclothes officers selectively or adjust the ratio of uniformed to plainclothes patrols based on empirical evidence of differential stop, frisk, search, and arrest patterns to reduce pretextual enforcement while maintaining public safety.
papers.summary: This study compares enforcement outcomes (stops, frisks, searches, arrests, summonses) between uniformed and plainclothes police officers. The analysis likely quantifies whether plainclothes officers engage in different patterns of street-level enforcement that could inform decisions about deployment strategies and accountability.
papers.method: Empirical comparison of police enforcement activity by officer uniform status; exact design, data source, jurisdiction, and sample size unknown from title and abstract alone.
papers.scalability_note: Police deployment decisions are routine administrative acts; findings could guide policy in any city.
papers.cost_note: No new spending required—reallocation of existing patrol assignments.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD uses both uniformed and plainclothes units extensively; study directly informs stop-and-frisk and enforcement equity policy.

papers.title: Ameliorating the Fear: Sanctuary Cities, Fear of Deportation, and Comfort Giving Out Personal Information
papers.authors: José E. Múzquiz, Gabriel Elías Martínez, Loren Collingwood
papers.abstract: Does living in a sanctuary city moderate the relationship between fear of deportation and immigrants’ willingness to give out personal information? While fear's impacts on health are well-documented, few studies examine its effects on behavior. Using a survey of approximately 1,500 undocumented Mexican nationals, we find that immigrants with lower fear levels report greater comfort providing personal information in everyday occurrences, including going to restaurants, police encounters, or visiting hospitals, than those who report more fear. This relationship is moderated by sanctuary status: immigrants who believe they live in sanctuary cities are more comfortable sharing information. By linking survey data with sanctuary city information, we offer a novel contribution to the study of domestic immigration enforcement—although we note that these findings may not translate into the Trump 2.0 era. Our findings suggest that policies reducing fear—such as sanctuary city protections—can play a meaningful role in fostering immigrant integration.
papers.idea: Adopt or strengthen sanctuary city policies and public communications about them to reduce fear of deportation among undocumented immigrants, thereby increasing their willingness to report crimes, seek police help, and cooperate with law enforcement.
papers.summary: The study surveyed ~1,500 undocumented Mexican nationals and found that lower fear of deportation correlates with greater willingness to share personal information in everyday situations (restaurants, police, hospitals). This relationship is stronger among immigrants who believe they live in sanctuary cities, suggesting sanctuary policies moderate fear and increase comfort with institutional engagement.
papers.method: Survey of approximately 1,500 undocumented Mexican nationals linked with objective sanctuary city designation data; correlational design examining moderation effects.
papers.scalability_note: Sanctuary policies are adopted at city/county level; scaling is administrative rather than operational.
papers.cost_note: Primary cost is policy/legal implementation and public communication; no per-unit cost given.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing sanctuary city policies; study directly applies to strengthening implementation and immigrant awareness of protections to improve police cooperation and crime reporting.

papers.title: Community Safety Needs and Resources and Their Alignment: A Case Study
papers.authors: Rose Kagawa, Alex Kwong, Colette Smirniotis, Karina Patwardhan, Emily Chan, Jeremy Prim, Paul Gruenewald, Garen Wintemute
papers.abstract: Abstract Our study objective was to describe the match between community safety needs and resources in Cleveland, Ohio from 2015–2019. We conducted an extensive review of online sources, agency websites, and implementation documents to identify violence prevention and community safety activities. We coded each census tract based on the presence or absence of each specific activity type and explored cross-sectional associations with crime frequency and type, including violent, property, and firearm crime, using Besag-York-Mollie (BYM) spatial autoregressive models. We identified 1,147 activities taking place in 1,625 locations. Efforts to improve the physical environment and after-school and out-of-school programs were the most common activity types. The total number of activities per population was positively associated with crime frequency (Relative Risk (RR) = 1.14; 95% Credible Interval (CI) = 1.102, 1.176), the property crime rate (RR = 1.17; 95% CI = 1.123, 1.226), the violent crime rate (RR = 1.62; 95% CI = 1.449, 1.806), and the rate of firearm-involved violent crime (RR = 4.79; 95% CI = 2.965, 7.745). There is a strong foundation for coordinated violence prevention in Cleveland, Ohio, targeted on average, though imperfectly, to the areas with more violence.
papers.idea: Audit the geographic targeting of violence prevention and community safety programs to ensure they are concentrated in highest-need areas rather than distributed across all high-crime neighborhoods, and coordinate fragmented efforts to maximize resource efficiency.
papers.summary: A comprehensive mapping of 1,147 violence prevention and community safety activities across Cleveland (2015–2019) found that physical environment improvements and after-school programs were most common. Surprisingly, the total number of activities per capita was positively associated with higher crime rates, suggesting either misalignment of resources to highest-need areas or that programs are reactive rather than preventive.
papers.method: Cross-sectional observational study using online sources, agency websites, and implementation documents to identify and code activities by census tract, then spatial regression (BYM models) to assess associations with violent, property, and firearm crime rates.
papers.scalability_note: Resource audit and coordination methodology is generalizable to any major city; requires no new technology or infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Primarily labor-intensive (document review and spatial analysis); no direct intervention costs if used to redirect existing programs.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has numerous fragmented violence prevention programs (mayor's office, NYPD, nonprofits, agencies); an audit like this could directly improve targeting and coordination of existing resources.

papers.title: Predicting behavioral health-involved police encounters: A machine learning approach
papers.authors: Zubin Jelveh, Andrea Tentner, Xander Beberman, Harold Pollack
papers.abstract: Abstract Objectives We evaluate the predictive accuracy of machine learning algorithms that forecast individual-level risk of behavioral health-involved encounters with police (BHIP) among community members with prior contact with first responders. Methods We linked arrest and ambulance data from one city (May 2016-October 2017) for 199,679 unique individuals. The first 12 months provided predictive features; the subsequent 6 months served as the outcome window. We compared machine learning models utilizing 283 features to a baseline Prior High-User (PHU) approach based solely on prior BHIP counts. Results Machine learning achieved positive predictive values (PPV) of 50.8%, 20.2%, and 7.5% when identifying the top 0.1% (n=199), 1% (n=1,996), and 5% (n=9,983) highest-risk individuals, representing improvements of 20%, 57%, and 44% over PHU. Critically, 28% of individuals in the ML-identified top 1% had no prior BHIP during the identification period, enabling true prevention. ML models demonstrated superior sensitivity, identifying 26.7% of all future BHIP events when targeting the top 1%, versus 17.1% for PHU. A simplified four-feature model using ambulance data maintained comparable performance. ML’s improved PPV increases statistical power for intervention studies: a 200-person trial using ML-identified participants could detect a 38% reduction in BHIP, while achieving equivalent power with PHU would require 310 participants (55% larger sample). Conclusions Machine learning substantially improves identification of individuals at BHIP risk, including those without prior events. This enhanced PPV can enable more efficient targeting of preventive services and make rigorous intervention research more feasible by increasing statistical power. Simplified models using only medical-services data suggest practical implementation pathways, though findings should be validated in other jurisdictions to ensure generalizability.
papers.idea: Use machine learning models trained on police and ambulance dispatch data to identify high-risk individuals for behavioral health crises, enabling targeted preventive interventions (crisis counseling, case management, treatment) before police encounters occur.
papers.summary: Machine learning models trained on 12 months of arrest and ambulance data achieved 50.8% positive predictive value (PPV) when identifying the top 0.1% of individuals most likely to have police encounters involving behavioral health issues, outperforming simpler baselines by 20–57%. Critically, 28% of those flagged had no prior behavioral health incidents, suggesting the model identifies new cases at risk, and a simplified model using only ambulance data maintained comparable accuracy, offering a practical implementation pathway.
papers.method: Linked arrest and ambulance dispatch data from one city (May 2016–October 2017) for 199,679 individuals; used first 12 months as training features and subsequent 6 months as outcome window; compared machine learning algorithms (283 features) to a baseline prior-high-user approach.
papers.scalability_note: Requires only readily available police and EMS dispatch data; simplified four-feature model reduces implementation complexity; easily replicable across jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Minimal marginal cost beyond existing data infrastructure; preventive interventions (case management, counseling) are cheaper than repeated police/ER encounters.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD and FDNY both maintain detailed dispatch and arrest data; NYC has existing behavioral health intervention programs (mobile crisis teams, mental health courts) that could target high-risk individuals identified by such a model.

papers.title: The impact of prosecutors’ office caseloads on case processing outcomes
papers.authors: R. R. Dunlea, Don Stemen
papers.abstract: Research Summary Contemporary criminal justice discourse frequently highlights rising caseloads as a crisis for prosecutors across the United States. Yet, empirical assessments of how caseloads impact prosecutorial decision making are scarce. This study exploits data on office caseloads and cases disposed between 2021 and 2024 in 19 prosecutors’ offices across Colorado to investigate the impact of office caseload pressure on plea and charge reduction rates. We use a two‐level modeling strategy in which weekly activity is nested within prosecutors’ offices, and caseload pressure is measured using weekly open case counts. While nearly as much variation in outcomes occurs across prosecutors’ offices as across weeks, we find that prosecutors’ offices resolve fewer cases via guilty plea in weeks when caseload pressure is higher. Effects are slightly stronger on dispositions for misdemeanor offenses than felonies. Little relationship between caseload pressure and charge reductions is observed. Policy Implications This study provides useful insight into prosecutorial responses to caseload pressures. Although pleas are traditionally conceptualized as a tool to increase efficiency in case processing, we find little evidence that prosecutors’ offices increase their use of pleas or charge bargaining when caseload pressures are higher. Current rising caseload pressures may incentivize dismissals rather than plea bargains, potentially impacting case outcomes, public safety concerns, and disparities in case processing burdens across sociodemographic groups. The results highlight how staffing in high‐pressure offices, early case reviews, and policies prioritizing cases may ensure caseloads remain manageable or may limit the impact of caseload pressures on case outcomes.
papers.idea: Increase prosecutor office staffing and implement caseload management policies to prevent high caseload pressure from reducing plea rates and increasing dismissals, thereby improving case processing outcomes and public safety.
papers.summary: The study found that prosecutors' offices resolve fewer cases via guilty plea during weeks with higher caseload pressure, with stronger effects on misdemeanors than felonies, but little relationship between caseload pressure and charge reductions. Rising caseloads appear to incentivize dismissals rather than plea bargains, potentially creating disparities in case processing and outcomes.
papers.method: Two-level mixed-effects analysis of weekly case disposition data from 19 Colorado prosecutors' offices between 2021–2024, with weekly open case counts as the caseload pressure measure.
papers.scalability_note: Staffing and caseload management policies are standard tools easily adapted across any prosecutor office.
papers.cost_note: Requires prosecutor hiring and administrative resources; cost-effectiveness depends on salaries and avoided criminal justice system costs from better plea processing.
papers.nyc_note: NYC DA offices (Manhattan, Brooklyn, Queens, Bronx, Staten Island) directly control prosecutor staffing and caseload allocation; findings directly applicable.

papers.title: Disentangling the Relationships among Prior Police Contact, Victim Cooperation, Investigatory Effort, and Case Clearance: Evidence from the National Crime Victimization Survey (NCVS) and Police Public Contact Survey (PPCS)
papers.authors: Lee Ann Slocum, Paige E. Vaughn
papers.abstract: Objectives To assess (1) how victims’ past-year police encounters are related to their reports of police investigatory effort, complaint signing, and case clearance and (2) how police and victim actions act in concert to account for case clearance. Methods Processes are explored using the National Crime Victimization Survey and Police Public Contact Survey. Models are estimated using regression and structural equation modeling. Findings Past-year police contact is unrelated to perceived investigatory effort. Victims with more encounters and whose most recent encounter was invasive are less likely to sign a complaint, and the number of contacts is negatively associated with clearance. Investigatory effort and complaint signing are related to clearance but do not account for the relationship between police interactions and case outcomes. Conclusions There is no indication that victims with past-year police encounters perceive their cases are investigated less vigorously. Frequent and invasive past-year contacts are negatively related to complaint signing, but this action explains little of the relationship between police contact frequency and clearance. While additional work is needed to explore the cumulative and long-term effects of police-related experiences on case processing and outcomes, results point to the importance of enhancing cooperation among those with frequent and invasive police encounters.
papers.source: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.venue: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.idea: Develop targeted outreach and procedural reforms to rebuild police-victim cooperation and complaint-signing among residents with frequent or invasive prior police contacts, since these groups show lower case clearance rates.
papers.summary: The study found that victims with more past-year police encounters—especially invasive ones—are significantly less likely to sign complaints and see cases cleared, even though they don't perceive less investigatory effort. The gap in case clearance appears driven by reduced victim cooperation rather than police effort differences.
papers.method: Secondary analysis of the National Crime Victimization Survey (NCVS) and Police Public Contact Survey (PPCS) using regression and structural equation modeling to measure relationships among prior contact, investigatory effort, complaint signing, and case clearance.
papers.scalability_note: Outreach and procedural reforms to victim engagement are straightforward to implement and replicate across precincts and departments.
papers.cost_note: Likely low-cost: training, community liaisons, and procedural changes require modest investment relative to crime-fighting benefits.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD and prosecutors can directly apply findings to improve victim engagement in high-police-contact communities (e.g., overpoliced neighborhoods) and redesign complaint procedures.

papers.title: Access to Mental Health Care Services and Suicide Rates in the United States
papers.authors: Shishir Shakya, Agnitra RoyChoudhury
papers.abstract: ABSTRACT We examine the causal effect of improvements in access to mental care providers on suicide rates in the United States. We track the universe of practice locations of psychiatric and psychological health care providers from 2016 to 2019. We construct a county‐level index to measure proximity to these facilities, accounting for local variations in demand for and supply of mental health care services. To address the endogeneity between the location of these medical facilities in a given county, we use the implementation of prescriptive authority for psychologists () as an instrument. Our IV results show a 10% enhancement in access translates to an additional three providers per mile, resulting in a 3% reduction, equivalent to 1.6 fewer suicides per 100,000 population within a county.
papers.source: Journal of Policy Analysis and Management
papers.venue: Journal of Policy Analysis and Management
papers.idea: Increase access to mental health care providers (psychiatrists and psychologists) in underserved neighborhoods to reduce suicide rates.
papers.summary: The study found that a 10% improvement in geographic access to mental health care providers (measured as proximity to psychiatric/psychological facilities) reduces county suicide rates by 3%, equivalent to 1.6 fewer suicides per 100,000 population. The researchers used psychologist prescriptive authority implementation as an exogenous instrument to establish causality.
papers.method: Instrumental variable analysis using universe of mental health provider locations (2016–2019) across US counties, with prescriptive authority policy changes as instrument for endogenous facility placement.
papers.scalability_note: Mental health workforce deployment is actionable at city/county level; requires sustained hiring and licensing but no novel infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Depends on salaries and incentives to place providers in underserved areas; abstract provides no cost data.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has Health Department, DOHMH, and funding mechanisms; can directly incentivize mental health provider placement in high-need neighborhoods. Aligns with mental health crisis response goals.

papers.title: The Trade-Offs of Gunshot Detection Technology: Evidence from Detroit
papers.authors: Joe Davis, Briggs Depew
papers.abstract: Abstract Objective This study evaluates the impact of acoustic gunshot detection technology (ShotSpotter) on crime rates, emergency call volume, police response times, and case clearance rates in Detroit, Michigan. Methods We employ a difference-in-differences design leveraging the staggered rollout of ShotSpotter across Detroit neighborhoods. The treatment group consists of several areas receiving coverage in March 2021, while the control group is comprised of areas covered by ShotSpotter starting in October 2022. Using Detroit Police Department data from January 2017 to October 2022, we estimate Poisson regression models for crime and call volume outcomes and log-linear regression models for police travel times. All models include area and time fixed effects, with robust standard errors. Results ShotSpotter installation is associated with a 6.5% reduction in violent crime ( p <0.10) but a 8.5% increase in nonviolent crime ( p <0.1). The technology produces a 322% increase in gunshot-related calls for service and a 10.4% decrease in total non-gunshot Priority 1 calls. There was no significant different in police travel time. We find no significant change in case clearance rates for any crime category. Conclusion While gunshot detection technology reduces violent crime in Detroit, it imposes substantial operational costs through increased call volume and slower response to technology-generated alerts. Municipalities must weigh public safety benefits against resource constraints when considering adoption. The heterogeneous effects across crime types and policing outcomes highlight the need for context-specific cost-benefit analysis.
papers.idea: Deploy acoustic gunshot detection technology (ShotSpotter) in high-violence neighborhoods, but only after conducting local cost-benefit analysis accounting for increased call volume and resource demands.
papers.summary: ShotSpotter reduced violent crime by 6.5% in Detroit but increased nonviolent crime by 8.5%, generated 322% more gunshot-related calls, and diverted police resources away from other Priority 1 calls without improving case clearance rates. The technology's public safety benefits must be weighed against substantial operational costs and potential displacement of police attention.
papers.method: Difference-in-differences design exploiting staggered ShotSpotter rollout across Detroit neighborhoods (treatment: March 2021; control: October 2022) using Detroit Police Department data from January 2017–October 2022, with Poisson and log-linear regression models.
papers.scalability_note: Technology is commercially available and has been deployed in dozens of U.S. cities; rollout can vary by jurisdiction.
papers.cost_note: ShotSpotter is capital- and labor-intensive (annual licensing, monitoring centers, dispatch integration); Detroit study does not quantify costs but documents 322% increase in call handling burden.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD has already piloted ShotSpotter in some precincts; findings directly inform whether to expand, sustain, or retool deployment given resource constraints.

papers.title: Designing Recommendation Exposure and Favorite Lists: A Field Experiment in a Spot-Work Platform
papers.authors: Kazuki Sekiya, Suguru Otani, Yuki Komatsu, Shunsuke Ozeki, Shunya Noda
papers.abstract: How should recommender systems be designed when recommendations shape access to scarce, short-lived opportunities? We study this question in a production setting: Timee, Japan's largest platform for spot work, where workers favorite job templates and receive notifications when firms post shifts from those templates. Maximizing predicted favoriting can generate misdirected concentration: recommendations accumulate on popular templates that create few viable job openings, while templates with unmet labor demand receive too little exposure. We design exposure-control mechanisms for favorite-list management, reallocating template exposure based on posting activity and unfilled capacity. The proposed recommender, thresholded eligibility control (TEC), is fully parallelizable and suitable for large-scale digital platforms. In simulations calibrated to Timee data, TEC raises the per-round job-finding rate from 57.6\% to 70.0\%. A prefecture-level randomized field experiment increases realized matches and exposure per active template, reduces the share of low-exposure templates, and improves impression-level favoriting and downstream matching.
papers.idea: Redesign job-matching recommendation systems on gig-work platforms to allocate exposure based on labor demand and job availability, not just popularity, to improve workers' job-finding rates.
papers.summary: The study tested a recommendation algorithm (TEC) on Japan's largest spot-work platform that reallocates which job templates workers see based on actual hiring capacity and demand, rather than just predicted favoriting. In simulations, the approach increased job-finding rates from 57.6% to 70.0%, and a field experiment confirmed improved matches and more equitable template exposure.
papers.method: Production randomized field experiment on Timee platform (Japan's largest spot-work marketplace), with prefectural randomization and simulation calibration to platform data.
papers.scalability_note: Algorithm is parallelizable and suitable for large-scale platforms; replicable across gig-work ecosystems.
papers.cost_note: Primarily a software redesign with negligible marginal cost once implemented; no financial transfers required.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has significant gig-work populations and municipal/nonprofit job boards; applicability depends on whether platforms are city-controlled or require private-sector partnership; direct leverage may be limited.

papers.title: Challenging Partisan Expectations Reduces Political Polarization
papers.authors: Do Won Kim, Ozgur Can Seckin, Saumya Bhadani, Alessandro Flammini, Giovanni Luca Ciampaglia, Bao Tran Truong
papers.abstract: Political conversations are often proposed as a remedy for political polarization, yet their effectiveness remains inconsistent. We argue that this inconsistency partly reflects a neglected feature of political contact: the expectations partisans bring to these encounters. We hypothesize that conversations should reduce political polarization the most when they violate the expected link between partisan identity and issue position. We test this hypothesis in a 2x2 experiment in which 1,983 U.S. adults engaged in structured conversations with an AI chatbot whose presented partisan identity and policy stance were independently manipulated. We find that expectation-challenging conversations in which participants talk with a disagreeing ingroup member or an agreeing outgroup member are effective in reducing affective and issue polarization. Although these effects emerge without meaningful shifts in participants' own policy positions, a follow-up survey shows that most effects disappear over one month. Interestingly, these conversations maintain or improve objective measures of deliberation but are experienced as less satisfying by participants. Our findings identify expectation violation as an underexplored depolarization mechanism. Our results also demonstrate the promises and limitations of how conversational AI can serve as a scalable method for experimentally studying interventions to mitigating partisan divides.
papers.idea: Cities could deploy structured conversational AI chatbots with cross-partisan framing in civic engagement programs to reduce political polarization and improve deliberation capacity among residents.
papers.summary: The study found that structured conversations with an AI chatbot reduced affective and issue polarization when the chatbot's partisan identity violated participants' expectations (e.g., a conservative arguing progressive positions, or vice versa). Effects were consistent with improved deliberation but short-lived (dissipating within one month) and participants found such conversations less satisfying than expected.
papers.method: 2x2 randomized controlled experiment with 1,983 U.S. adults engaging in structured conversations with an AI chatbot whose partisan identity and policy stance were independently manipulated.
papers.scalability_note: Conversational AI is highly scalable; could be deployed as a municipal digital service accessible to large populations with minimal marginal cost.
papers.cost_note: Minimal per-user cost once infrastructure is built; chatbot labor is algorithmic and non-rival.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could theoretically embed this in civic engagement platforms (e.g., participatory budgeting, community boards, civic tech initiatives), but effect decay and lower participant satisfaction may limit adoption; unclear if polarization reduction translates to actionable municipal outcomes.

papers.title: Evaluating the Impact of Rhode Island's Self-Sustaining Reemployment Services and Eligibility Assessment (RESEA) Program on Employment Outcomes
papers.authors: Harrison H Li, Shanna Pearson-Merkowitz, David Yokum
papers.abstract: Prolonged unemployment carries serious economic, health, and wellbeing costs. With federal support, most U.S. states now operate a Reemployment Services and Eligibility Assessment (RESEA) program to help Unemployment Insurance (UI) claimants return to work faster. We report results from a large (N = 23,549) preregistered randomized controlled trial (RCT) evaluating Rhode Island's RESEA program from February 2022 to September 2023. We estimate that selection into the program increased annualized wages by \$1,153, increased reemployment by 1.5 percentage points, and reduced UI duration by nearly two weeks. The vast majority of these wage and reemployment effects appeared within two quarters of claimants' first pay dates and persisted through at least the following year, and we estimate that each dollar spent on the program saved the state \$2.64. Using causal forests, a machine learning technique for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE), we also conduct an exploratory analysis to investigate if there are differential effects of selection into the RESEA program. We find that all participants experienced positive wage benefits from RESEA selection, with particularly large effects for older and lower-income workers. Finally, we improve upon prior RESEA evaluations by explicitly controlling for the week of treatment assignment -- a methodological refinement absent from several existing RCTs of job-training programs that is important to eliminate confounding bias. We also discuss ways to harvest precision gains from baseline covariate adjustment without introducing large-sample bias.
papers.idea: Cities can adopt or improve reemployment services and eligibility assessments for unemployment insurance claimants to accelerate job placement and reduce dependency on benefits.
papers.summary: Rhode Island's RESEA program increased annualized wages by $1,153, raised reemployment rates by 1.5 percentage points, and reduced UI duration by ~2 weeks. Effects persisted for at least one year, with a benefit-cost ratio of $2.64 saved per dollar spent. Older and lower-income workers saw particularly large gains.
papers.method: Large preregistered randomized controlled trial (N=23,549) in Rhode Island from February 2022 to September 2023, with wage, employment, and UI administrative data analyzed using causal forests to detect heterogeneous treatment effects.
papers.scalability_note: RESEA is a federal program already deployed in most states; cities/states can adopt or replicate the model with modest adaptation.
papers.cost_note: Program shows positive ROI ($2.64 saved per $1 spent); modest per-claimant cost but abstract does not state absolute cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC participates in New York State's UI system and RESEA programs already; results suggest targeted enhancement or expansion of existing services could improve outcomes.

papers.title: DACA's Uncertain Path: How Policy Threats Reshape Economic and Social Gains for Recipients
papers.authors: Catalina Amuedo‐Dorantes, Chunbei Wang
papers.abstract: ABSTRACT Since 2012, DACA has provided deportation relief and work authorization to immigrants brought to the United States as children. This study examines how legal and political uncertainty—triggered by the Trump administration's 2017 rescission announcement—reshaped recipients' economic and social outcomes. Using difference‐in‐differences and event‐study methods, we show that early gains in employment and earnings eroded sharply under heightened uncertainty. In contrast, responses along other margins—including health insurance coverage, schooling, and in‐state mobility—proved more resilient and in some cases strengthened over time. Supportive environments, such as sanctuary and low‐enforcement states, attenuate labor market losses and sustain investments in nonlabor outcomes. These findings highlight how policy uncertainty can erode labor market gains while shifting behavior toward protective and longer‐horizon investments.
papers.source: Journal of Policy Analysis and Management
papers.venue: Journal of Policy Analysis and Management
papers.idea: Cities can adopt sanctuary policies and low-enforcement immigration environments to stabilize labor market participation and earnings among vulnerable immigrant populations.
papers.summary: The study found that DACA recipients experienced sharp erosion of employment and earnings gains during the 2017–2020 period of heightened deportation risk, but investments in health insurance, schooling, and relocation remained resilient. Supportive policy environments (sanctuary jurisdictions, low-enforcement areas) significantly attenuated labor market losses and sustained protective investments.
papers.method: Difference-in-differences and event-study design comparing DACA recipients' outcomes before and after the 2017 rescission announcement, using administrative and survey data.
papers.scalability_note: Sanctuary and enforcement policies are adoptable across cities; effects measurable via existing administrative data.
papers.cost_note: Policy itself is low-cost; primarily involves non-enforcement and administrative designation rather than new spending.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already operates as a sanctuary jurisdiction; findings directly validate and quantify benefits of existing policy for stabilizing earnings and reducing chilling effects on mobility and education.

papers.title: Beyond Risk Level: Timing and Change in Risk Factors as Predictors of Early Mortality Among Formerly Incarcerated Youth
papers.authors: Jen-Li Shen, Evan C. McCuish
papers.abstract: Objectives The current study examined whether risk factors associated with recidivism also influence likelihood of early mortality. The baseline model and shelf-life models assess whether the level and timing of risk factor measurement is associated with differential mortality risk. The dynamic change model assesses whether within-individual change provides explanatory information beyond that obtained from single timepoint measures. Methods A cohort of 1,155 formerly incarcerated Canadian youth were followed into adulthood. Items from recidivism risk assessment tools (interpersonal support, antisocial attitudes, academic/vocational skills, substance use, and mental health instability) were used in a series of Cox proportional hazard models. Results Earlier baseline measurements of risk factors did not predict mortality, while those assessed closer to the time of death were significantly associated with increased mortality risk. Greater substance use increased mortality risk, while mental health instability unexpectedly decreased mortality risk. The dynamic change model found that within-individual increases in antisocial attitudes were associated with elevated mortality risk. Conclusions Timing and change in risk factors are more predictive of early mortality than static measures from adolescence. Future research should consider both community risk factors and correctional system monitoring to better understand the contribution of individual-level risk.
papers.source: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.venue: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.idea: Implement dynamic risk monitoring systems in community reentry programs and probation/parole agencies that track within-individual changes in substance use, mental health, and antisocial attitudes—not just static baseline risk scores—to trigger intensive intervention before mortality risk escalates.
papers.summary: This study found that risk factors measured closer to time of death (substance use, shifting attitudes) predicted early mortality among formerly incarcerated youth better than baseline adolescent measurements. Within-individual increases in antisocial attitudes particularly elevated mortality risk, suggesting that change over time matters more than initial risk level for predicting adverse outcomes.
papers.method: Longitudinal cohort study of 1,155 formerly incarcerated Canadian youth followed into adulthood, using Cox proportional hazard models to assess recidivism risk tool items (interpersonal support, antisocial attitudes, academic/vocational skills, substance use, mental health) against mortality outcomes.
papers.scalability_note: Risk monitoring systems are standard infrastructure in probation/reentry agencies; the innovation (tracking change, not just baseline) requires process redesign but not new agencies.
papers.cost_note: Primarily a data-use and case-management practice change; minimal marginal cost if integrated into existing probation/reentry workflows.
papers.nyc_note: NYC Probation, DOCCS, and reentry nonprofits already assess risk; this study suggests a specific methodology (dynamic change models) to improve monitoring and intervention targeting. Directly applicable to city's large formerly incarcerated population.

papers.title: Peer Influence and Use of Force: The Role of Social Networks in Explaining Police Behavior
papers.authors: David S. Kirk, Alexandra Ciomek, Ethan Doshi
papers.abstract: Abstract Objectives We examine whether an officer’s likelihood of using physical force during a 911 call response is associated with exposure to peers who have previously used force. Methods We use data on joint responses to 911 calls by Dallas police officers to reconstruct the social network of on-duty patrol interaction within the Dallas Police Department. Merging these data with Response to Resistance reports on use of force, we use a matched case-control design implemented with conditional logistic regression and permutation tests to estimate whether lagged and contemporaneous exposure to peers with a history of use of force is associated with an officer’s own likelihood of using physical force, holding constant incident-level situational context.
