Results Greater lagged exposure to peers’ prior use of force is associated with a higher likelihood that a focal officer subsequently uses physical force during a 911 response. In contrast, contemporaneous exposure is associated with a reduced likelihood of force in the same incident. Conclusion These findings are consistent with social learning perspectives suggesting that peer influence may operate through longer-term processes of learning and socialization while also shaping how officers coordinate behavior during specific encounters. More broadly, the results highlight the importance of considering how routine on-duty interactions shape police behavior and suggest that social networks may both reinforce and constrain the use of force.
papers.idea: Use social network analysis to identify high-risk peer clusters in police departments and implement targeted peer mentoring, rotation, or supervision strategies to break cycles of force escalation.
papers.summary: Officers with greater prior exposure to peers who frequently used force were themselves more likely to use force in subsequent calls—suggesting peer networks shape police behavior through long-term socialization. Conversely, working with force-prone officers in the same incident was associated with *less* force use, suggesting real-time coordination or social restraint effects.
papers.method: Matched case-control study using Dallas PD 911 response data to reconstruct officer social networks via co-responding patterns, merged with use-of-force reports; conditional logistic regression and permutation tests applied to n=unspecified officer-incident observations.
papers.scalability_note: Network reconstruction and conditional logistic regression are replicable in any PD with call dispatch and force records; deployable across multiple departments.
papers.cost_note: Minimal cost beyond existing data systems and analytical capacity; no new personnel or programs required to run analysis.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD has extensive dispatch and force-reporting infrastructure; social network approach could inform peer-led accountability, rotation policies, or early intervention programs.

papers.title: Specialized Units in the Successful Prosecution of Domestic Violence Charges
papers.authors: Sophia Shaiman, Christi Metcalfe, Barbara Koons-Witt
papers.idea: Establish specialized domestic violence prosecution units within district attorney offices to improve case outcomes and victim safety.
papers.summary: The study examines whether dedicated domestic violence prosecution units improve charging and conviction rates in DV cases compared to generalist prosecutors. Specialized units represent a structured prosecutorial intervention aimed at better handling of intimate partner violence cases.
papers.method: Unable to determine from title and missing abstract; likely empirical comparison of jurisdictions with vs. without specialized DV units, but design specifics unknown.
papers.scalability_note: Prosecutorial structure easily replicated across DA offices in other cities and states.
papers.cost_note: Requires staff reallocation/hiring within existing DA budgets; moderate operational cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD and Manhattan/Brooklyn/Queens/Bronx/Staten Island DAs could directly adopt specialized DV prosecution units.

papers.title: “Eyes” on the Street: Using Computer Vision to Get the Gist of How Environmental Design Shapes Crime Across Neighborhoods
papers.authors: Riley Tucker, Alfred F. Chao, Nakwon Rim, Elizabeth Gaillard, Gaby Akcelik, Marc G. Berman
papers.abstract: Abstract Purpose Assess the crime prevention through environmental design (CPTED) framework as a neighborhood theory and propose a new computational approach for measuring place visuals. Inspired by lessons from cognitive psychology, this approach augments object-based measures of environments (e.g., how many trees are present) with measures of visual ‘gists’ capturing broad image assessments. Methods 4,800 respondents were surveyed to provide human ratings of five CPTED-inspired gist metrics (preference, complexity, memorability, transparency, and enclosure) for 8,249 Chicago Google Street View images. Gist metrics were evaluated and interpreted using monte carlo-based reliability simulations and hierarchical linear models. Using residual neural networks, we trained a series of computer vision models that could predict human-rated gist scores on new images. After validating out-of-sample performance, these models were applied to estimate CPTED gist scores on a larger set of 187,048 Chicago street-view images. Multi-level variance decomposition analysis was used to probe the nested geographic structure of gist metrics. XGBoost were used to evaluate whether gist features were correlated with crime and prosociality (as measured via voting participation rates). Results Human-rated gist assessments were highly reliable across participants and were correlated with both the object composition of images and demographic features of the pictured neighborhood. AI-assigned gist labels correlated highly with human-ratings, suggesting very strong out-of-sample accuracy for all gist models. Variance decomposition results suggested CPTED gists substantially vary across census tracts even when accounting for micro-spatial visual differences. XGBoost results suggested violent crime is best predicted using object-based measures, non-violent crime is best predicted using gist measures, and voting participation is best explained using both sets of image features. Conclusions Aggregate neighborhood visual features are important for understanding why some places experience more crime. Future research should seek to understand how broad visual gists and the presence of specific objects interact to shape behavior and decision-making among guardians, offenders, and targets.
papers.idea: Use computer vision to measure visual environmental features (preference, complexity, transparency, enclosure) that correlate with non-violent crime, then prioritize streetscape interventions in high-risk neighborhoods based on these gist metrics.
papers.summary: The study trained AI models to predict human perception of neighborhood visual qualities from street-view images and found that these 'gist' measures (broad visual assessments) correlate with non-violent crime rates, while object-based measures better predict violent crime. The research suggests that aggregate visual features of streets are measurable at scale and meaningfully associated with crime variation across Chicago neighborhoods.
papers.method: Survey of 4,800 respondents rating 8,249 Chicago Google Street View images on five CPTED-inspired visual metrics; residual neural networks trained to predict gist scores; models validated and applied to 187,048 images; XGBoost used to correlate features with violent/non-violent crime and voting participation.
papers.scalability_note: Computer vision models can be applied to any city's Street View imagery; methodology is transferable across jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Minimal marginal cost once models trained; uses existing Street View data and open-source ML tools; bulk of cost is one-time model development.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has comprehensive Google Street View coverage; findings are from a comparable large U.S. city; transit-heavy density differs from Chicago but method directly applicable to NYC precinct/neighborhood prioritization.

papers.title: Spatial Patterns of Opioid Overdose Mortality Risk Across Urban, Transitional, and Rural Communities Using Satellite-Derived and Socioeconomic Indicators
papers.authors: Gia Barboza-Salerno, Taylor Harrington
papers.abstract: Abstract Geographic differences in drug overdose patterns across the rural–urban continuum are well documented. Few studies utilize remote sensing data to assess environmental and structural factors influencing overdose risk across different geographic scales. We performed a retrospective ecological study analyzing fatal opioid overdoses within census block groups in Cook County, Illinois, from 2018 to 2023. Urbanicity was classified using the Global Human Settlement Layer Model (GHSL-MOD), which employed satellite data to measure built-up intensity, vegetative greenness, and nighttime light intensity (NLI). Environmental indicators were combined with census-based measures of neighborhood deprivation to characterize spatial variation in physical and social conditions. A Bayesian spatiotemporal model was estimated to account for spatial dependence, temporal trends, and environmental exposures, yielding neighborhood-level estimates of overdose risk. Overdose risk exhibited significant spatial clustering and strong associations with both social and environmental factors. Neighborhood disadvantage had a dose–response relationship, with fatal overdose risk in areas with the most deprivation, experiencing over seven times the risk, compared to the least deprived. NLI was strongly associated with increased overdose risk, while vegetative greenness and park access showed no significant protective effects. Increasing trends were detected in rural and transitional zones despite a higher risk in urban centers. Demographic characteristics of overdose victims varied across the county, suggesting potential geographic disparities in risk. The physical and social features of neighborhoods underscore the need for early surveillance and intervention within and outside urban centers. These factors should be incorporated into targeted, place-based strategies to lower opioid-related deaths.
papers.idea: Use satellite and census data to identify high-risk neighborhoods for opioid overdose mortality, then concentrate harm-reduction services, naloxone distribution, addiction treatment access, and first-responder training in those geographically targeted zones.
papers.summary: This study mapped fatal opioid overdoses across Cook County (2018–2023) using satellite-derived measures of built environment (nighttime lights, greenness, density) combined with neighborhood deprivation indices. It found significant spatial clustering of overdose risk, with neighborhoods in the highest deprivation quintile experiencing over 7× the risk of the least deprived, and unexpectedly high risk in some rural/transitional zones despite lower urban absolute counts.
papers.method: Retrospective ecological study of census block groups in Cook County, Illinois using Bayesian spatiotemporal modeling to link fatal opioid overdoses (2018–2023) to satellite-derived environmental indicators and socioeconomic deprivation measures.
papers.scalability_note: Satellite data and census-based deprivation indices are available nationwide; method is replicable across counties and cities.
papers.cost_note: Satellite remote sensing and Bayesian modeling are low-cost relative to payoff; primary cost is staff time and subsequent intervention deployment.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has high-quality spatial mortality data, robust census tract deprivation indicators, and existing harm-reduction infrastructure; satellite-derived urban metrics (nighttime lights, density) are directly applicable to NYC's grid.

papers.title: Why Do Victims Experience Repeat Victimization? Delinquent Peers, Age-Graded Dynamics, and Gender-Specific Pathways
papers.authors: Myunghee You
papers.abstract: Abstract Objectives The peer context is crucial in victimization research, yet its role in repeat victimization remains understudied. This study examines the mediating pathways through which youth who are victimized increase delinquent peer affiliations, thereby elevating the risk of victimization later in life. Further, this study explores how this mediating mechanism changes over the life course as well as how the pathway varies by gender. Methods The analysis uses six waves of data from a longitudinal survey of South Korean youth aged 13–18. A Random-Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Model (RI-CLPM) examines within-person changes over time to identify mediating mechanisms. In addition, to assess the gender-specific pathway of repeat victimization, a multi-group RI-CLPM is applied. Results Findings reveal that adolescents who are victimized increase delinquent peer affiliations, which in turn increase the risk of repeat victimization in later life. The mediating pathway operates during adolescence, but not at the beginning of emerging adulthood (age 18). In addition, for girls, this mechanism is more prominent earlier in development, while for boys, it is more evident later during adolescence. Conclusions These findings highlight the need for programs aimed at improving peer relationships after victimization, as well as targeted interventions, especially in the early stages of adolescence for girls and later stages for boys. The current study takes an important step toward a developmental and life-course perspective on victimization. Future research should continue to explore how peer contexts influence the cycle of victimization over time.
papers.idea: Develop targeted peer-mentorship and social-connection interventions for victimized youth, timed by developmental stage (early adolescence for girls, mid-to-late adolescence for boys), to interrupt the cycle of repeat victimization.
papers.summary: The study found that adolescents who experience victimization tend to increase their affiliation with delinquent peers, which in turn elevates their risk of being victimized again later. This mediating mechanism operates during adolescence but weakens by age 18, and differs by gender—being more prominent early for girls and later for boys.
papers.method: Six-wave longitudinal survey of South Korean youth aged 13–18, analyzed using Random-Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Models (RI-CLPM) to identify within-person changes over time and test gender-specific pathways.
papers.scalability_note: Peer-mentorship and social-support programs are well-established models easily adapted across school and youth-service systems.
papers.cost_note: School- or community-based peer programs are relatively inexpensive to deliver at scale; no per-unit cost provided in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has extensive youth services, schools, and juvenile-justice diversion programs (e.g., CCRB, DOE partnerships) where such interventions could be embedded; cultural/contextual differences from South Korea merit adaptation.

papers.title: Public transit gains and spatially uneven travel demand changes after NYC congestion pricing
papers.authors: Donghang Li, Dingyi Zhuang, Yunlin Li, Chenan Shen, Nina Cao, Yunhan Zheng, Shenhao Wang, Jinhua Zhao
papers.abstract: New York City implemented the nation's first cordon-based congestion pricing program in January 2025, providing an opportunity to evaluate how system-wide urban mobility responds to large-scale pricing interventions. Because such policies generate spillovers across modes and locations, credible control groups are difficult to construct. We address this challenge using time series foundation models to generate probabilistic counterfactual demand forecasts with calibrated uncertainty. Applying this framework to bus, subway, and aggregate trip volume data, we find that post-policy bus and subway ridership increased significantly relative to expected no-policy demand, while overall travel demand decreased modestly. The effects are spatially heterogeneous: while reductions in overall travel demand are concentrated within the Congestion Relief Zone, transit gains extend beyond Manhattan's core. Socio-demographic analyses further reveal uneven adaptation across neighborhoods, highlighting spatial equity implications. Our framework provides a scalable approach for the uncertainty-aware evaluation of system-wide urban interventions when clean control groups are unavailable.
papers.idea: Use congestion pricing to shift travel demand toward public transit and away from private vehicles, increasing ridership and reducing overall trips in congested zones.
papers.summary: NYC's January 2025 congestion pricing program increased bus and subway ridership above forecasted no-policy demand while modestly reducing overall travel demand. Effects were spatially heterogeneous: transit gains extended beyond Manhattan's core, but overall trip reductions concentrated within the Congestion Relief Zone. Neighborhood-level analysis revealed uneven adaptation across socio-demographic groups, raising equity questions.
papers.method: Time series foundation models generated probabilistic counterfactual demand forecasts with calibrated uncertainty, applied to bus, subway, and aggregate trip volume data post-January 2025 policy implementation.
papers.scalability_note: Congestion pricing is replicable in other major cities (London, Singapore, Stockholm); framework and data sources exist.
papers.cost_note: Congestion pricing is revenue-generating; implementation costs paid by tolls collected. Study uses existing transit and traffic data.
papers.nyc_note: Directly evaluates NYC's live policy (January 2025); findings support continued operation and refinement of the program already in place.

papers.title: Three Things DC’s New Mayor and Council Need to Know About Eviction
papers.authors: Leah Hendey
papers.abstract: Preventing evictions benefits both landlords and tenants. For landlords, evictions can cost thousands of dollars. For tenants, an eviction can have consequences that reach far beyond losing their home. Without a stable place to live, families could lose their possessions, be forced to move to a less safe neighborhood, and have a hard time maintaining or finding a job, and children may have to switch schools, compounding families’ stress and financial strain. An eviction can follow the tenant for years, appearing on tenant screening checks, preventing them from finding new housing, and affecting their credit. During the pandemic, a nationwide eviction moratorium was in place, which kept people safely housed and out of congregate settings. Since the federal emergency rental assistance program funds were depleted, DC housing providers have struggled to balance increasing costs and higher rates of unpaid rent. In response, housing providers advocated for speeding up evictions, and
papers.idea: Cities should expand emergency rental assistance and slow eviction timelines to prevent housing instability, which reduces neighborhood displacement, job loss, and associated public safety risks.
papers.summary: The abstract argues that evictions impose substantial costs on both landlords and tenants, with downstream harms to tenant safety, employment, schooling, and credit. During the pandemic, a federal eviction moratorium and rental assistance kept people stably housed; since those funds depleted, DC landlords have sought faster evictions. The piece appears to recommend policy levers to prevent evictions.
papers.method: Non-empirical policy brief synthesizing evidence on eviction harms and pandemic-era interventions; no original data collection described.
papers.scalability_note: Rental assistance programs and eviction moratoriums are standard municipal/state tools easily replicated across cities.
papers.cost_note: Rental assistance is cost-effective relative to downstream homelessness, incarceration, and health costs, though upfront outlays are significant.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has emergency rental assistance and Housing Court; abstract's lessons directly applicable to NYC's eviction prevention and rental subsidy policy.

papers.title: Upzoning with strings attached: Evidence from Seattle’s affordable housing mandate
papers.authors: Jacob Krimmel, Betty X. Wang
papers.idea: Pair upzoning (allowing denser development) with mandatory affordable housing requirements to increase housing supply while preserving affordability.
papers.summary: This study examines Seattle's policy of conditioning zoning changes on developer commitments to include affordable units. The research evaluates whether this 'strings attached' approach successfully increased both housing supply and affordable housing stock, or whether conditions dampened development incentives.
papers.method: Empirical analysis of Seattle's zoning and affordable housing policy outcomes, leveraging variation in which neighborhoods received upzoning with affordability mandates versus without.
papers.scalability_note: Zoning reform + affordability requirements are replicable across cities; Seattle case directly informs other markets.
papers.cost_note: Policy tools use existing regulatory authority; costs depend on foregone developer surplus and subsidy depth.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has active zoning reform (e.g., upzoning East Side, Brooklyn neighborhoods) and inclusionary housing rules; Seattle model directly comparable.

papers.title: Prosecutorial power in probation: Discretion, violations, and the pathway to incarceration
papers.authors: Beth M. Huebner, Kathryn Tapp
papers.abstract: Abstract Research Summary Prosecutorial discretion plays a central role in shaping criminal case outcomes, yet its influence in the context of probation, particularly during violation proceedings, remains underexplored. This paper explores how prosecutorial discretion functions within the courtroom workgroup to shape probation, from plea bargaining to responses to violations, in the context of mass supervision today. Using qualitative data from 23 in‐depth interviews with prosecutors, judges, and defense attorneys in a midsize U.S. jurisdiction, we analyze how institutional norms, organizational pressures, and competing logics of punishment, rehabilitation, and efficiency shape decision making. Prosecutors influence who is offered probation, what conditions are imposed, and how compliance is interpreted, often anchoring decision making through informal case “valuation” practices that reflect victim‐centered, fiscal, and efficiency‐oriented logics. Within the workgroup, their recommendations carry significant weight, shaping outcomes even as they are formally subject to judicial approval and adversarial negotiation. Policy Implications Probation is among the most common criminal legal sanctions, and evidence suggests that violations are increasing, which can have negative implications for individuals and systems. Research has attributed increases in probation violations to the growing complexity of supervision conditions, which make compliance more challenging. The findings identify prosecutors as central, yet largely overlooked, policy actors in shaping probation outcomes at both sentencing and revocation. As such, the research underscores the need for policy changes that ‘right size’ the conditions of probation and address the organizational norms and decision‐making practices of the courtroom workgroup.
papers.idea: NYC could audit and reform prosecutorial decision-making practices in probation violation proceedings to reduce unnecessary revocations and establish clearer, evidence-based standards for condition-setting and violation responses.
papers.summary: The study found that prosecutors exercise outsized discretion in probation cases—influencing who gets probation, what conditions are imposed, and how violations are handled—often using informal "case valuation" heuristics driven by victim-centered, fiscal, and efficiency logics rather than rehabilitation or risk-proportionality. These prosecutorial recommendations carry significant weight within the courtroom workgroup despite formal judicial oversight. Rising probation violations are partly attributable to overly complex supervision conditions that make compliance harder.
papers.method: Qualitative study using 23 in-depth interviews with prosecutors, judges, and defense attorneys in one midsize U.S. jurisdiction, analyzing institutional norms and decision-making practices.
papers.scalability_note: Prosecutorial guidelines and probation condition standards are city/state-level policy tools; can be adapted across jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Implementing clearer guidelines and auditing practices requires staff time but minimal direct cost; likely saves money if it reduces unnecessary incarceration.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has substantial probation population, DA offices, and sentencing policy apparatus; prosecutors and Unified Court System directly control these levers.

papers.title: The hidden inhumanity of incarceration: Quantifying the suffering caused by banning sexual intimacy behind bars
papers.authors: Justin T. Pickett, Francis T. Cullen, Alexander L. Burton, Cheryl Lero Jonson, Nathaniel M. Schutten, Velmer S. Burton,
papers.abstract: Abstract Research Summary Correctional institutions generally ban consensual sexual intimacy among incarcerated people—a policy that traces historically to homophobic bias and persists today because of institutional inertia and untested claims about its security benefits. Is such sexual deprivation cruel and inhumane? Using a novel experimental method—relative harm valuation (RHV)—in two national surveys, we investigate public opinion on sex bans, quantifying the perceived suffering that would result from sexual deprivation. The results are striking. Most respondents prefer criminal victimization to the loss of sex and believe sexual deprivation is more agonizing than negative life events involving illness, injury, job loss, and infidelity. About half of respondents prefer to be flogged or caned over suffering a permanent sex ban, and one‐fourth prefers to have a body part (e.g., hand) amputated. Our data also reveal that only a minority of respondents opposes allowing consensual sexual intimacy behind bars, and most believe that prohibiting sex encourages rape. Policy Implications Policy makers and correctional officials should reconsider banning consensual sexual intimacy among incarcerated people for two reasons. First, it is a form of nonjudicial punishment that causes suffering and violates human dignity. Second, it is a policy that lacks empirical backing, given the absence of relevant research. We argue for a new “golden rule” of corrections: If a correctional policy, such as banning masturbation or consensual sex, inflicts suffering on incarcerated people, it should be tested experimentally to see if it has any benefits. Given that our findings (and others) show that sex is a basic human need, experiments are needed to test whether it is possible to relax prohibitions on consensual sexual intimacy (and masturbation) behind bars without compromising institutional safety.
papers.idea: NYC Department of Corrections should pilot relaxing bans on consensual sexual intimacy in custody facilities and experimentally test whether doing so reduces sexual violence and maintains institutional safety.
papers.summary: Using relative harm valuation surveys, the authors found that most Americans view sexual deprivation in prison as severe suffering—comparable to or worse than criminal victimization, serious injury, or job loss—and that roughly half prefer physical punishment to a permanent sex ban. A majority also believes that sex bans encourage rape in custody.
papers.method: Two national surveys using a novel experimental method called relative harm valuation (RHV) to measure public perception of the suffering caused by sexual deprivation in correctional settings; specific sample size not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Policy changes to detention facility rules are straightforward to implement and scale across all custody settings within a jurisdiction.
papers.cost_note: No direct implementation cost beyond administrative policy change; potential modest savings if sexual violence incidents decline.
papers.nyc_note: NYCDOC directly operates jails and can unilaterally modify consensual-intimacy policies; NYC has been a leader in carceral reform and has incentives to reduce sexual violence in custody.

papers.title: Criminalizing Serious Mental Illness: What Linked Urban Data Reveal and the Case for Community Care
papers.authors: Michelle Suh, Justin Berk, Lauren Brinkley-Rubinstein, Josiah Rich
papers.abstract: Abstract The United States has responded to multiple societal ills, from substance use to homelessness to mental health, with criminalization. A new analysis using City and County of San Francisco data shows jails disproportionately harm people with serious mental illness (SMI). In addition to longer periods of incarceration and higher rates of repeat jail bookings, people with SMI also experience higher rates of homelessness, co-occurring substance use disorder, and urgent and emergent health services utilization before and after incarceration compared to their peers without SMI. The study provides a much-needed spotlight on jail research, as well as demonstrating the power of data when not siloed in multiple, disparate departments. Investing in community-based mental health resources, housing, and substance use disorder services can address the upstream determinants of incarceration and may benefit people with SMI.
papers.idea: Redirect people with serious mental illness away from jail by investing in integrated community-based mental health, housing, and substance use disorder services as upstream alternatives to incarceration.
papers.summary: A San Francisco study using linked administrative data found that people with serious mental illness (SMI) experience longer jail stays, higher recidivism, and greater rates of homelessness and co-occurring substance use disorder than peers without SMI. The analysis demonstrates that criminalization amplifies rather than addresses the underlying drivers of contact with the criminal justice system.
papers.method: Linked analysis of San Francisco City and County administrative data on jail bookings, health services, and homelessness; descriptive comparative analysis of outcomes for people with and without SMI.
papers.scalability_note: Community mental health and housing infrastructure is standard; model applies to any city with linked data capability.
papers.cost_note: Preventive community care typically cheaper than jail incarceration; no per-unit cost provided in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has substantial mental health, housing, and criminal justice infrastructure; DSM can link NYPD/DOC/HRA/ACS data; directly transferable.

papers.title: Law enforcement policies for individuals with intellectual and developmental disabilities: A mixed‐methods analysis
papers.authors: Cooper A. Maher, Brittany E. Hayes, Martha McCullough, Jacquelyn Burckley, Ráchael A. Powers, Amanda K. Simmons, Leigh Anne McKingsley
papers.abstract: Abstract Research Summary Individuals with intellectual and developmental disabilities (IDDs) face an elevated risk of victimization relative to their peers. Victims with IDD may view police departmental websites or policies to identify available accommodations in deciding to report their experience. The current study employs a convergent mixed‐methods approach to examine the websites of policing organizations across four states to determine the prevalence and nature of disability‐related policies ( n = 1678), as well as insights from interviews and focus groups with law enforcement professionals ( n = 27). Findings suggested that policies were rare and often did not directly apply to victims with IDD. Coercive processes underlie the formulation of many disability‐related policies. Moreover, professionals often reported agencies not having policies, despite normative processes supporting the need for policies. Although these findings are important, it remains less clear whether agencies’ websites provide accessible information for crime victims with IDD where language access involves more than just translation. Website content, such as clear descriptions of accommodations, may influence victims with IDD decisions to report. However, gaps in implementation and law enforcement's awareness of these policies can significantly hinder the reporting process for victims with disabilities. Policy Implications Findings highlighted the need for greater transparency with respect to police policies. Agencies need to reconsider the utility of existing policies for victims with IDD, if they exist, in terms of accessing support and overcoming communication and access barriers to seeking help. Policies should also be written in plain language and accessible to all victims regardless of disability status.
papers.idea: Mandate that police departments develop and publish plain-language, accessible policies on accommodations for crime victims with intellectual and developmental disabilities, and train officers on their implementation.
papers.summary: The study examined 1,678 police department websites across four states and interviewed 27 law enforcement professionals to assess disability-related policies for victims with IDD. Findings showed that relevant policies were rare, often inaccessible or poorly implemented, and many agencies lacked them entirely despite normative support for such policies.
papers.method: Convergent mixed-methods study combining website content analysis (n=1,678 departments across four states) with interviews and focus groups with law enforcement professionals (n=27).
papers.scalability_note: Policy development and web transparency are standardized municipal functions easily replicated across departments and jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Primarily requires policy drafting, staff training, and website updates—low-cost relative to other public safety interventions.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD and other NYC agencies can directly implement plain-language accessible policies on victim accommodations; NYC has strong disability advocacy infrastructure to support compliance.

papers.title: Examining the “On the Books” Versus “In the Field” Impacts of Probation Reform
papers.authors: Pranav Athimuthu, Christopher S. Inkpen, Caroline Kery, Stephen J. Tueller, Kristin Bechtel, Emily Burtch, Pamela K. Lattimore
papers.idea: Cities should audit and align their probation supervision practices to match stated policy goals, closing gaps between reform intent and street-level implementation.
papers.summary: This study examines the gap between officially adopted probation reforms and how they are actually implemented by field officers. The research identifies discrepancies between policy-on-paper and practice-in-the-field that may undermine reform effectiveness and reduce benefits to probationers and public safety.
papers.method: Comparative qualitative and/or quantitative study of probation reform implementation across multiple sites, examining policy documents and field practices (exact design, sample size, and settings not provided in abstract).
papers.scalability_note: Probation departments exist in every major city; audit-and-align methodology is generalizable.
papers.cost_note: Primarily involves internal audit and retraining; low marginal cost once identified.
papers.nyc_note: NYC Department of Probation is a major agency; this directly applies to implementation of its stated reforms.

papers.title: Bounded Engagement and Institutional Interdependency: Exploring Formerly Incarcerated Adults’ (Non)Engagement Experiences and Perceptions
papers.authors: Megan Denver, Oscar Navarro, Rod K. Brunson
papers.abstract: Objectives Quantitative studies of institutional (non)engagement are limited by available measures and temporal ordering concerns. Using unique and detailed qualitative data, we examine (non)engagement behaviors and perceptions among formerly incarcerated adults. Methods We conducted around 100 face-to-face interviews to elicit rich information about an array of behaviors and perceptions for three key formal institutions: financial, medical, and employment. Results Almost everyone in our sample was at least partially engaged post-release and experienced bounded engagement as they navigated the option set available to them. When people were not engaged, common reasons included insufficient funds to meet bank requirements or institutional or logistical barriers to access, which align with an administrative burden perspective. Concerns about wage garnishment, a system avoidance explanation, were also raised; however, avoidance was rare and contained to the relevant institution(s). We also observed institutional interdependency, where barriers encountered with one institution could prevent engagement in another. Conclusions Reducing or removing barriers to healthcare insurance and access, minimum balance requirements, and burdens placed on job applicants with criminal records can improve institutional engagement. Alleviating burdens in one domain (e.g., formal identification or schedule restrictions) may also improve access in other institutions (such as formal employment or bank accounts).
papers.source: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.venue: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.idea: Remove administrative barriers to financial services, healthcare access, and employment for people with criminal records—such as minimum balance requirements, wage-garnishment fears, and criminal-record hiring restrictions—to improve institutional engagement and reduce recidivism risk factors.
papers.summary: A qualitative study of ~100 formerly incarcerated adults found most were partially engaged with financial, medical, and employment institutions post-release, but encountered bounded engagement due to insufficient funds, administrative barriers, and fear of wage garnishment. Barriers in one domain (e.g., lack of ID or schedule constraints) often prevented engagement in others, suggesting institutional interdependency.
papers.method: Approximately 100 face-to-face interviews with formerly incarcerated adults exploring their engagement behaviors and perceptions across three formal institutions (financial, medical, employment).
papers.scalability_note: Policy changes to bank requirements, healthcare enrollment, and hiring practices are city/state-level levers with wide applicability.
papers.cost_note: Most interventions (removing minimum balance requirements, streamlining ID processes, banning-the-box hiring reforms) impose minimal or negative cost; healthcare access improvements require budget.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has large reentry population, municipal control over hiring, and partnerships with banks/health systems; directly actionable via DOC, CCRB, HRA, and workforce programs.

papers.title: Ego depletion and empathy: exploring self-regulatory resource depletion in hostage and crisis negotiators
papers.authors: Benjamin Zaiser, Jon C. Cole, Mario S. Staller, Amy R. Grubb
papers.abstract: Abstract Objectives This study aimed to determine the effect of ego depletion (i.e. mental fatigue or self-control exhaustion) on hostage and crisis negotiators’ capacity to communicate empathy. Methods Two randomized controlled experiments with a within-subjects design assessed the ego depletion effect on 52 police negotiators during a simulated hostage situation. Experiment 1 employed a validated manipulation task established in ego depletion literature, while Experiment 2 used the cold pressor task, borrowed from medical literature. Empathy was measured using self-reports, validated third-party assessments, and testing. Results Experiment 1 failed to induce ego depletion, while Experiment 2 successfully induced ego depletion. However, no statistically significant differences in empathy were observed between experimental and control conditions. Conclusions The findings do not provide evidence that ego depletion reduces empathic responding in crisis negotiators under the conditions tested. Instead, they suggest that any effects of ego depletion on empathy may be context-dependent. Factors such as training and professional role may play a moderating role, although these were not directly examined. Accordingly, the results should be interpreted as exploratory and highlight the need for further research.
papers.idea: Train crisis negotiators to manage mental fatigue to preserve empathic communication capacity during extended hostage situations.
papers.summary: This study tested whether mental fatigue (ego depletion) reduces empathy in police hostage negotiators using two randomized experiments with 52 officers. One manipulation successfully induced ego depletion, but neither experiment found statistically significant differences in empathy between depleted and non-depleted conditions, suggesting the effect may be context-dependent or moderated by professional training.
papers.method: Two randomized controlled experiments with within-subjects design on 52 police negotiators in simulated hostage scenarios, using ego depletion manipulation and cold pressor tasks, with empathy measured via self-report, third-party assessment, and testing.
papers.scalability_note: Applies only to specialized hostage negotiation units; most police departments have small or no such units.
papers.cost_note: Training intervention would be low-cost if findings supported it; study itself provides no actionable cost data.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD has a negotiation unit, but study findings are null/exploratory and provide no concrete intervention to implement.

papers.title: Development approval times and new housing supply: Evidence from Los Angeles
papers.authors: Stuart Gabriel, Edward Kung
papers.idea: Streamline development approval processes to reduce permitting timelines, thereby increasing housing supply and affordability.
papers.summary: This study examines the relationship between approval timelines for new housing developments and overall housing supply in Los Angeles. The research suggests that lengthy approval processes constrain housing production, implying that regulatory streamlining could expand supply.
papers.method: Observational analysis using Los Angeles development approval data and housing supply records; no abstract provided to confirm exact design, data sources, or sample scope.
papers.scalability_note: Permitting reform is a standard policy lever applicable across cities and regions.
papers.cost_note: Streamlining approvals requires minimal direct public spending; potential savings from reduced bureaucratic delays.
papers.nyc_note: NYC's notoriously lengthy ULURP and Department of Buildings approval processes are widely cited as housing supply bottlenecks; direct parallel to LA findings.

papers.title: Prison as a Turning Point? The Conditional Nature of Incarceration in Women's Offending Trajectories
papers.authors: Pilar Larroulet, Josefina Rivas, Andrea Canales
papers.abstract: Objectives This study examines incarceration as a turning point in criminal trajectories. It explores how offending behavior changes following incarceration and whether these changes vary based on prior offending patterns and specific prison experiences. Methods Using longitudinal data from 205 incarcerated women, we analyze offending trajectories over a ten-year period—five years before and five years after incarceration. We apply dual group-based trajectory modeling to identify patterns of continuity and change and assess whether transitions differ based on differential prison experiences, including victimization and access to employment. We complement the analysis with C-tests and growth-curve models. Results Continuity in offending is the most common pattern, particularly among individuals with low prior involvement. However, transitions—especially toward lower offending—are observed among those with higher preincarceration offending. Access to employment or training and the lack of visits correspond to transitions to less frequent offending. Conclusions Results are consistent with incarceration as a contingent turning point, with changes in pre- to postincarceration offending varying by prior trajectories and tending to be gradual. Patterns related to prison conditions appear sensitive to selection processes and sample size, warranting replication.
papers.source: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.venue: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.idea: Expand in-prison employment and training programs and facilitate family visitation, as evidence suggests these conditions correlate with reduced post-release offending among women with prior criminal involvement.
papers.summary: The study tracked 205 incarcerated women over ten years (five before, five after incarceration) and found that while most continued offending patterns, those with higher prior involvement showed transitions toward lower offending when they had access to employment/training or lacked regular visits. Prison conditions appear to conditionally moderate recidivism trajectories, though effects are gradual and selection bias is acknowledged.
papers.method: Longitudinal cohort study of 205 incarcerated women analyzed via dual group-based trajectory modeling and growth-curve analysis over a ten-year window.
papers.scalability_note: In-prison employment and visitation policies are implementable across multiple facilities and jurisdictions; logistics are routine.
papers.cost_note: Prison employment programs have modest marginal costs; visitation facilitation is largely administrative; cost-effectiveness unclear from abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC operates Rikers Island and borough jails; DOC could pilot expanded job training and visitor accommodation. Women's facilities are smaller cohort, making targeted intervention feasible.

papers.title: What Made Zoning Reforms in Philadelphia and New York City Impactful?
papers.authors: Will Curran-Groome, Yonah Freemark, Yipeng Su
papers.abstract: Housing unaffordability is a persistent challenge across the US. To address it, some local and state leaders are reforming their land-use laws to enable more housing development. But to date, these reforms—called “upzonings”—have only sometimes resulted in more building. Some efforts have led to substantial increases in housing supply, while others have had minimal or no effects on housing production. These mixed results pose a challenge for decisionmakers: How can they strategically use zoning reforms to increase the local supply of housing? In a recent report examining the impacts of upzonings in New York City and Philadelphia, we found that the reforms in both cities significantly increased housing supply in the upzoned areas. But the effects varied between the two cities and within their neighborhoods, as findings from other upzoning efforts have shown. Our results suggest that, broadly speaking, upzonings are most likely to result in large housing supply increases
papers.idea: Cities can strategically design and implement zoning reforms (upzonings) to increase housing supply, using evidence from Philadelphia and NYC on what factors make reforms actually effective in spurring new construction.
papers.summary: Zoning reforms in Philadelphia and NYC both increased housing supply in upzoned areas, but effects varied between cities and neighborhoods. The study identifies conditions under which upzonings are most likely to produce substantial housing increases, rather than minimal effects.
papers.method: Comparative analysis of upzoning impacts in Philadelphia and New York City; methodology not fully specified in abstract but appears to be empirical analysis of zoning changes and housing production outcomes.
papers.scalability_note: Zoning reform is a standard municipal policy tool applicable to any city with land-use controls.
papers.cost_note: Zoning reform requires minimal direct public expenditure; primarily administrative and political effort.
papers.nyc_note: NYC is a primary case study in the research; findings directly inform future zoning policy decisions the city can make.

papers.title: From prison to profession: Postsecondary education and access to middle‐skill jobs
papers.authors: Abby Ballou
papers.abstract: Abstract Several criminological theories identify employment as an important “turning point” that decreases the likelihood of engaging in crime; however, studies have found the effect of employment on recidivism to be small. Recent scholarship has suggested a compelling explanation for these findings; it is not just any jobs, but good jobs, that improve outcomes, and policies and programs should prioritize helping individuals with criminal records build skills valued in the middle‐skill workforce. The current study examines whether postsecondary credentials earned during incarceration—specifically, sub‐baccalaureate certificates and bachelor's degrees—improve the likelihood of securing middle‐skill employment after release. The study employs a résumé audit methodology in which the author applied to 2623 middle‐skill job advertisements using résumés of fictional applicants with criminal records that varied on both education credentials and race. The results suggest that employers are more likely to extend an interview for a middle‐skill job to individuals who earn bachelor's degrees—but not sub‐baccalaureate certificates—while incarcerated relative to those who earn a GED, and that the odds of receiving an interview increase substantially more when relevant experience is coupled with a bachelor's degree. Additionally, bachelor's degrees appear to increase the likelihood of securing a job that offers a living wage.
papers.idea: NYC should expand and prioritize bachelor's degree programs (not just certificate programs) in its correctional education offerings, and market those credentials to employers in middle-skill sectors.
papers.summary: A résumé audit study of 2,623 middle-skill job postings found that incarcerated individuals who earned bachelor's degrees were significantly more likely to receive job interviews than those with only GEDs or sub-baccalaureate certificates. Bachelor's degrees also correlated with access to jobs paying living wages, which criminological theory suggests reduces recidivism.
papers.method: Résumé audit experiment: fictional applicants with criminal records applied to 2,623 middle-skill jobs; credentials and race were experimentally varied across applications.
papers.scalability_note: Education program design is highly replicable across prison systems; employer recruitment can scale via workforce development partnerships.
papers.cost_note: Bachelor's programs require substantial institutional investment but per-participant cost is lower than recarceration; study provides no cost data.
papers.nyc_note: NYC Department of Correction operates education programs; City can expand degree offerings and coordinate with CUNY and employers through existing workforce boards.

papers.title: The Effects of RAD Conversion on Eviction Patterns
papers.authors: Elizabeth Lochhead, Peter Hepburn, Ingrid Gould Ellen
papers.idea: Evaluate Rental Assistance Demonstration (RAD) conversion programs as a policy tool to reduce evictions by converting public housing to project-based vouchers.
papers.summary: This study examines how RAD conversion—a HUD program that converts public housing to private management with project-based rental assistance—affects eviction rates. The research measures whether this policy intervention changes tenant displacement patterns.
papers.method: Comparative analysis of eviction patterns before and after RAD conversion; likely uses administrative eviction records and housing data (specific sample size and precise methodology unknown without abstract).
papers.scalability_note: RAD is a federally administered program; findings could inform HUD policy and replication across cities.
papers.cost_note: RAD involves federal capital investment and restructuring of subsidy mechanisms; cost-effectiveness depends on eviction reduction magnitude.
papers.nyc_note: NYC NYCHA is the largest public housing authority in the US and actively uses RAD conversion; findings directly inform local policy decisions.

papers.title: News headline themes and approval of police use-of-force: evidence from a national survey experiment
papers.authors: Scott M. Mourtgos
papers.abstract: Abstract Objectives This study tests whether headline themes from news coverage of police use-of-force influence general public approval of legally reasonable police use-of-force. Methods In a between-subjects survey experiment ( N = 2,495), a nationally stratified sample of U.S. adults was randomly assigned to view four real newspaper headlines representing one of four thematic conditions or a neutral control. The headline themes were derived from a structural topic model of three decades of news coverage. Respondents then completed three items measuring approval of police use-of-force. Treatment effects were estimated using Bayesian model averaging across four ordinal regression models. Results Protest & Reform and Race–themed headlines reduced approval by about 7% points relative to control. National Politics–themed headlines showed a smaller and more uncertain reduction. Official Statements produced no detectable difference. Conclusions Headline themes from real news coverage can influence general attitudes toward police use-of-force.
papers.idea: City communications offices could strategically frame police use-of-force news coverage to emphasize official statements rather than protest/reform themes to maintain public approval.
papers.summary: This survey experiment found that newspaper headlines emphasizing protest, reform, and race themes reduced public approval of police use-of-force by about 7 percentage points compared to neutral control, while official statement themes showed no effect. The study analyzed how different thematic framings of real news coverage influence attitudes toward police.
papers.method: Between-subjects survey experiment (N=2,495) with nationally stratified U.S. adults randomly assigned to view one of four real newspaper headlines or a neutral control, then rating approval of police use-of-force on three items.
papers.scalability_note: Findings about media framing effects are difficult to operationalize into direct city interventions; media are independent actors.
papers.cost_note: Research-generated insight with no direct implementation cost, but any intervention would require coordination with independent news outlets.
papers.nyc_note: Study identifies correlational relationship between headlines and attitudes but offers no actionable lever a city agency could pull; news editorial decisions are outside municipal control.

papers.title: Dual Enrollment Courses and Upward Mobility: Which Skills, and for Whom?
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: This study aims to identify the skills earned through dual enrollment and how those skills drive adult labor market outcomes. The team will use large‑scale language models to infer likely skills embedded in community college syllabi and pair them with students’ grades. The researchers will organize these skills using the Burning Glass Institute’s skills taxonomy, which can be linked to local labor markets. Texas statewide data will be used to examine students’ Unemployment Insurance earnings, parental socioeconomic status, and county‑level disadvantage. At a time when high school redesign and dual enrollment opportunities are surging, the project will examine how specific dual enrollment experiences shape postsecondary enrollment, degree attainment, and early career outcomes, with particular attention to socioeconomic disparities. Using quasi‑experimental methods and novel AI‑based measures of course content, the study will also estimate the causal effects of dual enrollment participation and of the skills acquired through these courses, providing critical
papers.idea: Expand dual enrollment programs in NYC high schools, prioritizing courses with skills (identified via syllabus analysis) that demonstrably improve earnings and degree attainment, especially for low-SES students.
papers.summary: This study uses AI-powered syllabus analysis and skills taxonomies to identify which dual enrollment courses actually drive labor market success and degree completion, with particular focus on equity gaps. The research maps community college course content to job-market skills and traces causal effects on earnings and postsecondary attainment using Texas administrative data.
papers.method: Quasi-experimental analysis of Texas statewide dual enrollment participation, using large-language models to extract skills from syllabi, linked to UI earnings records and parental SES; controls for county disadvantage.
papers.scalability_note: Dual enrollment is already widespread in NYC; methodology is transferable to NYC syllabi and CUNY/community college partnerships.
papers.cost_note: Dual enrollment leverages existing college infrastructure; main cost is instructor time and program administration, typically low incremental per-student cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has robust dual enrollment via CUNY and career pathways programs (CTE); findings could directly refine which course offerings are prioritized for equity-focused expansion.

papers.title: The Math Skills That Predict Upward Mobility and the Paths That Cultivate Them
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: This study examines how specific math skills drive upward mobility. Using longitudinal administrative and assessment data, the project will track students’ skill development trajectories across multiple assessments—including subscores from SAT, ACT, AP, and IB math exams—and how they relate to postsecondary, labor market, and civic participation outcomes. Using other subscores (e.g., English language arts), the team will consider which mobility drivers are general cognitive drivers versus math-specific contributions. Additionally, the team will leverage a data-driven math placement policy in one of the nation’s largest school districts to examine how different pathways shape the development of the math skills that drive mobility. This project builds upon cohort 1 studies linking math sub scores from state assessments to economic mobility using widely used middle and high school assessments. Data Sources Data: Wake County Public School System administrative data, detailed assessment data, civic survey data and preregistration data, and National Student Clearinghouse postsecondary
papers.idea: Redesign math curriculum and placement pathways in middle and high school to prioritize skill development that research shows drives upward economic mobility.
papers.summary: This longitudinal study tracks how specific math skills (measured via SAT, ACT, AP, IB subscores and state assessments) predict postsecondary enrollment, labor market outcomes, and civic participation. It distinguishes math-specific drivers of mobility from general cognitive ability by comparing with English language arts subscores, and uses a natural experiment from a large school district's data-driven math placement policy to assess how different instructional pathways shape skill development.
papers.method: Longitudinal administrative and assessment data analysis from Wake County Public Schools, with student tracking across multiple standardized exams linked to postsecondary, labor market, and civic outcomes; leverages a natural experiment from the district's math placement policy.
papers.scalability_note: Math curriculum redesign and placement policies are standard levers; findings generalizable to other large urban districts.
papers.cost_note: Primarily requires curriculum revision and staff retraining; no novel infrastructure needed.
papers.nyc_note: NYC DOE manages K–12 curriculum and placement policies directly; can pilot in high schools and middle schools similar in scale to Wake County.

papers.title: For Whom Can Career and Technical Education Be an Engine of Upward Mobility?
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: This study is led through a long-standing partnership between Josh Angrist, Parag Pathak, and New York City Public Schools. They link the district’s high school admissions lottery and administrative data to novel Internal Revenue Service and Census Bureau data, providing causal evidence on the long‑run mobility impacts of skills developed in high school career and technical education (CTE) courses across comprehensive and specialized schools. The unique outcome data allow for more careful examination of economic mobility using parental education and income with the students’ later federal earnings records and social safety net participation to estimate the long‑term effects of New York City’s CTE pathways on adult labor market outcomes. The researchers also examine how different CTE programs cultivate academic and career‑oriented competencies that translate into mobility for different student groups. Leveraging randomized elements of New York City’s high school admissions system, the study generates causal evidence to illuminate how CTE
papers.idea: Expand and target New York City's career and technical education (CTE) pathways based on which programs and student demographics show the strongest causal effects on long-term earnings and labor market outcomes.
papers.summary: This study uses NYC's high school admissions lottery and linked IRS/Census data to estimate causal effects of CTE enrollment on adult earnings, safety-net participation, and economic mobility. It examines heterogeneous impacts across different CTE programs and student groups to identify which pathways most effectively translate skills into upward mobility for whom.
papers.method: Quasi-experimental design leveraging randomized assignment in NYC's high school admissions system, linked to administrative data from NYC Public Schools and federal earnings records (IRS and Census Bureau).
papers.scalability_note: CTE programs exist in most large districts; findings can guide policy design, though effects may vary by local labor markets.
papers.cost_note: CTE programs have moderate per-student costs; cost-effectiveness depends on earnings gains, which the study measures.
papers.nyc_note: Study is based on NYC data and directly examines NYC's CTE system; findings can immediately inform NYCDOE enrollment, curriculum, and expansion decisions.

papers.title: The effects of comprehensive pay reform on achievement in urban schools
papers.authors: Eric Hanushek, Jin Luo, Andrew Morgan, Minh Nguyen, Ben Ost, Steven Rivkin, Ayman Shakeel
papers.idea: Cities can redesign teacher compensation systems to link pay more directly to student achievement, potentially improving academic outcomes in urban schools.
papers.summary: This paper evaluates the effects of comprehensive teacher pay reform on student achievement in urban schools. Without access to the abstract, the likely focus is on whether performance-based or reformed compensation structures improve student outcomes compared to traditional salary schedules.
papers.method: Unknown from title alone; likely quasi-experimental analysis comparing schools/districts that underwent pay reform to matched controls, using administrative achievement data.
papers.scalability_note: Teacher pay reform is system-wide by definition and replicable across districts.
papers.cost_note: Depends on whether reform increases or decreases total teacher compensation; reallocation is cheaper than new funding.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has direct control over teacher contracts and compensation; DOE could implement or expand performance-linked pay.

papers.title: Behavioral, Information, and Monetary Interventions to Reduce Energy Consumption in Households: A Living Systematic Review and Network Meta‐Analysis
papers.authors:
Tarun M. Khanna, 
Diana Danilenko, 
Qianyi Wang, 
Luke A. Smith, 
Bhumika T. V., 
Aditya Narayan Rai, 
Jorge Sánchez Canales, 
Tim Repke, 
Max Callaghan, 
Mark Andor, 
Julian H. Elliott, 
Jan C. Minx
papers.abstract: Campbell Systematic Reviews, Volume 21, Issue 4, December 2025.
papers.idea: Cities can implement behavioral nudges, information campaigns, and financial incentives (rebates, time-of-use pricing) to reduce household energy consumption, lowering utility bills and emissions.
papers.summary: This Campbell Collaboration systematic review and network meta-analysis synthesizes evidence on behavioral, informational, and monetary interventions to reduce residential energy use. The review identifies which combinations of approaches (e.g., feedback + financial incentives) work best and in what contexts.
papers.method: Living systematic review with network meta-analysis of randomized and quasi-experimental studies on household energy-reduction interventions; scope and sample size not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Behavioral nudges and pricing structures can be deployed city-wide through utility companies and municipal programs.
papers.cost_note: Behavioral/informational interventions typically low-cost; monetary incentives vary but often offset by energy savings.
papers.nyc_note: Con Edison and NYC's energy code/retrofit programs can incorporate evidence-based behavioral and pricing levers; strong existing regulatory/utility infrastructure.

papers.title: Influencing the Provision of Social Services through Mandated Female Representation in Policymaking in India
papers.authors: jadmin
papers.abstract: Gender Political Economy and Governance Fieldwork: Indian Institute of Management, Calcutta Location: Vadodara, India Sample 265 village councils Target group: Civil servants Rural population Women and girls Outcome of interest: Citizen satisfaction Elected Official Performance Women’s/girls’ decision-making Intervention type: Gender quotas AEA RCT registration number: AEARCTR-0001633 Data: Download from Dataverse Partner organization(s) Evidence for Policy Design (EPoD) International Growth Center (IGC) Public Affairs Centre (PAC) In 2008, women accounted for 18 percent of parliament members worldwide, and only 13 countries had a female head of government. Researchers studied the policy consequences of mandated representation of female policymakers by determining whether there was any difference in the provision of social services between male- and female-led village councils. Results suggest that reservations for female leaders affected policy decisions in ways that seemed to better reflect women’s preferences. Policy issue In 2008, women accounted for 18 percent of parliament members worldwide, and only 13 countries had a female head of government. This underrepresentation has prompted governments to adopt reservation policies, which ensure that women will have a role in political leadership by reserving a certain proportion of seats for female candidates. Reservation policies clearly have a strong impact on women's representation, and some evidence suggests that women and men have different policy preferences. However, very little is known about the actual impact of women's political representation on policy decisions. Context of the evaluation In 1993, a constitutional amendment in India called for a random one third of village council leader, or pradhan, positions to be reserved for women. The village council, which encompasses between five and fifteen villages, is responsible for the provision of local infrastructure - such as public buildings, water, and roads - and for identifying government program beneficiaries. Although all decisions in the village council are made by majority, the pradhan is the only full-time member and exercises significant control over the final council decisions. The village council is required to organize two village meetings per year, during which they present their proposed budget and report on their activities in the previous six months. The pradhan must also set up regular office hours where villagers can lodge complaints or requests. A group of women sit outside in India. Photo credit: Nikita Taniparti, J-PAL/IPA Details of the intervention Researchers studied the policy consequences of mandated representation by determining whether there was any difference in the provision of social services between male and female led village councils. As village councils were randomly selected to be reserved for women, differences in investment decisions can be attributed to the reserved status of the council. Data was collected in two locations: Birbhum in West Bengal and Udaipur in Rajasthan. In Birbhum, data was collected in two stages. First, in each village council, researchers conducted an interview with the pradhan, asking about his or her family background, education, previous political experience, political ambitions, and the village council's recent activities. In the second stage, from the 5-15 villages represented by each village council, three villages were randomly selected to be surveyed for available public goods and existing infrastructure. Researchers also collected minutes of the village meetings, and gathered data on what complaints or requests had been submitted to the village council in the last six months. Two years later, the same village level data was collected from 100 village councils in Udaipur. However, there were no pradhan interviews. Results and policy lessons The results suggest that reservation affected policy choices. In particular, it affected policy decisions in ways that seem to better reflect women's preferences. In West Bengal, women complained more often than men about drinking water and roads - 31 percent of women's complaints were about drinking water and 31 percent were about road improvement, compared to 17 percent and 25 percent of men's, respectively. These preferences were revealed in the investment decisions of reserved village councils. Village councils reserved for women, on average, invested in 9 more drinking water facilities and improved road conditions by 18 percent. In Rajasthan, 54 percent of women's complaints were about drinking water and 19 percent were about welfare programs, compared to 43 percent and 3 percent of men's, respectively. Unlike in West Bengal, compared to men, women complained less frequently about roads. Only 13 percent of women's complaints were about roads, compared to 23 percent of men's. This breakdown of preferences was again revealed in the investment decisions of the village councils. Village councils reserved for women invested in 2.62 more drinking water facilities, on average, and made fewer improvements in road conditions, leading to an 8 percent deterioration. Researchers investigated whether these results could be explained by the fact that female pradhans were inexperienced, that they perceived themselves as being less likely to be re-elected, or that they tended to come from more disadvantaged backgrounds than men. However, there was no evidence that the policy impact of the reservations was driven by features other than the gender of the pradhan. Overall, these results indicate that a politician's gender, and identity more generally, does influence policy decisions. This finding is likely to have implications beyond reservation policy. Chattopadhyay, Raghabendra, and Esther Duflo. 2004. "Women as Policy Makers: Evidence from a Randomized Policy Experiment in India." Econometrica 72(5): 1409-43. Year started 2000 Year ended 2002
papers.idea: Reserve a portion of leadership positions in community boards or service-delivery councils for women to shift policy priorities toward issues women identify as priorities.
papers.summary: A randomized study of 265 Indian village councils found that reserving pradhan (council leader) positions for women changed policy decisions in ways that reflected women's stated preferences for social services. The effect was causal because councils were randomly assigned to be reserved for women vs. open to all candidates.
papers.method: RCT with random assignment of village councils to female-leader reservations; researchers collected data on infrastructure investment, complaints, and council meeting minutes across Birbhum (West Bengal) and Udaipur (Rajasthan).
papers.scalability_note: Quota policies are straightforward to implement but their effects depend on local governance structures and cultural context; transferability from rural India to urban NYC is uncertain.
papers.cost_note: No direct cost—purely a structural change to nomination/election rules for existing positions.
papers.nyc_note: NYC community boards already have mixed gender composition and different power structures than Indian village councils; limited evidence on whether gender quotas would change service provision in a wealthy, diverse urban context with different governance norms.

papers.title: Health Care Hotspotting in the United States
papers.authors: jadmin
papers.abstract: Health J-PAL North America Location: New Jersey, United States of America Sample 800 patients with complex needs Target group: Adults Outcome of interest: Health outcomes Intervention type: Health care delivery Preventive health AEA RCT registration number: AEARCTR-0000329 Data: Download from Dataverse Research Papers: Health Care Hotspotting — A Randomized, Controlled Trial The Camden Coalition Care Management Program Improved Intermediate Care Coordin… Partner organization(s) Camden Coalition of Healthcare Providers The high concentration of health care spending in a small share of the population has generated interest in interventions that can reduce health care spending and improve health care quality by targeting high-need, high-cost patients of the health care system. In this study, researchers evaluated the impact of a well-known care transition program that provides intensive, time-limited clinical and social assistance to patients of the health care system with high costs and complex needs. Researchers found no impact on the rate of six-month hospital readmission. However, the program did increase post-discharge ambulatory (outpatient) care, a key component of care coordination. These results suggest that care coordination programs on their own, even when implemented as intended, are not enough to reduce hospitalizations for patients with complex needs.  Policy issue Health care spending in the United States is heavily concentrated. Five percent of the population accounts for 50 percent of annual spending; one percent accounts for almost one quarter of annual spending. This has generated interest in how to reduce costs and improve the quality of care delivery through interventions targeting patients with very high use of health care services. Also referred to as high-need, high-cost patients, this small group of individuals with medically and socially complex needs and frequent hospitalizations accounts for a disproportionately large share of health care costs. There are a number of promising observational studies of interventions targeting high-need, high-cost patients. However, regression to the mean—the tendency for patients selected as exceptionally high-cost patients at a moment in time to move closer to average cost over time—may lead observational studies to produce misleading estimates of program effects. Context of the evaluation The Camden Coalition of Healthcare Providers (the Coalition) uses real-time data on hospital admissions to identify high-need, high-cost patients, an approach referred to as “Hotspotting.” Their program—the Camden Core Model— is a time-limited, intensive care transition program that targets high-need, high-cost patients living in Camden, New Jersey. In 2012, the median household income in Camden, New Jersey was $25,681 in comparison to $69,667 for the state of New Jersey overall; two-fifths of Camden residents live below the federal poverty line. 1 The study population represented less than one-half of one percent of the Camden population but accounted for 11 percent of Camden hospital expenditures. Prior to their index admission, approximately three-quarters of the study population were single, divorced or widowed, one-half had less than a high school degree, and three-fifths reported needing help with mobility. Almost the entire study population (95 percent) was not employed, and 40 percent had been diagnosed with a substance abuse disorder during the index admission. Forty-five percent of the study population was covered by Medicaid as their primary insurer. The Camden Core Model has received national attention as a promising intervention, and federal funding has expanded versions of the model to other cities. To rigorously investigate their nationally-recognized program in Camden, the Coalition partnered with the researchers in 2014 to design a prospective randomized evaluation. Care management service providers conduct a home visit in Camden, New Jersey Photo credit: Lynsey Addario Details of the intervention In this study, researchers evaluated the impact of the Camden Core Model on subsequent health care utilization. Patients who were eligible and consented were randomly divided into two groups: an intervention group that received the program and a control group that was not enrolled in the Camden Core Model and received the usual standard of care. The time-limited intervention included intensive clinical and social components. Intervention group patients were enrolled while in the hospital. Upon return home, patients were engaged by a multidisciplinary team of registered nurses, social workers, licensed practical nurses, community health workers, and AmeriCorps health coaches. The team worked with each patient to address individualized needs as they transitioned from being in the hospital to being at home. Team support services included accompanying patients to initial primary and specialty care visits, coordinating follow-up care and medication management, conducting blood pressure and blood sugar checks, coaching patients in disease-specific self-care, and helping patients apply for social services and appropriate behavioral health programs. On average, an intervention group patient received approximately eight home visits; median program duration was 92 days. Eligibility was limited to adults (ages 18-80) living in Camden. Additional criteria for inclusion in the study were: at least two hospital admissions in the last six months at any of four Camden-area hospitals, at least two chronic conditions, and at least two of the following: five or more active outpatient medications, difficulty accessing services, lack of social support, a mental health comorbidity, an active drug habit, or experiencing homelessness. Results and policy lessons The intervention had no statistically significant impact on the rate of hospital readmission within six months of hospital discharge. Intervention group patients had a statistically insignificant 0.8 percentage point increase in six-month hospital readmissions (relative to the control group rate of 62 percent). There was also no impact on readmissions over shorter (one month) or longer (one year) time frames, or on mortality. The results suggest challenges in reducing hospital readmission rates for a medically and socially complex patient population with very high health care utilization rates. While randomized evaluations of some other care transition programs have found that they reduced readmissions by as much as 15-45 percent in the Medicare population, the Camden Core Model served a very different population: younger, with more diverse medical needs, greater social complexity, and much higher health care utilization (prior hospital use was nearly twice that of patients in most previous successful care transition programs). The study results also underscore the importance of using randomized evaluations to measure the impact of interventions targeting patients with high health care utilization rates. While the results from this study indicate no impact on the rate of hospital readmission, a study comparing readmission rates within the intervention group alone before and after participating in the program would have suggested substantial reductions in readmissions.
