This is because high-need, high-cost patients are typically targeted for intervention during periods of high health care utilization, often when their illnesses are flaring, and many such patients will naturally return to lower rates of utilization. This phenomenon, known as regression to the mean, can lead observational studies to produce misleading estimates of program effects. Researchers conducted a follow-up analysis to explore the effects of the program on post-discharge ambulatory (outpatient) care for the subset of patients (70 percent) enrolled in Medicaid. Accessing timely ambulatory office-based care was just one element of the care coordination program but was an intermediate goal for which researchers could observe outcomes in both the treatment and comparison groups and where there was uncertainty about implementation quality in the original study. Researchers aimed to understand whether the lack of impact on hospital readmissions was due to a failure of implementation or a failure of the program's theory of change. In other words, were individuals not being connected to timely office-based care, muting the effects of the program on future readmissions? Or, were individuals being connected to ambulatory care, but that care did not reduce hospital readmissions? The follow up analysis found that although many participants still did not receive post discharge appointments, the intervention increased the percentage of participants with at least one ambulatory visit within fourteen days by 15.3 percentage points (relative to the control group rate of 27.1 percent). These effects persisted over time, with the percentage of the treatment group with at least one ambulatory visit still 7.5 percentage points higher 365 days after discharge (relative to the control group rate of 82.5 percent) and were comparable to what other care coordination programs had achieved. The program also increased the number of patients receiving any durable medical equipment. The results suggest that care coordination programs on their own, even when implemented as intended, are not enough to reduce hospitalizations for patients with complex needs and extremely high use of the health care system. In response, the Camden Coalition is including housing and legal services in their model in order to increase the focus on non-clinical support.  1. U.S. Census Bureau, "INCOME IN THE PAST 12 MONTHS (IN 2012 INFLATION-ADJUSTED DOLLARS) ," 2012. American Community Survey, ACS 1-Year Estimates Subject Tables, Table S1901, 2012, accessed on December 21, 2023,  https://data.census.gov/table/ACSST1Y2012.S1901?q=income%20camden%20city%20new%20jersey Year started 2014 Year ended 2024
papers.idea: Implement intensive care coordination and hotspotting programs targeting high-need, high-cost patients to improve outpatient care coordination, though expect limited impact on hospital readmission rates.
papers.summary: The Camden Coalition's intensive care transition program for high-need, high-cost patients increased post-discharge ambulatory care but found no significant impact on 6-month hospital readmission rates. The study suggests that care coordination alone, even when well-implemented, is insufficient to reduce hospitalizations for patients with complex medical and social needs.
papers.method: Randomized controlled trial with 800 complex-needs patients in Camden, New Jersey, evaluating the Camden Core Model's impact on hospital readmissions and care coordination outcomes.
papers.scalability_note: Model already replicated federally to multiple cities; processes and staff training are portable; relies on existing health data infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Intensive home-based care management is labor-intensive; cost per patient not specified in abstract but likely moderate-to-high.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has high-cost Medicaid population, existing care coordination infrastructure, and hospital data systems; easily adapted; relevant to managed long-term care and safety-net hospitals.

papers.title: The Anatomy of Scam Scenarios: Large-Scale Characterization and Conversation-Aware Detection
papers.authors: Shang Ma, Chen Yanai, Avichai Ben, Zichen Liu, Yanfang Ye, Xusheng Xiao
papers.abstract: Online scams have become a pervasive global threat, causing substantial financial, psychological, and operational harm. Scammers embed psychological techniques (PTs) within reusable operational schemes to scale scam campaigns with minimal adaptation. However, existing studies often analyze PTs as isolated features, overlooking the recurring scam scenarios in which they are systematically deployed. To address this gap, we first conduct a large-scale empirical study to jointly characterize scam scenarios and their associated PTs. Specifically, we develop a data-driven pipeline to derive a hierarchical taxonomy of scam scenarios, consisting of 18 fine-grained scenarios grouped into 6 high-level tactics based on their PT profiles. Furthermore, to transfer this scenario-level knowledge to practical defense, we design a conversation-aware scam scenario detection approach for financial-institution customer interactions, enabling timely warning and intervention. Our study on 102,054 real-world scam incident reports, spanning 2024-02-01 to 2025-10-31, reveals that PT usage is significantly associated with scam scenarios. We further show that scammers organize scenarios around different operational goals, such as broad victim exposure, high victim conversion, and high-value extraction, and reuse infrastructure, including IP addresses, domains, email addresses, and phone numbers, to launch coordinated campaigns at scale. Evaluation on
papers.idea: Financial institutions and law enforcement could deploy conversation-monitoring systems in customer service interactions to detect and intervene in real-time scam scenarios, using the study's taxonomic framework to flag high-risk exchanges.
papers.summary: Researchers analyzed 102,054 scam incident reports to develop a hierarchical taxonomy of 18 fine-grained scam scenarios grouped into 6 high-level tactics based on psychological manipulation techniques. They found that scammers systematically deploy psychological techniques within reusable operational schemes, and that infrastructure (IPs, domains, emails, phone numbers) is reused across coordinated campaigns. They prototyped a conversation-aware detection system for financial-institution customer interactions.
papers.method: Empirical analysis of 102,054 real-world scam incident reports (Feb 2024–Oct 2025) to build a data-driven taxonomy; development of a conversation-aware detection model for customer interactions.
papers.scalability_note: Detection systems can be integrated into existing customer service platforms across multiple financial institutions and call centers.
papers.cost_note: Primarily software engineering and integration; scales with minimal marginal cost once model is built.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has major financial institutions, consumer protection agencies (AG, DCA), and 311; could integrate with fraud-reporting pipelines and train customer-service staff on scenario taxonomy.

papers.title: Classifying by Proxy: Explainable and Reproducible Ensemble of Proxy Tasks for Child Sexual Abuse Imagery Classification
papers.authors: Clara Ernesto, Carlos Caetano, Sandra Avila, João Macedo, Camila Laranjeira, Leo S. F. Ribeiro
papers.abstract: Child Sexual Abuse Imagery (CSAI) classification systems are needed solutions for lessening the psychological impacts often felt by law enforcement agents responsible for evaluating these materials and for efficient removal of these materials from the web. However, due to the nature of the task, researching and developing such systems is not a trivial endeavor. The images are highly sensitive, and the related datasets are under restrictive access regimes, which means most studies in the area are not reproducible or distributable and are therefore hard to compare and validate. More concerning still, most models for this task today lack an aspect often desired by law enforcement agents: explainability. In this paper, we apply an ensemble of Proxy Tasks -- tasks that correlate to CSAI classification -- yielding improvements in reproducibility, explainability, and security for distribution. This concept is applied for the first time to real CSAI, with a novel selection of relevant Proxy Tasks (selected from the CSAI literature) and training adaptations to the original framework. Our final model achieves competitive results, yielding 91.9% balanced accuracy on the RCPD dataset with the best Proxy Task combination. We furthermore contrast these results with the best-in-class representation learning model, DINO, and show that our ensemble improves accuracy and provides explanations for its classification results, a feature that a single deep learning model can seldom provide.
papers.idea: NYC law enforcement could adopt this machine-learning classifier to automate detection and removal of child sexual abuse imagery from the web and digital platforms, reducing officer exposure to traumatic material.
papers.summary: The authors developed an ensemble machine-learning model using proxy tasks to classify child sexual abuse imagery (CSAI) with 91.9% balanced accuracy on a standard dataset. The approach improves reproducibility, explainability, and security compared to existing black-box deep-learning models, addressing key barriers to adoption by law enforcement.
papers.method: A machine-learning ensemble framework using proxy tasks correlated with CSAI classification was trained on the RCPD dataset; results were compared against a state-of-the-art representation learning model (DINO).
papers.scalability_note: Algorithmic tools can be deployed across departments and platforms; deployment at scale would require institutional integration and real-world validation.
papers.cost_note: Software/model deployment has minimal per-unit marginal cost once built; primary expense is development and integration.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD and NY law-enforcement agencies already conduct CSAI investigations; tool could reduce officer burnout and speed case processing if validated and legally approved.

papers.title: Prioritizing Core Factors Needed to Sustain Hospital-Based Violence Intervention Programs: A Delphi Study
papers.authors: Lars Erik Almquist, Sarah Cusworth Walker, Ali Rowhani-Rahbar, Christian D. Helfrich
papers.idea: Identify and operationalize core structural and organizational factors needed to sustain hospital-based violence intervention programs in urban settings.
papers.summary: This Delphi study engaged expert consensus to identify the critical factors required to maintain hospital-based violence intervention programs over time. The research synthesizes practitioner and stakeholder input on sustainability drivers, likely covering staffing, funding, clinical integration, and community partnerships. Results can inform which program elements are essential vs. optional for long-term viability.
papers.method: Delphi study (multi-round expert consensus methodology) with hospital-based violence intervention practitioners and stakeholders; exact sample size and setting not provided in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Delphi consensus findings on sustainability factors are generalizable across hospital systems and urban contexts.
papers.cost_note: Delphi studies are moderately priced; actual program costs depend on implementation model identified.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has active hospital-based violence intervention programs (e.g., Cure Violence, hospital trauma center programs); findings directly inform expansion and sustainability in existing infrastructure.

papers.title: Evaluating performances pretrial: The role of defendant performances in prosecutors’ plea bargaining decisions
papers.authors: Chiara Clio Packard
papers.abstract: Abstract Research Summary Existing research on plea bargaining focuses primarily on how fixed case and defendant characteristics, such as the strength of the evidence or the race of the accused, impact plea offers. Less is known about how prosecutors consider a defendant's behavior while their case is pending throughout the plea bargaining process. Drawing on an ethnographic study in two midwestern District Attorney's (DA) offices, this paper reveals how prosecutors evaluate defendants’ performances pretrial as they decide their plea offers. First, I identify four types of pretrial performances that prosecutors consider: abiding by the rules of the court, addressing the harm of the crime, engaging with social services, and demonstrating positive attitude and demeanor. Then, I demonstrate how the process through which performances shape plea offers is dynamic and interactional, as defense attorneys develop assumptions about which performances prosecutors consider and support their clients in meeting those expectations. Policy Implications Defendants with fewer financial resources may face challenges in meeting pretrial performance expectations, leading to inequalities in defendants’ ability to obtain more favorable plea offers. DA's offices should consider only making specific performance demands for services that are readily available to low‐income defendants, such as programs offered for free. Alternatively, prosecutors could decline to prosecute low‐level cases that stem from substance use or mental health issues and encourage law enforcement to make direct referrals to social service agencies instead. Otherwise, this practice risks exacerbating inequalities as more privileged defendants have greater resources to meet the performance demands compared to disadvantaged defendants. More broadly, these findings call for policies that address the underlying inequalities in access to health‐care and social services, which would reduce inequality in defendants’ ability to meet performance demands.
papers.idea: Prosecutors should condition plea-offer improvements only on pretrial performance demands (social services, court compliance) that are genuinely accessible to low-income defendants, or redirect low-level substance and mental health cases to social services rather than prosecution.
papers.summary: An ethnographic study of two midwestern DA offices found that prosecutors systematically evaluate defendants' pretrial behavior—including court compliance, addressing crime harm, engaging social services, and attitude—when setting plea offers. However, defendants with fewer financial resources struggle to meet these performance expectations, creating unequal access to favorable plea deals and compounding existing criminal justice disparities.
papers.method: Ethnographic study conducted in two midwestern District Attorney offices; observational fieldwork examining prosecutor decision-making in plea bargaining negotiations.
papers.scalability_note: Prosecutorial policies and service referral practices are easily standardizable across jurisdictions and require no new technology.
papers.cost_note: Redirecting cases to existing free social services and adjusting plea-offer criteria requires minimal incremental cost; may reduce prosecution and incarceration expenses.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has a centralized DA office, robust (if strained) social services infrastructure, and active policy focus on justice-system equity; recommendations align with prosecutorial discretion and existing diversion programs.

papers.title: “I Really Didn’t Need Treatment, It Was Just a Way for Me Not to Go to Jail”: Court-Mandated Drug Treatment Experiences among Racially Minoritized Individuals in Three US States
papers.authors: Laura Nicole Sisson, Adrienne Hurst, Daliah Heller, Sachini Bandara, Brendan Saloner, Olivia K. Sugarman
papers.idea: Redesign court-mandated drug treatment programs to ensure genuine therapeutic intent rather than incarceration avoidance, with voluntary enrollment and wraparound services tailored to racial equity.
papers.summary: This qualitative study documents experiences of racially minoritized individuals in court-mandated drug treatment across three states, revealing that participants often viewed treatment as a coercive alternative to jail rather than a genuine health intervention. The research suggests current mandated treatment programs may fail to achieve therapeutic goals and disproportionately burden communities of color.
papers.method: Qualitative interview study with racially minoritized individuals across three US states who experienced court-mandated drug treatment; no sample size or detailed methodology provided in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Treatment policy redesign is replicable across jurisdictions; criminal justice reform is established practice.
papers.cost_note: Reframing existing mandated treatment into voluntary models with wraparound services could reduce incarceration costs; no per-unit data in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has established drug court programs and criminal justice diversion infrastructure; findings directly applicable to local practice reform.

papers.title: A longitudinal examination of the effects of drug policy shifts on PCS charging practices in Oregon
papers.authors: Kelsey S. Henderson, Christopher M. Campbell, Brian Renauer
papers.abstract: Research Summary To estimate the effects of successive drug policy shifts on prosecutorial decisions, we employ a retrospective, longitudinal analysis of statewide aggregated charging trends (2008–2024). Focused on the changing landscape around possession of controlled substances (PCS), we examined the impact of three Oregon policies—Justice Reinvestment (2013), PCS drug defelonization (2017), and PCS drug decriminalization (2021)—on initial charge filings, charge bargaining, and charge dismissals and convictions. Across intervention periods, prosecutors filed a high percentage of PCS charges referred by law enforcement (average of 78.9%). Defelonization was associated with a significant drop in felony PCS charges, coupled with a significant increase in misdemeanor PCS charges. Following defelonization, the number of possession‐related dispositions began to steadily decline, with a lower conviction rate. Decriminalization was associated with an immediate decline in both misdemeanor and felony PCS charges and fewer overall dispositions, which coincided with an increase in dismissals, whereas conviction rates remained relatively stable. Across intervention periods, PCS charges, if not dismissed, were likely to remain consistent through dispositions (>92.5%), demonstrating little charge bargaining/variation. Despite fluctuations in the number of PCS charges filed, of those not dismissed, there appears to be a concerted effort on prosecuting those charges. Policy Implications With the advent of drug courts, diversion programs, defelonization, and Oregon's most recent period of decriminalization, states are experimenting with shifting drug policies. Defelonization and decriminalization impacted prosecutorial charging practices, more so than Justice Reinvestment. Policy makers should note that defelonization had the expected impact on PCS charges, and prosecutors seamlessly adopted the new law by switching their focus to misdemeanor PCS charges. Decriminalization contributed to a shrinking of actions related to prosecutorial charging; the majority of PCS offenses were no longer criminal, resulting in significantly fewer PCS arrest referrals, thereby forcing a dearth of prosecutorial charges.
papers.idea: Cities can study how prosecutors adapt charging practices after drug defelonization or decriminalization to anticipate prosecutorial workarounds and design complementary diversion, treatment, and justice-reinvestment programs.
papers.summary: This longitudinal study tracked Oregon prosecutorial charging decisions across three drug policy shifts (2008–2024): Justice Reinvestment (2013), defelonization (2017), and decriminalization (2021). Defelonization shifted charges from felony to misdemeanor without reducing overall prosecutorial effort; decriminalization reduced total charges filed and increased dismissals. Prosecutors maintained high conviction rates on charges that proceeded, with minimal charge bargaining across all periods.
papers.method: Retrospective longitudinal analysis of statewide aggregated charging trends in Oregon over 16 years (2008–2024), examining initial charge filings, plea bargains, dismissals, and convictions across three policy intervention periods.
papers.scalability_note: Drug policy frameworks are adopted by many states and cities; charging-practice adaptation is a generalizable phenomenon, though jurisdictional variations in prosecutorial culture and resources may affect replicability.
papers.cost_note: Study is observational retrospective analysis using administrative data; no new intervention cost. Policy changes themselves (decriminalization, diversion) have variable costs.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has prosecutorial agencies, can leverage criminal-justice data systems, and faces similar drug-policy pressures; findings directly inform how NYPD and DA offices may adapt to decriminalization or diversion mandates.

papers.title: Three Things DC's New Mayor and Council Need to Know About Community Safety
papers.authors: Anita Ravishankar, Jesse Jannetta, Lily Robin
papers.abstract: In mid-March, nearly 100 rounds were fired in a matter of seconds in a Hill East neighborhood. No one was injured, but bullets pierced a child’s bunk bed, struck the windows of a nearby school, and rattled the entire community. What followed was notable not just for its speed but also its breadth: community safety walks brought residents and officials together to survey the space and identify vulnerabilities; a coordinated multiagency response spanned law enforcement, environmental improvements, and building code enforcement; and both immediate crisis response and longer-term prevention measures were put in motion. Community and government partners, all at the table together. Research shows this type of coordinated interagency response, or ecosystem approach, is one of the most effective ways to improve public safety. DC’s response to this event demonstrated exactly how this approach looks in practice. It also demonstrated that the District has the capacity to work this
papers.idea: Adopt a coordinated multiagency ecosystem approach to community safety that integrates law enforcement, environmental design improvements, building code enforcement, and community input through joint safety walks and response planning.
papers.summary: Following a shooting incident in DC's Hill East neighborhood, a rapid coordinated response involving community members, law enforcement, and multiple city agencies identified vulnerabilities, made environmental improvements, and launched both immediate crisis response and longer-term prevention. Research supports this ecosystem approach as one of the most effective strategies for improving public safety.
papers.method: Case study examination of DC's response to a specific shooting incident, drawing on research literature supporting coordinated interagency approaches; no controlled experimental design described.
papers.scalability_note: Interagency coordination models are replicable across cities, though execution depends on existing agency relationships and political will.
papers.cost_note: Primarily involves convening existing agencies and staff time; environmental improvements and code enforcement leverage existing budgets but require coordination overhead.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has established public safety infrastructure, multiple city agencies (NYPD, DOE, DOE, Parks, Buildings), and community organizations; the ecosystem model maps directly onto existing governance structure.

papers.title: Outcomes of the VictimConnect Resource Center
papers.authors: Jennifer Yahner, Malore Dusenbery, Sam Bachman, Josh Fording
papers.abstract: With funding from the National Institute of Justice, Urban Institute researchers conducted an outcome evaluation of the VictimConnect Resource Center from 2023 to 2025. This brief summarizes our evaluation findings. Why This Matters VictimConnect is the only national helpline serving all crime victims by providing emotional support, information, and referrals. These services are offered through a website and a hotline available by phone, online chat, and text messaging. The hotline is operated by trained staff called victim assistance specialists who serve helpline visitors from a wide range of backgrounds in every state across the United States. The findings from this evaluation can provide helpful insights for service providers, funders, and policymakers into how the national hotline supports the accessibility and delivery of victim services. Key Takeaways Our goal was to assess the extent to which VictimConnect affects crime victims by increasing their knowledge and awareness of resources, improving their emotional
papers.idea: Cities can establish or expand multi-channel victim support hotlines (phone, chat, text, web) staffed by trained victim assistance specialists to improve crime victims' access to emotional support and service referrals.
papers.summary: VictimConnect is a national helpline serving crime victims across all states through multiple channels, operated by trained victim assistance specialists. This Urban Institute evaluation (2023–2025) assessed whether the service increases victims' knowledge of resources and improves emotional outcomes.
papers.method: Outcome evaluation conducted by Urban Institute researchers with NIJ funding; abstract does not specify sample size, control group, or detailed methodology.
papers.scalability_note: Hotline model is replicable; NYC could adopt or expand existing victim services with staff training and multi-channel infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Helpline operations are relatively low-cost per contact; per-unit cost not stated in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has victim services but could expand hotline accessibility and staff capacity; model fits within existing Office of the Victim Advocate and NYPD victim services.

papers.title: Findings from a Rigorous Evaluation of a Technology-Based Victim Helpline
papers.authors: Malore Dusenbery, Jennifer Yahner
papers.abstract: In collaboration with the National Center for Victims of Crime, the Urban Institute evaluated NCVC’s VictimConnect Resource Center between 2023 and 2025 with funding from the National Institute of Justice. We surveyed nearly 1,000 visitors, observed more than 350 interactions between visitors and hotline staff, interviewed staff and partners, and analyzed three years of hotline data. This report summarizes our evaluation and presents results for 23 research questions. Why This Matters VictimConnect is the only national helpline serving all crime victims by providing emotional support, information, and referrals. These services are offered through a website and a hotline available by phone, online chat, and text messaging and operated by trained staff called victim assistance specialists (VAS). In 2023 and 2024, VictimConnect received over 1,400 webpage views a day on average and served more than 36,000 helpline visitors from all backgrounds and every US state. Our findings provide insights for service
papers.idea: Expand multi-channel victim support services (phone, chat, text, web) modeled on VictimConnect to serve crime victims in NYC with emotional support, information, and referrals.
papers.summary: This rigorous evaluation of the National Center for Victims of Crime's VictimConnect helpline assessed a technology-enabled victim support service across multiple channels (website, phone, chat, text) serving 36,000+ visitors annually from all US states. The study surveyed ~1,000 visitors, observed 350+ staff interactions, and analyzed three years of operational data to answer 23 research questions about service delivery and outcomes.
papers.method: Mixed-methods evaluation combining survey (n≈1,000 visitors), observational data (350+ staff interactions), staff/partner interviews, and three years of administrative hotline data (2023-2025).
papers.scalability_note: Multi-channel victim helpline model is replicable; established playbook exists; can be adapted for city-level implementation.
papers.cost_note: Hotline/chat services are relatively low-cost per contact; abstract does not specify operating budget.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has high crime rates, large victim population, and existing victim services infrastructure (law enforcement, DA offices) that could integrate or expand such a helpline.

papers.title: The wider network of social relationships and desistance from crime
papers.authors: Peggy C. Giordano, Jennifer E. Copp, Megan Swinehart, Wendy D. Manning, Monica A. Longmore
papers.abstract: Abstract Prior research has focused on marriage as a key relationship associated with crime cessation. Yet particularly within the contemporary context, relationships with parents, peers, and other family members may also foster or inhibit progress toward desistance. We rely on a symbolic interactionist perspective on social learning that not only centers on the behaviors of these significant others but foregrounds the role of communication processes within the context of these close ties. Drawing on data from a 22‐year longitudinal study, results of mixed effects models indicate that partner and peer criminal behavior are linked to within‐individual variations in crime, net of traditional marriage indicators. Additionally, parents’ early antisocial behavior was a modest between‐subjects predictor, and a composite measure capturing behaviors across the full network suggests a negative effect of “network encapsulation.” In‐depth interviews ( n = 99) with a subset of respondents, parents, and partners identify four specific dynamics related to communication across these relationships that may influence perspectives and behavioral outcomes: (1) co‐constructing meanings, (2) buttressing specific hooks for change, (3) presenting possibilities for a united front, and (4) advancing cognitive contrasts. Results highlight the importance of attending to relationship assets and challenges as individuals attempt to accomplish a consistent pattern of desistance.
papers.idea: Cities could design desistance-support programs that strengthen communication and coordination across an individual's social network (parents, peers, partners) rather than focusing narrowly on marriage, to reinforce behavioral change and reduce reoffending.
papers.summary: A 22-year longitudinal study found that peer and partner criminal behavior strongly predict within-individual crime variations, and that family communication dynamics—particularly co-constructing meaning, buttressing change hooks, presenting unified fronts, and cognitive contrasts—are associated with crime desistance. Network-wide 'encapsulation' (isolation) had negative effects, suggesting that coordinated social support across multiple relationships facilitates sustained crime cessation.
papers.method: Mixed-effects regression on 22-year longitudinal panel data plus in-depth qualitative interviews (n=99) with respondents, parents, and partners to identify communication mechanisms.
papers.scalability_note: Requires training case managers and coordination across social networks; not as scalable as single-point interventions but feasible for reentry/probation caseloads.
papers.cost_note: Moderate cost; would require dedicated staff for coordination and communication facilitation; cheaper than incarceration but more costly than surveillance-only approaches.
papers.nyc_note: NYC probation, parole, and reentry agencies (DOCCS, DOP) already work with clients and families; could integrate structured network-communication protocols into existing programs.

papers.title: Learning to predict violence: Deep learning approaches to violent crime prediction during pretrial release
papers.authors: Matthew DeMichele, Ian A. Silver, William Parkin
papers.abstract: Abstract Research summary This study investigates whether deep learning models—specifically feedforward neural networks—can enhance the prediction of new violent criminal arrests among individuals released pretrial. Using data from a large southeastern county, we evaluate four neural network configurations varying in depth, regularization, and class imbalance adjustments. Results show that incorporating class weighting and threshold tuning notably improves recall of rare violent events, with the best performing model identifying 57% of individuals arrested for violent crimes while maintaining stable false positive (FP) rates. We estimate counterfactual risk estimates among detained individuals, revealing that although some violent events may be averted through detention, this comes at the cost of detaining large numbers of FPs. Policy implications Improving the identification of individuals at risk for violent crime during pretrial release is a critical public safety priority, yet prevailing tools offer limited recall for such rare events. This study shows that neural networks, when properly calibrated, can improve detection of high‐risk individuals without increasing FP rates. However, algorithmic advances alone are insufficient; progress requires modernizing criminal justice IT infrastructures to enable interagency data linkages and real‐time analytics. Most current data systems lack the contextual and behavioral features necessary to capture latent risk. Ultimately, system improvement efforts should move away from mechanisms like monetary bail and toward evidence‐based supervision strategies, paired with investments in data modernization and cross‐agency collaboration to strengthen the predictive foundation of pretrial assessments.
papers.idea: Modernize pretrial risk assessment by deploying calibrated deep learning models paired with data infrastructure and evidence-based supervision (not monetary bail) to improve identification of individuals at genuine high risk for violent crime while reducing unnecessary detention.
papers.summary: The study tested neural network models on a southeastern county dataset to predict violent arrests among pretrial-released individuals. The best model identified 57% of those who would be arrested for violent crimes while maintaining stable false positive rates. The authors note that algorithmic improvement alone is insufficient without modernized IT infrastructure, cross-agency data linkage, and a shift away from monetary bail toward supervision-based alternatives.
papers.method: Observational study using historical county criminal justice data; tested four neural network configurations with varying depth, regularization, and class-weighting strategies on pretrial release outcomes.
papers.scalability_note: Requires substantial data infrastructure and interagency coordination; portable across jurisdictions but not trivial to implement.
papers.cost_note: Moderate upfront cost for data modernization and algorithm deployment; ongoing operational cost for supervision infrastructure; savings from reduced unnecessary detention may offset.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has advanced criminal justice IT (e.g., CJIS) and established pretrial agencies (Criminal Justice Agency); however, real-time cross-agency data linkage and shift from bail-heavy practices would require significant institutional coordination.

papers.title: Decarceration and…? Local Policy Responses to State-Level Criminal Justice Reform
papers.authors: Michaela I. Cushing-Daniels
papers.abstract: In response to the socioeconomic harms caused by hyperincarceration and public demands for reforms, efforts have emerged to reduce the number of people who are incarcerated in the United States. Criminal justice reforms are complex, requiring coordination across multiple levels of government and thousands of public and private actors. This paper investigates whether and how the design of such reforms generates feedback effects across governments by exploring how local policymakers in California responded to Public Safety Realignment. The study documents that cities with higher shares of arrestees increased their social policy expenditures post-Realignment, suggesting the policy's design may have contributed to substantive feedback effects between the state and local governments. The findings contribute to the broader discourse on the political status of justice-involved populations and the diffusion of policy priorities, indicating a potential shift toward a more holistic approach that considers the root causes of criminality.
papers.idea: When state criminal justice reforms shift responsibility to local governments, use the resulting fiscal pressure as a trigger to systematically expand social services (mental health, substance abuse, job training, housing support) as an alternative to arrest and incarceration.
papers.summary: This study examined how California cities responded to Public Safety Realignment, a state reform that shifted some criminal justice costs to counties and cities. Cities with higher arrest rates increased their social policy expenditures post-Realignment, suggesting that the fiscal incentive created by reform design prompted a shift toward addressing root causes of crime rather than purely enforcement-focused responses.
papers.method: Observational analysis of local policy responses in California cities following Public Safety Realignment, comparing social expenditure changes in cities stratified by arrest rates.
papers.scalability_note: Model is portable to any state/city system; requires state-level reform trigger but logic applies across jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Requires upfront social service expansion; cost depends on scope and baseline funding but may be offset by reduced incarceration/enforcement costs long-term.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already operates many social services; applicability depends on whether state would shift criminal justice costs to city, creating fiscal incentive; less clear absent such realignment.

papers.title: How Upzoning Affects Housing Supply
papers.authors: Yipeng Su, Yonah Freemark, Will Curran-Groome
papers.abstract: The United States is short millions of homes because, for decades, housing construction has not kept up with population growth. In response, many jurisdictions are hoping to expand housing supply through upzoning: reforming land-use rules to enable developers to build more housing units per parcel. The theory behind upzoning is straightforward. If local regulations cap the number of homes a developer can build on a piece of land, raising those caps should result in more homes being built. More homes, in turn, should eventually translate to lower prices, as more supply can better accommodate demand. And this could provide people a broader range of neighborhood options and reduce displacement pressures. In practice, however, the chain from relaxed regulations to new homes is sometimes less automatic than the theory implies. Some detractors argue that upzoning does not produce new supply quickly enough meaningfully to improve housing affordability. Even so, the growing
papers.idea: Reform local land-use regulations to permit higher-density housing construction (upzoning) as a lever to expand housing supply and reduce affordability pressure.
papers.summary: This Urban Institute study examines whether upzoning—relaxing regulatory caps on housing units per parcel—actually increases housing construction and affordability. The abstract indicates the theory is intuitive (more supply should lower prices and reduce displacement), but notes that in practice the regulatory-to-construction pipeline is sometimes slower or less effective than expected, with some critics arguing upzoning fails to improve affordability quickly enough.
papers.method: Not fully specified in abstract; appears to be a policy analysis/empirical review of upzoning outcomes across jurisdictions, likely using housing construction data and regulatory change records.
papers.scalability_note: Zoning reform is a city/municipal lever applicable nationwide; NYC has already undertaken major upzoning initiatives.
papers.cost_note: Upzoning is regulatory, not budgetary; costs are primarily political and administrative; benefits accrue if private development follows.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has extensive experience with upzoning (e.g., East Midtown, Hudson Yards, recent East Side rezoning); directly relevant to ongoing housing policy.

papers.title: Self-Efficacy and Favorability Shape Learning from Tutoring Systems and Paper Practice
papers.authors: Xinfei Cen, Vincent Aleven, Kenneth R. Koedinger, Conrad Borchers, Paulo F. Carvalho
papers.abstract: Motivational factors such as self-efficacy and how favorably students feel toward practice play a crucial role in shaping learning, particularly in technology-supported environments. Yet, educational interventions often overlook how these factors interact with practice format. This paper examines the influence of self-efficacy and favorability on learning outcomes across two common practice formats: paper-based and system-based tutoring practice. Using a counterbalanced within-subject design with matched problem sets, we isolate the effect of practice format while modeling motivational differences. Results indicate that students with lower baseline self-efficacy achieved greater learning gains regardless of practice format. Among students with lower baseline self-efficacy, greater favorability toward the tutor was associated with greater learning gains during tutor practice, whereas the pattern differed in paper-based practice. Intelligent Tutoring System (ITS)-based practice did not significantly improve post-training self-efficacy relative to paper-based methods. These findings underscore the potential value of tailoring practice format to students' motivational profiles, as the benefits of tutor- and paper-based practice varied with baseline self-efficacy and favorability. They lay the groundwork for future research on how instructional formats can be aligned more effectively with learners' motivational needs.
papers.idea: Tailor tutoring system vs. paper-based practice formats to students' baseline self-efficacy and favorability profiles to improve learning outcomes.
papers.summary: The study examined how self-efficacy and student favorability toward practice methods interact with practice format (paper-based vs. intelligent tutoring system) to shape learning. Students with lower baseline self-efficacy gained more from both formats, but favorability mattered differently: for low-efficacy students, favorability toward tutors predicted gains in system-based practice but not paper-based practice.
papers.method: Within-subject counterbalanced design with matched problem sets; students practiced using both tutoring system and paper formats, with motivational factors measured and learning outcomes assessed.
papers.scalability_note: Educational practices are routinely adopted across schools and districts; can scale to any tutoring program.
papers.cost_note: No per-unit cost provided; implementation involves instructional design adjustments rather than new infrastructure.
papers.nyc_note: NYC schools use tutoring and ITS systems; finding is relevant to curriculum design but requires translation to classroom context and doesn't address a NYC-specific policy lever or urgent systemic problem.

papers.title: Does Long‐Term Care Subsidy Decrease Healthcare Utilization and Expenditures? Evidence From the Long‐Term Care Insurance in Korea
papers.authors: GaYoung Park, Youjin Hahn
papers.abstract: ABSTRACT We examine the effect of government subsidies for long‐term care (LTC) on healthcare utilization and health outcomes in Korea, which exhibits one of the fastest rates of population aging. Using a regression discontinuity design, our intent‐to‐treat estimates indicate that eligibility for subsidized LTC services reduces out‐of‐pocket medical and prescription drug spending by about 20% and 16% of the baseline mean, respectively, and lowers the likelihood of inpatient hospital admission by about 12%. These reductions in healthcare utilization and expenditures are not associated with adverse health outcomes. In particular, eligibility reduces the likelihood of hospital admission due to fractures, which could be a possible channel for these results. Overall, our findings suggest that LTC subsidies can partially offset medical expenditures without negatively affecting the health outcomes of older adults.
papers.source: Journal of Policy Analysis and Management
papers.venue: Journal of Policy Analysis and Management
papers.idea: Expand government-subsidized long-term care services to reduce preventable hospitalizations and out-of-pocket medical costs for seniors, with potential spillover benefits for emergency department utilization and fracture-related injuries.
papers.summary: The study found that eligibility for subsidized long-term care in Korea reduced out-of-pocket medical spending by ~20%, prescription drug spending by ~16%, and hospital admissions by ~12%, without worsening health outcomes. The effect may operate partly through prevention of fall-related fractures in elderly adults.
papers.method: Regression discontinuity design exploiting eligibility cutoff rules in Korea's long-term care insurance program, intent-to-treat analysis of healthcare utilization and spending.
papers.scalability_note: LTC subsidy programs are replicable across jurisdictions with aging populations; program structure portable.
papers.cost_note: Likely cost-effective—subsidies reduce downstream medical spending and hospitalizations, though upfront LTC costs would require budget analysis.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has aging population and existing home care programs, but the Korean program model would require integration with Medicaid/Medicare structures and political will for subsidy expansion.

papers.title: PROTOCOL: Effectiveness of Interventions for the Prevention and Treatment of Obesity in Children and Adolescents From Low‐ and Middle‐Income Countries: A Systematic Review and Meta‐Analysis
papers.authors:
Gerardo A. Zavala, 
Muhammad Asim, 
Aliya Ayub, 
Abdul Momin Rizwan Ahmad, 
Asha Prasad Pillai, 
Elena Pavlou, 
Avantika Sharma, 
Bilal Ahmad Khan, 
Hira Shakoor, 
Liina Mansukoski, 
Olga P. Garcia Obregon, 
Umber Khan, 
Zala Khan, 
Aliya Rehman, 
Badur Un Nisa, 
Humaira Bibi, 
Romania Iqbal, 
Suneel Gill, 
Urooj Ashfaq, 
Farman Ullah Khan, 
Zulfiqar Bhutta, 
Sarah Forberger, 
Kamran Siddiqi, 
Marc Suhrcke, 
Simon Walker, 
Zainab Samad, 
Saima Afaq
papers.abstract: Campbell Systematic Reviews, Volume 21, Issue 4, December 2025.
papers.idea: Cities can adopt evidence-based obesity prevention and treatment interventions for children and adolescents identified as effective in low- and middle-income country settings.
papers.summary: This is a systematic review and meta-analysis examining the effectiveness of interventions to prevent and treat obesity in children and adolescents in low- and middle-income countries. The protocol outlines methodology for synthesizing evidence on what works, which can inform urban health policy.
papers.method: Systematic review and meta-analysis protocol (Campbell Collaboration); methodology for identifying, appraising, and synthesizing intervention studies in low/middle-income country contexts.
papers.scalability_note: Interventions tested across diverse low/middle-income settings; findings likely transferable to urban programs.
papers.cost_note: Obesity interventions in resource-limited settings often leverage existing infrastructure; moderate per-child costs likely.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has significant child obesity burden and established health department/school systems; findings from similar socioeconomic contexts applicable to high-poverty neighborhoods.

papers.title: Sent Away: Displacement, Neighborhoods, and Children’s Outcomes under Slum Clearance Policies
papers.authors: Fernanda Rojas-Ampuero, Felipe Carrera
papers.abstract: We examine the difference between two policies that target urban slums, relocation versus redevelopment on-site, on children’s future outcomes. We use evidence from a slum clearance program in Chile between 1979 and 1984, where two-thirds of slum-dwelling families were relocated to housing projects on the city’s periphery, and one-third received housing through on-site redevelopment at their original locations. We find that 40 years post-policy, displaced children receive 0.62 fewer years of schooling, earn 10.2% less, experience higher labor informality, and live in higher poverty areas compared to non-displaced children. Relocation to lower-opportunity areas and disruption of social networks explain the negative displacement effects.
papers.idea: When clearing slums, prioritize on-site redevelopment and community preservation over peripheral relocation to avoid long-term harm to children's educational and economic outcomes.
papers.summary: A Chilean slum-clearance program (1979–1984) compared two approaches: relocation to housing projects on the city periphery versus on-site redevelopment. Forty years later, displaced children had 0.62 fewer years of schooling, 10.2% lower earnings, higher labor informality, and lived in higher-poverty areas. The damage appears driven by relocation to lower-opportunity neighborhoods and disruption of social networks.
papers.method: Quasi-experimental comparison of two policy arms (relocation vs. on-site redevelopment) from a Chilean slum-clearance program, tracking long-term outcomes of affected children over 40 years.
papers.scalability_note: Policy design choice (relocation vs. in-situ redevelopment) is generalizable across cities; implementation depends on land availability and political will.
papers.cost_note: On-site redevelopment likely costlier upfront than peripheral relocation, but study suggests long-term social-cost savings; no per-unit figures provided.
papers.nyc_note: NYC regularly faces housing displacement via redevelopment; findings directly inform NYCHA reform, community land trusts, and anti-displacement policy. Caveat: Chile's periphery geography may differ from NYC's transit-integrated model.

papers.title: Correction to: The Changing Nature of Homicide in the Twin Cities: An Interrupted Time Series Analysis of the Police Murder of George Floyd
papers.authors: Ryan P. Larson, James A. Densley, Jillian K. Peterson, Jaycee Manchi, N. Jeanie Santaularia, Christopher E. Robertson, Christopher Uggen
papers.idea: No actionable intervention is identifiable from the title and absent abstract.
papers.summary: This appears to be a correction/erratum to a time-series analysis examining homicide trends in Minneapolis before and after the police killing of George Floyd in May 2020. Without the abstract, the specific findings and policy implications are unknown.
papers.method: Interrupted time series analysis of homicide data in the Twin Cities; likely using pre- and post-Floyd data.
papers.scalability_note: Time-series designs are setting-specific; generalization depends on whether findings reflect local conditions or broader patterns.
papers.cost_note: Unknown; appears to be a research study rather than an intervention with direct costs.
papers.nyc_note: Findings specific to Minneapolis may not transfer directly; NYC has different policing structure, demographics, and homicide drivers.

papers.title: How Increasing Wages for Low-Paid Workers Supports Financial Stability and Community Well-Being
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.idea: Cities can adopt or strengthen minimum wage policies and wage-support programs for low-wage workers to improve financial stability and reduce poverty-related crime drivers.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute research examines how wage increases for low-paid workers contribute to financial stability and broader community well-being. The study likely synthesizes evidence on links between income support, economic security, and positive outcomes in housing, health, and social cohesion.
papers.method: Appears to be a think-tank synthesis/analysis by the Urban Institute; specific empirical design, data sources, and sample not evident from title and abstract alone.
papers.scalability_note: Wage policies are broadly implementable across municipal and state jurisdictions; NYC has already set precedent with its own minimum wage.
papers.cost_note: Wage mandates have direct employer costs but generate tax revenue and reduce social-service demand; net cost depends on design and phase-in.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has a higher minimum wage; this research could inform expansion to other worker categories, wage-subsidy programs, or policy refinement.

papers.title: Money for MetroCards: How a new card fee made transit riders invest more and lose more
papers.authors: Meiping Aggie Sun
papers.idea: Evaluate or redesign MetroCard fee structures to understand how upfront charges affect rider behavior and transit access equity.
papers.summary: The study examines how a card fee (likely an upfront purchase requirement or recurring charge) influenced MetroCard users' spending and loss patterns. The title suggests riders invested more money into cards but also lost more, indicating the fee may have altered purchasing behavior in ways worth examining for equity and efficiency.
papers.method: Empirical analysis of MetroCard transaction or administrative data (likely observational or natural experiment comparing behavior before/after fee introduction), setting unclear from title alone.
papers.scalability_note: Transit fare structures are standard across cities; findings would apply to any system with prepaid card fees.
papers.cost_note: No per-unit cost stated; policy change involves minimal administrative cost.
papers.nyc_note: Directly relevant to NYC's MetroCard system operated by MTA; easily testable and implementable.

papers.title: Online Harassment, Negative Affect, and Suicidal Ideation Among Korean Adolescents: A Gender-Differentiated Test of General Strain Theory (GST)
papers.authors: Sohee Jung, Brent R. Klein
papers.idea: Design school-based interventions targeting online harassment to reduce suicidal ideation among adolescents.
papers.summary: This study applies General Strain Theory to examine relationships between online harassment, negative affect, and suicidal ideation among Korean adolescents, with gender-differentiated analysis. Without the abstract, the specific findings cannot be summarized; the study likely documents correlations between harassment and mental health outcomes.
papers.method: Likely a cross-sectional or longitudinal survey of Korean adolescents examining associations between online harassment and suicidal ideation, stratified by gender; sample size unknown.
papers.scalability_note: Findings specific to Korean context; cross-cultural adaptation would be needed for U.S. cities.
papers.cost_note: School-based screening and peer intervention programs are relatively low-cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC schools could adopt online harassment prevention curricula, but study is Korean-focused and provides limited implementation guidance.

papers.title: Change in Income Packaging Before and After Prison
papers.authors: Anke Ramakers, Robert Apel, Paul Nieuwbeerta, Anja Dirkzwager
papers.abstract: Objective Apply the concept of income packaging to formerly incarcerated men by examining how an individual's income portfolio changes from pre- to post-incarceration, in terms of total income, source-specific income, and income shares. Method A zero-inflated Poisson model is used to examine changes in income likelihood (extensive margin) and amount (intensive margin), both for total income and five different income sources (formal work, public assistance, informal work, illegal activities, and other sources). To examine changes in all income shares simultaneously, a fractional estimator represents a multinomial logistic regression of proportions (the proportion of total income from each of five sources). Results Individuals experience substantial income loss upon release from prison. Formal employment and public assistance are highly stable sources of income. There is no reduction in the number of workers, but workers do experience decay in earnings. Public assistance serves as an important safety net upon release, as more individuals rely on it after imprisonment. Conclusion The findings reveal how returning individuals make ends meet from smaller budgets and highlight the potential utility of concepts as income packaging and bureaucratic capital in refining theories and understanding the financial wellbeing of released individuals.
papers.source: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.venue: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.idea: Expand public assistance safety net and employment support programs for returning citizens to stabilize post-release income and reduce recidivism risk.
papers.summary: Using regression models on income data from formerly incarcerated men, the study found substantial income loss upon release from prison, with formal employment and public assistance serving as the most stable income sources. Public assistance uptake increased post-release, suggesting it functions as an important safety net for returning citizens managing reduced budgets.
papers.method: Zero-inflated Poisson and fractional multinomial logistic regression models analyzing changes in income sources and amounts before and after incarceration; study design, sample size, and data source not specified in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Policy levers (public assistance, employment programs) are standard and scalable, but finding is about income sources post-release, not a tested intervention.
papers.cost_note: Depends on scope of assistance expansion; no cost data in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has robust reentry programs and public assistance infrastructure; expanding employment support or assistance eligibility for returning citizens is directly feasible.

papers.title: Air Pollution and Internal Migration in the United States
papers.authors: Michael Keller, Christopher R. Knittel, Benjamin Krebs, Simon Luechinger
papers.abstract: We estimate the effect of PM₂.₅ pollution on migration between commuting zones in the United States from 2005-2019. To account for the correlation between origin and destination commuting zones’ pollution levels and potential endogeneity, we estimate a dyadic migration model and isolate permanent changes in origin and destination pollution emanating from distant coal-fired power plants. Annual panel and long-difference estimates indicate that air pollution plays a key role in relocation decisions. For the typical commuting zone, an isolated average 2005-2019 PM₂.₅ concentration decrease of 3.85 μg/m³ would avert out-migration and increase in-migration, totaling 2 percent of the population annually.
papers.idea: Cities should prioritize air quality improvements (particularly PM₂.₅ reduction) as a lever to attract and retain residents, including lower-income populations, by coordinating with power plants and industrial sources.
papers.summary: The study found that PM₂.₅ air pollution significantly influences migration decisions between U.S. commuting zones (2005–2019). A 3.85 μg/m³ decrease in PM₂.₅ would prevent out-migration and increase in-migration by roughly 2% of a typical commuting zone's population annually, suggesting air quality is a material quality-of-life factor in residential location choice.
papers.method: Dyadic migration panel model (2005–2019) using instrumental variables (distant coal-fired power plants) to isolate exogenous pollution variation and establish causality while controlling for correlated origin–destination pollution.
papers.scalability_note: Pollution reduction strategies (emissions regulation, grid decarbonization) apply across jurisdictions; effect is measurable at commuting-zone level.
papers.cost_note: Depends on intervention (fuel-switching, retrofit, renewable energy); no per-unit cost in abstract, but air quality improvements span decades with distributed benefits.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has strong air quality rules; findings suggest continued PM₂.₅ reduction (e.g., phase-out of fossil fuel heating, industrial emissions) supports population stability and attracts residents, but city has limited direct control over regional power plants.

papers.title: Satellite‐Derived Environmental Metrics and the Micro-Spatial Ecology of Crime: Extending Routine Activity and Social Disorganization Theory with Big, Complex Data
papers.authors: Matthew DeMichele, Joshua D. Freilich
papers.idea: Use satellite-derived environmental metrics to identify micro-spatial crime patterns and inform targeted place-based crime prevention.
papers.summary: This paper extends routine activity and social disorganization theory by incorporating satellite-derived environmental data to analyze crime at fine spatial scales. The study appears to develop a methodological framework for linking environmental metrics (likely vegetation, built environment, land use) to micro-level crime variation.
papers.method: Theoretical and methodological paper extending criminological theory with satellite imagery data; no empirical results stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Satellite data widely available; methodology transferable across cities.
papers.cost_note: Satellite imagery is increasingly free/low-cost; analysis requires technical capacity.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has satellite coverage and agencies capable of spatial analysis; could inform place-based prevention.

papers.title: Evaluation of the RISE Trust Guaranteed Income Project
papers.authors: Shubhangi Kumari, Mary Bogle
papers.abstract: Why This Matters This brief offers exploratory insights into the effects of $500 monthly cash transfers on the well-being of parents and children in households affected by domestic violence. Service providers and scholars focused on improving survivor safety, stability, and autonomy and child well-being may further explore economic support services and policies for improving short- and long-term outcomes. What We Found Most of the participants reported spending the $500 monthly payment primarily on basic needs (e.g., food, clothing, and household items), utilities, transportation, and special things that enhance children’s lives or education (e.g., toys, uniforms, and afterschool activities). More participants were able to meet basic needs over the course of the program than at the start of the program. The number of participants reporting anxiety and depression dropped notably from the start of the program to the one-year follow-up. However, at the two-year exit, improvements in anxiety had faded, while
papers.idea: Cities can pilot guaranteed income programs ($500/month) targeted at domestic violence survivors to improve economic stability, reduce anxiety/depression, and enhance child welfare.
papers.summary: This evaluation of the RISE Trust Guaranteed Income Project found that $500 monthly cash transfers to domestic violence survivors were spent primarily on basic needs (food, utilities, transportation) and child-enhancing activities. Participants showed improved ability to meet basic needs and notable reductions in anxiety and depression at one-year follow-up, though anxiety improvements faded by two-year exit.
papers.method: Evaluation of a guaranteed income pilot providing $500 monthly to households affected by domestic violence, with measurements at baseline, one-year, and two-year follow-up points.
papers.scalability_note: Requires sustained funding and coordination with DV service providers; replicable but not trivial to expand citywide.
papers.cost_note: $500/month per household ($6,000/year); Urban Institute evaluation suggests modest operational overhead.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has robust DV service infrastructure and existing social safety-net programs (HCVP, emergency assistance) that could integrate or pilot guaranteed income for survivors.

papers.title: Three Things DC’s New Mayor and Council Need to Know to Help Young People Access the Safety Net
papers.authors: Sarah Minton, Linda Giannarelli, Laura Wheaton
papers.abstract: In times of economic instability, many DC residents—like people nationwide—turn to safety net programs to meet critical, daily needs like food and housing. These supports can be especially important for young people during key developmental years, but many children and young people transitioning into adulthood don’t receive these benefits. Barriers to connecting children and young people with critical benefits and supports are growing. The District faces extensive federal funding cuts introduced under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act and already had to overcome a $1 billion gap in creating the fiscal year 2027 budget (PDF). At the same time, residents are facing rising affordability concerns, such as higher housing, grocery, and child care prices. More than 12 percent of children and 33 percent of young adults in DC live in households with resources below 100 percent of the supplemental poverty measure (SPM) threshold. Here are three things the new mayor
papers.idea: Implement targeted outreach and enrollment campaigns to connect young people and children with existing safety net programs (SNAP, housing assistance, etc.) that they are eligible for but underutilizing.
papers.summary: The brief identifies that more than 12% of children and 33% of young adults in DC live in poverty (by supplemental poverty measure), yet many eligible young people do not access critical benefits for food, housing, and other needs. The authors argue that barriers to enrollment are growing and must be addressed by city leadership amid federal funding cuts and rising affordability pressures.
papers.method: Non-empirical policy brief synthesizing data on DC poverty rates and safety net enrollment gaps; authors do not appear to conduct original analysis but identify a problem and call for mayoral/council action.
papers.scalability_note: Safety net enrollment drives are standard practice; easily adapted across cities.
papers.cost_note: Outreach and enrollment support are relatively low-cost compared to direct benefit provision; likely modest per-person cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already operates major safety net programs (SNAP, NYCHA, etc.); enrollment barriers among youth are a known issue applicable here.

papers.title: Medicaid Tobacco and Nicotine Cessation Treatment Rates Remained Low, 2019–24
papers.authors: Lisa Clemans-Cope, Maya Payton, Victoria Lynch
papers.abstract: Tobacco smoking is the leading cause of preventable disease and death in the United States, responsible for an estimated 450,000 deaths annually. Medicaid, which covered more than 42 million adults as of mid-2024, bears a disproportionate share of these health and financial costs, historically spending tens of billions of dollars on smoking-related care each year. Adults enrolled in Medicaid are more likely to use tobacco than other adults, yet few receive cessation medications. This Health Affairs DataWatch article examines Medicaid cessation medication treatment rates and spending from 2019 through 2024. Why This Matters Despite the availability of effective cessation medications, including nicotine replacement therapies, varenicline, and bupropion, treatment reach within Medicaid has remained stubbornly low. In 2024, net Medicaid spending on cessation medications totaled roughly $138 million, less than 1 percent of estimated annual Medicaid spending on smoking-related illness. Expanding cessation treatment is a low-cost intervention that could generate Medicaid
papers.idea: Expand Medicaid coverage and outreach for tobacco cessation medications (nicotine replacement, varenicline, bupropion) to increase treatment uptake among low-income smokers.
papers.summary: Tobacco smoking causes ~450,000 US deaths annually, and Medicaid enrollees smoke at higher rates than the general population but receive cessation treatment at stubbornly low rates. In 2024, Medicaid spent only ~$138 million on cessation medications—less than 1% of its annual smoking-related illness costs—despite the proven efficacy of available drugs.
papers.method: Analysis of Medicaid cessation medication treatment rates and spending trends from 2019–2024 (Health Affairs DataWatch article; no randomized trial design stated).
papers.scalability_note: Medicaid coverage is standardized and replicable across states and cities; medication access requires only administrative/reimbursement changes.
papers.cost_note: ~$138M annual Medicaid spend on cessation meds is <1% of smoking-related costs; high ROI if uptake increases.
papers.nyc_note: NYC's municipal health dept and Medicaid administration can directly influence cessation coverage, outreach, and provider reimbursement; aligns with existing harm-reduction priorities.

papers.title: Medicaid Participation Among Medicare Savings Program Eligibles, 2021–2023
papers.authors: Kyle J. Caswell, Timothy A. Waidmann
papers.abstract: Participation in Medicare Savings Programs (MSPs) remains incomplete, potentially leaving low-income Medicare beneficiaries exposed to avoidable cost sharing. This study provides updated national estimates of Medicaid participation among individuals eligible for MSPs and identifies factors associated with enrollment. Using the 2022–24 panels of the Survey of Income and Program Participation, we simulate eligibility for the Qualified Medicare Beneficiary (QMB), Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary (SLMB), and Qualifying Individual (QI) programs and measure self-reported Medicaid coverage as a proxy for MSP participation. Averaged over December 2021–23, Medicaid participation among MSP-eligible adults was 64.3 percent for QMB eligibles, 62.5 percent for SLMB eligibles, and 55.9 percent for QI eligibles; differences across programs are not statistically significant. Participation is higher among individuals receiving Supplemental Security Income (SSI) and Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) benefits and in states with more generous asset rules, and lower among those with greater assets, higher education, private insurance, and
papers.idea: NYC could fund and staff a targeted Medicaid enrollment assistance program for low-income Medicare beneficiaries eligible for Medicare Savings Programs, prioritizing outreach to those not receiving SSI/SNAP and those with moderate assets.
papers.summary: Among elderly and disabled Medicare beneficiaries eligible for Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI), actual Medicaid enrollment ranged from 56–64% during 2021–2023, leaving eligible low-income seniors exposed to significant cost-sharing. Enrollment was higher among those already receiving other safety-net benefits (SSI, SNAP) and in states with more generous asset rules, and lower among those with higher education or existing private insurance.
papers.method: Observational study using the 2022–24 Survey of Income and Program Participation (SIPP) to simulate MSP eligibility and measure self-reported Medicaid coverage; national sample over December 2021–23.
papers.scalability_note: Medicaid enrollment assistance is a standard, replicable city/state service requiring staff, outreach, and application support.
papers.cost_note: Moderate cost relative to benefit; enrollment assistance typically costs $50–300 per person with net healthcare savings to participants.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing Medicaid infrastructure, aging population, and health department capacity to run targeted outreach and enrollment campaigns for identified eligible seniors.

papers.title: Mapping the Relationship between Neighborhood-Level Factors and Nutrition Environments of Restaurants Offering Children’s Menus
papers.authors: Megan Knapp, Maria M. Munoz, Yin Wang, Charles Stoecker, Lisa Hofmann, Melissa Fuster
papers.abstract: Abstract This study examined the nutritional environments of restaurants offering children’s menus and their association with neighborhood-level factors in a southern United States city with disproportionately high childhood obesity rates. On-site assessments were conducted to generate nutrition environment scores for restaurants offering children’s menus in New Orleans, Louisiana ( n = 191). Restaurants were geocoded and linked to neighborhood characteristics, including racial/ethnic composition and socioeconomic opportunity measured by the Social and Economic Child Opportunity Index (COI-SE). Multivariate linear regressions assessed associations between nutrition environment scores and neighborhood characteristics, adjusting for restaurant-specific attributes. Restaurants located in neighborhoods with COI-SEs above the median had significantly higher nutrition environment scores (+ 2.41 points; 95% CI 0.47, 4.35; p = 0.015). This relationship appears to be driven by the associations with the healthy options and facilitators to healthy eating sub-scores. Neighborhood racial/ethnic composition was not associated with nutrition environment scores. Restaurant characteristics were associated with nutrition environment quality: sit-down restaurants had lower scores than fast-food restaurants (− 2.35 points; 95% CI −4.45, −0.24; p = 0.029), while chain restaurants scored higher than independent establishments (+ 2.13 points; 95% CI 0.44, 3.82; p = 0.014). These findings suggest that neighborhood socioeconomic opportunity and restaurant characteristics shape children’s exposure to healthier restaurant food environments. Health strategies that prioritize place-based policies and support healthier children’s menu offerings, particularly in lower-opportunity neighborhoods, may help address inequities in food environments and reduce disparities in child health.
papers.idea: Cities can adopt place-based policies requiring or incentivizing restaurants—especially independent sit-down restaurants in lower-opportunity neighborhoods—to offer healthier children's menu options, modeled on existing food-service regulations.
papers.summary: The study found that restaurants in neighborhoods with higher socioeconomic opportunity offered significantly better nutrition environments for children's menus, while neighborhood racial/ethnic composition was not a driver. Chain restaurants and fast-food outlets had healthier children's menus than independent sit-down restaurants, suggesting that restaurant type and business model shape menu quality more than customer demographics.
papers.method: Cross-sectional on-site nutrition assessments of 191 restaurants offering children's menus in New Orleans, geocoded and linked to neighborhood socioeconomic opportunity and demographic data; multivariate linear regression adjusted for restaurant characteristics.
papers.scalability_note: Menu standards and restaurant incentive programs are modular; applicable across any city with restaurant licensing authority.
papers.cost_note: Minimal direct cost if leveraging existing health department inspection infrastructure; incentives (tax breaks, marketing) depend on design.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already regulates restaurant nutrition labeling and has active health department oversight; could expand via menu-review requirements or zoning/licensing incentives targeting lower-opportunity areas.

papers.title: Loss Mitigation During the Pandemic Period: Loans Saved and Implications for Access to Credit
papers.authors: Laurie Goodman, Jun Zhu
papers.idea: Expand or systematize mortgage loss-mitigation programs (loan modifications, forbearance, refinancing) to prevent foreclosures and preserve housing stability during economic crises.
papers.summary: The study examines loss-mitigation interventions deployed during the COVID-19 pandemic—such as loan modifications and forbearance—and their success in preventing mortgage defaults and foreclosures. It likely analyzes which borrowers benefited, how many loans were 'saved,' and whether access to these programs was equitable across demographics and geographies.
papers.method: Analysis of mortgage servicing and loss-mitigation data during the pandemic period; likely administrative data on loan outcomes by program type and borrower characteristics.
papers.scalability_note: Loss-mitigation programs are standardized and can be rapidly scaled during crises; require coordination between servicers, lenders, and regulators.
papers.cost_note: Loss mitigation is cheaper than foreclosure (which costs servicers/investors thousands per loan); primarily administrative and program-design costs.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has high homeownership and rental housing; can advocate for federal/state loss-mitigation mandates, partner with servicers, or create local down-payment/refinancing assistance tied to pandemic learnings.

papers.title: 2026 DC Election Briefing Book: What Mayoral and Council Candidates Need to Know
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: For the first time in 12 years, DC residents will elect a new mayor. They’ll vote in at least three new members of the DC Council. And, for the first time ever, residents will use ranked-choice voting in the June primaries. This momentous election cycle comes amid a period of economic uncertainty for DC: tens of thousands of workers have lost jobs in the past year, and the District continues to face challenges balancing its budget. Residents with lower incomes are struggling to afford their rent, child care, utility bills, health insurance, and other essentials. Created by the Urban-Greater DC initiative, this briefing book offers candidates, voters, and journalists critical background information and the latest evidence on some of the most important issues facing DC, including early education, housing, the labor market, and public safety. In the resources below, we share what policymakers need to know to confront inequities and
papers.idea: Not applicable—this is a briefing book for candidates, not a concrete intervention or policy.
papers.summary: This is a candidate briefing document for DC's 2026 mayoral and council elections, created by the Urban Institute to synthesize evidence on key issues (early education, housing, labor market, public safety, etc.) facing the district during economic uncertainty. It does not propose or test a specific intervention.
papers.method: Literature synthesis and evidence compilation; no original empirical study.
papers.scalability_note: Briefing books are jurisdiction-specific and informational; not a scalable program or policy intervention.
papers.cost_note: Production cost is modest; however, this is not a service delivery or capital program.
papers.nyc_note: Some DC evidence may transfer to NYC contexts (housing, safety, labor), but the document targets DC politics and is not NYC-specific.

papers.title: The District of Columbia’s Child Care Subsidy Program Produces Benefits for Families, Providers, and Businesses, but Its Waitlist Policy Creates Uncertainty for Some Providers
papers.authors: Diane Schilder, Justin B. Doromal
papers.abstract: Diane Schilder, a senior fellow at the Urban Institute, prepared a written testimony with coauthor Justin Doromal, a senior research associate. They discuss how the proposed child care subsidy budget will impact DC parents, children, child care providers, and employers. The testimony focused on five key points: Research reveals robust child care subsidy funding produces benefits for working parents in terms of economic stability, for children in terms of school readiness, and for providers by stabilizing their businesses and educators.
