Subsidized child care providers serve over 7,000 children annually and rely on subsidies as well as funding from the Pay Equity Fund to provide high-quality child care to young children. The proposed subsidy budget may result in retaining a waitlist, which will delay access to affordable care for many DC families. States that have implemented child care subsidy waitlists have documented negative impacts on parental employment, children’s stable participation in
papers.idea: Eliminate or substantially reduce child care subsidy program waitlists to ensure continuous access and reduce gaps in parental employment and child care stability.
papers.summary: DC's child care subsidy program demonstrates measurable benefits for working parents' economic stability, children's school readiness, and provider business sustainability, serving over 7,000 children annually. However, the program's waitlist policy creates uncertainty for providers and delays access for families, with research from other states showing documented negative impacts on parental employment and children's participation continuity.
papers.method: Written testimony synthesizing research findings on subsidy program outcomes; appears to combine program data from DC (7,000+ children served) with comparative evidence from other states on waitlist impacts.
papers.scalability_note: Child care subsidy programs are standard municipal/state infrastructure; waitlist elimination is straightforward policy lever applicable across jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Requires expanded funding to clear waitlists; abstract does not specify incremental cost but notes program already produces ROI for employers and parents.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already operates child care subsidy program (NYCCARE); waitlist management and funding allocation are direct levers for city policy; directly parallel to DC context.

papers.title: Oklahoma Shows How Affordable Housing Can Also Be an Economic Driver
papers.authors: Jorge González-Hermoso
papers.abstract: Solving the current housing crisis requires that all governments work together to produce and preserve affordable housing. But public agencies are often balancing how to allocate their scarce dollars in ways that address housing shortages, especially those of affordable units, with other policy goals, like strengthening local economies. Our new report shows the decision doesn’t have to be a trade-off. Analyzing 45 recent multifamily housing projects in Oklahoma that received low-income housing tax credit (LIHTC) equity, we found that financing the production and rehabilitation of affordable housing also delivers economic benefits alongside its core social mission. These projects are associated with the creation of more than 4,000 jobs and over $800 million in economic output, and for every $1 of public investment, 43 cents flow back to public coffers through tax revenues. Oklahoma’s experience makes a compelling case for other localities: Investing in affordable housing doesn’t just provide the shelter
papers.idea: Use low-income housing tax credit (LIHTC) financing to develop and rehabilitate affordable multifamily housing as a dual strategy that simultaneously addresses housing shortage and generates local economic activity and tax revenue.
papers.summary: Analysis of 45 LIHTC-financed multifamily projects in Oklahoma found they generated over 4,000 jobs and $800 million in economic output, while returning 43 cents per public dollar invested through tax revenues. The study demonstrates that affordable housing production need not trade off against economic development goals.
papers.method: Retrospective analysis of 45 completed multifamily housing projects in Oklahoma that received LIHTC equity, measuring employment creation, economic output, and tax revenue generation.
papers.scalability_note: LIHTC is a federal program already deployed nationwide; replication requires no new legislative infrastructure, only local strategy and coordination.
papers.cost_note: Strong cost-effectiveness implied by 43-cent return per public dollar; leverages tax credits rather than pure subsidy.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already uses LIHTC extensively; study provides evidence to justify expanded LIHTC deployment and multi-agency coordination around housing-as-economic-driver strategy.

papers.title: Improving High School Transcript Records via Item Response Theory to Predict Postsecondary College and Labor Market Outcomes
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: This project examines course grades and course‑taking patterns to develop a scalable measure of transcript strength that can identify which course choices most strongly correlate with postsecondary and labor market success, offering a more accurate way to account for course‑selection behavior and selection effects when comparing student trajectories. Building on prior work using Delaware data, the team will expand the transcript‑strength measure to Texas, refine it from an end‑of‑high‑school indicator to grade‑specific measures, and extend it into domain‑specific strength indexes, such as STEM (science, technology, engineering, and mathematics) and humanities, rather than a single unidimensional score. In so doing, the team will make transcripts a more useful tool for measuring skills. By linking domain‑specific measures to college major, occupational field, and outcome data (including college‑level earnings, employment, and early labor market trajectories), the study will generate actionable insights into how transcript‑embedded skills translate into later opportunities and how schools can
papers.idea: NYC schools could adopt transcript-strength measurement tools to guide course-selection counseling and identify high-impact course pathways that predict college completion and earnings, enabling targeted academic advising.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute developed a scalable measurement method using item response theory to identify which high school courses and course-taking patterns most strongly predict postsecondary success and labor market outcomes. The work expands prior Delaware analysis to Texas data and creates domain-specific transcript strength indexes (STEM, humanities, etc.) that link course choices to college major, occupational field, earnings, and employment trajectories.
papers.method: Correlational analysis of high school transcript data (grades and course-taking patterns) from Delaware and Texas, linked to postsecondary outcomes including college major, earnings, and employment; uses item response theory to develop a composite measure of transcript strength.
papers.scalability_note: Method tested across two states; transcript data exist in all school systems; limited by need for linked postsecondary outcome data.
papers.cost_note: Primarily a data-analysis tool requiring initial investment in linkage infrastructure, then modest ongoing cost per school/district.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has large, digitized transcript systems and growing postsecondary outcome data infrastructure; tool could be adapted for counseling dashboards and course-recommendation systems.

papers.title: Technical Education, Technical Skills, and Economic Mobility Through Careers: Evidence from Skill Credentials
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: While industry increasingly looks to recognized credentials, we still know little about the pathways through which these credentials contribute to higher education success and early economic mobility. This project uses statewide Michigan data—guided by a principal investigator with 15 years of experience working with the state—to understand how skills translate to adult outcomes and to examine students’ postsecondary pathways, including how focused they are on career-aligned course sequences and how efficiently they move through required sequences into the labor market. The study evaluates whether high school skill‑based industry credentials are valid, fair, and predictive measures of students’ technical skills (beyond other academic skills) and whether earning the credentials improves career clarity and early in‑field postsecondary momentum. Using administrative data, the team will map credentials and academic records to O*NET skill categories through natural language processing; will test convergent, divergent, and predictive validity of the resulting skill measures; and will construct
papers.idea: Cities can adopt industry-recognized skill credentials in high schools as a validated pathway to early labor-market entry and economic mobility, potentially reducing youth joblessness and improving career clarity.
papers.summary: The study uses Michigan administrative data to evaluate whether high school industry credentials validly measure technical skills beyond academics, improve career clarity, and predict early postsecondary success and labor-market entry. It maps credentials to O*NET skill categories and tests whether earning credentials accelerates career-aligned pathways into employment.
papers.method: Longitudinal observational study using statewide Michigan administrative data (high school records linked to postsecondary and employment outcomes), with natural language processing to align credentials to O*NET skill taxonomies and validity testing.
papers.scalability_note: Credential frameworks and O*NET mapping are portable across states; implementation depends on existing high school infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Credential programs typically low-cost add-ons to existing CTE; primarily administrative burden.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing CTE infrastructure (Career and Technical Education); finding could directly inform DOE credential strategy and align with economic-mobility goals.

papers.title: Do Social and Emotional Learning Trajectories Predict Upward Mobility: Evidence from California
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: Responding to our call for deeper insight into how socioemotional skills develop, this project leverages longitudinal data from a California school district serving 27,000 students to generate novel estimates of how social and emotional learning (SEL) competencies—as defined by the CASEL framework—change across multiple years for individual students, moving beyond traditional point‑in‑time snapshots to produce growth measures of SEL development. Using student‑level trajectories from grades 7 to 9, the team will model how competencies such as self‑awareness, self‑management, and relationship skills evolve and examine how these developmental patterns predict early mobility‑relevant outcomes, including absenteeism, academic performance, advanced course taking, and on‑time high school graduation. By comparing growth‑based SEL indicators with static measures and assessing whether they more accurately capture student development across racial and socioeconomic groups, the study aims to improve the precision and usefulness of SEL measurement for districts seeking to understand and support skills tied to long‑run educational
papers.idea: Adopt growth-based social-emotional learning (SEL) measurement in NYC schools to identify and support students on trajectories toward improved attendance, graduation, and economic mobility.
papers.summary: This study develops longitudinal growth measures of SEL competencies (self-awareness, self-management, relationship skills) across grades 7–9 in a California district of 27,000 students, and tests whether trajectory-based SEL indicators predict near-term outcomes like absenteeism, grades, advanced course enrollment, and on-time graduation. The work moves beyond static point-in-time SEL assessments to capture how skills develop over time, with the goal of improving measurement precision across racial and socioeconomic subgroups.
papers.method: Longitudinal analysis of student-level SEL trajectories from grades 7–9 in a single California school district, modeling growth patterns and their association with graduation, attendance, and academic outcomes; comparison of growth-based vs. static SEL measures.
papers.scalability_note: SEL frameworks and measurement tools already exist; primary work is implementation and staff training in any district.
papers.cost_note: Primarily staff time and modest assessment system cost; no explicit per-unit cost provided.
papers.nyc_note: NYC DOE already administers SEL curricula and surveys; could adapt this longitudinal growth-tracking methodology into existing data infrastructure.

papers.title: Mind the Gap: Institutional Capacity, Globalization, and the Discrepancy Between Criminal Justice and Health Homicide Data
papers.authors: Bo Jiang, Mateus R. Santos, Gary LaFree
papers.idea: Align criminal justice and public health data systems to improve homicide measurement and response coordination.
papers.summary: The study examines discrepancies between criminal justice and health sector homicide data, likely exploring how institutional silos and globalization patterns affect data quality and availability. The research appears to focus on measurement and institutional capacity issues rather than proposing a specific intervention.
papers.method: Unknown—no abstract provided; likely a comparative institutional or data quality analysis across jurisdictions.
papers.scalability_note: Data system alignment is reproducible across cities but requires coordination across multiple agencies.
papers.cost_note: System reconciliation is relatively low-cost compared to operational interventions, though implementation varies by current data infrastructure.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has multiple homicide data sources (NYPD, medical examiner, health dept); alignment is feasible but requires inter-agency cooperation.

papers.title: AI systems out-persuade expert humans
papers.authors: Kobi Hackenburg, Caroline Wagner, Luke Hewitt, Ben M. Tappin, Ed Saunders, Hannah Rose Kirk, Helen Margetts, Christopher Summerfield
papers.abstract: Many societal decisions are settled by contests of persuasion. Conversational AI is a powerful new entrant in these contests, but whether it can out-persuade skilled and highly incentivized humans has remained unclear. Here, in a series of four preregistered experiments (n = 18,978 conversations from 6,923 people), we pitted AI systems against a range of human persuaders, including laypeople, winners of a separately preregistered four-round online persuasion tournament, professional canvassers, and world championship debaters. We found that AI systems were reliably more persuasive than expert humans, even when expert humans chose their issues, researched in advance, underwent hours of live, structured practice, and were incentivized with £1,000 cash bonuses. In a follow-up study, AI's advantage persisted after experts received a coaching tool that let them practice against the AI that beat them, review their performance history, and see what AI would have said at key moments. We found converging evidence that AI's advantage stemmed from rapidly deploying larger quantities of information: after coaching, expert humans could tie an AI constrained to respond at human speeds and with human-length messages. In a final study, we show that AI's advantage extends to consequential real-world behavior: AI was nearly 3x more effective than professional canvassers from a UK fundraising firm at raising real-money donations to Save the Children. Together, these results establish that frontier AI systems out-persuade expert humans in conversation, with significant implications for political communication.
papers.idea: None identified—this is a foundational science paper on AI persuasion capacity, not an actionable city intervention.
papers.summary: Four preregistered experiments (n=18,978) found that AI conversational systems out-persuaded expert humans—including professional debaters, tournament winners, and canvassers—even when humans chose topics, had advance prep, and received cash incentives. AI's advantage persisted after expert coaching and was attributed to rapid deployment of larger information volumes; AI was also ~3x more effective than professional canvassers at fundraising.
papers.method: Four preregistered randomized experiments comparing AI persuasiveness against laypeople, expert persuaders, professional canvassers, and world championship debaters, plus a real-world fundraising follow-up with a UK nonprofit.
papers.scalability_note: Addresses a systemic risk (AI manipulation) rather than enabling a scalable city program; scaling would require citywide policy guardrails or messaging interventions, not straightforward replication.
papers.cost_note: No per-unit implementation cost given; study was experimental, not a deployed program.
papers.nyc_note: Identifies a risk (AI out-persuading humans in civic/political contexts) but offers no ready-made policy or program NYC could adopt; would require downstream work on guardrails, disclosure rules, or training responses.

papers.title: Upstream and Downstream Racial Disparities in Prison Rule Violation Processes
papers.authors: John Wooldredge, Joshua C. Cochran, Peter Tanksley
papers.abstract: Objectives To advance research on racial disparities in processing prison rule violators, we examined disparities at citation, “conviction,” and sanctioning to assess cascading race effects on sanctions via harsher upstream decisions. Methods Black and White persons admitted to Ohio prisons in a 5-year period and cited for rule violations were examined ( N = 39,356). Outcomes included total cited charges, total convicted charges, and sanctions. Path analysis was used to estimate direct and indirect race effects on dispositions, controlling for covariates of convicted charges and sanction severity. Results Black individuals were cited for more rule violations but convicted on fewer violations (controlling counts cited) than White individuals, had lower odds of placement in disciplinary segregation, and served less time in segregation. Indirect race effects on convicted counts and sanctions were statistically significant. Conclusions Rule infraction boards may serve as a downstream correction to upstream racial disparities in the prison infraction process. Despite a significant cascading race effect on convicted charges via cited charges, cascading race effects on prison sanctions via convicted charges were refuted, where correctional officers’ decisions did not culminate in harsher sanctions for Black individuals at rule infraction hearings. These hearings may constitute an important check on upstream decisions.
papers.source: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.venue: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.idea: Strengthen rule infraction board review processes in prisons to correct upstream racial disparities in officer citations and charges.
papers.summary: The study examined 39,356 Ohio prison admissions and found Black individuals were cited for more rule violations but convicted on fewer violations than White individuals, with lower placement in segregation. Rule infraction boards appeared to partially correct these upstream disparities, suggesting they may serve as a downstream check on officer decision-making.
papers.method: Observational path analysis of Ohio prison administrative data over a 5-year period, examining citation, conviction, and sanctioning outcomes across racial groups with covariate control.
papers.scalability_note: Applicable to any jurisdiction with prison systems, but requires existing administrative infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Low cost—mainly involves review of existing procedures; no major new expenditures implied.
papers.nyc_note: NYC does not operate state prisons (NY State does); limited direct municipal lever, though findings could inform DOC jail practices.

papers.title: Beliefs and Actions under Government Policy Uncertainty: Evidence from Student Loan Forgiveness
papers.authors: Dmitri K. Koustas, Michael Weber, Constantine Yannelis
papers.abstract: How does uncertainty about future government policy affect households’ beliefs and subsequent borrowing, spending and debt payment behavior? We study these questions through the lens of student loan forgiveness in the United States, which following electoral promises, was announced in 2022 but never implemented due to judicial rulings. We conduct a customized information provision experiment embedded in a survey eliciting real-time beliefs about future debt forgiveness and repayment, which we link to credit bureau data, employment verification data, and nondurables consumption. Eligible borrowers who are more optimistic about forgiveness reduce payments on student loans by \$40 per month and increase non-durable spending by $100 per month. We also find some evidence optimistic borrowers may postpone durable spending waiting for uncertainty to resolve. Borrowers optimistic about future payment pauses make fewer payments on their student loans, reduce payment by $40 per month and are 7.5 percentage points more likely to be delinquent after payments resume. A quantification exercise finds welfare losses from incorrect beliefs can exceed 43 percent of the initial loan balance. Our results provide micro-evidence on the role of policy uncertainty in household decision-making, and have implications for government announcements and commitment policy.
papers.idea: Cities could clarify and commit to transparent timelines for government benefits/policies to reduce household uncertainty and improve financial decision-making.
papers.summary: The study examined how uncertainty about student loan forgiveness affected household beliefs and behavior using an information experiment linked to credit bureau data. Borrowers optimistic about forgiveness reduced loan payments by $40/month and increased spending by $100/month, while those expecting payment pauses were 7.5 percentage points more likely to become delinquent; welfare losses from incorrect beliefs exceeded 43% of initial loan balance.
papers.method: Customized information provision experiment embedded in survey eliciting beliefs about debt forgiveness, linked to credit bureau data, employment verification, and consumption data; setting was U.S. student loan market during 2022 forgiveness announcement.
papers.scalability_note: Federal student loans are not a municipal lever; cities cannot implement loan forgiveness or repayment policy.
papers.cost_note: Study itself required survey + data linkage; policy implementation (if applicable federally) depends on mechanism.
papers.nyc_note: Student loan policy is federal. NYC has no direct authority over loan forgiveness or repayment terms.

papers.title: Shadows of Transparency: Signaling Transparency-Impeding Behavior Using Public Data
papers.authors: Jos Zuijderwijk, Iris Beerepoot, Thomas Martens, Eva Knies, Tanja van der Lippe, Hajo A. Reijers
papers.abstract: Freedom of Information (FOI) laws aim to increase government transparency, yet existing assessments focus mainly on legal compliance and procedural outcomes, leaving organizational behavior underexamined. As FOI processes are increasingly mediated through digital information systems, public response data offer traces of how organizations handle requests and disclosures in practice. This paper develops and evaluates a pattern-based approach that uses such data to signal transparency-impeding behavior across government agencies and over time. Drawing on prior literature, we identify nine recurring behavioral patterns that undermine transparency. Using a dataset of 15,277 Dutch FOI dossiers comprising 139,449 documents, we operationalize and evaluate measurable indicators for six of these patterns, demonstrating that they enable systematic comparison of disclosure practices across agencies and over time. Expert interviews with researchers, journalists, and civil servants confirm the interpretability and practical usefulness of the indicators for investigative prioritization, comparative research, and transparency oversight.
papers.idea: Adopt automated pattern-detection indicators to audit government agencies' Freedom of Information request handling and flag systemic non-compliance or delay tactics, enabling targeted oversight and enforcement.
papers.summary: This study developed a measurable framework to identify nine behavioral patterns that obstruct government transparency in FOI processes. Using 15,277 Dutch FOI dossiers, researchers operationalized six patterns into quantifiable indicators and validated them through expert interviews, showing they can systematically compare disclosure practices across agencies and flag problematic behavior.
papers.method: Pattern-based analysis of 15,277 Dutch FOI dossiers (139,449 documents) with operationalization of six behavioral indicators and expert validation interviews with researchers, journalists, and civil servants.
papers.scalability_note: Digital FOI systems exist in most developed cities; indicator framework portable to any jurisdiction with data.
papers.cost_note: Primarily software/analyst time to implement pattern detection on existing FOI databases; minimal marginal cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC FOIL law exists; city maintains FOI request data; municipal agencies already digitized; framework directly applicable to audit compliance.

papers.title: Three Things DC's New Mayor and Council Need to Know About Electricity Affordability
papers.authors: Isabella Remor
papers.abstract: In the past year, residents of Washington, DC, have seen the greatest increase in residential electricity prices in the US, reflecting both national trends and local realities. Increased electrification and data center growth concentrated in Northern Virginia has strained the regional electricity market. Wholesale prices that determine how much Pepco pays its suppliers for power increased more than 400 percent in 2025 compared with 2024, with generation charges going up 119 percent. These supply-side costs sit largely outside local regulators’ control, but the portion of DC residents’ bills that local regulators can directly influence, roughly 30 percent, is where the next mayor and council have real levers to pull. These rising electricity costs have the greatest impact on those already struggling to make ends meet. Residents in neighborhoods with the lowest incomes spend a disproportionate share of their income on electricity, and assistance programs aren’t reaching the households that need
papers.idea: Expand and improve utility assistance programs and regulate the ~30% of electricity bills under local control to reduce energy cost burden on low-income households.
papers.summary: DC residents experienced the largest residential electricity price increase in the US in the past year, driven by wholesale market pressures and regional electrification. While wholesale costs are largely outside local regulatory control, the 30% of bills influenced by local regulators represents a direct lever. Low-income neighborhoods disproportionately bear this burden, and existing assistance programs are not reaching eligible households.
papers.method: Policy analysis of DC electricity pricing structure, wholesale vs. regulated cost breakdown, and assessment of assistance program reach (descriptive/non-empirical).
papers.scalability_note: Utility regulation and bill assistance are standard municipal/state functions; applicable to most US cities with municipal oversight of utility commissions.
papers.cost_note: Program expansion costs depend on design, but regulatory reforms and improved targeting of existing assistance can reduce per-household burden with modest incremental cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has Con Edison utility regulation, LIHEAP/assistance programs, and municipal authority over utility commission; framework directly transferable.

papers.title: Transit-Oriented Development Can Help Cities Grow. Which Urban Areas Are Doing Best?
papers.authors: Yonah Freemark
papers.abstract: Transit-oriented development is the creation of dense, mixed-use, and walkable neighborhoods within easy access to public transportation. These communities can accommodate urban growth while encouraging more transit use and creating vibrant centers of activity. In turn, this approach can lower car congestion, reduce pollution, and make life more affordable by shifting residents out of their cars and onto trains or buses. The Urban Institute’s new interactive tool explores where and how effectively states and urban areas have invested in transit-oriented development, showing that nationally, there are millions more homes near transit compared with several decades ago. But how does this growth differ across urban areas? In this article, I leverage data from the tool to answer that question, examining transit availability, adjacency of housing to transit, and housing trends over time. Better transit service leads to higher transit ridership Despite the universal need for effective transportation, urban areas throughout the
papers.idea: Cities should strategically invest in transit-oriented development (dense, mixed-use neighborhoods near transit hubs) to increase transit ridership, reduce car congestion, and lower housing costs.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute created an interactive tool measuring transit-oriented development across U.S. urban areas, finding that housing near transit has grown substantially over decades. The analysis examines transit availability, housing adjacency to transit, and housing trends, with a finding that better transit service correlates with higher ridership.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis using an interactive geospatial tool to measure transit availability, housing density near transit, and longitudinal housing trends across multiple urban areas.
papers.scalability_note: Transit-oriented development is a standard urban planning strategy deployable across any city with transit infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Requires upfront zoning/planning reform and coordination but leverages private development; long-term affordability gains through density and transit access.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has extensive transit infrastructure and existing TOD around subway stations; directly applicable to zoning reform and development policy in the five boroughs.

papers.title: Tracking Transit-Oriented Housing Development
papers.authors: Yonah Freemark, Mitchell Thorson, Lydia Nguyen, Dana Ferrante, Lauren Lastowka, Alysheia Shaw-Dansby
papers.abstract: As communities across the US face housing shortages and high housing costs, more state and local leaders are turning to transit-oriented development (also known as TOD) as a solution. Transit-oriented development is a valuable tool that helps communities concentrate homes, offices, public services, and recreation in walkable, mixed-use areas with access to frequent, reliable, and fast public transportation. At a time when half of US families can’t afford essential costs of living, transit-oriented development can help reduce everyday living expenses for everyone, with particularly meaningful cost savings for families with low and moderate incomes. When people can reliably use transit to get to and from work, school, and recreation, they can reduce their reliance on cars and lessen the burden of high gas, vehicle, and insurance prices. Transit-oriented development also benefits communities overall. It can reduce pollution and traffic congestion, while increasing transit ridership and accommodating population growth. Although more
papers.idea: Cities can adopt transit-oriented development (TOD) policies to concentrate mixed-use housing, offices, and services near frequent public transit, reducing housing costs and car dependency for low- and moderate-income residents.
papers.summary: This Urban Institute study tracks transit-oriented development across US communities as a policy response to housing shortages and affordability crises. TOD concentrates homes and services in walkable areas with reliable transit access, reducing living expenses (especially car costs) and congestion while increasing ridership and accommodating growth.
papers.method: Appears to be a descriptive tracking/measurement study of TOD initiatives across US communities, though the abstract does not specify sample size, data sources, or analytic approach.
papers.scalability_note: TOD is a replicable policy framework adopted by dozens of cities; no special local conditions needed.
papers.cost_note: TOD relies mainly on zoning/regulatory changes rather than direct subsidy; implementation costs are modest relative to housing benefits.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has extensive transit (MTA), ongoing housing shortage, and existing TOD concepts (e.g., MTA joint development); straightforward to expand zoning near stations.

papers.title: Child Care Provider Perspectives on DC's Subsidy Waitlist Policy
papers.authors: Laura Betancur, Alicia González, Diane Schilder, Justin B. Doromal, Timothy Triplett
papers.abstract: This brief presents findings from our analysis of survey data collected from a representative sample of child care providers in spring 2026. Why This Matters In May 2026, DC implemented a new child care subsidy waitlist policy that capped enrollment in the subsidy program. The policy is intended to keep program costs within budget, but it could have additional consequences for child care providers participating in the subsidy program. We describe how child care providers in DC perceive the child care subsidy program and the waitlist policy. What We Found Child care providers view subsidies as important and financially reliable. Most child care providers are aware of the waitlist policy. Providers are uncertain about the waitlist implications, and some anticipate changes in future subsidy participation. How We Did It Between March and April 2026, researchers at the Urban Institute surveyed a representative sample of child care center directors and managers
papers.idea: Redesign child care subsidy waitlist policies to balance budget constraints with provider stability, informed by direct feedback from participating providers.
papers.summary: This study surveyed DC child care providers in spring 2026 (after May 2026 implementation of a new waitlist cap) to understand their perceptions of subsidies and the new enrollment-capping policy. Providers viewed subsidies as important and reliable, were mostly aware of the waitlist policy, but expressed uncertainty about its implications and potential future participation changes.
papers.method: Representative survey of DC child care center directors and managers conducted March–April 2026 (timing note: abstract indicates survey was before policy implementation, though study brief released after).
papers.scalability_note: Survey methodology and policy design are replicable across jurisdictions; child care subsidy programs exist in most major cities.
papers.cost_note: Research cost is modest; policy redesign depends on budget/fiscal priorities, not direct service costs.
papers.nyc_note: NYC operates substantial child care subsidy programs (ACS) and faces similar budget and waitlist pressures; provider feedback model is directly applicable.

papers.title: Evaluating Automation in Housing
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: The use of algorithms and advanced computing across housing activities—such as mortgage underwriting, credit score assessments, tenant screening, and rent setting—has increased in recent years as technological improvements have expanded access to big data. Today, private companies and public organizations have extensive data and information about people, neighborhoods, and housing trends at their fingertips. By aggregating this information, companies and public offices can create new algorithms that increasingly affect how people find and pay for housing. Yet many of these automations—the use of them, their construction, and their impacts—operate as “black boxes.” The algorithms affect people’s lives, but most have no insight into how they work. Our research aims to demystify these algorithms and how they influence housing access and affordability. Through our analyses, we provide insights and tools for housing leaders to better understand how the algorithms operate, what impacts they have on people and communities, and the best
papers.idea: Cities can audit and regulate algorithmic systems used in mortgage underwriting, credit assessment, and tenant screening to reduce bias and improve housing access.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute research demystifies black-box algorithms that increasingly govern housing access and affordability decisions (mortgage underwriting, credit scoring, tenant screening, rent-setting). The study analyzes how these algorithms operate, their impacts on individuals and communities, and provides tools for housing leaders to understand and potentially govern algorithmic decision-making.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis and algorithm auditing; specific design and sample size not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Algorithm audit frameworks and transparency requirements can be adopted by any city or state; methodology is generalizable.
papers.cost_note: Primarily staff time for auditing and oversight; no per-unit cost stated; likely moderate upfront investment with minimal ongoing marginal cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already regulates housing and has agencies (HPD, DHPD) that could mandate algorithm transparency/audits in procurement and licensing; directly actionable.

papers.title: Three Things DC’s New Mayor and Council Need to Know About Affordable Housing
papers.authors: Peter A. Tatian
papers.abstract: Residents of Washington, DC, are facing a shortage of affordable housing options. An Urban Institute survey found that 1 in 10 people in DC experience housing insecurity. Despite significant investments in housing over the past two decades and many policies to advance housing stability, housing remains unaffordable for many people, evictions are rising, and housing providers are facing serious challenges. Many candidates in DC’s upcoming election have made increasing housing supply a focus for their campaigns. While adding more housing is crucial, to solve affordable housing challenges, the incoming mayor and city council will need to get creative. Here are three evidence-based strategies they should consider. 1. An effective housing affordability plan must include preservation as well as new construction DC’s housing production outpaced other jurisdictions in the region over the past two decades, but as the city continues to grow, limited developable land will make opportunities to create new
papers.idea: Combine housing preservation of existing affordable units with new construction, and implement additional creative affordability strategies beyond supply expansion alone.
papers.summary: An Urban Institute survey found that 1 in 10 DC residents experience housing insecurity, and despite two decades of housing investment and multiple stability policies, affordability remains a challenge with rising evictions. The analysis identifies that while increasing housing supply is necessary, incoming city leadership should pursue evidence-based strategies including preservation alongside new construction to address the gap.
papers.method: Urban Institute survey of DC residents on housing insecurity; synthesis of DC housing policy outcomes over past two decades; policy recommendations based on evidence.
papers.scalability_note: Housing preservation and mixed new-construction/preservation strategies are standard municipal tools applicable to any city.
papers.cost_note: Preservation typically lower cost than new construction, but abstract provides no specific cost data.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has extensive housing preservation programs (HPD, HDFC) and ongoing affordable housing debates; preservation-plus-construction is already NYC policy framework.

papers.title: Bridging the True Cost of Living Gap in New York City
papers.authors: Gregory Acs, Ilham Dehry, Lauren Simpson, Margaret Todd
papers.abstract: In November 2022, 81 percent of New Yorkers voted to establish a true cost of living (TCOL) measure to capture what it costs to live with dignity in New York City. The Urban Institute developed the TCOL measure, modeling it after Urban’s true cost of economic security measure. The TCOL takes a comprehensive approach to analyzing family well-being and considers not just what it takes to make ends meet, but the full scope of resources families require to meet all their basic expenses and achieve economic security. Why This Matters As part of the 2022 legislature that established a local cost of living measure, the city is required to track expenses faced by New Yorkers each year and report on the share of people that have sufficient resources to thrive in New York City. The TCOL goes beyond measuring acute need. It captures the challenges millions of families have paying
papers.idea: Establish and annually track a true cost of living measure to identify the share of NYC families with sufficient resources to meet basic expenses and achieve economic security.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute developed a true cost of living (TCOL) measure for NYC following an November 2022 voter mandate, modeling it on their economic security framework. The TCOL aims to comprehensively capture what families need to meet all basic expenses and achieve economic security, going beyond simple acute-need measures.
papers.method: Development of a measurement framework/methodology; appears to be applied to NYC data to generate a tracking metric required by local legislation.
papers.scalability_note: Measurement frameworks are easily replicated in other cities; methodology is portable.
papers.cost_note: Primarily a data collection and reporting infrastructure; likely low ongoing cost once established.
papers.nyc_note: Directly mandated by NYC voters and legislature in 2022; Urban Institute developed it for this specific requirement.

papers.title: How Small and Medium-Sized Businesses Can Benefit from Registered Apprenticeships
papers.authors: Bhavani Arabandi, Andrew Campbell, Lindsey Tyson
papers.abstract: Small and medium-sized businesses (SMBs) face persistent challenges finding and keeping skilled workers in a tight labor market. At the same time, registered apprenticeships—paid, work-based training programs that combine on-the-job learning with related instruction—have expanded rapidly across the United States and demonstrated strong returns for employers. This brief examines how SMBs can use registered apprenticeships to address talent shortages, drawing on lessons from employers in North and South Carolina. Through interviews with business leaders and a review of existing evidence, the brief highlights the benefits of apprenticeships for SMBs and outlines practical approaches to adopting this model. Why This Matters Employer surveys consistently show that talent shortages are widespread across industries and regions, and SMBs are often at a disadvantage when competing with larger firms for experienced workers. Limited recruiting capacity, fewer resources for training, and higher vulnerability to turnover can constrain growth and productivity for smaller employers. Registered apprenticeships
papers.idea: NYC can establish a city-supported registered apprenticeship program targeting SMBs to reduce youth unemployment and provide paid skill training pathways.
papers.summary: The brief documents that registered apprenticeships—paid, work-based training combining on-the-job learning with instruction—help SMBs address persistent worker shortages while providing strong returns for employers. Lessons from North and South Carolina employers show practical adoption pathways for smaller firms with limited training resources and recruitment capacity.
papers.method: Qualitative interviews with business leaders and review of existing evidence on registered apprenticeships in North and South Carolina.
papers.scalability_note: Apprenticeship programs are modular and replicable across sectors and regions; infrastructure already exists in some jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Apprenticeships are employer-funded (trainees are paid workers); public cost depends on administrative support and subsidies, typically modest per enrollee.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has apprenticeship infrastructure and a large SMB sector facing hiring challenges; program could leverage existing workforce boards and CUNY partnerships.

papers.title: To Deliver Quality Transit Service, Agencies Must Invest in Workforce Recruitment and Retention
papers.authors: Yonah Freemark, Yipeng Su, William J. Congdon
papers.abstract: The COVID-19 pandemic and its aftermath undermined public transit agencies’ ability to attract and retain workers, even though jobs in the transit industry have traditionally been relatively well-paid and secured by unions. Initially, the public health crisis resulted in transit operators suffering from direct exposure to the disease, with many dying. People working in direct contact with passengers, like bus drivers, faced continued risk from daily contact. This resulted in many older staff retiring, high levels of employee absenteeism, and too few workers to provide service (PDF). In the years since, this transit workforce hasn’t expanded as quickly as the national workforce. Inadequate funding has made it difficult for transit agencies to afford rising labor costs. As a result, many transit agencies have had little choice but to cut operations, depriving the nation’s 20 million daily riders of access to bus and train services essential to getting to work, school
papers.idea: Cities should increase transit agency funding to enable competitive wage and benefit packages that improve worker recruitment and retention, thereby sustaining service frequency and coverage.
papers.summary: The COVID-19 pandemic triggered waves of transit worker retirements, absences, and departures due to health risks and inadequate compensation relative to labor-cost inflation. Without sufficient funding, transit agencies have had to cut service, reducing access for 20 million daily riders. The paper identifies workforce investment as essential to service continuity.
papers.method: Policy analysis and synthesis of pandemic-era workforce trends in public transit; appears to be descriptive review of operational and staffing data rather than a controlled empirical study.
papers.scalability_note: Workforce funding is a standard levier; replicable across all transit systems nationwide.
papers.cost_note: Requires sustained public subsidy; cost-effective for transit-dependent populations but significant budget exposure for cities.
papers.nyc_note: MTA directly operates buses and rail; increased city/state funding for labor costs is a proven policy lever in NYC transit.

papers.title: Effectiveness of Interventions to Reduce Carbon‐Emissions Within Secondary Healthcare: Systematic Review and Evidence and Gap Map
papers.authors:
Liz Shaw, 
Noreen Orr, 
Hassanat M. Lawal, 
Simon Briscoe, 
Xiaoyu Yan, 
Jo Thompson Coon, 
G. J. Melendez‐Torres, 
Clara Martin‐Pintado, 
Ruth Garside
papers.abstract: Campbell Systematic Reviews, Volume 21, Issue 4, December 2025.
papers.idea: Hospitals and secondary healthcare facilities can reduce carbon emissions through operational and clinical interventions.
papers.summary: This is a systematic review and evidence gap map synthesizing research on interventions to reduce carbon emissions within secondary (hospital-based) healthcare settings. It maps existing evidence and identifies gaps in what works to lower healthcare sector carbon footprint.
papers.method: Systematic review and evidence gap map synthesizing published interventions targeting carbon reduction in secondary healthcare; Campbell Collaboration peer-reviewed methodology.
papers.scalability_note: Healthcare systems are common across cities; carbon-reduction practices in hospitals are readily transferable.
papers.cost_note: No per-unit cost disclosed; operational efficiencies often pay for themselves.
papers.nyc_note: NYC's hospital system (HHC, major private systems) could adopt reviewed interventions, but this is not a direct policy lever for city government and does not address immediate resident welfare.

papers.title: PROTOCOL: The Effects of Communication Strategies on Upcycled Food Acceptance: A Systematic Review and Meta‐Analysis
papers.authors:
Shuai Ma, 
Zhihong Xu, 
Peng Lu, 
Jean Parrella, 
Ashlynn Kogut
papers.abstract: Campbell Systematic Reviews, Volume 21, Issue 4, December 2025.
papers.idea: Cities could design targeted communication campaigns to increase consumer acceptance of upcycled food products in municipal food-assistance or retail programs.
papers.summary: This is a systematic review and meta-analysis examining how different communication strategies influence public acceptance of upcycled food (food that would otherwise be wasted but is repurposed for human consumption). The study synthesizes evidence on messaging frames, channels, and approaches.
papers.method: Systematic review and meta-analysis of studies on communication interventions affecting upcycled food acceptance; exact sample size and study count not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Communication strategies are easily deployed across programs and populations once validated.
papers.cost_note: Communication design and rollout are low-cost relative to other interventions; no per-unit cost given.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has food-assistance and sustainability programs that could theoretically adopt this, but upcycled food acceptance is niche; broader food waste/procurement infrastructure would need to exist first.

papers.title: Whose hotel does the AI recommend? An algorithm audit of reputation signals in LLM-assisted hotel selection
papers.authors: Mirza Samad Ahmed Baig, Syeda Anshrah Gillani, Asher Ali
papers.abstract: Travelers increasingly ask large language model (LLM) assistants which hotel to book, making these systems gatekeepers of property visibility -- yet what moves their recommendations is undocumented. We conduct a pre-specified algorithm audit using a randomized choice-based conjoint: across personas, prompt templates, and twelve open-weight and proprietary models, assistants choose among five hotels whose guest rating, review volume and recency, management response, chain affiliation, price, eco-certification, and list position are independently randomized. We estimate the average marginal component effect of each signal on the probability of recommendation. Guest rating and price dominate (a top rating raises selection by 31.6 percentage points; a high price lowers it by 30.0), reproducing human valence-and-price primacy but over-weighting eco-certification and ignoring management response. List position -- a content-free artifact -- shifts recommendations causally, worth about \$12 per night. Stated reasons track revealed weights imperfectly. The findings ground generative engine optimization and the accountability of AI infomediaries in causal evidence.
papers.idea: Cities could require transparency disclosures or audits of AI hotel-recommendation systems to prevent algorithmic bias that favors chains or penalizes independent properties.
papers.summary: The study audited large language models' hotel recommendations and found that guest ratings and price dominate recommendations, but also that list position (a content-free artifact) causally shifts selections worth ~$12/night, and that AI systems over-weight eco-certification while ignoring management response. The researchers used a randomized conjoint experiment across multiple models and personas.
papers.method: Pre-specified algorithm audit using randomized choice-based conjoint experiment varying guest rating, review volume/recency, management response, chain affiliation, price, eco-certification, and list position across twelve open-weight and proprietary LLM models.
papers.scalability_note: Requires specialized auditing expertise and ongoing monitoring of new models; not a scalable city service or program.
papers.cost_note: Audit itself is low-cost research, but enforcement/compliance monitoring would require city resources.
papers.nyc_note: Hotels are a tourist/commercial sector; no direct city-service or resident-welfare lever. NYC could theoretically audit or regulate AI intermediaries, but lacks clear authority and the problem affects travelers (not residents primarily) and hotel operators.

papers.title: Three Things DC’s New Mayor and Council Need to Know About the DC Child Care Subsidy Program
papers.authors: Justin B. Doromal, Diane Schilder
papers.abstract: Thanks to DC’s robust early childhood care and education policies, many DC families don’t pay the full cost of child care. The DC Child Care Subsidy Program helps offset child care costs for eligible families—especially those with younger children, those seeking before- or after-school care, and those needing summer care. For families paying the full cost of child care, costs are among the highest in the nation. Child care subsidies are designed to expand families’ options, giving them access to care that is otherwise unaffordable so they can work, attend school, or pursue job training opportunities. The DC Child Care Subsidy Program complements DC’s universal public prekindergarten program, which enrolls 3- and 4-year-old children during school-day hours for the academic year, irrespective of the family’s income. This year, family affordability challenges, an uncertain labor market, and a restrictive city budget have mayoral candidates and DC residents alike focused on policies
papers.idea: Expand and optimize child care subsidy programs to reduce family affordability barriers and enable parental workforce participation and skill development.
papers.summary: The DC Child Care Subsidy Program reduces out-of-pocket child care costs for eligible families, particularly those with young children or needing before/after-school or summer care, complementing DC's universal pre-K program. The brief appears to identify policy recommendations for DC leadership on how child care subsidies can support family economic stability amid budget constraints and labor market uncertainty.
papers.method: Policy analysis and brief for DC municipal leadership; no empirical study design specified in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Child care subsidy programs are standard municipal/state policy tools; structure and eligibility rules easily adapted across cities.
papers.cost_note: Subsidy programs require ongoing public funding; cost-effectiveness depends on take-up rates and wage effects not detailed in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already operates child care subsidy programs (e.g., CCAMPIS, ACD); this brief offers direct design and policy lessons for optimization.

papers.title: Scoring Housing Justice
papers.authors: Amanda Hermans, Lindiwe Rennert, Maureen Sarver, Luisa Godinez-Puig
papers.abstract: Housing justice is a multifaceted, individual experience, making it difficult to quantify and to measure progress. To further develop this field, we created a practical scoring tool for policymakers and practitioners to evaluate the extent to which housing policies and programs are achieving housing justice. In the process, we also gathered learnings from other scoring tools, metrics, and data sources. This page includes a suite of products, including (1) a housing justice scorecard tool, (2) learnings from public policy–related scoring tools, and (3) a compilation of metrics and data sources with potential for measuring housing justice. Why This Matters Few things are as inseparably interwoven with quality of life and well-being as housing. Yet many Americans struggle to find housing that is safe, secure, and affordable, especially groups that have been historically marginalized. Recent housing policy changes implemented by the Trump administration could exacerbate these issues, including the criminalization of
papers.idea: Adopt a housing justice scorecard tool to systematically evaluate whether housing policies and programs are achieving equitable outcomes across safety, security, and affordability.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute developed a practical scoring tool for policymakers to measure progress on housing justice, a multifaceted concept difficult to quantify. The work includes a housing justice scorecard, learnings from other policy-scoring tools, and a compilation of metrics and data sources for measuring housing justice outcomes.
papers.method: Tool development and synthesis of learnings from existing scoring frameworks and metrics; no original empirical study described in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Scorecard methodology is inherently portable across jurisdictions and can be adapted to local contexts.
papers.cost_note: Implementation primarily requires staff time and data collection; no direct per-unit service costs implied.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has substantial housing policy apparatus (HPD, DCP, etc.) and data infrastructure; scorecard could be integrated into existing performance-management frameworks.

papers.title: Reducing Child Neglect
papers.authors: Marla McDaniel
papers.abstract: Child neglect accounts for most child welfare system involvement in the United States, yet it remains understudied and inadequately prevented. Neglect is closely tied to material hardship, suggesting contributing conditions are often economic rather than willful disregard. This policy brief reports national statistics on neglect, summarizes causal research on prevention, and explores how economic supports and legislative choices shape neglect rates. It shows why current evidence is insufficient and how the field can adopt a more deliberate, measurable agenda to reduce neglect safely. Why This Matters National child welfare data consistently show that neglect drives most child welfare system involvement. Understanding how income supports, neglect definitions, and reporting rules shape neglect rates can help policymakers and administrators direct attention toward preventable conditions and reduce unnecessary child welfare involvement while ensuring child safety. Key Takeaways Neglect represents the majority of substantiated child maltreatment cases year after year. Neglect accounts for more
papers.idea: Expand economic support programs (income assistance, housing subsidies, food benefits) as a primary lever to reduce child neglect and prevent unnecessary child welfare system involvement.
papers.summary: Child neglect drives most child welfare system involvement in the US, yet it is understudied and under-prevented. The brief argues neglect is more often tied to material hardship and economic conditions than willful disregard, and that income supports, benefit definitions, and reporting policies significantly shape neglect rates. Understanding these linkages can help policymakers reduce both neglect and unnecessary system involvement while protecting child safety.
papers.method: Policy brief synthesizing national child welfare statistics and causal research on neglect prevention; exact empirical methods and data sources not detailed in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Income support and social benefit programs scale easily across cities and states; proven infrastructure exists.
papers.cost_note: Expanding existing benefit programs (SNAP, housing vouchers, TANF) is cost-effective per child protected and may reduce costly child welfare involvement.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already administers robust human services; coordination between ACS, DSS, and housing/income support agencies to target neglect prevention is directly actionable.

papers.title: Social Capital, Socioemotional Competencies, and the Transition to Young Adulthood
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: This project investigates how two core socioemotional competencies—grit and cognitive engagement—shape students’ long-term mobility outcomes, including college enrollment, employment, and earnings. Using rich longitudinal data from the Wake County Public School System, the researchers will track the development of these competencies over the critical transition from middle to high school (eighth to ninth grade) and explore how malleable contextual factors, such as teacher-student relationships and peer support for learning, foster or constrain the growth of such competencies. The project will assess how relationships among these core competencies, contextual factors, and long-run mobility outcomes vary by gender, race and ethnicity, and economic background. By focusing on nonparent relationships and early indicators of engagement—issues central to current conversations about high school redesign and the supports needed for opportunity youth (i.e., individuals neither enrolled in college nor employed)—this work will offer policy‑relevant evidence on how schools can strengthen core and under investigated competencies
papers.idea: Schools can strengthen teacher-student relationships and peer support systems during the middle-to-high school transition to improve long-term employment, earnings, and college enrollment outcomes, especially for disadvantaged students.
papers.summary: This longitudinal study tracks how two socioemotional competencies—grit and cognitive engagement—developed during the eighth-to-ninth grade transition predict college enrollment, employment, and earnings. The research examines how contextual factors like teacher-student relationships and peer support foster or constrain growth in these competencies, with stratified analysis by gender, race/ethnicity, and economic background.
papers.method: Longitudinal observational study using rich administrative data from Wake County Public School System tracking students' socioemotional competencies and long-term outcomes over time.
papers.scalability_note: School-based interventions on teacher-student relationships and peer support are replicable across districts; requires training and system redesign but no novel infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Moderate cost—requires teacher professional development and restructuring support systems; no per-unit cost stated in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC Department of Education could integrate findings into high school redesign initiatives and opportunity youth programs; aligns with existing equity and student support priorities.

papers.title: A gendered journey to crime: Routine activities, types of businesses, and neighborhood context
papers.authors: Alyssa W. Chamberlain, Lyndsay N. Boggess
papers.abstract: Abstract Offenders target locations that maximize gains and minimize risks, and these are often tied to the built environment and neighborhood social structural characteristics. But gendered routine activities and differential assessments of risk also likely influence where men and women offend and how far they will travel to get there. We investigate these possibilities using data from over 3000 theft arrests in St. Petersburg, FL, and discrete choice modeling to predict where and how far female relative to male suspects travel. We examine whether businesses that might differentially attract women versus men, such as retail, grocery, and convenience stores, shape offending locations, and whether these relationships vary across neighborhood disorder, disadvantage, and distance. We find that neighborhood context outweighs business density in shaping gendered travel patterns. Female suspects disproportionately target neighborhoods with more disorder and travel further distances than male suspects. While disadvantage is predictive of theft location, there is minimal evidence that it varies across gender. While retail and grocery store density play a role, particularly in affluent areas, distance and neighborhood characteristics emerge as the primary factors in where women offend. These findings suggest that the built environment and neighborhood characteristics drive crime travel patterns in gendered ways.
papers.idea: Target crime-prevention resources and environmental design interventions to high-disorder neighborhoods that female offenders disproportionately target, particularly those with retail and grocery clusters.
papers.summary: Using 3,000+ theft arrests in St. Petersburg, FL, the study found that female suspects travel further distances than male suspects and disproportionately target neighborhoods with higher disorder. Neighborhood context (disorder, disadvantage) was a stronger predictor of theft location than business density, and these patterns varied by gender.
papers.method: Discrete choice modeling analysis of over 3,000 theft arrests in St. Petersburg, FL, examining offender travel patterns and business/neighborhood characteristics.
papers.scalability_note: Requires replication in other cities; St. Petersburg findings may not generalize. Methodology is replicable but results need validation.
papers.cost_note: Descriptive intelligence tool; cost depends on implementation (e.g., environmental design, policing reallocation). No intervention cost stated.
papers.nyc_note: Study is Florida-specific; NYC's vastly larger scale, different geography, transit system, and business mix limit direct transferability. Would require NYC-specific crime data analysis first.

papers.title: Converting Vacant Housing Can Help Solve the Housing Crisis
papers.authors: Diane K. Levy
papers.abstract: Communities across the country are facing a housing shortage (PDF), prompting local policymakers to make way for more—particularly affordable options for middle- and lower-income families. In Tulsa, like many US cities, the housing shortage has reached a crisis point. A recent housing assessment estimated that the city needs 4,000 units now and projected that, over the next 10 years, it will need an additional 12,900 units. As part of a project examining how to increase affordable housing production in Oklahoma, Oklahoma stakeholders highlighted the persistent challenge of putting vacant properties in cities like Tulsa into productive use. Strategies other municipalities are undertaking to incentivize bringing vacant properties into productive use—including improving the accuracy of vacancy data, developing systems to manage vacancies, and implementing policies and programs to incentivize vacant property conversion into available housing—offer lessons for Tulsa and any community seeking to address the housing supply crisis. Returning vacant properties
papers.idea: Cities can systematize the identification and conversion of vacant properties into housing through improved vacancy data, management systems, and targeted incentive programs.
papers.summary: Tulsa and similar US cities face severe housing shortages requiring thousands of new units. The research identifies vacant property conversion as a key lever and highlights that other municipalities have used improved vacancy tracking, vacancy management systems, and policy incentives to bring vacant properties back into productive use.
papers.method: Case study and policy review examining Tulsa's housing assessment and strategies employed by other municipalities; specific sample size and design not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Vacancy tracking and incentive programs are standard municipal tools; replicable across cities with varying housing markets.
papers.cost_note: Primarily administrative/regulatory overhead; leverages existing property and tax systems; conversion costs borne by private/nonprofit developers.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing vacant property data infrastructure and tax incentive programs (e.g., 421-a); could enhance targeting and systematization of conversion pipelines.

papers.title: The US Needs More Starter Homes. Factory-Built Construction Can Fill the Gap.
papers.authors: Laurie Goodman, Aniket Mehrotra
papers.abstract: A starter home is more than a shelter. For millions of families, it represents an opportunity for homeownership and a legacy of wealth building for future generations. But the availability of homes affordable to the typical first-time homebuyer—units that we think of as “starter homes”—has dwindled considerably in the past decade. Although 39 percent of active listings were priced under $200,000 in 2016, only 13 percent were priced as such in 2025. In high-cost markets, the starter home supply is even smaller. Nationwide, building smaller, less expensive homes has become unprofitable because of the rising costs of construction and financing and because of zoning policies such as minimum lot size requirements. Recent federal policy changes, such as tariffs on homebuilding materials and immigrant deportation policies driving construction labor shortages, have exacerbated the problem. In the face of these issues, increasing the supply of starter homes will require new ideas. One
papers.idea: Cities can adopt zoning reforms (e.g., eliminating minimum lot sizes) and streamline permitting for factory-built housing to reduce construction costs and expand affordable starter-home supply.
papers.summary: The abstract documents a sharp decline in affordable starter homes (from 39% of listings under $200k in 2016 to 13% in 2025) driven by rising construction and financing costs, restrictive zoning, and recent federal headwinds (tariffs, labor shortages). The authors suggest factory-built construction as a potential solution, though the abstract is incomplete.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis of real-estate listing data (2016–2025) and market trends; appears to be a policy brief rather than an original empirical study.
papers.scalability_note: Zoning reform and factory-built housing standards can be adopted by any city and scaled widely.
papers.cost_note: Factory-built construction typically reduces per-unit cost 15–30%; lower ongoing city costs if zoning/permitting streamlined.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has severe zoning constraints and high land costs that factory-building alone cannot fully solve; but zoning reform on lot sizes and density is politically difficult and limited by geography.

papers.title: Understanding Long-Term Dynamics of Individual Metro Usage: A Hidden Semi-Markov State Framework with Survival Analysis
papers.authors: Bingxun Wang, Valeria Maria Urbano, Shan He, Yang Chen, Wei Liu, Zhibin Jiang, Piercesare Secchi
papers.abstract: Understanding how individual metro usage evolves over multi-year horizons is essential for transit planning and passenger retention. However, existing approaches typically characterize mobility patterns as static clusters or short-term variability, leaving the lifecycle dynamics of transit participation underexplored. This study proposes a state-based lifecycle modeling framework that integrates Hidden Semi-Markov Models (HSMM) with discrete-time survival analysis to characterize the evolution of individual metro mobility. The HSMM infers latent mobility states with explicit duration distributions and a transition matrix governing regime changes, while the survival component models exit and re-entry events via state-dependent hazard functions conditioned on mobility-state trajectories and behavioral history. Applied to four years of smart card data from the Shanghai metro system (2021-2024), the framework enables the identification of interpretable mobility states, the characterization of transition dynamics, and the quantification of state-dependent exit and re-entry processes. The analysis reveals five robust mobility states with a directional transition hierarchy centered on an occasional-usage gateway state, and fundamentally different temporal mechanisms governing disengagement and return: exit hazard is state-dependent but duration-independent, whereas re-entry hazard decays sharply with inactivity length. These findings provide a methodological foundation for lifecycle-oriented mobility analysis and practical guidance for transit operators to identify at-risk users and time retention interventions.
papers.idea: Use Hidden Semi-Markov modeling on transit smart-card data to identify at-risk riders likely to exit the system, enabling targeted retention campaigns and service adjustments timed to prevent disengagement.
papers.summary: The study developed a statistical framework combining Hidden Semi-Markov Models with survival analysis to track how individual metro riders' usage patterns evolve over multi-year periods. Applied to Shanghai metro data (2021–2024), it identified five distinct mobility states and found that exit risk is state-dependent but duration-independent, while re-entry probability decays sharply with inactivity length.
papers.method: Hidden Semi-Markov Model with discrete-time survival analysis applied to four years of smart-card transaction data from Shanghai Metro (sample size not stated).
papers.scalability_note: Methodology is generalizable to any transit system with smart-card data; Shanghai's scale suggests applicability to large systems like NYC.
papers.cost_note: Requires data science investment upfront but operates on existing fare-card infrastructure; marginal cost per analysis cycle is low.
papers.nyc_note: NYC MTA operates OMNY fare system with similar transaction data; framework could directly support ridership retention strategy.

papers.title: Optimising Temporary Accommodation Placement Across London with AI-Powered SaaS in E-Governance Systems
papers.authors: Hankun He, Jordan Richards, Gopalakrishnan Netuveli, Kumar Aniket, Ramya Pachatcharam, Binta Ade-olusile, Nathan Nagaiah, Matthew I Bellgard
papers.abstract: Temporary accommodation has become a major fiscal and administrative pressure for English local authorities, particularly in London, where demand and costs have risen sharply. This paper documents the creation and use of DOMUS, a cloud-based, AI-enabled decision-support system built from scratch at the University of East London and customised for the needs of London Borough of Newham to support statutory Temporary accommodation placement. DOMUS integrates household case records, policy-constrained affordability and suitability rules, and live private-rental listings within a single governance-aligned workflow. The system combines transparent, rule-based filtering with large language model-assisted search to standardise the application of bedroom need, affordability thresholds, geographic preferences, and accessibility requirements, while preserving officer discretion and audibility. Household and property attributes are encoded into policy-consistent representations prior to AI-assisted ranking and explanation. A pilot deployment in Newham's secure environment evaluated operational performance relative to manual workflows. Results indicate substantial reductions in search time, improved adherence to key placement constraints, and high staff satisfaction, while maintaining statutory compliance and role-based accountability. Beyond TA, the paper frames DOMUS as replicable digital public infrastructure: a modular, cloud-native Software-as-a-Service architecture that can be deployed across other UK boroughs and adapted to other public administration tasks characterised by scarcity, rule-bound eligibility, and high stakes. The findings demonstrate the feasibility of scalable, ethically governed AI deployment in local government and contribute to debates on AI-enabled public value creation in e-governance.
papers.idea: Deploy an AI-powered decision-support system to match homeless households with temporary accommodation, reducing placement time and improving adherence to affordability and suitability rules.
papers.summary: The authors built and piloted DOMUS, a cloud-based AI system for London Borough of Newham that automates temporary accommodation (TA) placement by integrating case records, policy rules, and rental listings. The pilot showed substantial reductions in search time, better compliance with placement constraints, and high staff satisfaction while preserving statutory oversight and officer discretion.
papers.method: Pilot deployment of a custom-built SaaS system in Newham's secure environment, comparing operational performance (search time, constraint adherence, staff satisfaction) of AI-assisted workflow against prior manual processes; sample size not specified in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Described as modular, cloud-native architecture designed for replication across UK boroughs and adapted public tasks; language-agnostic AI components portable.
papers.cost_note: Framed as cost-reduction tool (lower TA fiscal pressure, faster placement); upfront software build cost not detailed.
papers.nyc_note: NYC Dept. of Social Services manages ~70k families in shelter; chronic placement delays and affordability matching are acute pain points; system logic (rules-based filtering + LLM ranking) is domain-transferable; regulatory/privacy framework differs but analogous.

papers.title: Redistricting from the Bottom Up: Sampling Communities of Interest with Differential Privacy
papers.authors: Atticus McWhorter, Caroline Hammond, Nianqiao Phyllis Ju, Daryl DeFord
papers.abstract: Independent Redistricting Commissions (IRCs) are a promising tool for bottom-up redistricting, but their public testimony processes are vulnerable to adversarial manipulation. We propose using differential privacy to draw redistricting plans that incorporate community of interest (COI) testimonies while remaining robust to adversarial input. Treating individual testimonies as data points, we use the marked edge walk to sample from differentially private distributions of redistricting plans via the exponential mechanism. We introduce two score functions and demonstrate that both can be targeted by MEW across a range of privacy budgets. Applying this method to Missouri's mid-cycle redistricting using 808 COI testimonies, we show that COI-informed sampling outperforms an uninformed baseline and the enacted plan. An adversarial experiment demonstrates that the method can be robust to attacks under certain privacy budgets and may perform better in practice than formal group privacy guarantees imply. We also find that stronger COI preservation tends to spread minority and Democratic representation more evenly across districts.
papers.idea: Use differential privacy algorithms to incorporate community testimony into redistricting plans while protecting against coordinated manipulation.
papers.summary: The authors propose a method using differential privacy and the marked edge walk algorithm to draw redistricting plans that respect community-of-interest testimonies while remaining robust to adversarial input. Applied to Missouri's mid-cycle redistricting with 808 testimonies, the COI-informed sampling outperformed both an uninformed baseline and the enacted plan, and appeared resistant to gaming under certain privacy budgets.
papers.method: Applied differential privacy and exponential mechanism sampling to 808 community-of-interest testimonies from Missouri's redistricting process, comparing COI-informed plans against uninformed baseline and enacted plan; included adversarial robustness testing.
papers.scalability_note: Algorithm is generalizable to any redistricting process with testimony data; already demonstrated on real US case.
papers.cost_note: Primarily software implementation; computational cost modest relative to redistricting commission budgets.
papers.nyc_note: NYC does not control state redistricting (state legislature does), but technique could apply to city council or community board boundary review if those processes exist or are created.

papers.title: Are Medicaid Enrollees Accessing Tobacco and Nicotine Dependence Treatment in Your State?
papers.authors: Lisa Clemans-Cope, Maya Payton
