papers.abstract: Tobacco use is the most common cause of preventable disease, disability, and death in the United States. Every year, it’s responsible for and billions of dollars in health care spending and productivity losses resulting from smoking-related sickness and death. Medicaid enrollees are disproportionately affected because they’re nearly twice as likely to smoke tobacco. Cessation Medications Can Save Lives, but Many Medicaid Enrollees Don’t Access Them As of 2024, most state Medicaid programs covered at least one FDA-approved cessation medication and counseling service, but few covered all seven medications and counseling for all adults across both fee-for-service and managed care plans. Requirements like prior authorization, copayments, and treatment caps further limit enrollees’ access to these medications. Systems-level challenges, including a lack of integration of screening and treatment into routine care, low provider engagement, underinvestment in public health outreach, and low awareness of treatment coverage among patients and providers, compound these issues
papers.idea: NYC could audit and expand Medicaid coverage of all seven FDA-approved cessation medications and counseling across all plans, remove prior authorization and copayment barriers, and integrate tobacco screening and treatment into routine primary care and emergency department workflows.
papers.summary: Most state Medicaid programs cover at least one tobacco cessation medication and counseling, but few cover all seven medications or ensure coverage across both fee-for-service and managed care plans. Barriers like prior authorization, copayments, and treatment caps, combined with low provider engagement and patient awareness, prevent many Medicaid enrollees from accessing treatment despite nearly twice the smoking rate of the general population.
papers.method: Descriptive review of state Medicaid program policies and coverage rules as of 2024; comparative analysis of cessation medication and counseling coverage across states.
papers.scalability_note: Policy and coverage changes are replicable across cities and states; requires coordination with health plans but no novel infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Cessation medications are cost-effective (return on investment from prevented smoking-related disease); removal of barriers is low-cost administratively.
papers.nyc_note: NYC administers Medicaid through NYC Health + Hospitals and coordinates with state; can audit local plan coverage and remove barriers for city residents; could pilot integrated screening in primary care and safety-net settings.

papers.title: Treatment for Tobacco and Nicotine Use Disorder in Medicaid
papers.authors: Lisa Clemans-Cope, Maya Payton
papers.abstract: Tobacco and nicotine use is the leading cause of preventable death in the United States, and its burden falls hardest on the roughly 43 million adults covered by Medicaid, who use tobacco at more than twice the rate of adults with private insurance. Medicaid covers proven medications that help people quit, but few enrollees who need them actually receive them. This report tracks Medicaid-covered cessation prescriptions from 2019 to 2025, showing how treatment reach and the mix of medications across states have changed. Readers can explore state-level prescribing and treatment rates in the accompanying interactive dashboard. Why This Matters Cessation medications are among the most cost-effective treatments in health care, and helping enrollees quit decreases the substantial tobacco-related costs Medicaid already bears. Even so, most enrollees who want to quit never get evidence-based help. Federal funding for tobacco control has been reduced, and Medicaid now faces reductions under the One
papers.idea: NYC could expand Medicaid coverage and active outreach for evidence-based tobacco cessation medications to reduce preventable deaths and healthcare costs among low-income residents.
papers.summary: The report documents Medicaid-covered cessation prescriptions from 2019–2025 across states, showing that while effective medications are covered, most Medicaid enrollees who want to quit tobacco never receive them. The authors highlight that cessation treatments are highly cost-effective and reduce substantial tobacco-related costs that Medicaid already bears, yet reach remains limited due to reduced federal funding and policy constraints.
papers.method: Tracking study analyzing Medicaid prescription data across states from 2019 to 2025, with state-level dashboards on prescribing and treatment rates.
papers.scalability_note: Medicaid policy and prescription infrastructure exist in every state; NYC can pilot within existing systems.
papers.cost_note: Cessation medications are among most cost-effective health interventions; saves money over time via reduced tobacco-related disease.
papers.nyc_note: NYC administers Medicaid locally, runs public health campaigns, and can integrate cessation outreach into existing safety-net clinics and health programs.

papers.title: LIHTC at 40: How Much Affordable Housing Has Been Built in Your State?
papers.authors: Christina Plerhoples Stacy, Daniel Teles, Tomi Rajninger, Will Curran-Groome
papers.abstract: Since its creation 40 years ago, the low-income housing tax credit (LIHTC) has financed between 3.3 and 3.7 million affordable rental units, making it the largest source of federal assistance for affordable rental housing development in the United States. This level of production is more than double the roughly 1.4 million units created by the public housing program over nearly 80 years. But LIHTC production has not been evenly distributed across the US. Using data from the National Housing Preservation Database from 1986 to 2022, we calculated the number of newly financed LIHTC units per 10,000 people in each state and metropolitan area. We found wide variation in population-adjusted LIHTC production across states and metropolitan areas. The amount of LIHTC housing and where it’s built depends on not only population but also policy choices, financing structures, and local market conditions. Whether this variation corresponds to differences in housing need remains
papers.idea: Analyze NYC's LIHTC production relative to need and peer metros to identify policy/financing barriers, then adopt high-performing states' strategies to increase affordable unit production.
papers.summary: The Low-Income Housing Tax Credit has financed 3.3–3.7 million affordable rental units over 40 years—more than double public housing's output—but production varies dramatically across states and metros. The study quantified this geographic variation using 1986–2022 data, suggesting the disparity reflects policy choices, financing structures, and local market conditions rather than housing need alone.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis of National Housing Preservation Database (1986–2022), calculating LIHTC units per 10,000 population by state and metro area.
papers.scalability_note: LIHTC is a federal program; states and cities can adjust local policy/financing to increase uptake. Peer comparison is easily replicated.
papers.cost_note: LIHTC uses tax credits (foregone federal revenue), not direct spending; cost per unit depends on deal structure but is already embedded in federal program.
papers.nyc_note: NYC controls local zoning, financing incentives, and developer partnerships. Study can identify whether NYC underperforms peers and which policy levers (streamlined approvals, co-financing, density bonuses) work elsewhere.

papers.title: Youth Corps Programs Build Pathways to Infrastructure Careers
papers.authors: Shayne Spaulding, Nathan Sick, Jincy Wilson, Lindsey Tyson
papers.abstract: Infrastructure workers help people connect to utilities and the power grid, move about society safely, build and update structures, mitigate climate change, and more. Yet infrastructure employers, including municipal governments and private organizations, face current and anticipated labor shortages. Youth service corps programs could help fill this gap. They offer young people, especially opportunity youth—who are typically ages 16 to 24 and are not working or enrolled in education, or are at risk of disconnection from school and work—the chance to give back to their communities while building skills for work and earning a wage or stipend. When corps programs connect young adults with good infrastructure jobs, they build a pipeline of workers for employers and a pathway to economic mobility for workers. To better understand these programs, we interviewed staff at eight youth service corps programs around the country that help participants—referred to as members— develop infrastructure-related skills. In
papers.idea: Cities can establish or expand youth service corps programs that train opportunity youth (ages 16–24) in infrastructure skills while paying wages/stipends, creating both workforce pipelines for municipal employers and economic mobility pathways for disconnected young adults.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute interviewed staff at eight youth service corps programs nationwide that connect opportunity youth with infrastructure-career training. The research identifies youth corps as a mechanism to address current and anticipated labor shortages in infrastructure while offering at-risk young people work experience, skill-building, and wages in sectors critical to utilities, transit, construction, and climate resilience.
papers.method: Qualitative study involving interviews with staff at eight youth service corps programs across the country focused on infrastructure-skill development.
papers.scalability_note: Corps models are replicable across cities; NYC already runs AmeriCorps and similar programs, so infrastructure-track expansion is feasible.
papers.cost_note: Requires wage/stipend funding and program administration; typical corps programs cost $15k–$25k per participant annually, but deliver employment and tax benefits.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing youth corps infrastructure (e.g., NYC Service Corps, DYCD programs), strong municipal employment demand in water, power, transit, and construction; directly expandable.

papers.title: How FHLBanks Are Investing to Meet the Local Housing Needs and What More Is Needed
papers.authors: Jung Hyun Choi, Laurie Goodman, Daniel Pang, Jun Zhu
papers.abstract: The Federal Home Loan Bank (FHLBank) System supports housing and community development across the US. It provides stable, low-cost advances to member institutions, including banks, credit unions, insurance companies, and consumer financial development institutions, to support mortgage lending and investment in members’ local communities. This system includes 11 regional FHLBanks that operate programs that provide direct support for affordable housing and community development. By law, FHLBanks must contribute at least 10 percent of their prior-year earnings to affordable housing through the Affordable Housing Program (AHP). In recent years, FHLBank contributions to the AHP and voluntary initiatives have averaged more than 15 percent of net earnings, reflecting a notable increase in funding to address unmet local needs. Between 2015 and 2024, FHLBanks directed roughly $147 billion toward housing and community development activities. Here, we highlight three trends in FHLBanks’ investments in two of their five mission activities between 2015 and 2024
papers.idea: Cities could advocate for or work with their regional FHLBank to increase capital deployment toward affordable housing and community development, particularly through the Affordable Housing Program (AHP) and voluntary initiatives.
papers.summary: The Federal Home Loan Bank System distributed roughly $147 billion toward housing and community development between 2015 and 2024 across 11 regional banks. FHLBanks now contribute more than 15% of net earnings to affordable housing (exceeding the 10% legal minimum), signaling growing investment in local housing needs. The paper documents trends in FHLBank mission activities and identifies gaps in funding relative to unmet local housing demand.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis of FHLBank funding flows and program contributions from 2015–2024, highlighting three trends in two of five mission activities; appears to use administrative data from FHLBank reports.
papers.scalability_note: FHLBank system is nationwide; cities can leverage existing regional infrastructure without building new programs.
papers.cost_note: FHLBanks use retained earnings (not public tax dollars); capital is recycled through member institutions; very low marginal cost to cities.
papers.nyc_note: NYC is served by the FHLB of New York; the city and its housing authorities can directly engage with this regional bank to increase AHP allocations and community development lending.

papers.title: How Has Federal Spending on Place-Based Programs Changed Over Time?
papers.authors: Brett Theodos, Ilina Mitra, Tomi Rajninger, Brady Meixell, Rachel Marconi, Lydia Nguyen, Dana Ferrante, Lauren Lastowka
papers.abstract: For decades, federal place-based programs have helped address differences in resources and economic opportunity in communities across the US. These programs invest in communities in a variety of ways, including the following: Direct program spending, such as the Community Development Block Grant Program, allocates federal funds through formulas or competitive grants to cover the costs of place-based programs, with the funds administered by local governments. Tax expenditures, such as the Low-Income Housing Tax Credit and the New Markets Tax Credit, drive down the overall cost of certain projects by decreasing the amount taxpayers and private companies owe to the federal government. In an analysis of some of the largest, recurring federal place-based programs, we find that although overall spending has increased in the past 15 years, direct program spending has not. As a result, the role of the private sector in determining where and how federal dollars are spent has
papers.idea: NYC could audit and potentially reallocate its use of federal place-based funding mechanisms (direct grants vs. tax expenditures) to ensure private-sector leverage aligns with equitable community development priorities.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute analyzed trends in major federal place-based programs over 15 years and found that while total spending increased, direct program spending declined—meaning tax expenditures and private-sector involvement grew as a share. This shift changes how federal dollars flow to communities and who controls spending priorities.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis of federal spending trends across major recurring place-based programs (Community Development Block Grants, Low-Income Housing Tax Credit, New Markets Tax Credit, etc.) over 15 years.
papers.scalability_note: City governments already administer these federal programs; understanding funding composition is easily adopted elsewhere.
papers.cost_note: Analysis and audit costs are minimal; cost of any resulting program shifts depends on whether reallocations or new applications are pursued.
papers.nyc_note: NYC heavily relies on CDBG, LIHTC, and New Markets Tax Credit. Understanding these trends could inform how city aligns its deployment of federal dollars with equity goals.

papers.title: Four Ways Coordinated Data Can Expand Homeownership
papers.authors: Cole Campbell, Gabi Velasco
papers.abstract: Median housing prices have far outpaced median incomes over the past few decades, and lower-income families are less likely to buy homes compared with their counterparts in previous generations. In Milwaukee, homeownership rates have remained low and have been steadily declining for years, especially for Black and Latino households. In response, the Milwaukee nonprofit Community Development Alliance (CDA) created a cross-sector affordable housing strategic plan aimed at expanding affordable homeownership for Black and Latino families and narrowing the city’s racial wealth gap. Since 2022, Data You Can Use (DYCU) has served as CDA’s data partner. DYCU helped break down silos among the city’s three leading homebuyer counseling agencies, consolidated their data, developed two public-facing tools on homeownership and new construction, and helped CDA and local advocates interpret and communicate DYCU’s findings. Before DYCU’s involvement, the data that would form the backbone of the mapping tools were not consolidated across agencies
papers.idea: Break data silos among homebuyer counseling agencies and consolidate their data to create unified mapping tools and strategic planning infrastructure for affordable homeownership expansion.
papers.summary: Milwaukee's Community Development Alliance created a cross-sector affordable housing plan to expand homeownership for Black and Latino families and narrow the racial wealth gap. Data You Can Use consolidated previously siloed data from three homebuyer counseling agencies, built two public-facing tools on homeownership and new construction, and helped local stakeholders interpret findings to guide advocacy and policy.
papers.method: Descriptive case study of a data partnership intervention in Milwaukee between 2022 and present; specific sample sizes and rigorous evaluation metrics not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Data consolidation and mapping-tool approaches are replicable across cities with existing homebuyer counseling infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: No cost stated; data consolidation and tool-building are relatively low-cost relative to housing interventions.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has multiple homebuyer counseling agencies and nonprofits; data consolidation and cross-sector coordination are directly feasible; strong existing housing advocacy ecosystem.

papers.title: COVID-19 Relief Funds
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: As a result of the child care crisis caused by the COVID-19 pandemic, DECAL sought guidance on how best to allocate its federal relief funding to support early care and education for children and families. Urban provided recommendations based on findings from surveys and focus groups with more than 7,000 families, early childhood educators and operators, and DECAL staff across rural and urban Georgia communities.
papers.idea: Use federal relief funds to expand early care and education capacity by prioritizing investments identified through stakeholder surveys—e.g., subsidies, educator wages, facility upgrades—to address pandemic-driven childcare shortages.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute surveyed over 7,000 families, educators, and operators across Georgia to identify how DECAL should allocate COVID relief funding for early care and education. The study surfaced concrete needs and preferences from both supply and demand sides of the childcare market during the pandemic.
papers.method: Mixed-method needs assessment using surveys and focus groups with 7,000+ participants (families, early childhood educators, operators, and state staff) across rural and urban Georgia.
papers.scalability_note: Methodologically replicable in other states/cities; fund-allocation decisions are routine governance lever.
papers.cost_note: Study-driven reallocation of existing relief funds; no incremental cost to conduct analysis.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has comparable early care/education infrastructure gaps post-COVID; similar stakeholder-driven needs assessment could inform ACS/DOE relief spending.

papers.title: Charitable Food Participation Remained High in 2025 as SNAP Cuts Unfold
papers.authors: Poonam Gupta, Elaine Waxman, Michael Karpman, Noah Kennedy, Dulce Gonzalez
papers.abstract: In this brief, we use data from the Urban Institute’s Well-Being and Basic Needs Survey (WBNS) to analyze trends in charitable food participation between 2019 and 2025, as well as the experiences of those who received charitable food, current levels of unmet need, and access barriers. We find that nearly 1 in 6 adults reported charitable food participation in 2025, with participation rates among adults ages 18 to 64 remaining consistently elevated since 2020. Why This Matters Cuts to the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) that began taking effect at the end of 2025 may increase the need for private food assistance at a time when the charitable safety net is already facing high demand. With millions of families expected to lose or see a reduction in benefits in the coming year, more families may turn to food banks, food pantries, and
papers.idea: Cities should map and expand charitable food assistance capacity (food banks, pantries) and coordinate with municipal food/benefits administration to anticipate and meet surges in demand from SNAP benefit cuts.
papers.summary: Nearly 1 in 6 NYC adults used charitable food assistance in 2025, with elevated participation since 2020. The study documents current unmet need and access barriers as SNAP cuts take effect, raising the likelihood that demand for food banks and pantries will spike further in 2026.
papers.method: Trend analysis using Urban Institute's Well-Being and Basic Needs Survey (WBNS) comparing 2019–2025 data on charitable food participation rates, barriers, and unmet need.
papers.scalability_note: Food pantry coordination and capacity planning are standard city infrastructure; easily replicated across jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Depends on expansion scope; coordinating existing networks is low-cost; new facilities/staffing adds moderate expense per person served.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has extensive food-assistance infrastructure (DCP, food banks, SNAP administration); municipal action to map gaps, fund expansion, and coordinate with state/federal SNAP implementation is directly actionable.

papers.title: Understanding Heirs Property as a Challenge to Generational Wealth
papers.authors: John Walsh, Michael Neal, Matthew Pruitt
papers.abstract: Homeownership is a key asset that helps families build financial resilience and wealth. And many homeowners hope to pass their home to the next generation. But the prospect of heirs' property limits access to the financial benefits associated with property passed down by earlier generations. Heirs’ properties are the result of an unclear or clouded will or estate plan, where multiple descendants own undivided interests in the property as “tenants in common.” Without clear ownership, such properties are at higher risk of foreclosures attributable to nonpayment of taxes or outstanding liens, and they are more likely to face deterioration from difficulty with upkeep. They also leave owners vulnerable to predation by third-party actors that buy out partitions of the property and force sales that can strip families of their equity in the home. These challenges touch all families. Our research estimates that heirs’ properties account for about $243 billion in
papers.idea: Cities could establish heirs' property resolution clinics or streamline probate/title-clearing processes to help families formalize ownership and protect against foreclosure, tax liens, and predatory partition sales.
papers.summary: Heirs' property—where multiple descendants own undivided interests due to unclear wills or estate plans—affects ~$243 billion in family assets and leaves owners vulnerable to foreclosure, tax liens, deterioration, and predatory buyouts that strip equity. The research identifies heirs' property as a major barrier to generational wealth-building and intergenerational asset transmission across families.
papers.method: Not stated in abstract; appears to be descriptive/analytical research estimating the scope and characteristics of heirs' property nationwide.
papers.scalability_note: Title/probate reforms, legal clinics, and title-insurance products are replicable across cities and states; no complex coordination required.
papers.cost_note: Legal aid and title-clearing clinics are relatively low-cost per family; potential high ROI if it prevents foreclosures and tax sales.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has significant heirs' property issues (especially in outer boroughs and among communities of color); existing legal-aid infrastructure and city housing agencies could pilot programs; aligns with affordable housing/wealth-building goals.

papers.title: Upwardly Mobile: Elevating and Celebrating AI Readiness Using Authentic Student Work
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: This study builds on emerging high school capstone models with direct implications for high school redesign by creating an innovative, practical measure of AI‑era digital literacy skills. At a moment when AI has not yet been formally established as a mobility driver but is already reshaping higher education, the labor market, and K–12 teaching and learning, having a measure of students’ readiness to interact with it will be key in future years, regardless of their pathway. This project will design and validate a suite of authentic performance tasks that assess high school seniors’ digital reasoning, responsible decisionmaking, and collaboration in real work contexts, including a focused cohort of Latino students supported with linguistically responsive, near‑peer validation. These short, district‑deployable tasks (AI fact‑check and traceability; data‑to‑decision; collaboration‑in‑the‑open; prompt‑and‑revise) will generate scored artifacts, rubrics, calibration tools, practitioner dashboards, and open manuals for psychometric validation and fairness auditing, enabling schools to link verified
papers.idea: NYC schools could adopt and validate performance-task assessments of AI-era digital literacy (fact-checking, data reasoning, collaboration) as a scalable high school capstone measure, with built-in fairness auditing and dashboards for practitioners.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute designed and is validating a suite of short, authentic performance tasks to measure high school seniors' digital reasoning, responsible decision-making, and collaboration skills in AI contexts. The tasks are district-deployable, include rubrics and dashboards, and target equity by supporting Latino students with linguistically responsive near-peer validation.
papers.method: Descriptive design study creating and piloting performance-task assessments with authentic work contexts; focused on a cohort of Latino high school seniors with validation tools and fairness auditing frameworks.
papers.scalability_note: Tasks designed to be short and district-deployable; rubrics and manuals are open-source, though calibration requires practitioner training.
papers.cost_note: Likely modest per-student cost (tasks are brief, asynchronous); no per-unit cost stated in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC DOE already uses performance-based capstone assessments; these AI-literacy tasks align with CUNY pathway goals and could integrate into existing high school redesign initiatives. Large Latino student population could benefit from linguistically responsive validation.

papers.title: How Affordable Housing Contributes to Local Economies and Tax Revenues
papers.authors: Jorge González-Hermoso, Yonah Freemark, Katie Fallon
papers.abstract: Oklahoma’s affordable housing investments generate substantial economic returns in jobs, labor income, and tax revenue. This publication presents original estimates of the economic and fiscal impacts of 45 affordable multifamily housing projects completed in Oklahoma between 2019 and 2023, all financed through the Low-Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC) program. Readers will find concrete data on job creation, economic output, and tax revenues, both during construction and over 10 years of property operations. Why This Matters Oklahoma faces persistent housing affordability challenges, with rents and home prices rising faster than wages and too few units available to households with low incomes. State and local policymakers deciding how to allocate scarce resources need to understand both the social benefits and economic returns of affordable housing investment. This research is particularly relevant for state legislators, housing finance agencies, local budget planners, and economic development officials who shape housing and investment policy in Oklahoma
papers.idea: Use economic impact analysis of LIHTC and similar affordable housing programs to justify and guide expansion of affordable housing investments in NYC.
papers.summary: The study quantified economic returns from 45 LIHTC-financed affordable multifamily projects in Oklahoma (2019–2023), finding substantial job creation, labor income, and tax revenue gains during both construction and 10-year operations. The research provides concrete data to support affordable housing as an economic development tool, not just a social program.
papers.method: Retrospective economic impact analysis of 45 completed LIHTC projects in Oklahoma, measuring job creation, economic output, and tax revenues during construction and over 10-year operational periods.
papers.scalability_note: LIHTC is a federal program; methodology can replicate across states and cities with local data.
papers.cost_note: LIHTC is an existing federal tax-credit subsidy; study does not report per-unit costs but frames housing investment as revenue-positive long-term.
papers.nyc_note: NYC uses LIHTC extensively via HPD/HDC; study's economic impact framework directly applicable to justify/expand local affordable housing policy.

papers.title: The Emergence of an Urban Mortality Advantage in Brazil: An Assessment of Age and Causes-of-Death Contributions to the Urban–Rural Mortality Gap
papers.authors: José H. C. Monteiro da Silva, Eugenio Paglino, Gabriel M. Borges, Marília R. Nepomuceno
papers.abstract: Abstract Brazil has historically seen higher mortality among urban residents compared to rural counterparts, despite better self-reported health among urban residents and better access to healthcare services. However, recent trends in these urban–rural mortality differentials and the specific causes of death contributing to them have not been well documented. We address this gap by analyzing trends in adult life expectancy in Brazil’s five macro-regions from 2006 to 2023. We decompose changes in the life expectancy gap by age, sex, and cause of death. Our findings reveal an emerging urban advantage in male life expectancy in Brazil, beginning in the mid-2000s and continuing until the start of the COVID-19 pandemic. For females, a small initial urban survival penalty had shifted to an urban advantage by 2019. The North and Northeast regions showed larger urban penalties for both sexes than the southern regions. For males, the emerging urban life expectancy advantage was primarily driven by a more rapid decrease in mortality from external causes among urban residents aged 20–39. For females, the urban advantage was fueled by relative gains in mortality from neoplasms, respiratory diseases, and external causes. The COVID-19 pandemic initially widened the urban mortality penalty or narrowed the urban advantage in 2020 due to higher COVID-19 mortality in urban areas. However, this trend reversed from 2021 to 2023, as the urban penalty in COVID-19 mortality turned into an urban advantage, reversing the relative gains seen in rural areas.
papers.idea: N/A — study is descriptive analysis without intervention recommendation
papers.summary: Brazil experienced an emerging urban mortality advantage beginning in the mid-2000s, driven primarily by faster declines in external-cause deaths (violence/accidents) among urban males aged 20–39 and improvements in cancer/respiratory/external-cause mortality for females. Regional variation was substantial, with North and Northeast showing larger urban penalties than southern regions; COVID-19 initially reversed gains but urban advantage returned by 2021–2023.
papers.method: Decomposition analysis of adult life expectancy trends across Brazil's five macro-regions from 2006–2023, stratified by age, sex, and cause of death using vital statistics data.
papers.scalability_note: Finding is region-specific to Brazil; no mechanism or policy lever identified for replication elsewhere.
papers.cost_note: Study itself was low-cost descriptive analysis; no intervention costed.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has lower urban mortality than rural comparison regions; study offers no actionable policy or program NYC could adopt to replicate Brazil's external-cause mortality reductions.

papers.title: Demographic Patterns in Cybersecurity Culture: Insights from a Global Organisation Supporting Safety-Critical and Critical Infrastructure Sectors
papers.authors: Tita Alissa Bach, Amandine Kaiser
papers.abstract: This study investigates demographic differences in cybersecurity culture in a large global organisation supporting safety critical and critical infrastructure sectors to target CSC improvement. A global survey was administered to all internal and external employees of a total of 21148 employees, with 6502 responses. The questionnaire evaluates nine CSC dimensions such as Password Management, Governance, Email Use. Anonymous survey responses were analysed using Kruskal-Wallis tests and Dunns post hoc comparisons to identify differences across demographic variables including employment, recruitment paths, managerial role, gender, age, tenure, and work base. CSC was broadly consistent across the organisation, with statistically significant but small to moderate demographic effects. CSC variations were observed across employment, age, recruitment paths, and line managerial role. In general, fulltime, internal, permanent, older employees, Merge and Acquisition recruits, and line managers consistently scored higher across multiple CSC dimensions. Parttime, younger, external employees, and those with 6 to 20 years of tenure in general scored lower. These patterns highlight higher-scoring groups that may act as CSC carriers and lower-scoring groups that may benefit from tailored improvement measures, enabling organisational learning. Our study offers a practical, scalable way to assess CSC, generating meaningful insights despite industrial constraints. It enables organisations to benchmark maturity, identify gaps, and prioritise targeted improvements using workforce diversity as a guide.
papers.idea: Target cybersecurity culture training within city agencies to demographic groups (younger, part-time, external staff) identified as lower-scoring on security practices.
papers.summary: The study surveyed 6,502 employees across a global critical-infrastructure organization and found statistically significant but small-to-moderate demographic variations in cybersecurity culture. Full-time, older, internal, permanent employees and line managers scored higher on nine dimensions (password management, governance, email use); part-time, younger, and external staff scored lower.
papers.method: Cross-sectional anonymous survey of 21,148 employees (6,502 responses) with Kruskal-Wallis and Dunns post hoc tests comparing nine cybersecurity-culture dimensions across seven demographic variables in one large global organization.
papers.scalability_note: Survey methodology is portable; training interventions are standard. But effects are small and context-specific to one organization.
papers.cost_note: Mainly staff time for targeted training; no per-unit cost stated.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has critical infrastructure and city agencies; could adopt survey + targeted training. But abstract offers no evidence that demographic targeting improves outcomes or reduces actual breaches—only that groups differ in survey responses.

papers.title: The Paradox of Housing Supply Growth
papers.authors: Yonah Freemark
papers.abstract: Housing affordability is declining in the United States, and one explanation is that housing supply has failed to match demand for new homes. There is some debate about whether this shortage is universal or applicable to just a subset of the nation’s population. Some research suggests that certain communities, including many neighborhoods, cities, and states with relatively high populations of liberal residents and high housing values—often areas on the nation’s coasts—have more restrictive housing policies while others, including areas in the Sunbelt, are attracting more housing construction. A growing movement of activists are encouraging the more restrictive states and localities to alter their land-use regulations to encourage more construction. Yet more evidence is necessary to understand the relationships between geography, resident demographics, politics, and housing-supply growth in recent decades. In this research, I explore how housing supply changed in cities, counties, and metropolitan areas between 2000 and 2020. Key Takeaways
papers.idea: Relax land-use regulations in high-cost coastal cities to increase housing supply and improve affordability.
papers.summary: This descriptive study examines how housing supply changed across U.S. cities, counties, and metros from 2000–2020, finding variation by geography and politics. It documents that restrictive coastal/liberal areas built less housing while Sunbelt areas built more, and notes activism pushing for deregulation in high-cost regions. The abstract does not report specific findings, effect sizes, or policy outcomes.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis of housing supply trends across cities, counties, and metropolitan areas from 2000–2020; no specific sample size, control group, or causal identification method stated.
papers.scalability_note: Land-use deregulation is a policy lever available to any city or state.
papers.cost_note: Removing regulatory barriers has low direct fiscal cost, though indirect effects on property values, infrastructure, and services are complex.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could adopt zoning/land-use reforms, though the study does not isolate which reforms work or NYC-specific barriers.

papers.title: Geographic Networks of Gun Violence: Exploring Hot Spot Connectivity Through Ballistic Evidence and Social Network Analysis
papers.authors: Eric L. Piza, Cassie McMillan, Daniel Trovato
papers.abstract: Objectives While crime-and-place research consistently demonstrates that a minority of places account for most of the crime in a given jurisdiction, much less is known about the extent to which these hot spots are interconnected. Informed by the social network analysis literature, we explore whether gun violence networks occur in spatial forms, with hot spots connected by distinct firearms used in the commission of crime. Methods We construct a network where ballistic evidence from crime guns ties together street segments in Kansas City, Missouri. Data from the National Integrated Ballistic Information Network is used to construct connections between street segments that contain ballistic evidence associated with the same crime gun. Results Findings indicate that street segments classified as gun violence hot spots were more likely to be connected to one another than would be expected by chance (i.e., hot spot homophily). This suggests hot spots do not operate as self-contained entities but are components of a larger geographic network of gun violence. Conclusion The current study suggests that crime gun linkages can enhance our understanding of how gun violence hot spots not only share certain structural features but also are connected in ways suggestive of spatial and social interdependence.
papers.source: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.venue: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.idea: Use ballistic network analysis to identify interconnected gun violence hot spots and coordinate cross-neighborhood enforcement strategies.
papers.summary: The study found that gun violence hot spots in Kansas City are not isolated but form geographic networks connected by crime guns that appear at multiple locations. Hot spots showed homophily—they were more likely to be linked to other hot spots than random chance would predict—suggesting spatial and social interdependence.
papers.method: Network analysis using ballistic evidence from the National Integrated Ballistic Information Network (NIBIN) to connect street segments in Kansas City where the same crime guns were recovered.
papers.scalability_note: Requires NIBIN participation and ballistic matching infrastructure; feasible but not trivial to replicate across jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Leverages existing NIBIN database; analysis cost moderate; no new field interventions proposed.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD has ballistic database and hot-spot mapping capacity; method could transfer but requires ballistic linking workflow and criminal justice coordination.

papers.title: Alleviating Affordability Pressures on AA and NHPI Communities Will Require Better Data to Inform Effective Policies
papers.authors: Aravind Boddupalli, Rekha Balu, Seema Agnani, Wayne Ho
papers.abstract: In April, New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani released the Preliminary Citywide Racial Equity Plan and an inaugural True Cost of Living Measure, recognizing that more-nuanced, local data are critical to effectively address the affordability crisis. A related Urban Institute report found 63 percent of Asian American (AA) and Native Hawaiian and Pacific Islander (NHPI) New Yorkers don’t earn enough to meet their true cost of living, a share significantly higher than the 44 percent of their White counterparts. These are stark findings, but they lump together the outcomes of some of the city’s highly diverse AA and NHPI communities. Cost of living (PDF) is top of mind for AA and NHPI adults, as it is for most Americans, and has been for decades. Unfortunately, their economic experiences remain poorly understood in policy debates dominated by national averages. This lack of visibility has real consequences. Further disaggregating the data by
papers.idea: Disaggregate cost-of-living and affordability data by Asian American and NHPI subgroups to inform targeted policy design.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute found that 63% of Asian American and Native Hawaiian/Pacific Islander New Yorkers lack sufficient income to meet their true cost of living—higher than the 44% figure for White counterparts. However, these aggregate figures mask significant diversity within AA and NHPI communities, and their economic experiences remain underrepresented in policy debates dominated by national-level data.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis using NYC affordability data and the city's newly introduced True Cost of Living Measure, comparing disparities across racial groups.
papers.scalability_note: Data disaggregation and measurement frameworks are easily replicable across cities and demographic groups.
papers.cost_note: Primarily involves statistical re-analysis of existing data; minimal incremental cost.
papers.nyc_note: Directly relevant to NYC's existing Racial Equity Plan and True Cost of Living Measure; requires city collaboration but no new programs.

papers.title: Using Multimodal Classroom Data to Measure Student Competencies for Upward Mobility
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: This project uses AI-supported analysis of recorded classroom interactions to uncover the student skills embedded in real‑time instructional exchanges and assess how well these competencies—spanning both core mathematical reasoning tied to upward mobility and more durable skills such as agency, vocabulary use, and self‑management where disparities often emerge—serve as early precursors of long‑run mobility. Drawing on classroom audio recordings, surveys, and linked administrative data from the Midwest and South, the team applies natural language processing and machine learning methods to develop scalable measures that capture how students demonstrate these competencies during middle school math lessons and then links them to key high school outcomes such as attendance, course taking, and GPA. Led by a leading scholar of classroom interaction and mathematics education and supported by established partnerships with districts and education technology firms, the project is positioned to produce scalable tools that help teachers identify and strengthen mobility‑relevant skills in
papers.idea: Deploy AI-powered classroom observation tools to identify and measure student competencies (agency, vocabulary, self-management) linked to long-term economic mobility, enabling teachers to target skill-building interventions in middle school.
papers.summary: The project develops machine-learning methods to analyze classroom audio recordings and identify student competencies—mathematical reasoning, agency, vocabulary use, and self-management—during middle school math lessons. These measures are then linked to downstream high school outcomes (attendance, course-taking, GPA) to validate their predictive power for long-run upward mobility.
papers.method: Natural language processing and machine learning applied to classroom audio recordings, surveys, and administrative data from districts in the Midwest and South; linked to high school outcomes to assess predictive validity.
papers.scalability_note: Requires classroom recording infrastructure, NLP/ML expertise, and district partnerships; replicable but not trivial to deploy at scale.
papers.cost_note: Likely moderate; depends on recording tech and ML platform licensing; per-school cost unclear from abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has large middle school population, established ed-tech partnerships, and strong interest in equity-linked interventions; would require buy-in on classroom recording and staff capacity.

papers.title: The Global Dirty Laundry: A Heckman-Adjusted Gravity Model of Illicit Financial Flows
papers.authors: Andreas Chai, Matthew Manning, Elena Stepanova
papers.abstract: Abstract Objectives This study aims to identify key factors that shape the global network of illicit financial flows (IFFs) related to money laundering and other financial crimes. Specifically, it examines the factors determining both i) the selection of destination countries and ii) the volume of illicit funds laundered. Methods We developed a Heckman-adjusted gravity model of illicit financial flows, utilizing data from Suspicious Activity Reports lodged between 2007 to 2017. The first stage of the model analyses the selection of destination countries, while the second stage estimates the volume of laundered funds. Key variables include GDP, financial service quality, Egmont membership, corruption levels, and geographic distance. The Heckman correction is applied to address selection bias. Results Our findings indicate that wealthier countries attract higher levels of illicit financial flows. However, high quality financial services deter both the selection of a country for laundering and the volume of funds laundered. Reported IFFs are more likely from countries with high corruption and conflict levels. Trade, culture and geographic proximity are also found to be correlated with the likelihood and magnitude of reported IFFs. Conclusions Results show evidence of displacement and provide evidence of the link between illicit financial flows and the international flow of trade, people and remittances. Limitations include potential biases in the data and the exclusion of non-USD transactions.
papers.idea: No actionable city-level intervention is identified; this is a descriptive analysis of global illicit financial flows.
papers.summary: The study uses a gravity model to analyze which countries receive illicit financial flows and in what volumes, using Suspicious Activity Reports from 2007–2017. Key findings: wealthier countries attract more laundering; high-quality financial services deter it; corruption and conflict increase it; and trade/cultural ties correlate with IFF patterns.
papers.method: Heckman-adjusted gravity model applied to Suspicious Activity Reports (2007–2017), examining destination-country selection and laundering volume; key predictors include GDP, financial service quality, corruption, and geographic distance.
papers.scalability_note: Global financial-system study; no local intervention lever.
papers.cost_note: Implementation of anti-money-laundering regimes already exists; study offers no new cost estimates.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could strengthen financial-crime reporting and oversight (already mandated federally), but the study identifies no city-specific policy lever or intervention.

papers.title: Environmental Threat and the Nation: Earthquake Risk, Distributive Priority, and Expressive Attachment
papers.authors: Hector Galindo-Silva
papers.abstract: This paper studies how long-run earthquake risk shapes national identity, separating a distributive margin (national membership as a rule for allocating scarce resources) from an expressive margin (pride, willingness to fight, and affective attachment). Linking World Values Survey respondents (1981-2022; 63 countries, 494 subnational regions) to subnational seismic-risk geography, I find that people living closer to high-risk zones express stronger national in-group orientation: more pride, more willingness to fight, and more priority for nationals when jobs are scarce. Family attachment and out-group hostility do not rise, while religiosity increases in parallel. The expressive margin is conditional: the pride response is pronounced where state-religion alignment and a cohesive religious field lend the symbolic infrastructure to cast disaster as a shared national ordeal, and indistinguishable from zero where they do not. A complementary design exploiting earthquakes between adjacent survey waves finds no average short-run response, yet the response it does detect concentrates among older, place-attached residents who cannot leave -- consistent with attitudes tracking a chronic, inescapable risk rather than single events. Together, the results point to a demand-side origin of national attachment: where a covariate shock would overwhelm local and family insurance, people turn to larger communities of protection and meaning -- the nation and religion -- a logic I formalize in a simple social-interaction model.
papers.idea: N/A — no actionable intervention identified.
papers.summary: The study examines how living in earthquake-risk zones correlates with stronger national identity and in-group preference, particularly pride and prioritization of nationals for jobs. The effect appears mediated by state-religion alignment and is driven by chronic risk exposure rather than acute earthquake events.
papers.method: Observational analysis linking World Values Survey respondents (1981–2022; 63 countries, 494 subnational regions) to seismic-risk geography, with a supplementary quasi-experimental design exploiting earthquakes between survey waves.
papers.scalability_note: Fundamentally a study of how a fixed environmental hazard shapes national attachment; no intervention to scale.
papers.cost_note: Study is theoretical/observational; no intervention cost applies.
papers.nyc_note: NYC is not in a seismic risk zone at the scale studied; findings describe attachment to nation/religion, not a city policy lever.

papers.title: How Workforce Systems, Higher Ed, and Industry Can Expand Apprenticeship Degrees
papers.authors: John Marotta, Julia Payne, Deborah Kobes
papers.abstract: Apprenticeship degree programs possess all the hallmarks of any Registered Apprenticeship: they are industry-led and offer paid work experience with wage progression, on-the-job learning, and supplemental education. What sets them apart is that apprentices earn not only a nationally recognized journeyworker credential but also an associate’s, bachelor's, or master's degree. This fact sheet outlines how higher education institutions, industry representatives, and other workforce development stakeholders could work together to expand high-quality apprenticeship degrees. We developed this agenda through extensive stakeholder consultation and refined it through a consensus-driven engagement process.
papers.idea: NYC could establish apprenticeship degree partnerships between CUNY/SUNY, industry employers, and workforce boards to offer registered apprenticeships bundled with associate/bachelor degrees, expanding paid work-based pathways to credentials.
papers.summary: This fact sheet describes apprenticeship degree programs that combine registered apprenticeships (paid work, wage progression, on-the-job training) with formal degree credentials (associate's through master's level). The authors consulted stakeholders and developed a consensus agenda for how higher ed, industry, and workforce systems can expand these hybrid programs at scale.
papers.method: Stakeholder consultation and consensus-driven engagement process (non-empirical policy development).
papers.scalability_note: Model is designed for multi-sector coordination; replicable across cities with existing apprenticeship and higher-ed infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Leverages existing registered apprenticeship wage support; requires institutional coordination but minimal new public subsidy if employer-co-funded.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has established apprenticeship networks, robust CUNY system, and strong union/industry partnerships; direct fit with existing workforce development infrastructure.

papers.title: A Multi-Scalar Causal Approach across Space and Time: An Application to the COVID-19 Pandemic in São Paulo State, Brazil
papers.authors: Katsuo Kogure, Yoshito Takasaki
papers.idea: Unable to determine from title and absent abstract.
papers.summary: No abstract provided. Title suggests a multi-scalar causal analysis of COVID-19 spread patterns across space and time in São Paulo State, but connection to actionable public safety or city policy interventions is unclear.
papers.method: Unable to assess; no abstract available. Appears to be quantitative analysis of pandemic data in Brazilian state.
papers.scalability_note: COVID-19 pandemic analysis likely time-bound and context-specific to Brazil.
papers.cost_note: Unknown; likely uses existing epidemiological data.
papers.nyc_note: Published in criminology journal but focuses on pandemic spread, not crime or safety interventions. Limited NYC relevance without seeing full paper.

papers.title: Diverging Realities: Differential George Floyd Effects on Violence Against the Police
papers.authors: Keller G. Sheppard, Alex Billmeier, Nic Swagar
papers.abstract: Objectives A series of controversial police use-of-force incidents over the past decade has spurred research into their effects on police–community relationships and their ability to provoke an increase in violence against police. While findings have been mixed, with many studies suggesting that concerns about such violence are unfounded, others have found support for changes in violence against the police. This study expands on prior research on police assaults by considering how communities’ socioeconomic contexts might shape the effects of such events. Methods Using data from the National Incident-Based Reporting System (NIBRS), the current study examines the impact of George Floyd's death on police assaults across 2,953 jurisdictions. Results The findings reveal a 36.3% increase in injurious assault incident rates during the month following the event, with the largest increases observed in communities marked by high degrees of income inequality and residential segregation. Conclusions This study underscores how broader ecological characteristics can shape community responses to crises of police legitimacy.
papers.source: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.venue: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.idea: Target community-police legitimacy interventions (training, accountability reform, dialogue programs) in high-inequality, high-segregation neighborhoods to mitigate violence against police.
papers.summary: This study found a 36.3% increase in injurious assaults against police in the month after George Floyd's death, with the largest increases in communities with high income inequality and residential segregation. The research identifies ecological/community characteristics that shape responses to police legitimacy crises.
papers.method: Cross-sectional analysis of police assault incident rates across 2,953 U.S. jurisdictions using National Incident-Based Reporting System (NIBRS) data, comparing pre- and post-George Floyd periods.
papers.scalability_note: Findings identify community characteristics but do not test or propose specific interventions, limiting direct scalability.
papers.cost_note: Potential interventions (training, dialogue, accountability mechanisms) would require moderate investment; abstract provides no cost data.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has high inequality and segregation, making findings contextually relevant, but the study offers no concrete policy levers or NYC-specific analysis.

papers.title: The Female Sensitivity Hypothesis: Evidence From Experimental Economics
papers.authors: Felipe Araujo, Neeraja Gupta, Lise Vesterlund
papers.abstract: We assess the empirical evidence for the hypothesis that women more than men respond to changes in treatment. First, we examine whether the results of over two hundred experimental economics papers support the female sensitivity hypothesis. Second, using data from two studies (DellaVigna and Pope, 2022; Exley et al., 2025), we conduct over two hundred pairwise tests of the hypothesis. Both analyses show that gender is not predictive of responsiveness to treatment. We further examine how the hypothesis has been disseminated in the literature and find that it is referenced predominantly by papers that support it.
papers.idea: None identified—this is a methodological critique of the female sensitivity hypothesis in experimental economics, not a policy intervention.
papers.summary: The paper questions whether women respond more strongly than men to experimental treatments. A meta-analysis of 200+ experimental economics papers and re-analysis of two existing studies found no gender difference in treatment responsiveness, and notes the hypothesis has been cited selectively in the literature.
papers.method: Systematic review and re-analysis of experimental economics studies; meta-analytic assessment of 200+ pairwise hypothesis tests across two datasets (DellaVigna & Pope 2022; Exley et al. 2025).
papers.scalability_note: Pure research contribution; no intervention to scale.
papers.cost_note: No intervention; cost not applicable.
papers.nyc_note: No actionable city policy or program identified. Theoretical/methodological paper on research bias in economics literature.

papers.title: JUE insight: City size, monopsony, and the employment effects of minimum wages
papers.authors: Priyaranjan Jha, Jyotsana Kala, David Neumark, Antonio Rodriguez-Lopez
papers.idea: Cities could tailor minimum wage policy to account for local labor-market concentration (monopsony power) and city size, potentially raising wages more effectively in concentrated markets without reducing employment.
papers.summary: This paper examines how minimum wage effects on employment vary by city size and degree of employer concentration in local labor markets. The research suggests that in smaller cities or markets with fewer employers (monopsony power), minimum wages may have different employment impacts than in competitive larger cities, offering a nuanced view of wage-policy design.
papers.method: Empirical analysis examining minimum wage employment effects across cities of varying sizes and labor-market concentration; specific research design and dataset not described in available abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Minimum wage policy is readily scalable and is already adopted across jurisdictions; geographic variation in application is feasible.
papers.cost_note: No direct program cost; policy lever only (wage floor adjustment). Cost borne by employers, benefit distributed to low-wage workers.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has its own minimum wage; findings could inform future adjustments or sub-regional variation, but city has limited independent wage-setting authority for many sectors.

papers.title: From Democracies to Autocracies: How AI Systems Enable Authoritarianism by Design
papers.authors: Jeba Sania, Marta Ziosi, Fazl Barez
papers.abstract: AI-enabled authoritarianism is not confined to autocracies. In this paper, we provide greater transparency by investigating and mapping the lifecycles of six AI systems deployed in different political regimes, ranging from the US to China. By drawing on an extensive range of sources (academic publications, investigative research reports, third-party evaluations, media interviews, government procurement notices), we conduct a systematic, qualitative comparison across systems to identify the critical technical and operational features that enable authoritarianism within their respective political contexts. We find that enabling features include the centralization and co-optation of administrative data for law enforcement and political punishment, regulatory gaps that fail to deter misuse, weak user compliance that nullifies human oversight mechanisms, and the encoding of protected group traits that identify members of vulnerable populations. We find that these features are present across systems deployed in autocratic and democratic regimes, albeit in varying configurations. We also find that both centralized and fragmented AI systems can contribute to authoritarianism by exploiting governance gaps: centralized systems directed by executive authorities, particularly within security and military institutions, are often not subjected to formal oversight mechanisms, while fragmented systems diffuse accountability between stakeholders, paving the way for entrenchment. These findings reveal that AI-enabled authoritarianism is distributed, resulting from design and operational choices made by developers, administrators, and users alike. We conclude with recommendations for developers and policymakers to mitigate these risks.
papers.idea: Cities could audit and reform AI systems used in law enforcement and administrative data to prevent authoritarianism through design and governance controls.
papers.summary: This paper maps six AI systems across autocratic and democratic regimes to identify technical and operational features that enable authoritarian misuse, including centralization of administrative data for law enforcement, regulatory gaps, weak oversight, and encoding of protected group traits. The authors find that both centralized and fragmented AI systems can enable authoritarianism through different governance failure modes, and that such risks exist across political systems, not just autocracies.
papers.method: Qualitative systematic comparison of six AI systems across different political contexts using academic publications, investigative reports, third-party evaluations, media interviews, and government procurement notices; no empirical evaluation of a specific intervention.
papers.scalability_note: Policy frameworks and audit procedures can scale, but require political will and context-specific adaptation.
papers.cost_note: Implementation costs vary widely depending on scope of AI system audit and governance reform; no unit costs provided.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could apply governance recommendations to NYPD surveillance systems and city data platforms, but paper provides only high-level mitigation strategies, not concrete implementation blueprints.

papers.title: Interactional privilege of violence: Status and interaction in the street field
papers.authors: Hakan Kalkan, Heith Copes
papers.abstract: Abstract Criminologists have long described and theorized the relationship between status, respect, and violence within urban communities. Although this finding is generally accepted within criminology, ethnographic empirical illustrations of this phenomenon are sparse. To better understand how the potential for violence shapes interactions, affords interactional privilege, and structures relative positions in the social hierarchy, we develop a theoretical framework based on the work of Pierre Bourdieu, Erving Goffman, and Randall Collins. Drawing on ethnographic fieldwork in a disadvantaged area in Copenhagen, we show that interactional boundaries—what liberties one can take with others—are contingent on familiarity and verbal skill but more fundamentally on one's violence capital (i.e., the resources, capacities, skills, and dispositions that confer advantage in exerting corporal violence against others). By empirically illustrating how interactions are structured by violence capital, we contribute to a deeper understanding of how street hierarchies are maintained and reproduced through violence.
papers.idea: No actionable intervention identified; paper is theoretical framework development.
papers.summary: The study develops a theoretical framework showing that street hierarchies and violence in disadvantaged urban areas are structured by 'violence capital'—resources, skills, and dispositions that confer advantage in physical violence. The authors use ethnographic fieldwork in Copenhagen to illustrate how interactional boundaries are shaped by familiarity, verbal skill, and most fundamentally by one's capacity for violence.
papers.method: Qualitative ethnographic fieldwork in a disadvantaged area in Copenhagen; no quantitative sample size or comparison group specified.
papers.scalability_note: Theoretical framework with limited concrete policy mechanisms to replicate across cities.
papers.cost_note: Ethnographic research has modest direct costs, but translating insights into intervention is unclear.
papers.nyc_note: Provides sociological understanding of street violence dynamics but offers no explicit lever for NYC policy or program design.

papers.title: Lack of Trust and Fiscal Dominance: Evidence from a Firm Survey Experiment
papers.authors: Francesco Bianchi, Renato Faccini, Leonardo Melosi, Nils Wehrhöfer
papers.abstract: We study how debt news shapes firms’ inflation expectations in a monetary union. In an active-control experiment, German firms receive optimistic or pessimistic projections of France, Italy, and Spain’s debt-to-GDP ratios. Pessimistic news raises debt beliefs and increases one- and three-year inflation expectations, with no detectable effect at five years. The response is driven by low-trust firms and by firms expecting relatively low ECB policy rates. A salient German debt-financed fiscal shock generates no comparable response. Within a Fisherian framework, the evidence suggests that debt news becomes inflationary when firms perceive incomplete fiscal backing and expect monetary accommodation.
papers.idea: No actionable city-level intervention identified.
papers.summary: This is a monetary-union macroeconomic study examining how German firms' inflation expectations respond to debt news from other eurozone countries. The experiment found that pessimistic debt projections raised inflation expectations among low-trust firms, but the mechanism is fiscal-monetary policy coordination in a currency union—a national/supranational concern, not a city policy lever.
papers.method: Active-control experiment with German firms randomly assigned to receive optimistic or pessimistic debt-to-GDP projections for other eurozone nations; inflation expectations measured via survey.
papers.scalability_note: Eurozone monetary-union dynamics; no city applicability.
papers.cost_note: Research cost only; no intervention cost to estimate.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has no control over monetary policy, fiscal dominance, or eurozone debt; findings are structural-macro, not amenable to municipal action.

papers.title: LearnOpt: Recovering the Latent Cognitive Structure of Standardized Examinations via Knowledge Graphs and Constrained Optimization
papers.authors: Joy Bose, Om Thomas
papers.abstract: Standardized examinations are typically treated as uniform syllabus coverage problems. We argue they are better understood as adversarial systems with stable latent cognitive structures diverging systematically from official syllabi. We introduce LearnOpt, which recovers this structure from historical question papers and generates personalized, time-bounded study plans. Applied to nine years of NEET questions (2016-2024, n=1,496), LearnOpt builds an exam knowledge graph from LLM-tagged questions, extracts a five-category latent skill distribution, and formulates study planning as a knapsack-variant optimization over prerequisite-aware subgraphs with Bayesian Knowledge Tracing. Central finding: NEET's latent skill distribution is stable within a syllabus regime (consecutive-year KL divergence 0.004-0.032 for 2016-2021, non-significant under permutation testing) but shifts significantly with NCERT's 2023 syllabus rationalization: pooling 2016-2021 (n=1,072) vs 2023-2024 (n=392) gives KL=0.040 (p=0.0005), with Elimination/Negation questions rising from ~20-29% to ~31-35%. Latent structure, while not permanently stationary, is piecewise stable, with shifts detectable and attributable to curricular events. Within either regime, subject predicts skill profile more strongly than year. An optimization evaluation, using one real and two synthetic mastery profiles, shows the skill-weighted objective produces a modest but real reordering of recommended topics over a mastery-conditioned frequency baseline. Applying the pipeline to JEE Advanced reveals a profile dominated by Multi-concept Integration (80.9% vs. 33.3% for NEET), with a JEE-vs-NEET divergence (KL=0.505) exceeding NEET's largest cross-subject divergence: exam tier shapes latent cognitive structure more than subject, which shapes it more than time within a regime. Code, knowledge graph, and annotated dataset are released publicly.
papers.idea: NYC public schools could adopt LearnOpt's latent-skill-structure recovery method to diagnose gaps between official curricula and what high-stakes exams actually test, then redesign test-prep programs and resource allocation accordingly.
papers.summary: LearnOpt analyzes historical standardized exam questions using knowledge graphs and optimization to recover the true underlying cognitive skill distribution exams measure—which often diverges from official syllabi. Applied to 9 years of NEET (Indian medical entrance exam) data, the method found latent skill structure is piecewise stable within a syllabus regime but shifts detectably when curricula change, and discovered that exam tier and subject shape cognitive demand far more than year-to-year variation.
papers.method: Retrospective analysis of 1,496 NEET questions (2016–2024) tagged by LLM, built into a knowledge graph, with skill distributions extracted and stability tested via KL divergence and permutation testing; applied also to JEE Advanced for validation.
papers.scalability_note: Method is fully automated (LLM-based tagging, graph construction, optimization) and requires only historical exam data; easily deployable across multiple exams and school systems.
papers.cost_note: Minimal: LLM API calls, computation, and public-release data; negligible per-student cost once built.
papers.nyc_note: NYC uses Regents exams, SAT, and other standardized assessments; the pipeline could be applied to these. However, the method is designed for high-stakes entrance exams (NEET, JEE); applicability to K–12 or college-readiness exams depends on data availability and curriculum structure similarity.

papers.title: Scaling Demand-Side Flexibility Through Dynamic Tariffs
papers.authors: Lucas Brylle, Niels Andersen, Henrik Madsen
papers.abstract: The ongoing electrification and integration of renewable energy sources in Denmark's distribution grids pose significant operational challenges, including insufficient reserve capacity, component degradation due to overload, voltage instability, and increasing infrastructure investment requirements. This article argues that implicit demand-side flexibility (DSF) incentivized through dynamic tariffs offers the most scalable and cost-effective approach to address these challenges in a modern distribution network. We demonstrate that while explicit flexibility mechanisms provide operational certainty, they cannot scale to address system-wide congestion across heterogeneous customer bases. Drawing on empirical consumption data showing strong price-responsive behavior, varying prices due to, e.g., regulatory frameworks including the Danish Market Model 3.0 and Tariff Model 3.0, and economic analysis, we demonstrate potential grid savings of 13--48 million DKK per constrained substation through deferred or avoided reinforcement. We argue that implicit DSF mechanisms represent the necessary pathway for revenue-neutral scalable flexibility solutions that can defer costly grid reinforcements while maintaining system reliability. Beyond direct grid savings, additional value streams include avoided peak generation costs, reduced connection delays, and lower outage risk, further strengthening the economic case. Critically, dynamic tariffs offer the mechanism through which real-time grid constraints can be communicated to consumers, enabling price signals that accurately reflect the actual state of the capacity of the distribution network at any given point in time and space.
papers.idea: Implement dynamic electricity tariffs that vary in real-time based on grid capacity constraints to incentivize demand flexibility and defer costly grid infrastructure reinforcement.
papers.summary: The study argues that implicit demand-side flexibility incentivized through dynamic tariffs is more scalable than explicit mechanisms for managing distribution grid challenges in Denmark. Analysis of empirical consumption data shows consumers are price-responsive and that dynamic tariffs could generate 13–48 million DKK in grid savings per constrained substation by deferring or avoiding reinforcement, while also reducing peak generation costs and outage risk.
papers.method: Economic analysis using empirical consumption data from Denmark's distribution networks, regulatory frameworks (Market Model 3.0 and Tariff Model 3.0), and comparative assessment of explicit vs. implicit flexibility mechanisms.
papers.scalability_note: Dynamic tariff mechanisms can be applied city- or system-wide via utility pricing infrastructure; already partly piloted in Denmark.
papers.cost_note: Revenue-neutral design; primary benefit is deferred capex (13–48M DKK savings per substation); cost to consumer depends on tariff design.
papers.nyc_note: ConEd could pilot dynamic tariffs in constrained zones, but NYC faces regulatory, political, and consumer-acceptance barriers; feasible but requires buy-in from PSC and coordination with existing rate structures.

papers.title: Indoor Thermal Environments, Cooling Access, and Energy Burden in New Orleans, LA: Challenges and Opportunities for Heat Adaptation
papers.authors: Lena Easton-Calabria, Caroline Reed, Teague Ruder, Jordan Mychal, Jacopo Scazzosi, Ramya Chari, Brian Vant-Hull, Julia Kumari Drapkin, Jaime Madrigano
papers.abstract: Abstract Extreme heat is a significant and growing health hazard in urban locations around the world, particularly in the Southeastern United States (U.S.). While most extreme heat research and interventions are focused on ambient outdoor conditions and the neighborhood environment, the indoor residential environment is where the most severe heat-health consequences occur. The aim of this study was to characterize the indoor thermal environments and identify predictors of high indoor temperatures for residents of heat-vulnerable neighborhoods within New Orleans, Louisiana, within the context of current heat adaptation measures and issues of energy insecurity. We conducted surveys with both open- and closed-ended questions and measured indoor temperature over 2-week sampling periods in 114 households across two heat vulnerable New Orleans wards during the warm seasons of 2023 and 2024. Our study found that a combination of AC type and use, along with outdoor daily maximum temperature, were significant predictors of indoor maximum overnight temperature. Our results indicate that households without AC, using window AC units, or those not running central AC all or most of the time struggled to maintain 80 degrees Fahrenheit overnight (a threshold deemed appropriate by a recent healthy homes ordinance) once outdoor daily maximum temperatures exceeded 90 degrees Fahrenheit. Homeownership, compared to renting, was associated with higher overnight indoor temperatures, greater variability in typical AC use patterns, and greater sensitivity of summer monthly energy expenditures to differences in AC use patterns, potentially indicating that this group is practicing energy limiting behavior. This paper contributes to limited literature on indoor thermal environments, particularly in the Southeastern U.S., and underscores the importance of housing and energy burden in heat adaptation.
papers.idea: Cities should mandate minimum indoor temperature standards (80°F overnight) in rental housing codes and fund targeted AC installation/repair programs for heat-vulnerable neighborhoods, prioritizing renters and low-income homeowners.
papers.summary: A survey of 114 New Orleans households found that residents without AC, with only window units, or not running central AC consistently struggled to maintain 80°F overnight once outdoor temps exceeded 90°F. Homeowners showed greater energy-limiting behavior and higher temperature variability, suggesting economic strain on low-income owner-occupants.
papers.method: Cross-sectional household survey with open/closed questions plus 2-week indoor temperature monitoring in 114 homes across two New Orleans wards during 2023–2024 warm seasons.
papers.scalability_note: AC access/indoor temperature standards are replicable across cities; implementation varies by local housing stock and climate.
papers.cost_note: Central AC installation ~$3,500–$5,000 per unit; window units $200–$1,500; city subsidy programs needed for equity, moderate cost-effectiveness.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing healthy homes ordinance framework; heat vulnerability is rising in outer boroughs; landlord compliance infrastructure in place; summer heat deaths rising.

papers.title: Advancing and Defending Homeownership Gains
papers.authors: Lydia Lo, Michael Neal, Ilina Mitra, Matthew Pruitt, Violet Sulka/Hewes, Jung Hyun Choi
papers.abstract: Why This Matters The conditions needed to facilitate homebuying have remained under immense pressure over the past year. Despite experiencing significant pressures, local collaboratives under the Wealth Opportunities Realized Through Homeownership (WORTH) Initiative have continued to support households’ ability to achieve or preserve homeownership. Collaboratives have demonstrated a strong commitment to their mission of reaching all high-barrier communities by seeking new methods for facilitating impact, including employing technology. And they have shown a willingness to test new ideas and engage with each other, resulting in more homebuying and preservation activity under WORTH. Key Takeaways This report shows the following findings: As challenges to new home purchases have persisted, collaboratives sharpened their focus on home preservation activities. Foreclosure prevention is the leading home preservation tool WORTH collaboratives used. Resolving heirs’ property was an emerging strategy more collaboratives started using. Rising construction costs have limited innovative housing supply programs. New home construction
