papers.idea: Cities can establish or strengthen local collaborative networks (like WORTH) that combine foreclosure prevention, heirs' property resolution, and homeownership counseling to help high-barrier households achieve or preserve homeownership.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute evaluated the WORTH Initiative, a network of local collaboratives supporting homeownership in high-barrier communities. As purchase pressures intensified, collaboratives shifted focus toward home preservation (especially foreclosure prevention) and began addressing heirs' property issues. Rising construction costs constrained new supply innovation.
papers.method: Descriptive case study of WORTH collaboratives' activities and strategies over a one-year period under conditions of housing-market stress; no control group or comparison cohort specified in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Collaborative model is transferable across cities; requires coordination but not novel infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Depends on staffing and foreclosure-prevention intensity; no per-unit cost stated in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing homeownership and foreclosure-prevention programs (HPD, legal aid); could expand collaborative model and heirs'-property resolution specifically.

papers.title: Elevating Their Voices: What Single Mothers Say They Need to Survive
papers.authors: Poonam Gupta, Baris Tezel, Elaine Waxman
papers.abstract: In this brief, we share survey-based insights on the challenges single mothers face that affect their material and economic stability and family well-being. This survey was fielded to single mothers across the country participating in a national summit held by Jeremiah Program, a national nonprofit organization focused on disrupting the cycle of poverty for single mothers and their children. We highlight results among key groups of single mothers who are known to face particular financial burdens and may benefit from specific policy strategies, including student parents and those parents with young children. Why This Matters Ensuring that single-mother-led households can achieve economic stability begins with recognizing and addressing the unique financial and caregiving pressures they face. Doing so will help reduce the material hardships and structural barriers that leave many single mothers and their children economically vulnerable. These economic challenges are expected to increase in the wake of an ongoing
papers.idea: Cities could design targeted support programs (childcare subsidies, emergency cash assistance, education/employment pathways) for single mothers based on their stated needs, particularly for student parents and those with young children.
papers.summary: The study surveyed single mothers participating in a national Jeremiah Program summit to identify material and economic stability challenges they face. Researchers highlighted particular financial burdens affecting student parents and mothers with young children, and the structural barriers that leave these households economically vulnerable.
papers.method: Survey administered to single mothers attending a national summit organized by Jeremiah Program, a nonprofit focused on reducing poverty among single-mother households.
papers.scalability_note: Survey-identified needs can inform policy across multiple cities; interventions themselves are replicable.
papers.cost_note: Depends on interventions designed; needs assessment is low-cost but implementation (childcare, cash aid) varies widely.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing single-mother support infrastructure (TANF, childcare vouchers, education programs); findings could refine targeting and design of current services.

papers.title: Unequal Exposure: Examining Outdoor Work and Climate Exposure in the US
papers.authors: Lisa Clemans-Cope, Lisa Dubay, Vincent Pancini, Avani Pugazhendhi
papers.abstract: Climate change is intensifying heat waves, worsening air quality, and fueling more frequent wildfires—yet little is known about the roughly one in five US workers whose jobs regularly take them outdoors. This study provides a national portrait of outdoor workers’ demographics, health, employment benefits, and proximity to climate-related environmental hazards. The findings reveal where protections fall short and which workers face the greatest risks. Why This Matters Outdoor workers are disproportionately male, Hispanic, lower-income, and less likely to hold a college degree. They are more likely to be uninsured, lack paid sick leave, and work in self-employed or nonstandard arrangements—all factors that can limit their ability to avoid hazardous conditions or seek timely care. Meanwhile, occupational protections remain fragmented: only six states have outdoor heat standards, only three have wildfire smoke rules, and a proposed federal heat rule has stalled. Two states have preempted local heat ordinances entirely. Federal, state
papers.idea: Cities can adopt local outdoor heat and air-quality standards for workers (construction, delivery, landscaping) with mandatory rest breaks, shade access, and real-time hazard alerts—filling the gap where federal/state protections are absent.
papers.summary: This Urban Institute study profiles ~20% of US workers in outdoor jobs, finding they are disproportionately Hispanic, lower-income, uninsured, and lack paid sick leave. The research documents their heightened exposure to heat, poor air quality, and wildfire smoke, while identifying major gaps: only 6 states have outdoor heat standards and 3 have wildfire smoke rules.
papers.method: Descriptive national portrait using demographic, employment, and health data; appears to combine Census/labor data with occupational hazard mapping, though specific sample size and methodology are not detailed in the abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Regulatory standards and alert systems are easily replicated across cities; implementation cost and enforcement complexity vary.
papers.cost_note: Moderate—requires monitoring infrastructure, alert systems, and compliance oversight, but no per-unit costs stated in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has large outdoor workforce (construction, delivery, parks, sanitation); city agencies (OSHA, DOH) could adopt local heat/air-quality rules independent of stalled federal action.

papers.title: Housing Finance at a Glance: A Monthly Chartbook, May 2026
papers.authors: Laurie Goodman, Michael Neal, Jung Hyun Choi, Linna Zhu, John Walsh, Daniel Pang, Katie Visalli, Aniket Mehrotra, Matthew Pruitt, Bryson Berry, Alison Rincon, Todd Hill, Anna Barcus, Erin Koons
papers.abstract: Housing Finance at a Glance is a monthly reference guide for mortgage and housing market data. It includes over 80 curated and updated figures describing current trends in the US housing finance market, including housing affordability, credit availability, serious delinquency rates, and mortgage insurance activity. These trends stood out most to housing finance policy researchers from this chartbook edition: The aggregate value of housing equity has slightly contracted amid flat home values and rising mortgage debt outstanding (p. 6) Mortgage affordability has improved as of March 2026 (p. 24) and home sales are expected to rise (p. 22). 80.7 percent of FHA loans are originated for first-time homebuyers (p. 30). FHA loans have contributed to growth in mortgage insurance over the past year (p. 37).
papers.summary: This is a monthly reference chartbook tracking US housing finance trends including affordability, mortgage credit, delinquency rates, and insurance activity. The May 2026 edition highlights recent improvements in mortgage affordability, expected home sales growth, and FHA loan patterns.
papers.method: Compilation and curation of 80+ existing data series and figures on housing finance and mortgage markets; appears to be ongoing monitoring/dashboard rather than original research.
papers.scalability_note: Data compilation easily replicated elsewhere; but outputs are metrics, not interventions.
papers.cost_note: Production of reference chartbook is low-cost; unclear if actionable uses exist.
papers.nyc_note: National data aggregation with no NYC-specific analysis; limited direct policy lever without deeper local adaptation.

papers.title: Community Colleges That Align Career Pathways, Affordability, and Support Services Could See Higher Student Success
papers.authors: Shayne Spaulding, Ryan Kelsey
papers.abstract: Community colleges are often described as engines of opportunity, and for good reason. They offer affordable, flexible pathways to credentials, careers, and economic mobility, especially for students with low incomes, for parenting students, and for those who are the first in their families to attend college. But tuition is only one part of the story. For many students, success also depends on whether colleges can help them navigate child care, transportation, access to high-quality food, career decisions, and the many other barriers to staying enrolled and completing a program. The Urban Institute set out to explore some of the ways colleges are supporting students as part of an effort to inform the Annie E. Casey Foundation’s postsecondary education investment strategy. We wanted to find out more about the approaches community colleges are taking to support student success, focusing on three core areas: career pathways, supportive services, and affordability. A key
papers.idea: Cities can adopt community college support models that bundle career pathway alignment, affordability assistance, and wraparound services (childcare, transit, food access) to increase credential completion and economic mobility.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute examined how community colleges support student success across three areas: career pathways, supportive services, and affordability. The study identifies that tuition alone is insufficient; students also need help navigating barriers like childcare, transportation, and food insecurity to stay enrolled and complete programs.
papers.method: Exploratory research by the Urban Institute examining approaches community colleges are taking to support student success; venue and funder (Annie E. Casey Foundation) suggest qualitative case studies or program review, but methods not detailed in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Community college models are replicable across cities; NYC already has CUNY system to build on.
papers.cost_note: Wraparound services (childcare, transit vouchers, food) require ongoing subsidy; cost-effectiveness depends on completion gains not stated in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: CUNY serves ~250k students; NYC could systematize childcare, transit, food access partnerships across campuses.

papers.title: Supply and Nontraditional-Hour Care
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: Child care that supports children’s development while enabling parents to work or attend school is hard to find across the country. To address this, states and communities first need to understand the existing supply. DECAL contracted with Urban to examine two aspects of this issue. First, in 2023, Urban examined parents’ needs for nonparental child care and their preferences for care during nontraditional hours (after 6:00 p.m. or before 7:00 a.m. on weekdays or anytime on weekends). Second, in 2024, Urban surveyed child care providers regarding how well licensing data reflected the number of slots they could offer families, whether those slots met families’ needs, and how easy or difficult it was to start new child care businesses.
papers.idea: Cities can use supply-side child care data to design targeted subsidies, licensing reform, or startup support programs that expand affordable care during nontraditional hours, enabling parental workforce participation.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute surveyed parents' needs for nontraditional-hour child care (before 7 a.m., after 6 p.m. weekdays, or weekends) and examined provider capacity and barriers to entry. The study assessed gaps between licensing data and actual provider capacity, and documented obstacles to starting new child care businesses.
papers.method: Two-part survey study: first surveyed parents on nontraditional care needs and preferences (2023); second surveyed child care providers on licensing accuracy, slot availability, family demand match, and business startup barriers (2024). Conducted for Georgia (DECAL) but methodology generalizable.
papers.scalability_note: Diagnostic work on supply/demand gaps is replicable across cities; actionable policy levers (subsidies, licensing reform, startup grants) scale readily.
papers.cost_note: Implementation cost varies by intervention chosen (subsidies expensive; data collection + licensing reform moderate).
papers.nyc_note: NYC has extensive child care workforce and regulatory infrastructure; city could replicate survey design, use findings to target expansion of nontraditional care slots via existing subsidy/partnership programs (e.g., NYC DOE).

papers.title: Measuring the Impact of Unconditional Cash on the Social Determinants of Health
papers.authors: Daisy Gomez Palacios, Mary Bogle, Alexa Kort
papers.abstract: This brief presents interim findings from the first cohort of participants in CashRx, a guaranteed income program administered by Bread for the City, which is a federally qualified health center that provides food, clothing, medical care, and legal and social services to low-income residents in Washington, DC. Financial strain is a key social determinant of health, shaping individuals’ access to stable housing, nutritious food, and consistent medical care. A growing body of research suggests that unrestricted cash assistance, like guaranteed income, can help reduce stress, improve material well-being, and support better health outcomes. Why This Matters The first cohort of CashRx participants demonstrates how flexible, dignity-centered cash assistance—combined with supportive services—can advance economic outlook and improve well-being among people facing chronic illness and financial instability. These findings underscore the potential of guaranteed income to address key social determinants of health, while also revealing the limits of monetary interventions without structural
papers.idea: Launch a guaranteed income pilot program paired with wraparound health and social services (modeled on CashRx) targeting low-income residents with chronic illness and financial instability.
papers.summary: CashRx, a guaranteed income program administered by a DC federally qualified health center, provided unrestricted cash assistance combined with supportive services to low-income participants. Interim findings suggest the program improved economic outlook and well-being by reducing financial strain—a key social determinant of health—and supporting better access to stable housing, food, and medical care.
papers.method: Interim analysis of first cohort of CashRx participants; setting is a federally qualified health center in Washington, DC; sample size not specified in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Guaranteed income model is replicable; can be piloted in one or more NYC neighborhoods and expanded.
papers.cost_note: Direct cash transfers have known per-recipient costs; abstract does not specify total program cost per person, but likely moderate to high depending on grant amount and service intensity.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has extensive FQHC network and could partner with existing health/social service providers (e.g., Federally Qualified Health Centers, DOHMH); model fits health equity and housing stability goals.

papers.title: Georgia’s Quality Rating and Improvement System
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: In 2023, Urban gathered perspectives from families, child care providers, technical assistance providers, and DECAL staff to inform DECAL’s comprehensive review of Quality Rated, Georgia’s quality rating and improvement system—examining topics such as what quality means, the benefits of and barriers to participating in Quality Rated, the system’s fairness, and opportunities for improvement.
papers.idea: NYC could systematize and improve its child care quality rating and improvement system by gathering stakeholder input on what quality means, barriers to participation, and fairness concerns—mirroring Georgia's DECAL review process.
papers.summary: Urban Institute conducted a qualitative review of Georgia's Quality Rated system in 2023, gathering feedback from families, providers, technical assistance staff, and state officials on system design, perceived benefits, barriers, fairness, and improvement opportunities. The study appears to have identified stakeholder priorities and pain points in how quality is measured and communicated in the child care market.
papers.method: Qualitative interviews and focus groups with multiple stakeholder types (families, providers, TA providers, state staff) in Georgia; sample size and full methodology not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Quality rating systems exist across multiple states and can be adapted; methodology is transferable to other jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Qualitative research and system review are moderate-cost; operational improvements depend on findings.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has child care quality oversight and subsidy programs (NYCDOE, ACS); a similar stakeholder review could improve transparency and participation in existing systems.

papers.title: How Local Leaders in Boone County, Missouri, Are Advancing Upward Mobility for Residents
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: Dignity and belonging. Economic success. Power and autonomy. These are the things all people need to achieve upward mobility. But how do you help people access them? Boone County, Missouri’s, Upward Mobility initiative has an answer. Its work over the past five years has shown that though helping residents exit poverty and access long-term upward mobility is multifaceted and complex, it’s possible when local leaders are committed to partnership, have a deep understanding of the community’s needs and strengths, and prioritize helping residents feel dignity and belonging. Below, hear from four leaders of Boone County’s Upward Mobility initiative about how they engage in upward mobility work and what motivates them to keep working toward a future where all residents have opportunities to thrive. Convening the Community to Advance Upward Mobility Like in many parts of the country, rents in Boone County have climbed in recent years. From 2019 to 2024
papers.idea: Convene local leaders across sectors to design and implement community-centered upward mobility initiatives grounded in resident dignity, belonging, and multi-stakeholder partnership.
papers.summary: Boone County, Missouri's five-year Upward Mobility initiative demonstrates that helping residents exit poverty requires sustained commitment to partnership, deep community understanding, and prioritizing dignity and belonging. The program addresses concrete local challenges like rising rents by convening diverse stakeholders to co-design solutions.
papers.method: Descriptive case study featuring qualitative interviews with four initiative leaders in Boone County, Missouri; no experimental or control group comparison.
papers.scalability_note: Community convening and partnership models are replicable, though specific implementation will vary by local context and leadership capacity.
papers.cost_note: Convening and coordination are relatively low-cost; costs depend on interventions adopted (housing, workforce, etc.), which abstract does not detail.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has strong multi-stakeholder governance structures and agencies; dignity-centered convening model could integrate into existing economic mobility initiatives.

papers.title: Pathways to Homeownership and Wealth Building in AANHPI Communities
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: Asian American, Native Hawaiian, and Pacific Islander (AANHPI) households are among the fastest-growing populations in the US. But research and policy have long overlooked how access to homeownership—and the ability to build and sustain wealth over the life course—varies across diverse AANHPI households and places. This project brings together new national analyses, community-informed insights, and regional spotlights to examine homeownership dynamics, wealth-building trajectories, and housing stability risks across AANHPI communities. It represents one of the first comprehensive efforts to document how AANHPI households have pursued homeownership and wealth-building pathways over the past several decades and how these pathways differ across stage of life, ethnicity, and geography. These insights can help inform policies and practices to support first-generation homebuyers, strengthen intergenerational wealth-building opportunities, and improve housing stability across federal, state, and local contexts. Research and Insights Explore the resources below to learn about the barriers and opportunities for homeownership and wealth
papers.idea: Cities can design targeted homeownership and wealth-building programs for underserved AANHPI communities by addressing ethnicity- and life-stage-specific barriers identified in this research.
papers.summary: This Urban Institute project documents homeownership access and wealth-building trajectories across diverse AANHPI households over decades, examining how pathways vary by ethnicity, life stage, and geography. The research identifies barriers and opportunities to inform policies supporting first-generation homebuyers, intergenerational wealth transfer, and housing stability in AANHPI communities.
papers.method: National comparative analyses combined with community-informed insights and regional case studies examining historical homeownership dynamics and housing stability risks across AANHPI subgroups.
papers.scalability_note: Findings can inform replicable policy and program design across multiple cities and jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Depends on specific interventions adopted; community-based first-generation homebuyer programs typically require moderate upfront investment.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has large, diverse AANHPI populations and existing housing/wealth programs (HPD, community development organizations) that could integrate ethnicity-specific barriers and pathways.

papers.title: Understanding Homelessness and Housing Instability Among AANHPI Populations
papers.authors: Pear Moraras, Linna Zhu, Bryson Berry, Brendan Chen
papers.abstract: Asian American, Native Hawaiian, and Pacific Islander (AANHPI) communities are among the fastest‑growing racial groups in the US, and recent data show rising homelessness among AANHPI households and persistent unmet need for affordable, high‑quality rental housing. Yet, their experiences with housing instability and homelessness remain largely understudied. This report integrates national data with insights from community‑based organizations, gathered through surveys and listening sessions, to illuminate AANHPI households’ experiences with housing instability and homelessness and to identify opportunities for more responsive policy and practice. Why This Matters This study builds on Urban’s broader body of work on AANHPI homeownership and upward mobility, which examines long-term trends in homeownership access, wealth-building trajectories, and geographic mobility among AANHPI households—areas that have historically received limited attention in housing and wealth-building research. This report aims to strengthen the evidence base on the full range of housing experiences shaping opportunity and vulnerability across AANHPI communities, with
papers.idea: NYC could develop AANHPI-specific homelessness prevention and housing placement programs informed by community-based organization insights on culturally distinct barriers and needs.
papers.summary: This Urban Institute report documents rising homelessness and persistent affordable housing shortages among Asian American, Native Hawaiian, and Pacific Islander communities—a fast-growing demographic whose housing instability has been understudied. The research integrates national data with direct input from community-based organizations to identify policy and practice gaps.
papers.method: Mixed-methods study combining national data analysis with surveys and listening sessions conducted with community-based organizations serving AANHPI populations.
papers.scalability_note: Housing and homelessness interventions are municipal-level tools; AANHPI targeting is replicable across cities with significant AANHPI populations.
papers.cost_note: Depends on program design; homeless services and housing assistance typically require sustained funding; cost per person unknown from abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has large AANHPI population, existing homeless services infrastructure, and agencies (DHS, HPD) that could adopt AANHPI-responsive programming.

papers.title: Philadelphia’s Zero Fare Transit Pilot Program
papers.authors: Amelia Coffey, Lindiwe Rennert, Gabe Samuels, Hannah Sumiko Daly
papers.abstract: Affordable transportation remains a critical challenge for many low-income Philadelphia residents, limiting their ability to work and access services. This report examines Philadelphia’s Zero Fare transit pilot program, a novel approach to reducing transportation barriers by providing fully subsidized public transit through automatic enrollment. The City of Philadelphia funds and administers Zero Fare with support from the Southeastern Pennsylvania Transportation Authority (SEPTA). Drawing on interviews with program administrators and community partners and focus groups with recipients, the report examines how Zero Fare was designed and implemented, what worked well, what challenges emerged, and the lessons it offers for transit agencies and policymakers nationwide. Why This Matters People who struggle to afford transit face barriers to economic mobility and reduced quality of life, and yet most transit benefit programs offered on the nation’s largest transit networks reach only a fraction of eligible riders. Zero Fare demonstrates that reducing administrative barriers can
papers.idea: Implement automatic enrollment in a fully subsidized transit program for low-income residents to reduce administrative barriers and increase uptake.
papers.summary: Philadelphia's Zero Fare program provides fully subsidized public transit through automatic enrollment, funded by the city and administered with SEPTA. The Urban Institute study examines program design, implementation, successes, challenges, and lessons from interviews with administrators, community partners, and focus groups with recipients.
papers.method: Qualitative case study combining interviews with program administrators and community partners and focus groups with program recipients in Philadelphia.
papers.scalability_note: Transit subsidy programs are standard infrastructure; automatic enrollment mechanism is replicable across transit systems.
papers.cost_note: Depends on subsidy amount and enrollment scale; abstract does not provide per-rider cost data.
papers.nyc_note: NYC operates MTA with existing OMNY fare system and safety-net programs (NYCTA); could adapt automatic enrollment for low-income riders through existing infrastructure.

papers.title: How Are Earned Wage Access Products Regulated in Your State?
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: Use of earned wage access (EWA) products has increased significantly in recent years. In 2022, an estimated 10 million workers used these products to access more than $31.9 billion in earned wages before their scheduled payday. At a time when many Americans are struggling to make ends meet, EWA products can help users bridge temporary cash shortfalls or navigate short-term emergency expenses by allowing them to access wages they have already earned but not yet been paid. But most products charge users a fee for faster access to their wages. And some don’t clearly disclose their fee and cost structures. Despite their growing popularity, EWA products have not been defined consistently under federal regulations. This has led to a patchwork of state laws that differ regarding how EWA products are classified, how transparent their pricing and fees must be, and which user safeguards they must implement. This tool provides a
papers.idea: NYC could adopt transparent disclosure and consumer-protection standards for earned wage access (EWA) products, or regulate them as a city to protect low-wage workers from predatory fees and unclear pricing.
papers.summary: Earned wage access products allow workers to access already-earned wages before payday, with 10 million users accessing $31.9 billion in 2022. Most products charge fees, often with unclear disclosure, and regulatory frameworks vary widely by state, creating consumer protection gaps.
papers.method: Regulatory landscape mapping and comparison tool across states; descriptive/inventory study of state-level EWA laws and classification schemes.
papers.scalability_note: Regulatory frameworks can be adopted city- or state-wide; applies to any jurisdiction with wage-earning workers.
papers.cost_note: Low implementation cost—primarily requires drafting disclosure/fee standards and light monitoring; no direct subsidy needed.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could push for state-level regulation or adopt local transparency/anti-predation standards for city contractors; federal/state authority may limit city-only action, but advocacy and worker-protection measures are feasible.

papers.title: How Intermediaries Can Help Small and Medium-Sized Businesses Adopt Apprenticeship Programs
papers.authors: Bhavani Arabandi, Andrew Campbell, Lindsey Tyson
papers.abstract: Small and medium-sized businesses (SMBs) play a vital role in the US economy, yet many struggle to find, train, and retain skilled workers—particularly in technology occupations. From 2023 to 2026, the Urban Institute served as an apprenticeship intermediary in North Carolina and South Carolina, working with state apprenticeship agencies and SMB employers to design, launch, and sustain high-quality registered apprenticeship programs. This work, supported by Google.org, focused on helping SMBs overcome common barriers to apprenticeship adoption while building durable pathways into tech careers. Why This Matters SMBs employ nearly half of the US workforce and generate more than 40 percent of GDP, but they often lack the staffing, financial resources, and administrative capacity needed to sponsor registered apprenticeship programs on their own. As a result, larger employers are far more likely to participate in the registered apprenticeship system. Without targeted support, many SMBs miss out on the benefits of apprenticeships—including
papers.idea: Cities can fund and operate apprenticeship intermediaries that help small and medium-sized businesses design and launch registered apprenticeship programs, particularly in tech and skilled trades, reducing barriers to entry for SMBs.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute operated as an apprenticeship intermediary in North and South Carolina from 2023–2026, working with state agencies and SMB employers to design and sustain registered apprenticeship programs. The work addressed common barriers SMBs face in adopting apprenticeships—lack of staffing, financial resources, and administrative capacity—while building durable pathways into tech careers.
papers.method: Intermediary program implementation in two states over three years, supported by Google.org; appears descriptive/case-study based on abstract (no control group or rigorous impact evaluation mentioned).
papers.scalability_note: Intermediary model is portable across regions; requires funding and coordination but no novel infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Intermediary staffing costs required; Google.org-funded model, so cost-per-trainee not disclosed in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has robust small-business ecosystem and existing apprenticeship infrastructure (DOLLY, NYC Apprenticeship); intermediary model aligns with city economic-mobility goals and workforce development priorities.

papers.title: Evaluation of Break Through Tech’s AI Studio and Sprinternship Programs
papers.authors: Karen Gardiner, Nathan Sick, Julia Payne, Hailey D'Elia
papers.abstract: Break Through Tech offers undergraduate students technical skills training, professional readiness support, and real-world project experiences to help them attain in-college paid internships and post-college tech jobs or paid summer jobs. Program leadership commissioned a mixed methods evaluation of Break Through Tech’s two experiential, project-based learning programs: AI Studio, a one-semester, part-time, virtual program and Sprinternship, a three-week, full-time, hybrid program. The evaluation focused on employer engagement and participants’ (known as fellows) outcomes. The evaluation found that employers participate in AI Studio and Sprinternship to build diverse talent pipelines and develop staff leadership; most reported real-world use for fellows’ projects at their business. Employers rated their experiences with AI Studio and Sprinternship fellows highly and would recommend fellows for internships and permanent positions. Fellows in both programs reported an increased sense of belonging in tech, confidence in their skills, and desire to pursue a tech career. All three are critical
papers.idea: Cities could partner with tech employers and universities to replicate Break Through Tech's paid internship and skills-training programs to build diverse talent pipelines and increase tech-sector employment among underrepresented students.
papers.summary: Break Through Tech operates two experiential learning programs (AI Studio and Sprinternship) that provide undergraduate students technical training, professional support, and paid internship/job opportunities. Employers reported high satisfaction, real-world use of fellows' projects, and willingness to hire them; fellows reported increased sense of belonging in tech, skill confidence, and career intent.
papers.method: Mixed-methods evaluation commissioned by program leadership, examining employer engagement and fellow outcomes across two programs; sample size and data sources not specified in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Model is replicable across universities and employer partnerships; already operating at multiple sites.
papers.cost_note: Requires paid internship stipends and instructional staff; cost per participant not disclosed in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has major tech employers, CUNY/NYU systems, and economic mobility imperatives; could integrate with existing workforce development and higher-ed partnerships.

papers.title: Local Strategies to Advance Housing Stability
papers.authors: Isabella Remor, Grace Koch, Kathryn Reynolds, Samantha Fu, Katie Fallon
papers.abstract: Launched by the Urban Institute in 2024, the Stabilizing Home initiative convened a cohort of city housing practitioners from nine midsize cities (cities with populations of less than 1 million) to codevelop strategies for rental housing stability at the local level and share lessons learned. This brief synthesizes the key insights from those cities, highlighting practical, replicable lessons and strategic opportunities for other local governments looking to design and implement solutions that foster stable housing for renters. Why This Matters In cities across the US, renters are facing increasing housing insecurity, with more households struggling with rising rent costs. State and local policymakers, especially housing agencies and authorities, can use this brief as a practical reference for designing or refining rental housing stability programs, drawing on tested approaches from peer cities to benchmark their programs against, identify gaps, and adopt replicable strategies for improving housing stability outcomes for the renters
papers.idea: Cities can adopt peer-tested rental housing stability programs—including cost-mitigation, eviction prevention, and landlord engagement strategies—codeveloped across nine midsize cities and documented in a replicable playbook.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute convened housing practitioners from nine midsize US cities (under 1M population) to codevelop and share strategies for rental housing stability. The brief synthesizes practical, tested approaches and strategic opportunities that local governments can adopt to reduce housing insecurity and rising rent burdens among renters.
papers.method: Multi-city practitioner convening (nine midsize cities) with synthesis of lessons learned; no empirical data collection or randomization described.
papers.scalability_note: Strategies codeveloped across multiple cities and explicitly framed as replicable; directly designed for peer adoption by other local governments.
papers.cost_note: Abstract does not provide cost estimates; housing programs vary widely but often require sustained public funding.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has extensive housing agencies and rental stock; strategies from peer cities likely adaptable, though NYC's scale and cost structure differ from midsize cities studied.

papers.title: A context-driven meta-analysis of job satisfaction in policing: evidence from South Korea
papers.authors: Jae Seong Jang
papers.idea: Improve police officer job satisfaction through targeted interventions identified in cross-national research.
papers.summary: Unable to assess—no abstract provided. Likely a meta-analysis synthesizing job-satisfaction drivers among police officers in South Korea, potentially identifying organizational or management levers.
papers.method: Meta-analysis of job satisfaction research in South Korean policing context; no details on included studies, sample, or outcomes available.
papers.scalability_note: Job-satisfaction interventions are typically organizational and replicable, but context-specific to Korean policing.
papers.cost_note: Likely low-cost HR/management changes, but absent concrete findings, cost unclear.
papers.nyc_note: South Korean police system differs materially from NYPD in structure, labor law, and culture; transferability unproven without actionable findings.

papers.title: AI Is Becoming a Go-To for Data Questions. How Reliable Are the Answers?
papers.authors: Erika Tyagi, Kristin Blagg, Emily Gutierrez
papers.abstract: Tools like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini are quickly becoming a new “front door” to information. For those working to democratize access to high-quality data, this is a real opportunity: Large language models (LLMs) can help people more easily derive insights from public data through tools they’re already using. But how well do LLMs handle questions grounded in public data? To find out, we tested them. We curated 100 questions written from the perspectives of parents, advocates, researchers, data analysts, Capitol Hill staffers, and education or career advisors, across 10 education and workforce topics, to ask the LLMs (GPT-5.2, Claude Sonnet 4.5, and Gemini 3 Flash). Each prompt was sent as a standalone query with no custom system prompt, conversation history, or tool access beyond what each model provides by default, approximating the out-of-the-box experience a typical user would have had in early 2026. From this exercise, we found four significant
papers.idea: Cities could deploy LLMs as a public-facing interface to municipal data to democratize access to information for residents and advocates.
papers.summary: This study tested three large language models (GPT, Claude, Gemini) on 100 real-world questions about education and workforce topics, posed by parents, advocates, researchers, and policymakers, to assess how reliably they answer queries grounded in public data. The abstract cuts off mid-finding but indicates the researchers identified four significant issues with LLM reliability in this context.
papers.method: Experimental testing of three LLMs with 100 curated questions across 10 education/workforce topics, posed as standalone queries without custom prompts or tool access, simulating typical user behavior in early 2026.
papers.scalability_note: LLM deployment is technically straightforward and can reach many users at once; reliability problems may limit impact.
papers.cost_note: Minimal marginal cost once LLM infrastructure is in place; mostly API fees.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could adopt LLMs as a data-query interface for residents, but only if reliability problems documented here are resolved first; education/workforce focus is relevant but abstract is incomplete.

papers.title: Is ride-sharing good for environment? Evidence from combining satellite and survey data on U.S. cities
papers.authors: Yoshifumi Konishi, Akari Ono
papers.idea: No actionable intervention identified; appears to be an empirical assessment of ride-sharing's net environmental impact.
papers.summary: Study combines satellite imagery and survey data to evaluate whether ride-sharing services (Uber/Lyft) improve or worsen urban environmental outcomes in U.S. cities. Without the abstract, the specific findings are unknown, but the title suggests it addresses a policy-relevant question about ride-sharing regulation.
papers.method: Combination of satellite data (likely traffic, congestion, or emissions proxies) and survey responses across multiple U.S. cities; no sample size provided.
papers.scalability_note: Environmental impacts of ride-sharing are city-context-dependent; findings may not generalize uniformly.
papers.cost_note: Ride-sharing regulation is low-cost to implement administratively but has indirect economic effects on consumers and operators.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has substantial ride-sharing use and Taxi & Limousine Commission oversight; findings could inform congestion or emissions policy. However, NYC's transit-rich environment differs from many U.S. cities studied.

papers.title: Glucagon‐like peptide‐1 receptor agonist use and violent crime among US adults
papers.authors: Daniel C. Semenza, Christopher Thomas
papers.abstract: Abstract Glucagon‐like peptide‐1 receptor agonists (GLP‐1 RAs), a class of medications widely prescribed for diabetes and obesity, have exhibited emerging effects on substance use, reward processing, and impulse control. This study examines whether current GLP‐1 RA use moderates established behavioral pathways to violent crime from impulsivity and alcohol use. Data come from a 2025 nationally representative US survey ( n = 7521; 821 lifetime GLP‐1 RA users). Negative binomial regression models with overlap weighting compared current ( n = 597) and former ( n = 224) users on self‐reported violent criminal behavior in the past year. Although impulsivity and alcohol use were strongly associated with violent criminality, these associations were significantly weaker among current GLP‐1 RA users (GLP‐1 RA × impulsivity incidence rate ratio (IRR) = 0.38, p = 0.002; GLP‐1 RA × alcohol use IRR = 0.48, p = 0.023). Robustness checks consistently supported the GLP‐1 RA × impulsivity interaction, whereas less consistent support was found for the alcohol interaction. Results suggest that GLP‐1 RAs may attenuate widely documented behavioral risk mechanisms like impulsivity linked to aggression, pointing to novel biosocial hypotheses regarding pharmacological influences on violent criminality. Future longitudinal and experimental research should confirm these findings and test causal mechanisms.
papers.idea: Expand access to GLP-1 receptor agonist medications (e.g., semaglutide, tirzepatide) through city health programs and criminal justice diversion initiatives, as a complementary intervention to reduce violent crime among high-risk individuals with impulsivity or substance-use issues.
papers.summary: This cross-sectional study of 7,521 US adults found that current GLP-1 RA users showed significantly weaker associations between impulsivity and violent crime (IRR = 0.38) and between alcohol use and violent crime (IRR = 0.48) compared to non-users. The authors suggest GLP-1 RAs may attenuate behavioral risk pathways to violence by affecting reward processing and impulse control.
papers.method: Cross-sectional negative binomial regression analysis using 2025 nationally representative US survey data; 597 current GLP-1 RA users and 224 former users compared on self-reported violent criminal behavior in past year, with overlap weighting for covariate balance.
papers.scalability_note: GLP-1 RAs are expensive (~$1,000–$1,500/month), limiting universal deployment; requires integration into existing health/criminal-justice systems; observational evidence does not yet support population-level rollout.
papers.cost_note: GLP-1 RAs cost $1,000–$1,500/month; NYC would need insurance or subsidy; cost-per-crime-prevented not estimated.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could pilot GLP-1 access via Medicaid/health dept. or criminal-justice reentry programs, but evidence is observational and causal mechanisms unproven; medication cost and medical contraindications limit reach.

papers.title: Every Eval Ever: A Unifying Schema and Community Repository for AI Evaluation Results
papers.authors: Jan Batzner, Sree Harsha Nelaturu, Anastassia Kornilova, Jon Crall, Tommaso Cerruti, Yanan Long, Yifan Mai, Sanchit Ahuja, Asaf Yehudai, Marek Šuppa, John P. Lalor, Oluwagbemike Olowe, Jatin Ganhotra, Brian H. Hu, Eliya Habba, Andrew M. Bean, Chang Liu, Sander Land, Steven Dillmann, Aniketh Garikaparthi, Elron Bandel, Saki Imai, James Edgell, Wm. Matthew Kennedy, Jenny Chim, Patrick Meusling, Asteria Kaeberlein, Venkata Ramachandra Karthik Chundi, Manasi Patwardhan, Martin Ku, Austin Meek, Leon Knauer, Brian Wingenroth, Srishti Yadav, Usman Gohar, Felix Friedrich, Michelle Lin, Jennifer Mickel, Arman Cohan, Stella Biderman, Irene Solaiman, Zeerak Talat, Anka Reuel, Mubashara Akhtar, Gjergji Kasneci, Avijit Ghosh, Leshem Choshen
papers.abstract: AI evaluations are widely used for testing and understanding progress. However, the diverse evaluators bring with them inconsistencies that challenge analysis and comparison. First, results are saved in incompatible formats, scattered across leaderboards, papers, blog posts, evaluation harness logs, and custom repositories. Second, results are created by different evaluation frameworks, which produce divergent scores for nominally identical evaluations and record metadata inconsistently, hindering comparison, cross-community evaluation science, cost reduction, and reuse. We introduce Every Eval Ever, the first shared schema and community-crowdsourced repository for AI evaluation results. The schema standardizes how evaluations are represented in a unified, single JSON document. It is source-agnostic by design, ingesting results from evaluation harnesses and papers alike, and optionally stores per-instance outputs for fine-grained analysis. We contribute: (i) a community-governed metadata schema with a companion instance-level schema, the first standardization effort of its kind; (ii) automatic converters from popular formats, evaluation harnesses, and leaderboards to the unified schema; and (iii) a crowdsourced community database hosted on Hugging Face, currently spanning to date 22,235 models, 2,273 unique benchmarks, and 31 evaluation formats.
papers.idea: Not applicable; this is a methods paper on standardizing AI evaluation metadata schemas, not an intervention.
papers.summary: The paper introduces 'Every Eval Ever,' a unified JSON schema and crowdsourced repository for standardizing how AI evaluation results are recorded and compared across different frameworks, leaderboards, and papers. It addresses fragmentation in how AI model evaluations are stored and reported, providing automatic converters and a community database of 22,235 models and 2,273 benchmarks.
papers.method: Non-empirical; describes the design of a schema and repository architecture, with contributions of converters and a crowdsourced database on Hugging Face.
papers.scalability_note: Software infrastructure scales globally with minimal marginal cost.
papers.cost_note: Infrastructure and curation costs likely minimal once established; hosted on Hugging Face.
papers.nyc_note: This is a computer science infrastructure tool for AI researchers; no direct hook to city services, policy, or resident welfare.

papers.title: Restoring Flexibility to Support Head Start Program Access ACF-2026-0364
papers.authors: Diane Schilder, Justin B. Doromal, Catherine Kuhns
papers.abstract: In this letter, senior fellow Diane Schilder and senior research associates Justin Doromal and Catherine Kuhns offer comments on the US Department of Health and Human Services Administration for Children and Families’ proposed rulemaking on Restoring Flexibility to Support Head Start Program Access ACF-2026-0364. The research team cites research underscoring the importance of retaining workforce provisions in the 2024 Final Rule that are being proposed for recission. Specifically, the authors report the following: The lack of qualified educators able to provide the required services, with inadequate compensation, is a primary reason Head Start programs cite as why they are not fully enrolling children. Investments in the Head Start workforce and strategies to attract and retain a qualified Head Start workforce are needed. Strategies include providing comparable salaries to local preschool teachers, establishing salary scales to reflect qualifications and ensuring salaries cover the basic cost of living do not come at
papers.idea: Cities should increase Head Start teacher compensation to match local preschool wages and establish transparent salary scales tied to qualifications and cost-of-living to improve program enrollment and educator retention.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute comments on proposed federal Head Start rule changes, finding that inadequate educator compensation is a primary barrier preventing Head Start programs from fully enrolling eligible children. The authors argue that retaining workforce provisions in the 2024 Final Rule and implementing comparable salary strategies are critical to attracting and retaining qualified educators.
papers.method: Non-empirical: comment letter citing existing research on Head Start workforce challenges and compensation gaps, submitted to HHS Administration for Children and Families rulemaking process.
papers.scalability_note: Head Start is a federal program; NYC could advocate for/implement city-level wage supplements or co-investments in educator compensation across all participating programs.
papers.cost_note: Requires sustained budget allocation for salary increases; cost-effectiveness depends on enrollment gains and long-term child outcomes, not stated in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC operates Head Start programs and could pressure federal rulemakers or directly supplement wages via city funds; aligns with municipal early childhood expansion goals.

papers.title: Alternatives to Free Tuition Programs Can Help with Living Expenses
papers.authors: Jason Cohn, Shana Metcalf
papers.abstract: State college promise programs, in which states offer free tuition to qualified students, have become increasingly common, but program design varies significantly. This brief examines how the structure of free college programs affects affordability for low-income students and models an alternative approach: a living stipend tied to financial need. We compare the effects of two tuition-focused programs—first-dollar and last-dollar programs—and three living stipend designs on students’ net cost of attendance. Why This Matters Living expenses are major costs that can make college unaffordable for many students, even if their tuition is low or fully covered. Focusing on making tuition less expensive is an important step in making college accessible and affordable, but college affordability efforts should also consider living expenses. First-dollar designs indirectly provide aid to many students for living expenses. But policymakers could address affordability challenges related to living expenses in other ways. Key Takeaways We estimate and compare
papers.idea: Cities could supplement state tuition-free college programs with living stipends tied to financial need, to reduce dropout risk and improve completion rates among low-income students.
papers.summary: The brief models the comparative effects of first-dollar vs. last-dollar tuition programs and three living-stipend designs on net cost of attendance for low-income students. It argues that tuition coverage alone leaves living expenses as a major affordability barrier, and that stipend-based designs can more directly address this gap.
papers.method: Policy modeling and cost-comparison analysis across program structures; no empirical trial or quasi-experimental evaluation reported in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: City could integrate with CUNY/SUNY tuition-free frameworks via existing aid infrastructure; replicable in other metros with public universities.
papers.cost_note: Depends on stipend amount and enrollment; abstract provides no cost estimates or per-student figures.
papers.nyc_note: NYC funds CUNY Accelerated Study in Associate Programs and could layer living stipends via existing financial-aid offices; aligns with city workforce-development priorities.

papers.title: Transportation infrastructure and college admissions quality: Evidence from China's National College Entrance Examination
papers.authors: Tianyu Wang, Lei Li, You Lu
papers.idea: Improve transit access to schools and exam centers to reduce barriers to college-entrance exam participation and performance, potentially widening access to higher education.
papers.summary: This study examines the relationship between transportation infrastructure and college entrance exam performance in China, likely finding that students with better transit access to schools or exam sites achieve better outcomes. The research suggests transit quality is a lever affecting educational opportunity.
papers.method: Cross-sectional or panel analysis of Chinese college entrance exam (gaokao) results linked to transportation infrastructure data at regional or municipal level.
papers.scalability_note: Transit-to-school improvements are standard policy tools; findings would apply across cities.
papers.cost_note: Depends on transit investment scale; typically moderate marginal cost if piggybacking on existing transit expansion.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has extensive transit; study uses Chinese context (different exam system, geography). Relevance requires adaptation to SAT/Regents access disparities.

papers.title: College and Career Access for Young Parents
papers.authors: Kate Westaby, Rachel Johnson, Kimberly Salazar, Stephanie Gramann, Jessie Loeb, M. Muska Nataliansyah
papers.abstract: Young parents—those who have their first child before age 25—face steep and preventable barriers to finishing high school, accessing higher education, and securing family-sustaining careers. Only 1 in 10 young parents enrolls in college within a few years of high school, compared with almost half of their peers. Approximately 30 percent of young parents are disconnected from both school and work, compared with just 11 percent of their peers. This report synthesizes insights from a national literature scan, an advisory board of practitioners and young parents, and focus groups with current young parents to identify actionable strategies K–12 institutions can use to better support young parents' pathways to college and family-sustaining careers. Why This Matters Young parents are an often-overlooked population whose outcomes shape not only their own futures but those of the children they are raising. Despite strong motivation to pursue education and career pathways, young parents face structural
papers.idea: K–12 schools adopt structural supports (childcare, flexible scheduling, mentoring, career pathways) to enable young parents to complete high school and access college/career training.
papers.summary: Young parents (first child before age 25) enroll in college at one-tenth the rate of peers and face 3× the disconnection from school/work. The report synthesizes literature review, practitioner advisory input, and focus groups with young parents to identify actionable K–12 institutional strategies for improving educational and career outcomes.
papers.method: National literature scan, practitioner and young-parent advisory board input, and focus groups with current young parents; no experimental design or new empirical data collection reported.
papers.scalability_note: Institutional supports (childcare, scheduling) are replicable across districts; policy levers exist in most school systems.
papers.cost_note: On-site childcare and flexible programs have meaningful implementation costs; abstract does not specify per-unit investment.
papers.nyc_note: NYC Department of Education operates large network of high schools and community colleges; existing programs could integrate these supports; substantial young-parent population.

papers.title: How Governments Can Help Small and Medium-Sized Businesses Adopt Apprenticeship Programs
papers.authors: Andrew Campbell
papers.abstract: Registered Apprenticeships are a proven solution for helping employers meet their talent needs and workers gain the skills to access high-quality career pathways. Yet only a small fraction of employers and workers participate in the apprenticeship system. Further, research shows that small and medium-sized businesses (those with fewer than 100 employees, or between 100 and 499 employees, respectively) both experience the greatest return on investment from Registered Apprenticeship programs and face the greatest financial and administrative barriers to participating in the Registered Apprenticeship system. Why This Matters SMBs employ nearly half of the US workforce and generate more than 40 percent of GDP, yet they often lack the staffing, financial resources, and administrative capacity needed to sponsor Registered Apprenticeship programs on their own. Without targeted support to SMBs, the US apprenticeship system risks excluding many SMBs from sharing in the benefits of the apprenticeship model and system—including stronger talent pipelines
papers.idea: Cities can create targeted subsidy, administrative support, and capacity-building programs to help small and medium-sized businesses launch and sustain Registered Apprenticeship programs, expanding workforce pathways for residents.
papers.summary: Registered Apprenticeships effectively build workforce skills and meet employer talent needs, but SMBs (under 500 employees)—which employ nearly half the US workforce—face disproportionate financial and administrative barriers to participation. The paper identifies SMBs as both highest-ROI users and most-excluded participants in the apprenticeship system, arguing that without targeted support, the system risks excluding a major employment sector.
papers.method: Literature review and descriptive analysis of barriers faced by small and medium-sized businesses in accessing Registered Apprenticeship systems; no original empirical study described.
papers.scalability_note: Apprenticeship support programs are modular and have been piloted in multiple jurisdictions; replicable across cities.
papers.cost_note: Depends on subsidy model; administrative support and regulatory streamlining are relatively low-cost; wage subsidies higher.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing apprenticeship infrastructure (NYATEP, DOE programs); could extend support explicitly to SMBs; aligns with workforce-development priorities.

papers.title: Setting the Stage for Scaling Apprenticeship Degrees
papers.authors: Deborah Kobes, John Marotta, Julia Payne
papers.abstract: Apprenticeship degree programs possess all the hallmarks of any Registered Apprenticeship: they are industry-led and offer paid work experience with wage progression, on-the-job learning, and supplemental education. What sets them apart is that apprentices earn not only a nationally recognized journeyworker credential but also an associate’s, bachelor's, or master's degree. This brief explores the definitions of apprenticeship degrees, their current state of practice, research from the US and abroad, and policies that facilitate the adoption of apprenticeship degrees. We recommend coordinated action across practice, policy, and research to drive the expansion of high-quality apprenticeship degrees. Why This Matters Apprenticeship degrees are a relatively recent innovation with the potential to benefit apprentices and employers alike—as well as harmonize the workforce and postsecondary systems. Both Registered Apprenticeships and postsecondary degrees help individuals build skills, increase earnings, and advance their careers, but the two systems have historically operated in isolation from each other
papers.idea: NYC could partner with CUNY and industry employers to expand dual Registered Apprenticeship–degree programs that combine paid work experience with associate's or bachelor's credentials, particularly in high-demand sectors.
papers.summary: The brief identifies apprenticeship degree programs—which combine industry-led paid apprenticeships with postsecondary degree credentials—as a relatively recent innovation that could benefit both workers and employers by bridging historically isolated workforce and postsecondary systems. It reviews current practice, research evidence, and policies facilitating adoption of such programs.
papers.method: Non-empirical policy brief synthesizing definitions, current state of practice, domestic and international research findings, and policy recommendations; no primary data collection or experiment design stated.
papers.scalability_note: Program model is standardized and replicable; requires coordination across education and workforce agencies but no novel technology.
papers.cost_note: Leverages existing Registered Apprenticeship infrastructure and employer funding; cost-sharing between government and private sector; no per-unit cost provided in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has large CUNY system, strong union apprenticeship tradition, and multiple high-demand sectors (construction, healthcare, technology); requires coordination with CUNY and NYC Department of Small Business Services.

papers.title: How Researchers Can Help Advance Understanding of Apprenticeship Degrees
papers.authors: Julia Payne, Deborah Kobes, John Marotta
papers.abstract: Apprenticeship degree programs possess all the hallmarks of any Registered Apprenticeship: they are industry-led and offer paid work experience with wage progression, on-the-job learning, and supplemental education. What sets them apart is that apprentices earn not only a nationally recognized journeyworker credential but also an associate’s, bachelor's, or master's degree. This fact sheet outlines which areas of research could help advance the apprenticeship degree field. We developed this agenda through extensive stakeholder consultation and refined it through a consensus-driven engagement process.
papers.idea: Cities could expand registered apprenticeship degree programs that combine paid work experience with associate's, bachelor's, or master's credentials to improve economic mobility for residents.
papers.summary: This fact sheet from the Urban Institute identifies research gaps and priorities for advancing apprenticeship degree programs—hybrids that combine industry-led, paid apprenticeships with traditional degree credentials. The authors developed a research agenda through stakeholder consultation to clarify what evidence would help scale and improve these programs.
papers.method: Non-empirical consensus-driven agenda-setting via stakeholder consultation and refinement process; no primary data collection or causal analysis.
papers.scalability_note: Apprenticeship models are transportable across sectors and geographies; NYC already has registered apprenticeship infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Apprenticeships leverage employer funding and wages; degree costs vary by institution but offset by paid work.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has CUNY, community colleges, and industry partnerships (construction, healthcare, tech) suited to hybrid apprenticeship-degree models.

papers.title: How Policymakers Can Scale Apprenticeship Degrees
papers.authors: Deborah Kobes, John Marotta, Julia Payne
papers.abstract: Apprenticeship degree programs possess all the hallmarks of any Registered Apprenticeship: they are industry-led and offer paid work experience with wage progression, on-the-job learning, and supplemental education. What sets them apart is that apprentices earn not only a nationally recognized journeyworker credential but also an associate’s, bachelor's, or master's degree. This fact sheet outlines how state and federal policymakers can scale high-quality apprenticeship degrees across the country. We developed this agenda through extensive stakeholder consultation and refined it through a consensus-driven engagement process.
papers.idea: Cities can partner with industry and education providers to scale registered apprenticeship degree programs that combine paid work experience with associate's, bachelor's, or master's credentials.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute fact sheet outlines how state and federal policymakers can expand apprenticeship degree programs—which blend industry-led, paid apprenticeships with formal degree attainment. The authors developed a policy agenda through stakeholder consultation and consensus-building to identify levers for scaling these programs nationally.
papers.method: Policy review and consensus-driven stakeholder engagement process; no empirical data or experimental design reported in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Apprenticeship models are already operating; scaling requires coordination across multiple actors but no novel technology or infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Apprenticeships are employer-funded and wage-generating (low public cost per participant); cost-effectiveness depends on degree completion and job placement rates not stated in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has apprenticeship programs (CUNY, union partnerships, NYSERDA); adding degree pathways via existing agencies (CUNY, DCWP, DOE) is feasible.

papers.title: The complexity of neighborhood effects: A developmental and ecological framework for violent victimization
papers.authors: Myunghee You, Brian J. Stults
papers.abstract: Abstract Extensive research has established a link between low self‐control and child victimization. However, the specific neighborhood conditions under which low self‐control most strongly influences victimization have been little examined, and, more importantly, no previous studies have investigated the complex ways in which neighborhood context interacts with self‐control and other individual characteristics to impact victimization. Drawing on data from the Project on Human Development in Chicago Neighborhoods, the current study tests whether the effect of low self‐control on victimization is lessened in neighborhoods with high collective efficacy and further examines the multifaceted nature of neighborhood effects that interact with individual characteristics—particularly age and poverty. Employing three‐level multivariate Rasch models and cross‐level three‐way interactions, the findings reveal that low self‐control increases victimization risk, and that the effect of low self‐control is contingent on the combined effect between neighborhood collective efficacy of children, age, and poverty. The study concludes that child victimization is a product of the intricate interplay between neighborhood context—especially collective efficacy—and individual factors such as self‐control, age, and socioeconomic conditions. This research contributes importantly to building a developmental and life‐course perspective on victimization, by highlighting the intricate nature of neighborhood effects.
papers.idea: Build neighborhood collective efficacy as a protective factor against child victimization, particularly targeting low-efficacy areas with high poverty and young populations.
papers.summary: The study found that low self-control increases victimization risk in children, but that this effect is significantly reduced in neighborhoods with high collective efficacy. The relationship is complex and varies by age, poverty level, and the combined neighborhood context.
papers.method: Longitudinal analysis using data from the Project on Human Development in Chicago Neighborhoods, employing three-level multivariate Rasch models and cross-level three-way interactions to examine interactions between individual and neighborhood factors.
papers.scalability_note: Collective efficacy is difficult to engineer through policy; causality runs unclear.
papers.cost_note: Increasing collective efficacy would require sustained community-building efforts with uncertain ROI.
papers.nyc_note: Study is Chicago-based; findings are neighborhood-level patterns, not tested interventions that NYC could readily deploy.

papers.title: The company you keep: The influence of popular delinquents and deviant brokers on offending trajectories
papers.authors: Daniel Trovato
papers.abstract: Abstract Research on how delinquent peer associations affect individuals’ life courses is limited. This paper addresses this gap by examining delinquent peer network characteristics and their impact on offending trajectories through social network analysis (SNA) and group‐based trajectory modeling (GBTM). SNA helps minimize same‐source bias and offers a detailed view of delinquent peer networks, whereas GBTM identifies developmental pathways. Using data from the National Longitudinal Study of Adolescent to Adult Health (Add Health), this study explores how the popularity and betweenness centrality of delinquent peers affect an individual's placement in offending trajectories. Four trajectories were identified: non‐offending, adolescent‐limited (AL), low‐offending‐desister, and high‐offending‐desister. The study finds that delinquent friend popularity increases the likelihood of placement in AL and high‐offending‐desister trajectories, whereas delinquent peer betweenness centrality is also associated with increased placement in the low‐offending‐desister and high‐offending‐desister trajectories. These findings enhance theoretical understanding by showing how the unique positionality of delinquent peers influences offending behavior over time. The results suggest that public policy should focus on understanding adolescent friendships and providing appropriate restorative resources.
papers.idea: Target youth intervention and mentorship programs toward individuals whose delinquent peers occupy high-status or bridge positions in social networks.
papers.summary: The study used social network analysis and trajectory modeling on Add Health data to show that having delinquent friends who are popular or occupy central broker positions in peer networks increases adolescents' likelihood of following persistent offending pathways rather than desisting. The findings suggest peer network structure, not just peer delinquency itself, shapes individual offending over time.
papers.method: Observational longitudinal study using Add Health dataset; social network analysis combined with group-based trajectory modeling to classify offending patterns and examine how peer network characteristics (popularity, betweenness centrality) predict trajectory membership.
papers.scalability_note: Requires detailed peer network data collection and real-time social mapping; difficult to implement at scale without intrusive surveillance or reporting systems.
papers.cost_note: Targeting requires upfront social network mapping; intervention costs depend on program type (mentorship, restorative justice, etc.), not specified.
papers.nyc_note: Schools could map peer networks, but doing so ethically and sustainably poses privacy and operational challenges; compatible in theory with restorative justice programs already piloted in NYC schools.

papers.title: PROTOCOL: Health Visiting Interventions With 0–5 Year Olds and Their Families: An Evidence and Gap Map
papers.authors:
Michael Fanner, 
Jane Barlow, 
Sarah Cowley, 
Karen Whittaker, 
Fiona Campbell, 
Anita Schrader McMillan, 
Mary Malone, 
Helen Critcher
papers.abstract: Campbell Systematic Reviews, Volume 21, Issue 4, December 2025.
papers.idea: Cities can expand or optimize health visiting programs (nurse/midwife home visits) for families with young children to improve child development, parental wellbeing, and health outcomes.
papers.summary: This is a Campbell Collaboration evidence and gap map protocol that will systematically review and synthesize evidence on health visiting interventions (typically nurse or health worker home visits) for families with children aged 0–5 years. The map will identify what works, for whom, and where evidence gaps exist, covering outcomes such as child health, development, parental mental health, and family functioning.
papers.method: Protocol for a systematic evidence and gap map (not yet completed); will synthesize existing trials and observational studies of health visiting programs targeting 0–5 year-olds and their families.
papers.scalability_note: Health visiting is a proven, replicable model already embedded in many health systems; can be adapted and scaled across municipalities.
papers.cost_note: Home visiting is labor-intensive but typically cost-effective relative to downstream benefits; exact per-family cost depends on program model and frequency.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing health department infrastructure and community health worker networks; could integrate evidence-based visiting protocols into maternal/child health services.

papers.title: Social Embeddedness, Multiplexity, and Criminal Collaboration Within the Sinaloa Cartel
papers.authors: Francesco Calderoni, Valentin Pereda, David Décary-Hétu
papers.abstract: Objectives This study examines how social embeddedness and multiplex social ties shape criminal collaboration in the Sinaloa Cartel. It investigates how different types of relationships—such as kinship, friendship, meetings, and compadre ties—influence participation in drug crimes and broader forms of collaboration. Methods Drawing on trial transcripts from Joaquín “El Chapo” Guzmán Loera's U.S. federal case, we reconstructed social networks among 188 actors. Using social network analysis and Logistic Regression Quadratic Assignment Procedure (LRQAP) models, we assessed the conditional associations of different social ties with drug crimes and criminal collaboration, controlling for demographic and organizational characteristics. Results Friendship and meeting ties exhibited the strongest associations with drug crimes and criminal collaboration. Familial ties were initially significant but lost their effect once shared cartel affiliation was considered, suggesting a mediating role of organizational membership. Compadre and prison ties showed no significant associations. Conclusions Trust-based relationships such as friendships and meetings play a pivotal role in fostering collaboration within organized crime groups. Familial ties may exert indirect effects through shared cartel affiliation. The findings underscore the importance of distinguishing between types of social ties and demonstrate the value of trial transcripts as a source for advancing empirical research on organized crime.
papers.source: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.venue: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.idea: Use social network analysis of criminal trial transcripts to identify trust-based relationship patterns (friendships, meetings) as leverage points for disrupting organized crime collaboration.
papers.summary: This study analyzed 188 actors from the Sinaloa Cartel using trial transcripts to understand how different types of social ties—kinship, friendship, meetings, compadre, prison—correlate with drug crimes and criminal collaboration. Friendship and meeting ties showed the strongest associations with criminal participation, while familial ties were mediated by shared cartel affiliation; compadre and prison ties were not significant.
papers.method: Social network analysis and Logistic Regression Quadratic Assignment Procedure (LRQAP) models applied to reconstructed networks from trial transcripts of the El Chapo federal case (N=188 actors).
papers.scalability_note: Relies on trial transcript data from major cases; not a generalizable intervention or practice.
papers.cost_note: Research-stage only; no operational cost model for a city program.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD/prosecutors could mine trial data similarly, but study offers no concrete tactical or policy intervention for local implementation.

papers.title: Demographic Divergence: The Legacy of the Opioid Epidemic
papers.authors: Carolina Arteaga, Victoria Barone, Stephen Claassen
papers.abstract: We study how the opioid epidemic shaped local population dynamics in the United States. Exploiting variation in exposure that stems from Purdue Pharma's targeted marketing of OxyContin to high-cancer-mortality areas, we find that more exposed commuting zones experienced lower population growth. By 2020, a one-standard-deviation increase in exposure reduced population growth among individuals aged 18 to 64 by 2.4 percentage points. Direct mortality from drug-induced deaths made only a limited contribution to these changes. Instead, population losses were primarily driven by migratory responses: exposure increased out-migration rates, especially among college-educated individuals. These responses are consistent with the opioid epidemic operating as a disamenity shock, deteriorating local quality of life. Working in the opposite direction, we also document a rise in fertility rates that, by 2020, partially counteracts these population losses. Our findings show that the opioid epidemic reshaped local demographic composition and contributed to the long-run divergence in population dynamics across U.S. commuting zones.
papers.idea: No actionable intervention identified; paper is diagnostic analysis of opioid epidemic's demographic consequences.
papers.summary: The study documents how exposure to Purdue Pharma's OxyContin marketing (instrumented by high-cancer-mortality areas) reduced population growth in exposed commuting zones by 2020, primarily through out-migration of college-educated individuals rather than direct mortality. It finds that opioid exposure operated as a local disamenity shock, though fertility increases partially offset population losses.
papers.method: Quasi-experimental design leveraging geographic variation in pharmaceutical marketing exposure as instrument for opioid epidemic impact; long-run analysis of U.S. commuting zones through 2020.
papers.scalability_note: Describes existing outcome patterns; no intervention to scale.
papers.cost_note: N/A — diagnostic study with no program implementation.
papers.nyc_note: Identifies legacy demographic shifts but offers no policy lever or programmatic intervention for NYC to adopt.

papers.title: Communities Across the Nation Want to Add Housing. Which Metropolitan Areas Are Adding the Most?
papers.authors: Yonah Freemark
papers.abstract: During the 2010s, the United States constructed fewer new housing units than in any decade since the 1970s, despite the country’s population growing by more than 50 percent. This lack of construction is partly contributing to the nation’s rising housing costs.
