Faced with these failures, many cities and states have sought to encourage building more homes by reforming their land-use policies. Thus far in the 2020s, those changes, combined with the broader economic environment, have resulted in a higher rate of construction. But not every city has grown similarly. In new analysis, I find that large metropolitan areas in Texas have accounted for a vastly disproportionate share of new housing since 2000. Each of Texas’s four main metropolitan areas has increased their housing supply by more than 12 percent through 2025. That growth has been largely concentrated in exurban areas far from existing neighborhoods because of the availability of low-cost
papers.idea: Study which land-use policy reforms have proven most effective at scaling housing production, then adopt high-performing reforms in NYC.
papers.summary: The US severely underbuilt housing in the 2010s despite population growth, contributing to rising costs. Texas metros have dramatically outpaced other regions in housing construction (>12% growth since 2000) through land-use policy reforms and development in lower-cost exurban areas. The analysis identifies which metropolitan areas are successfully adding housing at scale in the 2020s.
papers.method: Comparative analysis of housing construction rates across US metropolitan areas from 2000-2025, examining policy changes and geographic patterns of development.
papers.scalability_note: Housing policy reforms are readily transferable across cities, though implementation details vary.
papers.cost_note: Cost depends on specific reforms adopted (zoning changes are cheap; infrastructure investment expensive); study doesn't quantify per-unit costs.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has different constraints (density, geography, existing built fabric) than Texas exurbs; zoning reforms are applicable but results may differ significantly.

papers.title: Narratives That Limit the Possible: Interrupting Narrative Closure in Computing Practice
papers.authors: Samuel Mann, Ruth Myers, Dave Guruge, Lucky Hawkins, Kylie McKee, Rex Alexander, Jamie Vaughan, Tim Lynch, Danny Fridberg
papers.abstract: Computing's dominant concepts - innovation, efficiency, resilience, professionalism - often migrate from reflective ideals to instruments that limit behaviour, redirect responsibility, and foreclose critique. We call this drift weaponisation: the discursive repurposing of professional concepts so that they stabilise business-as-usual while making structural alternatives appear unreasonable, illegible, or out of scope. Using collective autoethnography across education, justice, public administration, research management, and computing, we identify recurring mechanisms (simplification, individualisation, binary framing, metric substitution, hero/resilience scripts, organised ignorance). From this synthesis we propose Reframing - peaceful, practice-ready shifts (e.g., From Simplified Slogans to Structural Literacy; From Performative Compliance to Meaningful Outcomes; From Coping to Justice) - each paired with a 'do-now' prompt. The levers restore complexity, surface assumptions, and redirect attention to structural conditions without requiring formal authority. We contribute: (1) a cross-field account of weaponisation as a patterned phenomenon; (2) a portable reframing toolkit grounded in narrative and systems thinking; and (3) implications for computing within limits, including day-to-day practice shifts toward care, sufficiency, and justice.
papers.idea: Reframe how city agencies communicate about public safety, justice, and resilience to surface structural conditions rather than reinforce status-quo narratives.
papers.summary: This paper identifies how professional concepts like 'innovation,' 'efficiency,' and 'resilience' migrate from reflective ideals to discourse that forecloses critique and legitimizes structural inaction. The authors propose a 'reframing toolkit' using narrative and systems thinking to interrupt this pattern across education, justice, and public administration.
papers.method: Collective autoethnography across five fields (education, justice, public administration, research management, computing); authors synthesize recurring mechanistic patterns and propose reframing prompts without formal empirical testing.
papers.scalability_note: Toolkit is portable but depends on adoption by individual practitioners; no implementation pathway or evidence of uptake provided.
papers.cost_note: Primarily training/reframing labor; minimal direct cost if embedded in existing staff practice.
papers.nyc_note: Relevant conceptually to justice and city services sectors, but abstract offers no concrete policy, program, or operational lever; no pilot data or implementation case study shown.

papers.title: Improving Capstone Team Outcomes through Dynamic Skill Matching and Preference Alignment
papers.authors: Brandon Pardi, Garret Castro, Michael Pisman, Avash Adhikari, Santosh Chandrasekhar
papers.abstract: Team-based projects are a cornerstone of engineering and computing courses, but unstructured team formation often leads to poor project outcomes due to misaligned student interests and inadequate skill coverage. This paper introduces a novel, three-stage methodology for creating effective student teams by integrating student preferences with project skill requirements. In the first stage, students complete a survey to report their project interests and self-assessed skills. Next, a Large Language Model (LLM) analyzes project descriptions to extract the necessary skills for each project's success. Finally, a dynamic assignment algorithm matches students to projects, simultaneously maximizing skill coverage and preference alignment. The algorithm iteratively prioritizes projects with unfulfilled skill needs to optimize team balance. Preliminary evaluations show our approach produces teams with higher skill coverage and better preference satisfaction compared to random or manual assignment approaches. Our approach also overcomes limitations of widely-used tools like CATME Team-Maker, which do not explicitly account for project skill fulfillment. Our findings point toward an effective and customizable strategy for improving student motivation and learning outcomes in project-based courses.
papers.idea: Use LLM-based skill-matching algorithms to assign students to capstone team projects in engineering and computing courses.
papers.summary: The paper presents a three-stage methodology that combines student surveys, LLM-extracted project skill requirements, and a dynamic matching algorithm to form student teams. Preliminary evaluations show the approach produces higher skill coverage and preference satisfaction than random or manual assignment, and outperforms the CATME Team-Maker tool.
papers.method: Descriptive/methodological paper introducing an algorithm; preliminary evaluation comparing assignment outcomes across methods (sample size and empirical details not stated in abstract).
papers.scalability_note: Algorithm is automatable and could be adopted across universities; requires minimal infrastructure beyond LLM access.
papers.cost_note: Minimal marginal cost once LLM infrastructure exists; primarily development and deployment overhead.
papers.nyc_note: Applicable only to university capstone programs within CUNY/NYU; no direct lever for city agencies or public safety, housing, transit, or other civic-policy domains.

papers.title: Local Violence as an Environmental Exposure with Increasing Relevance under Climate Change: A Conceptual Framework
papers.authors: Jane E. Clougherty, Ellen J. Kinnee, Perry E. Sheffield
papers.abstract: Abstract Violent crime is increasing worldwide with climate change and ambient heat, with greater increases projected in communities already high in crime or lacking resources for mitigation, exacerbating inequities in exposures and health. Our work has shown that violent crime—as a severe psychosocial stressor—can exacerbate effects of climate-related heat and pollution on health. In addition, as heat is associated with greater violence, and both heat and violence are shown to negatively influence child mental and physical health, some proportion of heat impacts on health may plausibly be mediated through local violence. Taken together, there is a need for frameworks and methods to disentangle the independent and synergistic effects of climate-related violence, heat, and pollution on child health, and to translate this understanding into clinical and public health action to protect patients and families. Here, we propose a framework for examining violence as a climate-related environmental exposure, and discuss (1) conceptualization of violence as an environmental variable, (2) impacts of violent crime on perceived stress, (3) measuring local violence for epidemiology, (4) pathways for impacts of local violence on health in the context of climate change, in combination with other climate-related exposures, and (5) implications for policy and practice.
papers.idea: Integrate violence-heat-health data to identify neighborhoods where combined climate and crime stressors warrant coordinated heat-relief and violence-prevention interventions.
papers.summary: The paper proposes a conceptual framework treating violent crime as a climate-related environmental exposure, arguing that heat, violence, and pollution interact to harm child health—with violence plausibly mediating some health effects of heat. The authors call for frameworks and methods to disentangle these synergistic effects and translate findings into public health action.
papers.method: Conceptual framework paper synthesizing prior work; no new empirical study or intervention described.
papers.scalability_note: Framework is general but requires substantial operationalization and local data infrastructure before any intervention could be tested or scaled.
papers.cost_note: Likely moderate; would require integrated surveillance and coordination across heat-response and public-safety agencies, but no specific costs stated.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has heat and crime data and agencies (NYPD, DoH, Parks) that could theoretically collaborate; however, the paper offers no concrete program or policy, only a conceptual scaffold.

papers.title: Madeline Brown on Trump account enrollment systems
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: Since the program began last summer, 6 million children have been enrolled in Trump accounts. But enrollment isn’t automatic, meaning millions of eligible children may miss out on this wealth-building opportunity. “If a program like this is really going to be as transformative as it has the potential to be, we have to focus on who already is not taking advantage of [early life wealth-building] programs,” said Madeline Brown, senior policy associate at the Urban Institute, in an interview with MarketWatch. “What we set up is an enrollment strategy system where they are still getting lost.” For inquiries, please email media@urban.org.
papers.idea: Redesign enrollment systems for child savings/wealth-building accounts to reduce friction and automatically enroll eligible children, increasing uptake among disadvantaged populations.
papers.summary: Since launching last summer, a Trump-account program enrolled 6 million children, but enrollment is not automatic, leaving millions of eligible children unenrolled. Urban Institute researcher Madeline Brown identifies enrollment systems as a barrier, highlighting that enrollment-strategy design is causing eligible families to be lost from the program.
papers.method: Interview-based commentary by a policy researcher at the Urban Institute; no original empirical study described.
papers.scalability_note: Enrollment-system design is easily replicable across jurisdictions; automatic enrollment is a proven, scalable mechanism.
papers.cost_note: Enrollment redesign is a low-cost intervention; leverages existing administrative infrastructure.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could pilot automatic enrollment in savings programs for eligible children; applies to any city-administered or city-aligned child wealth program.

papers.title: Letter in Response to the Department of Education’s Request for Comment on the Proposed Rule ED-2026-OPE-0100, Accountability in Higher Education and Access through Demand-driven Workforce Pell
papers.authors: David Pitts
papers.abstract: In a public comment to the US Department of Education, David Pitts, vice president of Urban's Justice and Safety Division and interim vice president of the Work, Education, and Labor Division, responds to a proposed rule that would establish a single earnings-based accountability framework covering nearly every Title IV program. The comment focuses on how the framework would affect Prison Education Programs (PEPs) and the broader population of students with histories of incarceration. It identifies three concerns: IRS earnings data systematically undercapture the earnings of formerly incarcerated workers, the proposed exclusion for PEP students does not reach those who finish credentials at a main campus after release from prison, and community colleges, HBCUs, and certificate programs serving justice-impacted students face compounded vulnerability under the rule. The comment offers targeted recommendations the Department can implement through preamble language and existing discretionary authority.
papers.idea: Cities could advocate for federal accountability rules that better capture earnings of formerly incarcerated workers and protect prison education programs from penalization under earnings-based metrics.
papers.summary: This is a public comment letter responding to a proposed federal Department of Education rule on accountability for Title IV programs. It identifies three concerns specific to Prison Education Programs and justice-impacted students: IRS earnings data undercaptures formerly incarcerated workers' income, the proposed exclusion fails to cover those completing credentials post-release, and community colleges and HBCUs serving this population face compounded vulnerability.
papers.method: Public comment letter analyzing a proposed federal regulation and offering recommendations; non-empirical advocacy document.
papers.scalability_note: Primarily a federal policy advocacy document; city-level leverage is indirect and limited to lobbying or coordinating with state/federal actors.
papers.cost_note: If implemented, involves regulatory clarification only; no direct program costs implied.
papers.nyc_note: NYC cannot unilaterally change federal education accountability rules; relevance is only through advocacy, lobbying, or alignment with state-level action on prison education.

papers.title: The Value of the FHLBank System's Housing and Community Mission-Oriented Programs
papers.authors: Jung Hyun Choi, Laurie Goodman, Daniel Pang, Kathryn Reynolds, Jun Zhu
papers.abstract: This report provides an in-depth look at the FHLBank System’s targeted mission activities—the Affordable Housing Program (AHP), the Community Investment Program (CIP), the Community Investment Cash Advance (CICA) program, the Acquired Member Asset (AMA) program, and voluntary initiatives—that support affordable housing and community development. The study documents activities and trends and introduces a framework to estimate their economic value. Our analysis illustrates how FHLBank mission programs generate economic ripple effects. Under base-case assumptions, mission-oriented programs generated an estimated $47.1 billion in economic impact between 2015 and 2024, with impact estimates ranging from $16.5 billion to $94.8 billion under conservative and high-end scenarios, respectively. Why This Matters The FHLBank System has a broad mission to provide reliable liquidity and to support housing and community development. Policymakers and stakeholders are increasingly interested in how FHLBank programs advance housing affordability, address local community needs, and fulfill the system’s public purpose. What We Found
papers.idea: NYC could advocate for or partner with FHLBank system programs (AHP, CIP, CICA) to increase capital flows into affordable housing development and preservation in the city.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute quantified the economic impact of Federal Home Loan Bank System mission programs supporting affordable housing and community development from 2015–2024, estimating $47.1 billion in total economic impact (range: $16.5–$94.8 billion). The study introduces a framework for measuring how these programs generate economic ripple effects across housing and community development sectors.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis documenting FHLBank mission program activities and trends from 2015–2024, with economic impact modeling under base-case, conservative, and high-end scenarios.
papers.scalability_note: FHLBank system is national; NYC could tap existing infrastructure but scale depends on member bank participation and policy changes.
papers.cost_note: No per-unit cost stated; programs leverage existing federal infrastructure and member bank capital, suggesting lower marginal cost to city.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has many banks in FHLBank system; city could lobby for prioritization of NYC affordable housing in AHP/CIP allocations, but direct city control is limited.

papers.summary: Unable to assess. The abstract is not provided. Title suggests the study examines correlations between main street/high street closures, social disorder, and urban change, but without the abstract it is impossible to determine what findings, if any, support a specific policy lever.

papers.title: Understanding Scam Trends and Rail Paths from Reddit Self-Disclosure Narratives
papers.authors: Yangjun Zhang, Mirko Bottarelli, Mark Hooper, Carsten Maple
papers.abstract: Online scam behavior is inherently multi-stage, and the lifecycle includes temporally ordered rails and events rather than isolated signals. Existing works analyze characteristics of scam types and rails, but they do not track scam trends across years. Moreover, the work on the relations between rails is hampered due to the lack of open-source datasets with annotations and coverage of different scam types. To address these gaps, we build a dataset to analyze the yearly trend of scam characteristics and rail paths using Reddit self-disclosure narratives from 2023 to 2025. We collect 21,304 posts from scam-related subreddits with at least one rail among identity, communication, platform, and payment for trend analysis by heuristic annotation. Then, we label 1,800 posts containing explicit or recoverable scam chains by an LLM-assisted method for scam path analysis. The method is evaluated with human annotation. Lastly, we run a topic model on the comments of the posts to analyze the community support behavior. The results reveal that scam processes are predominantly multi-rail. Across years, different scam types and rail components dominate. Different scam types vary systematically in path complexity. Reddit support behaviors have become more detailed over time. This work supports synthetic scam chain data simulation and AI-related scam risk assessment, though findings may not generalise to other platforms.
papers.idea: Use Reddit scam narratives and LLM-assisted analysis to identify emerging fraud patterns and alert consumer-protection agencies and residents to new scam tactics.
papers.summary: The study analyzed 21,304 Reddit posts from 2023–2025 to map scam trajectories across identity, communication, platform, and payment stages. Results show that scams predominantly involve multiple interconnected stages (rails), with different scam types and dominant components varying year-to-year, and community support discussions becoming more detailed over time.
papers.method: Analysis of Reddit self-disclosure narratives using heuristic annotation for trend analysis (21,304 posts) and LLM-assisted labeling of scam chains (1,800 posts), with human validation and topic modeling of community comments.
papers.scalability_note: Method relies on Reddit data; generalizability beyond Reddit unclear; requires ongoing data collection and LLM infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Moderate cost: LLM annotation at scale, data infrastructure, and human validation needed; no per-unit cost provided.
papers.nyc_note: Paper offers pattern-detection infrastructure but no concrete NYC consumer-protection policy, enforcement, or resident intervention. Would require downstream translation into alerts, education, or fraud task-force guidance.

papers.summary: This appears to be a study examining correlations between sexist attitudes, perceptions of minority threat, and public opinion on abortion criminalization. Without an abstract, the exact findings are unclear, but the title suggests it analyzes attitudes rather than testing a concrete intervention.
papers.method: Unknown; title and abstract unavailable. Likely survey or correlational analysis of attitudes.

papers.cost_note: No intervention; research costs only.

papers.title: To conform or not to conform: Experimental evidence on stereotypes and stereotype congruence in the context of hate crime against Asian women
papers.authors: Jason R. Silver, Luzi Shi
papers.abstract: Abstract Objectives This study evaluates public responses to bias-motivated sexual assault against Asian women, focusing on the roles of pre-existing stereotypes of Asian women and victim stereotype congruence (including trait stereotype congruence and country of origin). Methods We use OLS and logistic regression to analyze results from a vignette experiment embedded in a national YouGov survey ( N = 1,300). Results Respondents were more likely to categorize offenses as hate crimes, less likely to blame victims, and more likely to punish perpetrators when victims were stereotype congruent. Pre-existing stereotypes of Asian women were associated with greater likelihood of hate crime categorization and victim blaming. Conclusions The public is less sympathetic toward Asian women victims who violate stereotypes, while pre-existing stereotypes of Asian women may also reduce concern for victims. Future efforts are needed to reduce the multi-dimensional stereotypes of Asian women.
papers.idea: Design public education campaigns to counter stereotypes of Asian women in order to increase hate-crime reporting and reduce victim-blaming in sexual assault cases.
papers.summary: The study used a vignette experiment (N=1,300) to measure how public attitudes toward hate crimes against Asian women vary based on victim stereotype congruence and pre-existing stereotypes. Respondents were more sympathetic and more likely to categorize offenses as hate crimes when victims conformed to stereotypes, but pre-existing stereotypes of Asian women overall reduced victim concern.
papers.method: Vignette experiment embedded in a national YouGov survey (N=1,300), analyzed via OLS and logistic regression.
papers.scalability_note: Public education campaigns are deployable at scale, but this study does not test any intervention's effectiveness.
papers.cost_note: Campaign design and execution would require sustained investment; cost per reached resident unclear.
papers.nyc_note: Study identifies a problem (stereotype-driven victim-blaming) but offers no tested solution; no actionable policy lever or program prototype is evaluated.

papers.title: Structural Racism and the Segregation of Violence in Ethnoracial Neighborhoods
papers.authors: Thomas L. McNulty, Paul E. Bellair, Man-Kit Lei, Arlana Henry
papers.idea: No actionable intervention identified from title/abstract alone.
papers.summary: Paper title suggests an examination of how structural racism relates to violence concentration in ethnoracial neighborhoods. Without an abstract, the study's actual findings, mechanisms, or policy implications cannot be determined.
papers.method: Unknown; no abstract provided to assess design, data source, or sample.
papers.scalability_note: Likely a theoretical or observational analysis without a specific intervention to scale.
papers.cost_note: Unknown; no intervention described.

papers.title: Beyond Legal Deterrence: A Replication and Extension Study on Psychopathy, Self-Control, and Crime-Specific Willingness to Offend
papers.authors: Valentina Macías-Vasileff, Stefan Bogaerts, Elien De Caluwé, Yasemin Erbaş, Carlo Garofalo
papers.abstract: Objectives This study explored the extent to which law-abiding behavior depends on legal punishment by replicating and extending Meldrum et al. who found that 18% of participants were willing to offend if all crimes were legal during a hypothetical day (i.e., “purging”) and that these individuals exhibited higher psychopathic traits and lower self-control. We sought to replicate their findings and extend them through methodological refinements. Methods A community convenience sample of 865 predominantly European participants completed an online survey. Participants responded to a single item asking whether they would offend in the absence of legal controls, followed by a 33-item questionnaire assessing willingness of committing specific crimes under the same conditions, and self-report measures of psychopathy and self-control. Results Thirty percent of participants reported willingness to offend on the single-item measure, and 76.7% endorsed at least one specific crime. Minor offenses (e.g., theft; ∼50%) were more frequently endorsed than severe ones (e.g., violent- or sex-crimes; 0.3%-4.3%). An exploratory factor analysis of the purge questionnaire identified seven crime categories with distinct endorsement levels. Regression analyses indicated that psychopathic traits and low self-control were positively related to purging, with psychopathy emerging as the stronger predictor of purging and most crime categories. Conclusions Legal punishment alone does not explain law-abiding behavior; individuals appear to refrain from severe crimes for extralegal reasons, whereas minor crimes may require legal deterrence. Psychopathic traits and low self-control represent robust psychological risk factors, underscoring the need for prevention strategies that target these predispositions alongside traditional legal deterrence.
papers.source: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.venue: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.idea: Screen for and target psychopathy and self-control deficits in offender rehabilitation or prevention programs.
papers.summary: This replication study surveyed 865 European participants on hypothetical willingness to commit crimes if legal consequences were absent. The authors found that ~30% would offend generally and 76.7% would commit at least one specific crime; psychopathic traits and low self-control were strong predictors, with psychopathy being the stronger factor.
papers.method: Cross-sectional online survey of 865 European convenience sample respondents; single-item and 33-item questionnaire measures plus self-report psychopathy and self-control scales, analyzed via exploratory factor analysis and regression.
papers.scalability_note: Hypothetical willingness to offend does not translate to actual behavior; unclear how to operationalize prevention targeting psychopathy/self-control at scale.
papers.cost_note: Screening is low-cost, but effective interventions for psychopathy and low self-control remain unclear and potentially expensive.
papers.nyc_note: Study is purely descriptive and correlational; no tested intervention or implementation pathway for NYC agencies (NYPD, courts, DOC) is proposed or evaluated.

papers.title: Selection and Facilitation: Is the Gang Membership-Psychopathic Traits Link a Product of Individual Differences, Social Influences, or Both?
papers.authors: Jennifer J. Tostlebe
papers.abstract: Objectives The issue of psychopathology among gang populations is controversial. Classic gang scholarship points to psychopathic traits as time-stable characteristics of those who join gangs, others argue gangs facilitate attitudes and behaviors endogenous to psychopathy, stisll others reject a gang-psychopathic traits link. Building off an interdisciplinary theoretical basis that adapts a tripartite theoretical model (i.e., selection, facilitation, and enhancement) on the gang membership-offending link to the mixed positions on the gang membership-psychopathic traits relationship, this study tests selection and facilitation models. Methods Using longitudinal data from Pathways to Desistance, I examine whether psychopathic traits are an antecedent and/or consequence of gang membership. Specifically, I test whether (a) there are between individual differences in psychopathic traits that contribute to selection into gangs and (b) gangs facilitate within-individual change in psychopathic traits among their members. Results The findings suggest that the enhancement model (i.e., both selection and facilitation) best represents the relationship between gang membership and psychopathic traits. Conclusions Analyzing the role of individual propensities—like psychopathic traits—and social-environmental influences—like gang membership—is critical for understanding crime and criminality. This study demonstrates that these factors do not operate in isolation but instead interact bidirectionally. By moving beyond the selection-facilitation dichotomy, this study contributes to a more integrated framework for explaining problem behaviors and helps establish best practices for prevention and rehabilitation.
papers.source: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.venue: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.idea: N/A — study is descriptive/theoretical with no actionable intervention proposed
papers.summary: This longitudinal study tests whether psychopathic traits precede gang membership (selection), follow it (facilitation), or both (enhancement). Using Pathways to Desistance data, the authors found evidence for the enhancement model, suggesting individual propensities and gang social influences interact bidirectionally to shape psychopathic traits.
papers.method: Longitudinal observational analysis using the Pathways to Desistance dataset; sample size not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Study is theory-building only; identifies no specific preventive program, policy reform, or intervention mechanism for cities to adopt.
papers.cost_note: No intervention costed; research study only.
papers.nyc_note: Findings could inform gang intervention strategy design, but the abstract proposes no concrete program NYC could implement; 'best practices' mentioned but not specified.

papers.title: Amortized Probabilistic Retrieval of Atmospheric CO2 from OCO-2 Spectra Using Deep Learning with Laplace Approximations and Normalizing Flows
papers.authors: Alejandro Calle-Saldarriaga, Felix Jimenez, Jack Grosskreuz, Jiazheng Wang, Jonathan Hobbs, Matthias Katzfuss
papers.abstract: Space-based monitoring of atmospheric carbon dioxide (CO2) is essential for constraining the global carbon budget. NASA's Orbiting Carbon Observatory-2 (OCO-2) estimates column-averaged dry-air mole fractions of CO2 (XCO2) using high-resolution spectra. However, current operational retrieval algorithms are computationally expensive and do not properly quantify uncertainties. We present a novel deep learning framework that addresses these challenges. Due to the difficulties of ground-truth data for real satellite observations, we develop and validate our approach using a high-fidelity simulation dataset. This dataset, created to support OCO-2 uncertainty quantification (UQ), incorporates realistic forward model errors. Our architecture encodes spectral bands using a multi-branch neural network and estimates posteriors of the full CO2 column or desired summaries thereof using two scalable UQ methods: Laplace approximations and normalizing flows. Our approach has five key advantages relative to operational "full-physics" solvers: (1) Amortization: Inference is orders of magnitude faster, enabling real-time processing of massive data streams; (2) Model error robustness: By training on simulations that explicitly include model discrepancies, our method accounts for systematic errors often neglected by standard inversions; (3) Point estimate accuracy: We achieve superior predictive accuracy compared to baseline methods; (4) Improved UQ: The probabilistic outputs yield better-calibrated uncertainty estimates; and (5) Non-Gaussian posteriors: When utilizing normalizing flows, our framework successfully models complex, asymmetric posterior distributions, overcoming the limitations of the Gaussian assumption. These results suggest that simulation-based deep learning is a viable path toward next-generation operational processing systems.
papers.idea: N/A — methods paper with no city-policy intervention
papers.summary: The paper develops a deep learning framework to retrieve atmospheric CO2 measurements from NASA satellite spectra faster and with better uncertainty quantification than current operational algorithms. The method uses Laplace approximations and normalizing flows to model complex posterior distributions from simulated spectral data.
papers.method: Simulation-based validation using a high-fidelity OCO-2 dataset with realistic forward model errors; deep learning architecture with multi-branch neural network and two UQ methods tested on synthetic data.
papers.scalability_note: Satellite-based monitoring is inherently global and scalable once operational.
papers.cost_note: Software/algorithm; marginal deployment cost once developed.
papers.nyc_note: No local policy lever; global atmospheric monitoring with no city-specific intervention or decision point.

papers.title: Rational choice models of crime decision‐making: A flexible utility framework
papers.authors: Shaina Herman, Timothy C. Barnum, Daniel S. Nagin, Greg Pogarsky
papers.abstract: Abstract Rational choice models of the decision to commit a crime have been prominent in the criminological literature for decades. Their theoretical components, however, remain fragmented, leading to misconceptions about the framework's explanatory power and policy relevance. The present paper aims to synthesize these fragmented theoretical elements and demonstrate the flexibility of rational choice models in accommodating various recent advancements in research on crime decision‐making. To do so, we construct a series of equations that formalize the core mechanisms underlying the decision to offend. We begin with a baseline equation derived from Beccaria's classical deterrence model—centered on the certainty, severity, and celerity of legal sanctions—which is extended to include noncriminal alternatives to crime. We then build on this structure to account for the situational bases for risk perception, discrete stages of the criminal justice process, nonlinear risk processing, ambiguity, moral disutility, safety concerns, and visceral influences such as emotion. The result is a series of compact yet flexible models that address how incentives and constraints shape criminal behavior and that can be used to specify statistical models to test key model predictions.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a theoretical framework paper with no specific intervention.
papers.summary: The authors synthesize rational choice theory in criminology by formalizing decision-making equations that extend classical deterrence models to include noncriminal alternatives, risk perception, emotion, and moral factors. The result is a flexible set of theoretical models intended to guide future empirical testing of how incentives and constraints shape criminal behavior.
papers.method: Non-empirical; theoretical synthesis and formalization of existing rational choice models in criminology literature; no new data collection or empirical test reported in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Theoretical framework; requires downstream empirical validation and policy translation.
papers.cost_note: Framework itself is zero-cost, but any intervention derived from it would depend on implementation design.
papers.nyc_note: This is foundational theory, not a policy or program; NYC would need applied research translating it into specific interventions before adoption.

papers.title: How Career and Technical Education for Students Expands Work Opportunities
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.idea: Expand career and technical education (CTE) programs in public schools to increase work opportunities and economic mobility for students.
papers.summary: Unable to determine specific findings from abstract alone. The title suggests the study examines how CTE programs expand work opportunities for students, likely through improved labor-market outcomes or employment pathways.

papers.title: Are Transportation Issues Important in Local Elections?
papers.authors: Xavier J. Harmony
papers.abstract: Transportation is a core responsibility of local government, yet its role in shaping electoral outcomes remains unclear. Using data on 548 candidates for local office in the United States, this study analyzes campaign website content with logistic regression and descriptive statistics to examine which candidates include transportation issues and whether doing so is associated with electoral performance. Results show that the inclusion of transportation platforms varies across candidate and institutional contexts, with differences between city and county elections. Importantly, candidates who include transportation policies perform better, on average, among non-incumbents, mayoral candidates, and those in mayor–council or strong mayor cities. Overall, these findings suggest that transportation is not only a policy domain but also a salient electoral issue in many local contexts, particularly in city elections. The results further indicate that including transportation issues may offer a strategic advantage to candidates for local office.
papers.idea: Candidates who emphasize transportation in their platforms may win more votes, suggesting voters care about the issue.
papers.summary: This study analyzed 548 local candidates' campaign websites to see if they mentioned transportation issues and whether doing so correlated with electoral success. Candidates who included transportation platforms performed better on average, particularly non-incumbents, mayoral candidates, and those in mayor–council or strong mayor systems.
papers.method: Logistic regression and descriptive statistics on campaign website content analysis of 548 U.S. local candidates across multiple elections.
papers.scalability_note: Insight about voter preferences, not a scalable intervention; requires different work to convert into policy.
papers.cost_note: No direct implementation cost; the finding is about electoral messaging, not a program.
papers.nyc_note: Tells NYC candidates that highlighting transit may help them win, but does not identify a concrete service, policy, or program NYC government could adopt.

papers.title: Three Things DC’s New Mayor and Council Need to Know About the District’s Early Educator Compensation Program
papers.authors: Elli Nikolopoulos, Erica Greenberg
papers.abstract: DC’s upcoming elections have reignited interest in addressing the high cost of child care amid threats to a key program in the District’s child care system—the Early Childhood Educator Pay Equity Fund. This voluntary compensation program is one of many innovations that has made DC a national leader in early childhood policy. It addresses a long-standing problem: low pay for early educators, which in turn leads to high rates of turnover (PDF), limited access for families (PDF), and poor outcomes for young children. With DC well above the national average in terms of child care prices— $47,200 on average for families with two young children in 2024—the compensation program is helping more DC families afford quality child care. The compensation program allows participating child care businesses to receive funding to pay early educators according to a minimum salary schedule (PDF) and allows all child care businesses and staff to opt
papers.idea: Establish or expand a voluntary early-educator pay-equity fund that subsidizes minimum salaries for child care workers, reducing turnover and increasing access to affordable quality child care.
papers.summary: DC's Early Childhood Educator Pay Equity Fund is a voluntary compensation program that addresses low early-educator wages—a key driver of high turnover, limited family access, and poor child outcomes. The program helps participating child care businesses pay staff according to a minimum salary schedule, helping offset DC's above-average child care costs ($47,200/year for two young children).
papers.method: Non-empirical policy brief summarizing DC's existing program and its rationale, with citations to external research on turnover and access impacts.
papers.scalability_note: Wage-subsidy models are replicable across cities; DC's structure is designed for adaptation.
papers.cost_note: Requires sustained public funding; abstract does not specify DC program budget per educator.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already operates child care subsidy programs; pay-equity model could be layered onto existing infrastructure, though funding allocation would need approval.

papers.title: Silent Decline, Loud Indicators: Depopulation and the Measurement of Per Capita Fiscal Outcomes in Small Municipalities
papers.authors: Nerea Almela-López, Bernardino Benito, María-Dolores Guillamón, Ana-María Ríos
papers.abstract: This study examines the relationship between depopulation from 2015 to 2022 and changes in key per capita fiscal indicators in Spanish municipalities with fewer than 20,000 inhabitants. Using a long-difference approach and disaggregating by population size, it analyzes associations between population decline and debt, nonfinancial expenditure, and nonfinancial revenue per capita, distinguishing between taxes and transfers. Results show that depopulation is associated with higher expenditure and revenue per capita, while its association with debt is significant only in the full sample and the smallest municipalities. Municipalities under 1,000 inhabitants display patterns consistent with more persistent fiscal pressures. Higher income per capita is also associated with more favorable observable fiscal outcomes. A central contribution is to show that increases in per capita fiscal indicators under demographic decline may reflect shrinking population bases rather than stronger fiscal capacity, implying that per capita indicators may overstate fiscal strength and should be interpreted with caution.
papers.idea: Use caution when interpreting per capita fiscal metrics in shrinking municipalities, as they may mask fiscal weakness rather than reveal fiscal strength.
papers.summary: This study of Spanish municipalities under 20,000 people (2015–2022) found that population decline is associated with higher per capita expenditure and revenue, but this reflects a shrinking denominator rather than improved fiscal capacity. The relationship with debt was weaker and inconsistent. The authors caution that per capita indicators can be misleading under depopulation.
papers.method: Long-difference regression analysis of Spanish municipal data, disaggregated by population size cohorts, examining associations between population decline and per capita fiscal outcomes.
papers.scalability_note: Applicable mainly to municipalities experiencing depopulation; NYC is growing.
papers.cost_note: No intervention proposed; methodological guidance only.
papers.nyc_note: NYC faces growth and density challenges, not depopulation. Finding concerns fiscal accounting in shrinking towns, not city operations or resident welfare.

papers.title: Evidence on the Benefits of the District of Columbia's Early Childhood Educator Pay Equity Fund for Child Care Affordability and DC's Growth
papers.authors: Erica Greenberg, Eve Mefferd
papers.abstract: Erica Greenberg and Eve Mefferd have prepared testimony for the District of Columbia’s Committee of the Whole, highlighting Urban Institute research on the benefits of the District’s early childhood educator compensation program. Their testimony explains how the program is more than just a budget line item: it works in concert with other public and private funding to stabilize and improve the supply of affordable quality child care in DC.
papers.idea: Establish a pay-equity fund for early childhood educators to increase affordable childcare supply, enabling parental workforce participation and economic mobility.
papers.summary: The District of Columbia's early childhood educator compensation program improves childcare affordability and supply by using public funding to stabilize educator pay. The program works alongside other funding sources to make quality childcare more accessible and sustainable for DC residents.
papers.method: Testimony prepared for DC Committee of the Whole based on Urban Institute research; no empirical study design specified in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Pay-equity models are replicable across cities; DC's program provides a direct template.
papers.cost_note: Requires significant public investment to fund educator compensation; specific per-unit costs not stated in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already funds childcare through ACS/NYCHA; educator pay equity aligns with existing policy goals and infrastructure.

papers.title: Ambient Information: Measuring Student Capacities from AI-Interaction Chat Data
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: This project develops novel, highly scalable measures of cognitive, social, and emotional skills by analyzing behavioral data from students’ interactions with AI tools. The study is led by a distinguished scholar with deep research-practice partnerships with school systems nationwide and conducted in collaboration with leading AI companies. The project will draw on naturally occurring student–AI interaction data from several widely used AI platforms and traditional data from school district partnerships, including surveys, assessments, and administrative records. These data partnerships enable the project to develop new measures of higher-order thinking and self-management skills (such as engagement, persistence, and self-regulation) that can be captured frequently from passively generated AI interaction data and validated against traditional measures of student skills. The project lays the groundwork for building a scalable and comparable system for assessing and strengthening the durable skills students need for long‑run success. Data Sources Data: Administrative data from school districts and
papers.idea: Use AI interaction data to develop scalable measures of student cognitive and social-emotional skills for assessment and intervention.
papers.summary: The project develops novel measurement approaches by analyzing behavioral data from students' interactions with AI tools to capture higher-order thinking and self-management skills (engagement, persistence, self-regulation). These AI-derived measures are validated against traditional assessments and administrative data from school districts. The goal is to create a scalable, comparable system for assessing durable skills linked to long-term student success.
papers.method: Observational analysis of naturally occurring student–AI interaction data from widely used AI platforms, combined with school district administrative data, surveys, and assessments; partnership-based approach with school systems and AI companies.
papers.scalability_note: AI interaction data is passively generated and widely available; platform-agnostic methodology could apply across many districts.
papers.cost_note: Relies on naturally occurring data; minimal direct intervention cost once measurement infrastructure built.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has large school system with potential AI tool adoption, but abstract provides no concrete actionable policy or program a city could adopt—only measurement development.

papers.title: Towards a Theory of Modular Natives: Explaining Superscaling, China's Greatest Innovation Yet
papers.authors: Bent Flyvbjerg, Alexander Budzier, Maria Christodoulou
papers.abstract: First, we present a new theory of "modular natives." A modular native is a basic building block that is born modular, e.g., a solar cell. The theory predicts that using modular natives in building things reduces complexity and improves predictability, resulting in better outcomes and faster scale-up. Second, we test the theory on the largest dataset of its kind. We find, at a high level of statistical significance, that modular natives operate under a fundamentally different risk regime than other project types, with finite and predictable risk, in contrast to non-natives that have infinite and unpredictable risk. The findings help explain why modularity is key to successful building while bespokeness often leads to failure. Third, we relate our findings to economic and geopolitical development, arguing that China understands modular natives and scale-up better than any other geography and that this is key to China's swiftly growing dominance in renewables, batteries, EVs, robots, etc. We argue that China's mastery of modularity and scale-up is a major innovation in its own right, among the greatest and most impactful in human history, falsifying the common notion that China cannot innovate. Business and government outside China ignore these findings at their peril. Finally, we spell out policy and practice implications and identify areas for further research.
papers.idea: Cities could adopt modular design principles for infrastructure projects to reduce complexity and cost overruns.
papers.summary: The authors propose a theory of 'modular natives'—building blocks designed to be modular from inception—and argue that modularity reduces risk and improves predictability in large projects. They claim China's superior understanding of modular scaling explains its dominance in renewables, batteries, and EVs, and that this represents a major innovation in how projects are conceived and deployed.
papers.method: The authors tested their theory on a large dataset of projects, using statistical analysis to compare risk profiles of modular versus non-modular approaches, though the abstract does not specify sample size, design, or setting details.
papers.scalability_note: Modular design principles are general and transferable across geographies and sectors.
papers.cost_note: No costs estimated in abstract; modularity may reduce per-unit costs but upfront design investment required.
papers.nyc_note: NYC's focus is on retrofitting legacy systems and fixing specific projects; the paper's insights apply mainly to greenfield infrastructure (renewables, EVs) where NYC has limited direct control.

papers.title: A Nationwide Benchmark for Wildfire Initial Attack Failure Prediction with Public Environmental Data
papers.authors: Runyang Xu, Xueqi Cheng, Yushun Dong
papers.abstract: Initial attack (IA) is the first wildfire suppression phase, when agencies must quickly decide which fires may escape early control. Existing IA failure prediction studies often use non-public response records or regional settings, so it remains unclear how well public data available at fire discovery time can support IA failure prediction at national scale. We present WILDFIREIA, the first U.S. national-scale benchmark for IA failure prediction from environmental and contextual data available at fire discovery time. WILDFIREIA aligns 38,128 naturally caused FPA-FOD wildfire events with FIRMS/VIIRS thermal detections, gridMET weather and fire-danger variables, LANDFIRE vegetation, fuel, and topography, OpenStreetMap access features, and WorldPop population density. To prevent data leakage, the benchmark fixes the event unit, size-based label rule, chronological split, metrics, and forbidden-feature list, and excludes final fire size, containment timestamps, and post-discovery satellite detections from model inputs. We evaluate 16 representative models across tabular, temporal, spatial, and spatiotemporal families under the same protocol. Results show that public discovery-time data provides useful but incomplete signal for IA failure prediction: XGBoost achieves the best AUPRC of 53.3%; FIRMS/VIIRS is the least redundant source; and fuel is the strongest static predictor when dynamic observations are unavailable. We release preprocessing outputs and model-ready caches to support reproducible research on early wildfire risk assessment: https://github.com/LabRAI/WildfireIA#.
papers.idea: Use machine-learning models trained on public environmental data to predict wildfire initial-attack failure and prioritize suppression resources.
papers.summary: The authors created WILDFIREIA, a national benchmark dataset of 38,128 wildfires with environmental, weather, vegetation, and access features to predict whether initial wildfire suppression will fail. XGBoost achieved best performance (AUPRC 53.3%), suggesting public discovery-time data provides incomplete but useful signal for early risk assessment.
papers.method: Retrospective observational study aligning 38,128 FPA-FOD wildfire records with satellite thermal detections, weather, fuel, topography, and population data; evaluation of 16 models under standardized protocols with chronological train-test splits.
papers.scalability_note: Methods and datasets are national-scale; models are transferable and code is released.
papers.cost_note: Relies on existing public data sources; primary cost is model development and deployment infrastructure.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has minimal wildfire risk; suburban/exurban areas of NY, NJ, PA may benefit, but not NYC proper.

papers.title: How Expanding Housing Supply and Supporting Demand Can Increase Homeownership
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.idea: Expand housing supply and support demand-side interventions to increase homeownership rates.
papers.summary: Title and venue suggest a policy brief on mechanisms linking housing supply expansion and demand-support programs to homeownership outcomes. No abstract available to confirm findings, mechanisms, or evidence base.
papers.method: Unknown; Urban Institute policy analysis (likely descriptive synthesis or modeling).
papers.scalability_note: Housing supply and demand policies are standard levers used by cities and states.
papers.cost_note: Supply-side costs vary widely; demand-side support (down-payment assistance, subsidies) requires budget allocation.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing housing and homeownership programs; supply constraints and affordability are urgent policy issues.

papers.title: The Role of Public Health Services in Reducing Maternal and Newborn Health Inequalities in Urban India: A Survey Analysis of 200,000 Births Over Two Decades
papers.authors: Andrea K. Blanchard, Banadakoppa Manjappa Ramesh, Usha Ram, Prakash Kumar, Kerry Scott, James F. Blanchard, Ties Boerma
papers.abstract: Abstract India has experienced rapid urbanisation, straining the healthcare system. The National Urban Health Mission was launched in 2013 to improve access to public healthcare, particularly among socio-economically disadvantaged urban populations. This study aimed to assess whether inequalities in maternal and newborn health (MNH) service coverage and outcomes between richer and poorer groups have improved at public and private sources across urban India in the last two decades. We used pooled data from four national cross-sectional surveys, the District Level Household Surveys from 2002 to 2008 and National Family Health Surveys from 2015 to 2021, covering 94,826 and 108,152 births in urban India, respectively. We analysed trends in coverage of antenatal, delivery, and postnatal care services and neonatal mortality by source across wealth deciles, and summarised inequalities using the slope index of inequality, concentration index, and inequality pattern index. The study found that coverage of all MNH services, and to a lesser extent neonatal survival, increased substantially between 2002–2008 and 2015–2021 in urban India. Improvements were steeper among the poorest groups. Coverage by public health facilities notably increased, and neonatal mortality rates were lower at public than private facilities, particularly among the poorest. However, the poorest decile remained well behind all other groups, reflecting bottom inequalities. Rapid improvements with reduced inequalities in MNH service coverage appear to be driven by increased access to public sector services in urban India. It remains critical for the public healthcare system to understand and address the particular needs of the poorest groups to reduce ongoing bottom MNH inequalities in urban India.
papers.idea: Strengthen public health facility capacity and remove barriers to access for the poorest urban populations to reduce maternal and newborn mortality disparities, following the model of India's National Urban Health Mission.
papers.summary: Over two decades in urban India, coverage of antenatal, delivery, and postnatal care services and neonatal survival improved substantially, with steeper gains among poorer groups and notably better outcomes at public facilities than private ones. However, the poorest decile remained significantly behind other wealth groups, indicating persistent "bottom inequalities" despite overall progress.
papers.method: Pooled cross-sectional analysis of 200,000+ births from four national surveys (District Level Household Surveys 2002–2008 and National Family Health Surveys 2015–2021) tracking service coverage and neonatal mortality across wealth deciles.
papers.scalability_note: Health policy and facility expansion readily scalable across urban settings; requires sustained infrastructure investment.
papers.cost_note: Public health system expansion is moderately costly; study suggests public provision more efficient than relying on private sector.
papers.nyc_note: NYC maternal/newborn mortality rates are substantially lower than urban India; applicable mainly as comparative benchmark for equity-focused service delivery in underserved neighborhoods.

papers.title: Formulation and Evaluation of Two Citywide Gentrification Measures
papers.authors: Alex Mozell
papers.abstract: The following exposition describes two novel empirical methods devised to measure citywide gentrification: the Dynamic Measure of Citywide Gentrification and the Cross-Sectional Measure of Citywide Gentrification. This paper tests for these measures’ viability. These measures were regressed against demographic variables, and many of the coefficients are statistically significant. These measures are associated as expected with each other when graphed and regressed, though the correlation and R 2 coefficients are lower than expected. Prominent cities identified as highly gentrified by each measure conform to expectations, and prominent cities identified as least gentrified by the Dynamic Measure also conform to expectations. For prominent cities, each measure's identification of highly gentrified cities generally conforms to the other measure. Ultimately, this paper provides evidence for the viability of these measures to assess gentrification at the level of the city. Local policymakers should expect gentrification to emerge across downtown districts.
papers.idea: None identifiable—this is a measurement paper with no policy intervention.
papers.summary: The paper develops two statistical methods to measure gentrification at the citywide level and tests their validity against demographic variables and against each other. The measures show expected associations with demographics and identify major gentrified cities as expected, though inter-measure correlation is weaker than anticipated.
papers.method: Descriptive/methodological: two novel gentrification indices (Dynamic and Cross-Sectional) were constructed, regressed against demographic variables, and compared against each other across cities.
papers.scalability_note: Measurement approach is generalizable but requires data infrastructure; actual utility depends on how cities use the metrics.
papers.cost_note: Developing measurement tools is relatively low-cost; implementation costs depend on downstream policy use.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has sophisticated demographic data and gentrification tracking; unclear what new policy action these measures would enable beyond existing monitoring.

papers.title: The Next Million Manufactured Homes Should Be Safer Than the Last
papers.authors: Andrew Rumbach
papers.abstract: As congressional lawmakers work to address the nation’s affordable housing crisis, manufactured housing is receiving bipartisan support at both the federal and state levels. Federal policymakers are looking to expand the supply of manufactured homes through the updated 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act that’s moving quickly through the House of Representatives. Meanwhile, several states have moved to reduce barriers to manufactured housing supply. Virginia recently enacted a law that requires local jurisdictions to allow manufactured homes anywhere site-built single-family homes are permitted, following similar laws passed in Kentucky, Maryland and Idaho. The logic is straightforward: Manufactured homes cost significantly less per square foot to build than site-built homes and already provide housing for more than 22 million Americans, most earning less than the median national income. Manufactured housing is only truly affordable if it can withstand the environment it’s placed in, however. In a recent Urban Institute report, my
papers.idea: Cities should adopt or strengthen building code standards and enforcement mechanisms for manufactured homes to ensure durability and safety in local climate conditions.
papers.summary: The abstract argues that manufactured homes are an important affordable housing solution (serving 22+ million Americans), with bipartisan federal policy momentum and state-level regulatory reforms to expand supply. However, the piece emphasizes that affordability is undermined if units cannot withstand local environmental conditions, suggesting a need for stronger durability/safety standards.
papers.method: The item cites a recent Urban Institute report but the abstract is incomplete; appears to be a policy brief or opinion piece rather than empirical research.
papers.scalability_note: Building code adoption and enforcement are standard municipal functions; can be adapted by any city.
papers.cost_note: Cost to municipalities is primarily regulatory/administrative; enforcement via existing building departments. Likely cost-effective if prevents costly repairs/replacement.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has limited manufactured-home stock due to high land costs and zoning; more relevant to outer boroughs or regions with existing manufactured communities. State-level policy (NYS Building Code) would be the key lever.

papers.title: A Privacy-Preserving Validation Server Version 3.0
papers.authors: Erika Tyagi, Silke Taylor, Graham MacDonald, Deena Tamaroff, Claire McKay Bowen, Joshua Snoke
papers.abstract: This technical white paper provides an overview of the Urban Institute’s privacy-preserving validation server version 3.0 prototype. The goal of the prototype is to provide a testable solution to government agencies looking to improve and automate statistical disclosure control processes. Building on version 2.0, the latest prototype introduces a dedicated administrator interface, enhanced privacy protections and statistical validity, and usability updates informed by feedback from researchers, agency staff, and privacy experts. We hope that testing with researchers and agencies, continuous improvement, and dissemination of our learnings will lead to significantly increased access to valuable data and insights used to craft better public policy. Why This Matters Our work responds to the growing recognition that traditional, manual disclosure control methods are increasingly inadequate in the face of modern re-identification risks and often slow in releasing valuable data. What We Found The version 3.0 prototype introduces several key improvements: a newly developed
papers.idea: A privacy-preserving server tool to automate statistical disclosure control in government data releases.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute developed version 3.0 of a privacy-preserving validation server prototype to help government agencies automate and improve statistical disclosure control—the process of removing re-identification risk from datasets before public release. The update adds an administrator interface, enhanced privacy protections, and usability improvements based on feedback from researchers and agency staff.
papers.method: Technical prototype development informed by iterative feedback from researchers, agency staff, and privacy experts; no empirical trial or outcome measurement reported in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Software tool can be deployed across multiple agencies with minimal marginal cost once built.
papers.cost_note: Software infrastructure; likely modest per-agency deployment cost after initial development.
papers.nyc_note: Potentially useful for NYC agencies managing sensitive datasets, but requires agency adoption and integration into existing workflows.

papers.title: Evaluating How LLMs Handle Education and Workforce Pathway Data
papers.authors: Erika Tyagi, Kristin Blagg, Emily Gutierrez
papers.abstract: AI tools are rapidly becoming one of the primary ways people find and interpret information—including questions about education and workforce pathway data. These tools have the potential to make public data more accessible, but how well do they handle questions grounded in it? Can they find the right sources, use the data correctly, and communicate information in ways that serve their users? To find out, we conducted a structured evaluation in which subject-matter experts assessed how three leading large language models (LLMs) responded to 100 curated questions about education and workforce pathway data. Why This Matters Most public data systems were built to serve human users navigating websites and documentation, not to be discovered and used by AI systems. When that gap goes unaddressed, models often return answers that look authoritative but aren't—and by attributing incorrect information to specific tools and sources, they risk eroding trust in the data infrastructure
papers.idea: Audit and improve how AI systems surface education and workforce data to ensure accuracy and maintain public trust in data infrastructure.
papers.summary: The authors evaluated three leading LLMs on 100 curated questions about education and workforce pathways, finding that AI tools often return confident-sounding but inaccurate answers and misattribute information to sources. The study highlights a gap between how public data systems were designed (for human web navigation) and how LLMs discover and use them.
papers.method: Structured evaluation where subject-matter experts assessed LLM responses to 100 curated questions about education and workforce pathway data; no sample size given for models or institutions tested.
papers.scalability_note: Diagnostic tool useful for government but requires ongoing audits per LLM; not a scalable intervention per se.
papers.cost_note: Primarily staff expertise (subject-matter experts); marginal deployment cost once audit framework built.
papers.nyc_note: NYC publishes education and workforce data (CUNY, workforce boards); audit results could inform vendor selection or API design, but impact on resident outcomes is indirect.

papers.title: Model-based clustering of compositional trajectories for the analysis of mobility data
papers.authors: Andrea Panarotto, Manuela Cattelan, Ruggero Bellio
papers.abstract: Understanding urban mobility patterns is crucial for designing efficient and sustainable transportation systems. Motivated by an application to the municipality of Padova and its surroundings, we propose a novel statistical framework for the analysis and clustering of mobility trajectories derived from telephonic data. We introduce a compositional representation of individual movements that integrates the uncertain device location with information on the surrounding road network, encoding at each time point the proportions of different road types compatible with the observed position. This formulation naturally accounts for measurement uncertainty and yields trajectories evolving in the simplex. To model these data, we develop a state-space framework for compositional time series that captures both the telephonic measurement error and the temporal dynamics of the latent mobility process. Building on this representation, we propose a model-based clustering approach based on mixtures of state-space models to identify groups of trajectories with similar evolution. This allows us to aggregate individual movements into interpretable mobility patterns at the population level. The results of the case study demonstrate the ability of the approach to uncover meaningful mobility behaviors, providing insights that are potentially relevant to policy makers.
papers.idea: Use statistical clustering of mobile phone trajectory data to identify population-level mobility patterns for transportation planning.
papers.summary: The authors develop a compositional state-space model to cluster individual mobility trajectories derived from cell phone location data, accounting for measurement uncertainty and road network information. Applied to Padova, Italy, the method uncovers interpretable groups of similar mobility behaviors at the population level.
papers.method: Descriptive statistical framework using cell phone location data from Padova municipality; develops novel state-space model for compositional time series and proposes model-based clustering via mixture models; case study application (sample size not stated).
papers.scalability_note: Cell phone data collection is routine in most cities; method is generalizable but requires technical expertise to implement.
papers.cost_note: Leverages existing telecom data; computation costs are modest; no intervention costs.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has extensive transit data (MTA, Citibike, taxi) and mobility studies; this adds incremental methodological value but no concrete policy lever or novel actionable recommendation is identified.

papers.title: "Stuck in a Spiral": Shame and Guilt as Social Regulators of AI Use in Computing Education
papers.authors: Kate Hamilton, Irene Hou, Dev Patel, Sheena Nnam, Hena Patel, Stephen MacNeil
papers.abstract: While prior work has examined patterns of adoption and social norms around AI use, less is known about how emotional factors, such as shame and guilt, shape students use of AI tools. We present an interview study with 19 computing students through a functionalist perspective of shame and guilt, which interprets emotions as social signals that regulate behavior. Our findings show that these emotions regulate when and how students make their use visible, as they engage in hiding behaviors and selective disclosure. Students described shaming themselves, their peers, and even faculty for using AI. Shame and guilt often coexist with continued AI use, creating cycles of reduced agency and moral tension rather than promoting behavior change. Students described feeling tensions between their AI use and their identities as competent, hardworking, or ethical computing students. Students also used language and metaphors of addiction to describe their experiences. These results highlight the need to consider the socio-emotional aspects of AI use, which may be influenced by how AI policies are implemented and enforced. We discuss classroom practices that can foster healthy, open discussion and support responsible AI use.
papers.idea: Implement classroom policies and practices that foster open, non-punitive discussion of AI tool use to reduce shame-driven hiding behaviors and support responsible student adoption.
papers.summary: An interview study of 19 computing students found that shame and guilt regulate when students hide or disclose their AI tool use, often coexisting with continued use rather than changing behavior. Students experienced moral tension between AI use and their identities as competent or ethical, with some using addiction language to describe their experiences.
papers.method: Qualitative interview study with 19 computing students, analyzed through a functionalist lens of shame and guilt as social regulators.
papers.scalability_note: Classroom practices are implementable in education systems but highly dependent on institutional buy-in and faculty training.
papers.cost_note: Low cost—primarily requires curriculum and policy redesign, faculty professional development, and facilitated classroom discussion.
papers.nyc_note: NYC Department of Education could adopt in public schools, but limited direct city-policy lever; primarily a district/school-level intervention.

papers.title: Georgia’s Child Care Subsidy Program
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: In 2023, Urban began a multiyear collaboration with DECAL to design a study focused on understanding parents’ experiences with Childcare and Parent Services (CAPS), Georgia’s child care subsidy program. This research can help the agency identify and better meet the needs of the people it serves.
papers.idea: Improve child care subsidy program design by studying parent needs and experiences.
papers.summary: This is a collaborative research study between Urban Institute and Georgia's DECAL agency designed to understand how parents experience the state's child care subsidy program (CAPS). The study aims to help the agency identify and better meet the needs of families it serves, but no findings are reported in the abstract.
papers.method: Multiyear qualitative/mixed-methods study with DECAL, examining parent experiences with Georgia's CAPS program; specific design and sample size not stated.
papers.scalability_note: Program model is replicable but findings are Georgia-specific; generalization requires validation.
papers.cost_note: Subsidy programs are relatively cost-effective per family served, but abstract gives no cost data.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has child care subsidy programs (ACS, DOE pre-K) but Georgia's CAPS structure and findings may not directly transfer.

papers.title: How Do Households with Low and Moderate Incomes Build Wealth, and How Can Practitioners Improve These Pathways?
papers.authors: Brett Theodos, Brady Meixell
papers.abstract: Building even a small amount of wealth can reduce a household’s risk of economic hardship, create stability, and serve as a springboard to economic mobility. But many American households today feel that building wealth is out of their reach, particularly in the face of the broader affordability crisis. This raises two questions: How much wealth do households with low and moderate incomes (LMI) have? What pathways could help these households build greater wealth? To identify potential pathways, we explore wealth and wealth building among working households—those whose primary earner is in the labor force and in their prime working years (ages 25 to 65)—with LMI, defined as income below the national median of $83,730. We find that despite substantial disparities in wealth among working households with LMI, these households use business equity, home equity, and retirement savings to create wealth. Understanding these pathways can help practitioners and policymakers design better
papers.idea: Design and scale wealth-building interventions targeting LMI households through business equity, home equity, and retirement savings pathways.
papers.summary: The study examines how working households with low and moderate incomes (below $83,730 annually) currently build wealth, identifying three key pathways: business equity, home equity, and retirement savings. The authors argue that understanding these existing mechanisms can inform practitioner and policymaker design of better interventions to reduce economic hardship and improve mobility.
papers.method: Descriptive study exploring wealth and wealth-building patterns among working LMI households aged 25–65; method of data collection and sample size not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Wealth-building pathways are broadly applicable across jurisdictions, though implementation mechanisms vary.
papers.cost_note: Depends entirely on which intervention is designed; not specified in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing housing, small-business, and retirement-savings programs but abstract offers no concrete policy levers—only diagnostic insight.

papers.title: Who Drifted: the System or the Judge? Anytime-Valid Attribution in LLM Evaluation Pipelines
papers.authors: Yitao Li
papers.abstract: Continuous evaluation of LLM products relies on a strong LLM judge treated as ground truth: a cheap monitor scores every interaction and a team is paged when the score drifts down. But the judge is itself a model behind an API, and a silent version bump or scoring-prompt update changes how it scores -- so every drift alarm is ambiguous between a worse product and a changed judge. We resolve the ambiguity with a fixed, human-labeled anchor set that the current judge re-scores at a steady interleave, a second betting e-process on the judge-versus-human gap, and a guard-window rule returning a verdict in {none, system, judge}. We prove anytime-validity, one-way identification (only the judge can move the anchors), an attribution race whose design law is that the anchors must out-run the main process they guard, and process orthogonality. On two real judge changes, a silent version bump is detected as judge drift in 60/60 runs with zero judge-to-system misattribution, and a contaminating strict-prompt change is correctly attributed on 110 of 120 runs at guard width 300 -- while the industry-default rolling z-test false-alarms on 75% of drift-free streams. Every experiment replicates on a second domain (TL;DR summarization) with nothing re-tuned, and where the domains differ the differences are the ones the race predicts: the strict-prompt change shifts scores harder there, so the anchors fire faster and attribution becomes perfect (240/240). The monitor runs at approximately 0.64 of the cost of strong-judging every item, or 0.21 in a cheaper-but-deafer regime.
papers.idea: N/A — This is a methods paper on detecting drift in LLM evaluation systems, not a civic intervention.
papers.summary: The paper presents a statistical technique to distinguish whether performance degradation in an LLM evaluation system is due to the product declining or the judge model changing. It uses human-labeled anchor sets and betting e-processes to attribute drift to either the system or the judge with high accuracy.
papers.method: Algorithmic development with validation on two domains (LLM product evaluation and TL;DR summarization) using real judge version changes; no human subjects or city-level implementation.
papers.scalability_note: Applicable to any LLM-based monitoring pipeline but requires custom implementation per domain.
papers.cost_note: Runs at ~64% of full judging cost; primarily a software optimization with minimal marginal expense.
papers.nyc_note: Technical infrastructure improvement for companies using LLM evaluation; no direct relevance to NYC city services, safety, or resident welfare.

papers.title: A Koopman-PINN Framework for Epidemic Models: Parameter Inference and Forecasting
