papers.authors: Achraf Zinihi, Matthias Ehrhardt, Moulay Rchid Sidi Ammi
papers.abstract: We propose a Koopman-enhanced physics-informed neural network (K--PINN) framework for parameter inference and forecasting in nonlinear epidemic models. This method combines Koopman operator theory and physics-informed learning. It maps epidemic states into a latent observable space where the dynamics evolve approximately linearly while satisfying the governing epidemic equations through automatic differentiation. This integration improves interpretability, parameter identifiability, and long-term predictive stability. We apply the proposed framework to a normalized SEIRSD epidemic model and evaluate it using synthetic monkeypox (Mpox) data and real-world datasets from Germany, Morocco, and Sweden for the SARS-CoV-2 virus. Synthetic trajectories are generated using a structure-preserving, nonstandard finite difference scheme to ensure reliable training data. Numerical results demonstrate that K--PINN achieves more accurate parameter estimation, trajectory reconstruction, and long-term forecasting than classical PINNs and Koopman-EDMD approaches. These results suggest that K--PINN is an effective machine learning framework for epidemic modeling that can be extended to more complex systems.
papers.idea: None identifiable—this is a methodological paper on epidemic forecasting with no policy or program lever.
papers.summary: The authors propose K-PINN, a machine-learning framework combining Koopman operator theory with physics-informed neural networks to improve parameter estimation and forecasting in epidemic models. They demonstrate that it outperforms classical PINNs and EDMD on synthetic monkeypox and real SARS-CoV-2 data from Germany, Morocco, and Sweden.
papers.method: Comparative evaluation of a novel neural-network architecture on synthetic and real epidemic datasets using a SEIRSD model; no randomization, no intervention, no human subjects.
papers.scalability_note: Forecasting tool deployable across jurisdictions if operationalized; depends on uptake by epidemiologists and public-health agencies.
papers.cost_note: Computational overhead modest once trained; deployment cost low relative to traditional epidemiological modeling.
papers.nyc_note: DOHMH could in principle adopt better forecasting; but paper offers no guidance on when/how to act on forecasts, integration with response systems, or local validation.

papers.title: How the Basel Re-Proposal Will Impact the Mortgage Market
papers.authors: Jim Parrott, Laurie Goodman, Mark M. Zandi
papers.abstract: On March 19, 2026, the US banking regulators released a proposal to revise the Basel framework that will govern bank capital requirements. In this paper, the authors discuss how the revised framework would affect the mortgage market. They conclude that it would have a moderate impact as drafted, primarily by incentivizing banks to originate and hold more lower-risk mortgages and encouraging nonbanks in the mortgage market to pursue bank charters or partnerships with banks to take advantage of the changes and remain competitive. The revised framework’s impact would be more substantial if policymakers were to clarify the definition of “regulatory residential real estate exposure,” expand the definition of “securitization,” and give credit for the transfer of mortgage credit risk, attracting greater bank participation in the market and providing a larger mortgage rate reduction for most borrowers.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a federal banking regulation analysis with no city-level policy lever.
papers.summary: The paper analyzes how proposed revisions to the Basel banking framework would affect the mortgage market by incentivizing banks to originate lower-risk mortgages and encouraging nonbank lenders to seek bank partnerships. The authors project moderate impacts on mortgage availability and rates, with potential for larger effects if regulators adopt clarifying policy amendments.
papers.method: Non-empirical policy analysis of proposed federal banking regulations and their projected effects on mortgage market behavior.
papers.scalability_note: Federal regulation applies uniformly nationwide.
papers.cost_note: No per-unit costs stated; implementation is regulatory (low marginal cost to government) but may shift lending costs to banks/borrowers.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has no independent authority over federal banking capital requirements or mortgage market regulation; this is purely a federal policy domain.

papers.title: Phantoms and Disclosures: a Causal Framework for Auditing Synthetic Data
papers.authors: Kareem Amin, Rudrajit Das, Alessandro Epasto, Adel Javanmard, Dennis Kraft, Mónica Ribero, Sergei Vassilvitskii
papers.abstract: The rapid adoption of generative AI and Large Language Models (LLMs) has spurred interest in synthetic data as a privacy-preserving alternative to sensitive real-world datasets. However, generating high-utility synthetic data often carries the risk of memorizing and regurgitating private information from the training corpus. In this work, we present a customizable empirical auditing framework designed to detect and explain such data disclosures. Our framework introduces a mechanism to distinguish between "true disclosures"-where the system directly reproduces a user's information-and "phantom disclosures''-where the system incidentally generates a user's data. By partitioning input data into training and holdout sets and applying rigorous statistical hypothesis testing, we determine if observed disclosures are consistent with strict privacy baselines, such as zero-learning or specific Differential Privacy (DP) bounds. Crucially, this approach requires no model access, no canary insertion, and no reference model training -only the synthetic output and a held-out control set. We demonstrate that this framework effectively functions as a membership inference attack, providing empirical lower bounds on privacy leakage that are tighter than prior data-based auditing methods. Our approach is model-agnostic, applies to any synthetic data generation mechanism, and requires orders of magnitude fewer computational resources than shadow-model or canary-based alternatives.
papers.idea: Use privacy auditing framework to test synthetic datasets before agencies release them publicly.
papers.summary: This paper presents a computational framework for detecting whether generative AI systems have memorized and leaked private information from their training data. The method distinguishes between intentional disclosures and accidental patterns, using only synthetic outputs and held-out control data without requiring model access or canary insertion.
papers.method: Theoretical/computational methods paper demonstrating an auditing framework through statistical hypothesis testing on synthetic vs. held-out data; no empirical application to a real city program or dataset described.
papers.scalability_note: Framework is model-agnostic and computationally lightweight, but adoption requires institutional willingness to audit synthetic data pipelines.
papers.cost_note: Requires only synthetic output and control data; orders of magnitude cheaper than shadow-model alternatives.
papers.nyc_note: NYC agencies do not yet systematically use synthetic data generation; framework is a tool for *if* they do, not a direct service improvement.

papers.title: Area-Level Socioeconomic Inequalities in Adiposity among Older Adults: the Moderating Effect of Neighborhood Walkability
papers.authors: Antoni Colom, Napoleón Pérez-Farinós, Francisco Javier Barón-López, Maurici Ruiz-Pérez, Marc Dominguez Mallafré, Xavier Delclòs-Alió, Antonio Garcia-Rios, Aurora Bueno-Cavanillas, Francisco J. Tinahones, Víctor Urbano-Fernández, José J. Gaforio, Jordi Salas-Salvadó, Montserrat Fitó, Julia Wärnberg
papers.abstract: Abstract Socioeconomic disadvantage is associated with higher obesity risk in older adults, but whether walkable neighborhoods modify these inequalities remains unclear. We examined whether neighborhood walkability moderates the association between area-level socioeconomic status (SES) and adiposity among older adults with overweight or obesity living in Mediterranean cities. This cross-sectional study analyzed baseline data from 1286 urban-dwelling adults aged 55 to 75 years with overweight or obesity and metabolic syndrome residing in six Andalusian cities. Area-level SES was assessed using the 2011 Spanish Deprivation Index, and neighborhood walkability was derived from an open-source index incorporating population density, street intersection density, and access to daily amenities. Adiposity outcomes included waist circumference, waist-to-hip ratio, and a body shape index, all objectively measured. Two-level linear regression models, with individuals nested within census tracts, evaluated SES-walkability interactions while adjusting for demographic and lifestyle factors. No overall differences in adiposity were observed between higher- and lower-SES neighborhoods. However, walkability significantly modified the SES-adiposity association. In highly walkable and dense neighborhoods, participants living in more deprived areas had larger waist circumference and higher body shape index compared with those in less deprived areas, indicating a steeper SES gradient in central adiposity. In low-walkability neighborhoods, SES differences in central adiposity were minimal. No interaction was observed for body mass index. Highly walkable urban environments may therefore amplify socioeconomic inequalities in abdominal obesity among older Mediterranean adults with overweight or obesity. Urban planning strategies promoting walkability should incorporate equity-focused, age-friendly approaches to ensure benefits reach socioeconomically disadvantaged populations.
papers.idea: Cities should design walkable neighborhoods with targeted equity programs to prevent walkability from amplifying socioeconomic disparities in abdominal obesity.
papers.summary: This cross-sectional study of 1,286 older adults in six Mediterranean cities found that neighborhood walkability paradoxically amplified socioeconomic inequalities in central adiposity (waist circumference, body shape index): in highly walkable areas, residents of deprived neighborhoods had worse central obesity outcomes than those in affluent areas, whereas in low-walkability areas, SES differences were minimal. No difference in overall obesity (BMI) was observed by neighborhood SES.
papers.method: Cross-sectional analysis of 1,286 urban-dwelling adults aged 55–75 with overweight/obesity in six Andalusian cities, using census-tract-level SES (Spanish Deprivation Index), walkability (population density, street intersection density, amenity access), and objectively measured adiposity outcomes, with two-level linear regression adjusting for demographic and lifestyle factors.
papers.scalability_note: Walkability metrics are computable at scale; intervention design and equity safeguards are context-dependent and require piloting.
papers.cost_note: Walkability improvements (mixed-use zoning, transit, street design) are moderate-to-high capital; equity interventions add uncertain marginal cost.
papers.nyc_note: Study population (older Mediterranean adults with metabolic syndrome) and findings (walkability-SES interaction) may not generalize to NYC's diverse age/ethnicity/climate; abstract offers no actionable lever beyond vague 'equity-focused approaches.'

papers.title: The Role and Impact of Federal Home Loan Banks
papers.authors: Jung Hyun Choi, Jun Zhu, Laurie Goodman, Bryson Berry, John Walsh, Daniel Pang
papers.abstract: The Federal Home Loan Banks (FHLBanks) are a group of 11 independent, member-owned cooperative regional banks established by Congress in 1932 to promote financial stability and help revive the housing market following the Great Depression. The FHLBanks’ core mission is to provide reliable liquidity to member financial institutions—which include commercial banks, savings institutions, insurance companies, credit unions, and community development financial institutions—to support housing and community development. FHLBanks fulfill their mission by offering members collateralized loans (i.e., advances), standby letters of credit, and mortgage purchases backed by private capital. The FHLBanks are also required by law to contribute 10 percent of their annual net earnings to their Affordable Housing Program to support affordable housing development and expand access to homeownership. Recently, the FHLBanks have increased funding for the program beyond the statutory minimums. The FHLBanks also operate multiple voluntary programs that offer tailored support for local housing and community needs
papers.idea: Cities could partner with Federal Home Loan Banks to access liquidity and affordable housing funding programs.
papers.summary: The paper describes the structure, mission, and programs of the 11 Federal Home Loan Banks, which provide liquidity to member financial institutions and are required to fund affordable housing initiatives. The FHLBanks offer advances, letters of credit, mortgage purchases, and voluntary community development programs, with recent increases in affordable housing contributions beyond statutory minimums.
papers.method: Descriptive review of FHLBank structure, statutory mandates, and program offerings; no empirical study or impact evaluation.
papers.scalability_note: FHLBank programs operate regionally across 11 districts; replicable nationwide but not a city initiative.
papers.cost_note: Programs leverage private capital and member contributions; no per-unit costs stated.
papers.nyc_note: NYC cannot create or directly manage FHLBanks (federal entities); can only advocate for member institutions to participate or lobby for program expansion.

papers.title: The good, the bad, the ugly: A qualitative exploration of life‐course posttraumatic growth for individuals formerly sentenced to juvenile life without parole
papers.authors: Abigail R. Henson, J. Z. Bennett, Megan Forney, Grace Martinez
papers.abstract: Abstract This study explores the profound and often overlooked developmental journeys of individuals sentenced to life without parole as minors. Through in‐depth life‐history interviews with 30 formerly incarcerated people, we examine how they forged personal growth amid the cumulative trauma of long‐term confinement. Our analysis reveals developmental paths aligned with the posttraumatic growth framework, which recognizes how strength and transformation can emerge from profound adversity. Participants shared accounts of how incarceration shaped their identities, values, and sense of purpose, even in the absence of institutional support for meaningful change. Findings challenge readers to reflect on the paradox between the human capacity for change over time and long‐term incarceration, particularly as it relates to individuals who entered confinement during a critical period of ongoing psychosocial development.
papers.idea: N/A — study documents lived experience but does not propose a specific intervention, program, or policy lever.
papers.summary: Through interviews with 30 formerly incarcerated individuals sentenced to juvenile life without parole, the study documents how some experienced personal growth and transformation despite long-term confinement, even absent institutional support. Findings highlight a paradox between human capacity for change and the practice of indefinite juvenile incarceration.
papers.method: Qualitative life-history interviews with 30 formerly incarcerated individuals; posttraumatic growth framework applied to analysis.
papers.scalability_note: Qualitative exploration of individual narratives; no program or intervention to scale.
papers.cost_note: No intervention proposed; research-only.
papers.nyc_note: Study documents a phenomenon rather than a concrete policy or program NYC could adopt. Could inform sentencing or re-entry reform advocacy but does not itself propose actionable mechanics.

papers.title: Evaluating Large Language Models as Judicial Decision-Makers
papers.authors: Oren Gazal Ayal, Zohar Elyoseph, Adir Solomon
papers.idea: Replace or augment human judges with large language models to improve consistency and speed in judicial decisions.
papers.summary: Unable to assess; no abstract provided. Title suggests evaluation of LLMs as judicial decision-makers, likely examining performance, bias, or consistency compared to human judges.
papers.method: Unknown; no abstract available to determine study design, data source, or sample.
papers.scalability_note: Requires major legal/constitutional change; unclear generalization from study to real deployment.
papers.cost_note: LLM infrastructure is moderately expensive; savings vs. judicial salaries unclear.
papers.nyc_note: NYC courts are bound by state judicial processes; replacing judges would require state-level legislation and face massive legal/ethical barriers.

papers.title: Enhancing Policy Impact Through Knowledge Translation: The Role of Innovative Evidence Products
papers.authors:
Ashima Mohan, 
Howard White
papers.abstract: Campbell Systematic Reviews, Volume 21, Issue 4, December 2025.
papers.idea: Improve how research findings are translated and communicated to policymakers to enhance evidence-based policy implementation.
papers.summary: This is a Campbell Collaboration systematic review examining methods and practices for translating research evidence into policy impact. It appears to synthesize existing knowledge about how to better package and communicate research findings to decision-makers.
papers.method: Systematic review of literature on knowledge translation practices and evidence communication strategies (specific sample size and study designs not stated in abstract).
papers.scalability_note: Knowledge translation methods are generalizable but implementation requires institutional buy-in.
papers.cost_note: Primarily involves communication and process redesign; minimal direct costs.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could adopt improved evidence communication practices across agencies, but abstract provides no concrete NYC-relevant interventions.

papers.title: Your AI Travel Agent Would Book You a Bullfight: An Agentic Benchmark for Implicit Animal Welfare in Frontier AI Models
papers.authors: Jasmine Brazilek, Oliver Tulio, Joel Christoph, Miles Tidmarsh, Carol Kline, Arturs Kanepajs
papers.abstract: AI agents are moving from advisors to actors, booking travel, planning menus, and running procurement on behalf of users. Existing benchmarks for AI and animal welfare evaluate model text responses to question-answer prompts, leaving open whether the welfare reasoning surfaced in those responses transfers to agentic deployment where the model must take actions with tools. We introduce TAC (Travel Agent Compassion), the first agentic benchmark measuring whether AI agents avoid options involving animal exploitation when acting on behalf of users. TAC presents an AI agent with twelve hand-authored travel booking scenarios across six categories of animal exploitation, augmented to forty-eight samples to control for price, rating, and position confounds. We evaluate seven frontier models from four labs. Every model scores below the chance level of sixty-four percent, with the best performer (Claude Opus 4.7) at fifty-three percent. A single welfare-aware sentence in the system prompt yields gains of forty-seven to sixty-three percentage points in Claude and GPT-5.5, twenty-six points in GPT-5.2, and under twelve points in DeepSeek and Gemini. An auxiliary Inspect Scout audit of 288 base-condition transcripts from the top two performers, using Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite as judge, flags zero transcripts for evaluation awareness, suggesting the below-chance rates do not stem from the models recognising the evaluation. We discuss implications for category-level variation across cultural domains, the limits of text-response welfare benchmarks, and the EU General-Purpose AI Code of Practice systemic risk framework.
papers.idea: Require welfare-aware system prompts in AI travel-booking tools used by city agencies or tourism boards.
papers.summary: The study benchmarks seven frontier AI models on their tendency to recommend animal-exploitation activities (bullfights, animal attractions) when booking travel. All models performed below chance level (53% best vs 64% baseline), but a single welfare-aware sentence in system prompts improved performance by 26–63 percentage points depending on model.
papers.method: Agentic benchmark (TAC) with 48 hand-authored travel booking scenarios across six animal-exploitation categories, evaluated on seven frontier models; auxiliary audit of 288 transcripts using another model as judge.
papers.scalability_note: Narrow applicability; only relevant to organizations running AI travel-booking agents (not a widespread city service).
papers.cost_note: Minimal cost—adding a system prompt is trivial; no new infrastructure or budget required.
papers.nyc_note: NYC does not operate a public AI travel-booking system; no direct lever for city policy. Animal welfare is important but orthogonal to the civic-safety/housing/transit/health priorities of urban governance.

papers.title: How Much Can We Trust LLM Search Agents? Measuring Endorsement Vulnerability to Web Content Manipulation
papers.authors: Yimeng Chen, Zhe Ren, Firas Laakom, Yu Li, Dandan Guo, Jürgen Schmidhuber
papers.abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based search agents synthesize open-web content into actionable recommendations on behalf of users, creating a risk that attacker-published pages are transformed into endorsed claims. We introduce SearchGEO, a controlled evaluation framework for measuring endorsement corruption in LLM-based web-search agents, combining a web-evidence manipulation pipeline, a five-mode attack taxonomy, and multiple output-level metrics. We evaluate 13 LLM backends on 308 cases each. Results show that vulnerability patterns vary across backends: overall attack success rate (ASR) ranges from 0.0% on Claude-Sonnet-4.6 to 31.4% on Gemini-3-Flash, the strongest attack mode differs by model family, and the same deployment scaffold could amplify or decrease ASR on different backends. An auxiliary agent-skill probe, where endorsement becomes an install command, exposes a sharp split among otherwise robust backends: Claude over-rejects while GPT over-trusts. These findings argue for treating recommendation reliability under adversarial search content as a first-class dimension of backend safety evaluation.
papers.idea: City agencies could audit LLM-based search tools they deploy for public-information services to measure vulnerability to adversarial web-content manipulation.
papers.summary: This paper introduces SearchGEO, a framework for testing whether large language model search agents can be manipulated into endorsing false claims planted on attacker-controlled web pages. Testing 13 LLM backends across 308 cases, the authors found attack success rates ranging from 0% (Claude-Sonnet) to 31.4% (Gemini-3-Flash), with variation in which attack modes work best on different models.
papers.method: Controlled evaluation framework combining web-evidence manipulation pipeline, five-mode attack taxonomy, and output-level metrics applied to 13 LLM backends on 308 test cases each.
papers.scalability_note: Applicable only if a city adopts LLM search agents; methodologically useful but not a scalable intervention itself.
papers.cost_note: Testing framework itself is low-cost; deployment of mitigations would vary.
papers.nyc_note: NYC agencies do not yet widely rely on LLM search agents for public services. Relevant only if/when they do. No immediate policy or program lever.

papers.title: Distributional Forecasting of EU Asylum Applications with Dynamic Multivariate Count Models
papers.authors: Gregor Zens, Jakub Bijak
papers.abstract: We propose a Bayesian framework for joint distributional forecasting of monthly asylum applications across the EU-27. The model decomposes latent application intensities into country-specific random walks and common factors, with idiosyncratic and shared shocks allowed to exhibit heavy tails or stochastic volatility. Using Eurostat data from 2008 to 2026, we evaluate predictive distributions in a rolling out-of-sample exercise, scoring overall distributional accuracy and upper-tail risk. Three findings emerge. First, the preferred specification varies across countries, scoring rules, and horizons, underscoring the need to align models with policy-specific loss functions. Second, joint EU-27 models improve on country-by-country benchmarks, with the largest gains in the upper tail, where preparedness costs are most relevant. Third, random-walk log-intensities provide a useful short-run description of national asylum-application dynamics, especially when combined with flexible innovation dynamics. We conclude by discussing implications for national and EU-level agencies involved in asylum forecasting and preparedness planning.
papers.idea: None—this is a forecasting methodology paper with no actionable intervention lever.
papers.summary: The authors develop a Bayesian statistical model to forecast EU-27 asylum applications month-by-month, decomposing application intensities into country-specific trends and shared factors. The model improves on single-country benchmarks, especially for upper-tail risk forecasting (sudden spikes), and shows that random-walk specifications work well for short-term prediction.
papers.method: Bayesian multivariate count model estimated on Eurostat monthly data (2008–2026), evaluated via rolling out-of-sample distributional forecasts and scoring rules.
papers.scalability_note: A forecasting tool could be deployed across agencies, but implementation depends on uptake by national/EU policy bodies.
papers.cost_note: Statistical modeling is cheap relative to operations; cost is in adoption and integration into planning workflows, not material per-unit cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has no direct asylum-application forecasting role; EU asylum policy is national/supranational. Tangential relevance only to immigrant services planning if NYC wished to forecast intake.

papers.title: The Tone of Awareness: Topic, Sentiment, and Toxicity Maps During Mental Health Month on TikTok
papers.authors: Henrique Ferraz de Arruda, Andreia Sofia Teixeira, Pranay Gundala Reddy, Anindya Mondal, Kleber Andrade Oliveira, Filipi Nascimento Silva
papers.abstract: Despite raising concerns about the mental health effects associated with the usage of TikTok, little is known about how related content is framed by creators and received by audiences. We collect the content of 28,341 TikTok videos and 80,130 comments from Mental Health Awareness Month (May) in 2023 and 2024 via the TikTok Research API, and study how the tone of awareness varies across topics and years. We characterize "tone" as the emotional and interpersonal framing of mental health discourse, operationalized through sentiment and toxicity measures. We extract topics from video text using BERTopic and log-odds keywords, then quantify topic-conditioned sentiment (XLM-T) and toxicity (Detoxify) separately for video transcriptions and comments. Sentiment captures the affective valence of content, while toxicity reflects the presence of harmful or abusive language. We find a stable set of recurring themes across years, spanning clinical conditions, emotional disclosure, self-care, and campaign-oriented content, with engagement highly skewed toward a small subset of topics. All sentiment and toxicity analyses are computed separately for video content and comments, allowing us to distinguish between content production and audience reception. Sentiment in videos is often negative for emotionally charged topics, while comments tend to shift toward more mixed or positive polarity, especially for suicide prevention. Toxicity is low in median overall, but exhibits longer-tailed outliers in comments than in videos that are more pronounced in comments and concentrated in specific topics (e.g., "Duet", "Suicide Prevention", and "Psychisch"). Overall, our results provide a topic-level decomposition of mental health discourse on TikTok during awareness-month campaigns.
papers.idea: No actionable intervention identified; this is a descriptive measurement study of social media discourse.
papers.summary: The study analyzed 28,341 TikTok videos and 80,130 comments from Mental Health Awareness Month (May 2023–2024) to characterize how mental health content is framed by creators and received by audiences. Researchers found recurring themes (clinical conditions, emotional disclosure, self-care, campaigns), with negative sentiment in videos about emotionally charged topics but mixed/positive sentiment in comments; toxicity was generally low but with outlier spikes in specific topics like suicide prevention.
papers.method: Cross-sectional content analysis of TikTok videos and comments collected via TikTok Research API, with automated topic extraction (BERTopic), sentiment analysis (XLM-T), and toxicity detection (Detoxify) applied to transcriptions and comments separately.
papers.scalability_note: Passive measurement of existing discourse; no intervention to scale.
papers.cost_note: Zero implementation cost; observation-only study with no program or service delivery.
papers.nyc_note: While NYC could theoretically monitor mental health discourse on social platforms, the study offers no actionable policy lever, intervention design, or evidence base for a city program. It is descriptive only.

papers.title: State Revenues Continue to Grow Amid Fiscal Pressures
papers.authors: Lucy Dadayan
papers.abstract: State and local tax revenues posted modest growth during the first half of fiscal year 2026. Overall, gains were driven by strong personal income tax collections concentrated in a few states with progressive tax structures and high reliance on high-income taxpayers, where growth was closely tied to strong stock market performance and elevated capital gains realizations. However, these gains masked a broader pattern of uneven and generally subdued revenue growth across states, with many states seeing little or no growth. States continue to face mounting uncertainty from both economic and policy developments, including elevated oil prices associated with geopolitical tensions, renewed inflationary pressures, and ongoing federal policy uncertainty.
papers.idea: None identified—this is a fiscal forecast with no concrete policy intervention.
papers.summary: State and local tax revenues grew modestly in the first half of FY2026, driven primarily by strong personal income tax collections in a few progressive-tax states tied to stock market gains and capital gains. However, broader revenue growth across states remains uneven and subdued, with mounting economic and policy uncertainties.
papers.method: Fiscal monitoring/analysis of state revenue data during FY2026; no experimental or quasi-experimental design described.
papers.scalability_note: Forecast/diagnostic only; no intervention to scale.
papers.cost_note: No implementation costs—analysis only.
papers.nyc_note: Identifies revenue pressures NYC faces but offers no actionable lever for city policy.

papers.title: Homeownership Among Young Adults Has Fallen, and It’s Worse Than You Think
papers.authors: Jung Hyun Choi, Laurie Goodman, Jun Zhu
papers.abstract: As housing has become less affordable, young adults are feeling the pinch the most. The homeownership rate among adults ages 25 to 34 is lower than for previous generations at the same age, and more attention has been directed toward alternate ways young adults might purchase a home. But even this dire situation may be worse than it seems. According to the American Community Survey, the homeownership rate for household heads ages 25 to 34 declined sharply following financial crisis—from 47 percent in 2005 to 37 percent in 2015—before partially recovering to 40 percent by 2024. But this conventional measure overstates the extent of the recovery. The traditional homeownership rate is calculated as the share of households that are owner occupied. This measure excludes young adults who have not formed independent households, such as those living with parents, other relatives, or roommates. Because independent household formation declined substantially between 2005
papers.idea: None identified; this is a measurement/diagnostic paper, not an intervention proposal.
papers.summary: Young adult homeownership (ages 25–34) has declined from 47% in 2005 to 40% in 2024, but conventional measures overstate recovery because they exclude young adults living with family or roommates rather than forming independent households. The study shows that lack of independent household formation masks how severe the crisis actually is.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis using American Community Survey data comparing homeownership rates and household formation across cohorts and time periods (2005–2024).
papers.scalability_note: Measurement approach is universally applicable; no policy lever specified.
papers.cost_note: Research-only; no intervention cost data provided.
papers.nyc_note: Identifies a problem but offers no actionable NYC policy or program; housing affordability solutions require separate evidence.

papers.title: Who’s Involved and What Factors Are Considered in Decisions About School District Secessions?
papers.authors: Harley Webley, Luisa Godinez-Puig, Karishma Furtado, Adriana Vance
papers.abstract: Since 2000, 19 states have had school district secessions, which occur when a community leaves the district it was part of, creating both a new and a left-behind district. Secessions can reshape the distribution of educational resources and opportunities, often concentrating them in the new district. But how secessions happen—specifically the laws and regulations used to approve or deny a secession—differ from place to place. Relying on previous work, we analyzed state laws and regulations that define the criteria and steps used to approve or deny secession efforts in the 19 states with successful secessions since 2000, according to our database. We find that laws regulating secession vary significantly across states, often leaving decisionmakers with incomplete or inconsistent information to assess potential impacts. But state policymakers can help. They could provide communities and local policymakers with standardized guidance for secession, such as planning templates and data sources, and require that
papers.idea: States could adopt standardized guidance templates and data requirements to help communities and local officials assess school district secession impacts more consistently.
papers.summary: The study analyzed state laws and regulations governing school district secessions (where communities leave existing districts) across 19 states since 2000. Authors found that secession laws vary widely across states, often leaving decision-makers with incomplete or inconsistent information about impacts. The research suggests state policymakers could provide standardized planning templates and data sources to improve secession decision-making.
papers.method: Comparative legal and regulatory analysis of state laws governing school district secessions in 19 states with successful secessions since 2000, based on authors' database.
papers.scalability_note: State-level policy intervention; would require legislative action; applicable across multiple states but implementation varies by jurisdiction.
papers.cost_note: Low-cost intervention (templates, data standardization); primary cost is staff time for policy development and dissemination.
papers.nyc_note: NYC operates as a single unified school district; school district secession is not a live governance issue in the city. Research is primarily relevant to suburban/regional contexts.

papers.title: When AI Says "I have been in similar situations": Synthetic Lived Experience in Peer-Like Caregiver Support
papers.authors: Drishti Goel, Violeta J. Rodriguez, Daniel S. Brown, Ravi Karkar, Dong Whi Yoo, Koustuv Saha
papers.abstract: Caregivers often turn to online communities for informational and emotional support. In these spaces, peer supporters frequently draw on personal narratives to respond to emotionally complex caregiving situations. As LLMs are increasingly designed as peer-like sources of support, they introduce a critical tension: AI can provide immediate, private, and nonjudgmental support, but it cannot authentically possess the lived experiences that make human peer support meaningful. Yet, when prompted to sound peer-like, LLMs may generate language that implies lived experience. This creates a synthetic lived experience paradox: the same experiential language that may make AI support feel warm, relatable, and peer-like can also falsely position the system as someone with lived experience. We examine this paradox in the context of family caregivers of people living with Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias (ADRD). Drawing on caregiver support exchanges from online communities and prompted peer-like responses from three LLMs -- LLaMA, GPT-4o-mini, and MedGemma -- we analyze how human peers use personal narratives and how AI incorporates similar narrative forms. Psycholinguistic analysis shows that peer responses used significantly more first-person and past-focused language than peer-like AI responses. Qualitatively, we identify seven types of personal narratives in human peer support and show that AI often captures their emotional work, but can fabricate experiential grounding. These findings reveal a narrative authenticity gap: peer-like AI can generate synthetic lived experience without the real experience that makes peer support meaningful. We argue that caregiver-support AI systems need mechanisms to distinguish supportive peer-like framing from fabricated lived experience, ensuring that models can offer warmth and validation without falsely positioning themselves as experiential peers.
papers.idea: Develop design guidelines for AI peer-support systems to distinguish authentic peer support from fabricated lived experience, enabling cities to responsibly deploy AI caregiving assistants.
papers.summary: The study analyzed how human peer supporters and three LLMs (LLaMA, GPT-4o-mini, MedGemma) respond to Alzheimer's caregivers in online communities. It found that peer-like AI can generate emotionally supportive language that mimics lived experience without authentic grounding, creating a "narrative authenticity gap." The researchers call for mechanisms to ensure AI caregiving systems offer warmth and validation without falsely positioning themselves as experiential peers.
papers.method: Psycholinguistic analysis of caregiver exchanges from online communities and prompted LLM responses; qualitative coding of seven narrative types in human peer support versus AI responses; no stated sample size.
papers.scalability_note: Design principles could inform multiple AI systems, but requires case-by-case implementation and user-testing.
papers.cost_note: Primarily a design/training issue for existing LLM platforms; minimal marginal cost once guidelines developed.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could incorporate these principles into caregiving AI deployed via public health programs or DOHMH digital health initiatives, but the study offers no specific policy mechanism or local proof of concept.

papers.title: Measuring Whether LLM Tutors Teach or Solve: A Diagnostic for Educational Impact
papers.authors: Junyi Yao, Zihao Zheng, Baichuan Li
papers.abstract: Large language models are increasingly proposed as educational tutors, yet stronger task-solving ability does not necessarily imply stronger learning support. Motivated by recent calls to measure the social impact of NLP systems in practice, we study whether public LLM tutoring benchmarks distinguish learning-supportive behavior from mere answer production. We propose a lightweight diagnostic based on the gap between solving-oriented and pedagogy-oriented benchmark performance. Using public MathTutorBench leaderboard results, we show that these dimensions are only partially aligned: across eight publicly reported models, the correlation between solving and pedagogy composites is 0.421, and several models shift meaningfully in rank when evaluation moves from solving to pedagogy. We then analyze the public TutorBench sample and show that agency-relevant behaviors are explicitly encoded in benchmark rubrics, especially in active-learning settings that reward guiding questions, calibrated hints, and non-disclosive scaffolding. Together, these findings suggest that educational-impact evaluation should not treat task success as a sufficient proxy for learning support. We argue that public tutoring benchmarks can better support positive-impact evaluation by reporting solving-oriented and pedagogy-oriented scores separately and by making disclosure-sensitive, student-agency-preserving criteria more explicit.
papers.idea: City education agencies should adopt separate pedagogical and task-solving evaluation metrics when procuring or deploying LLM tutoring systems.
papers.summary: The paper proposes a diagnostic to measure whether large language models function as effective educational tutors versus mere answer-generators. Analyzing public benchmarks (MathTutorBench, TutorBench), the authors found solving ability and pedagogical quality correlate weakly (r=0.421), and models rank differently depending on which dimension is measured. The study highlights that tutoring effectiveness depends on behaviors like guided questioning and student agency, not just correct answers.
papers.method: Comparative analysis of public leaderboard results from eight LLM models on existing tutoring benchmarks, with rubric-based coding of behaviors that preserve student agency.
papers.scalability_note: Applicable wherever LLMs are deployed in education, but requires benchmark redesign rather than scaling a single intervention.
papers.cost_note: Largely a measurement/evaluation framework with minimal direct implementation cost; savings come from avoided poor procurement decisions.
papers.nyc_note: Relevant only if/when NYC schools consider adopting LLM tutoring systems; currently a niche use case with no indication of city-scale deployment.

papers.title: What is the public's social welfare function?
papers.authors: Richard Layard, Ekaterina Oparina
papers.abstract: Optimal public policy requires a social welfare function defined over individual utilities. While there is substantial research on income-based social welfare functions, no published study has directly elicited public preferences over utility when measured by subjective wellbeing. Using a novel survey instrument with a representative UK sample (N=2,068), we estimate the public's social welfare function for life satisfaction. We find significant aversion to wellbeing inequality, with a median isoelastic parameter $α$=0.48. This implies a social welfare function approximately equal to the sum of square roots of individual utilities. The median respondent values improving the wellbeing of the least satisfied by one unit roughly twice as much as improving the most satisfied by one unit. Our findings provide ethically grounded distributional weights for wellbeing policy evaluation and cost-benefit analysis.
papers.idea: Use empirically derived social welfare weights that prioritize improving wellbeing of the least satisfied to guide distributional choices in cost-benefit analysis of public policies.
papers.summary: This study directly surveyed UK residents (N=2,068) to estimate their preferences over how to distribute wellbeing improvements across populations. Respondents showed strong aversion to wellbeing inequality, with a median preference implying that improving life satisfaction of the least satisfied is roughly twice as valuable as improving the most satisfied.
papers.method: Representative UK survey (N=2,068) using a novel instrument to elicit public preferences over utility distributions; estimates isoelastic social welfare function parameters.
papers.scalability_note: Findings are country-specific preferences; would require re-elicitation in US context and integration into existing policy frameworks.
papers.cost_note: One-time research cost; implementation adds minimal marginal cost (adjusting weights in existing cost-benefit analyses).
papers.nyc_note: Provides ethical guidance for weighting distributional impacts in policy evaluation, but is UK-based, abstract, and requires institutional adoption of wellbeing-focused CBA methodology not yet standard in NYC agencies.

papers.title: State Profiles of Medicaid Managed Care and Type 2 Diabetes
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: Type 2 diabetes is among the most prevalent and costly chronic conditions covered by Medicaid, affecting a significant share of the low-income adults who rely on the program for coverage and care. Most states deliver Medicaid benefits through comprehensive, risk-based managed care, contracting with managed care organizations (MCOs) to coordinate and provide services for enrolled beneficiaries. State Medicaid agencies set program requirements and payment requirements, while MCOs administer covered benefits within those parameters. Many MCOs are owned by larger parent companies—including both for-profit and nonprofit entities—that operate across multiple states. We developed a series of state profiles to provide a structured, comparable overview of how comprehensive Medicaid managed care programs address type 2 diabetes. The profiles focus on programs using this model to serve adults without disabilities; we do not include programs serving only children or individuals with disabilities. Each profile covers five areas: the structure and history of the
papers.idea: Cities could benchmark Medicaid managed care diabetes programs against state profiles to identify best-practice care coordination models.
papers.summary: This work creates comparable state-level profiles of how Medicaid managed care organizations (MCOs) address type 2 diabetes in low-income adults. The profiles map program structure, MCO ownership, payment models, and benefit design across states.
papers.method: Structured comparative state-profile analysis of Medicaid managed care program documentation and design parameters; no empirical outcome data or evaluation mentioned in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Profile methodology is replicable across jurisdictions; but requires state-level policy authority to implement changes.
papers.cost_note: Implementation cost depends on MCO contract renegotiation; profiling itself is low-cost research.
papers.nyc_note: NYC does not set Medicaid policy (state does); city has limited direct leverage over MCO contracting. Value mainly as input to state advocacy.

papers.title: Leveraging AI to Measure Mobility-Relevant Competencies from Student Volunteer Reflections
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: This project uses a unique combination of data from a large-scale, lieutenant governor–backed high school service program and rich administrative datasets from Utah. These data will be used to develop measures of self-management, relationship skills, and self-awareness through large-scale, AI-informed text analysis of student reflections. By integrating administrative records with the Panorama Student Survey, the Youth Civic and Character Measures Toolkit, and reflective writing generated through the statewide service initiative, the team will assess whether AI-based measures capture the same socioemotional competencies identified by validated survey instruments and determine which of these skills are most strongly correlated with postsecondary outcomes and civic engagement. Linking AI-derived SEL indicators to mobility-boosting college enrollment patterns and civic behaviors such as voter registration, the study offers an innovative test of whether scalable AI methods can reliably measure the skills most predictive of students’ educational and civic trajectories. Data Sources Data: Student reflections on volunteer
papers.idea: Use AI to analyze student volunteer reflections to measure socioemotional competencies that predict college enrollment and civic engagement.
papers.summary: This is a methods-development study, not a report of findings. The abstract describes a planned project to use AI text analysis on student reflections from a Utah volunteer service program to measure self-management, relationship skills, and self-awareness, then validate these AI measures against existing surveys and correlate them with postsecondary outcomes and civic behaviors.
papers.method: Planned research combining AI-informed text analysis of student reflections from a lieutenant governor–backed Utah high school service program with administrative data, Panorama Student Survey, and Youth Civic and Character Measures Toolkit; exact sample size not stated.
papers.scalability_note: AI text-analysis methods are scalable once developed, but requires volunteer/service program infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Development and validation costs unclear from abstract; relies on existing administrative datasets and volunteer program.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has service programs but no equivalent statewide lieutenant governor–backed initiative; would require new infrastructure and data integration.

papers.title: PROTOCOL: Understanding the Content, Context, and Impact of Far‐Right Extremist Propaganda Disseminated Online: A Systematic Review
papers.authors:
Mia Doolan, 
Katie Cox, 
Kiran M. Sarma
papers.abstract: Campbell Systematic Reviews, Volume 21, Issue 4, December 2025.
papers.idea: Not identified—this is a protocol for a systematic review of propaganda content analysis, not an intervention or policy lever.
papers.summary: This is a protocol (pre-registered plan) for a Campbell Collaboration systematic review that will examine far-right extremist propaganda online: its content, context, and documented impacts. No findings are presented because the review has not yet been conducted.
papers.method: Protocol paper (methodology blueprint) for a planned systematic review; actual empirical data collection and synthesis not yet completed.
papers.scalability_note: Protocol only; no intervention to scale.
papers.cost_note: No intervention; cost only to conduct the review itself.
papers.nyc_note: Once completed, findings might inform city counter-extremism strategy, but the protocol stage offers no actionable policy input.

papers.title: How More People Are Supporting Their Aging Parents Financially, and How It Might Affect Their Financial Futures
papers.authors: Mingli Zhong
papers.abstract: Intergenerational financial support doesn’t flow only from parent to child. According to a new Urban Institute brief, more adult children are providing financial assistance and elder care to aging parents—a phenomenon known as “reverse transfers.” In an analysis of the Health and Retirement Study (HRS) supported by AARP, we find that about 13 percent of adult children provided financial support to aging parents between 2010 and 2022. That’s more than double the share that provided support before 2010 (5 percent). These transfers can help older adults meet their basic needs as public safety nets and retirement savings fall short in a time of heightened affordability pressures. But they may simultaneously limit younger adults’ ability to save, invest, or support their own children, potentially hindering upward mobility and wealth building across generations. Older adults of color are more likely to receive assistance from a child, reflecting systemic barriers to wealth building
papers.idea: Cities could establish reverse-transfer financial counseling or elder-care subsidy programs to reduce burden on adult children supporting aging parents.
papers.summary: Between 2010 and 2022, the share of adult children providing financial support to aging parents more than doubled from 5% to 13%, a trend called 'reverse transfers.' While this helps older adults meet basic needs, it may constrain younger adults' savings, investment, and wealth building—effects that disproportionately affect families of color due to systemic wealth barriers.
papers.method: Analysis of the Health and Retirement Study (HRS) survey data from 2010–2022, supported by AARP.
papers.scalability_note: Financial counseling and elder subsidies exist but would require redesign; reverse-transfer support is a niche intervention.
papers.cost_note: Elder care and income-support programs are costly; abstract provides no cost data.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has aging and poverty services but no explicit reverse-transfer policy lever; would require new program design.

papers.title: Informative Missingness to Generate Irregular Clinical Time Series
papers.authors: Hadi Mehdizavareh, Gabriele Santangelo, Giovanna Nicora, Simon Lebech Cichosz, Arianna Dagliati, Arijit Khan, Riccardo Bellazzi
papers.abstract: Laboratory tests in electronic health records are collected irregularly, and the absence of a test order can be as informative as the measurement itself. Such missingness reflects clinicians' decisions and patient physiology, making it important to model it directly rather than treat it as a preprocessing artifact. Here we present a diffusion-based approach for generating clinical time series that jointly models laboratory values and their observation patterns using the public Data Analytics Challenge on Missing Data Imputation (DACMI) benchmark derived from MIMIC-III. To preserve realistic sampling, we align chart times into 4-hour intervals and segment admissions into 7-day windows, producing trajectories that pair each lab value with a corresponding observation indicator. Standard transformations and normalization are applied to stabilize training. Our method extends the TimeDiff framework to learn continuous lab values and discrete missingness patterns through complementary diffusion objectives. Experiments show that the generated data closely match real patient trajectories across individual lab distributions and joint value-missingness embeddings, demonstrating that diffusion models can capture clinically meaningful dependencies between patient physiology and clinicians' testing behavior under MNAR-like (missing-not-at-random) missingness. These preliminary results indicate that our model can serve as an initial component toward developing clinical foundation models. By producing synthetic priors that preserve key physiology-missingness relationships, this work motivates the subsequent training of Prior-Data Fitted Networks capable of leveraging informative missingness, which we will investigate in the extended work.
papers.idea: None—this is a methods paper on synthetic data generation for clinical time series; no actionable intervention.
papers.summary: The paper presents a diffusion-based machine learning approach to generate synthetic clinical time series data that jointly model laboratory test values and missingness patterns. The method was validated on MIMIC-III hospital data, showing that generated synthetic trajectories closely matched real patient physiology-testing behavior relationships.
papers.method: Technical validation using the DACMI benchmark derived from MIMIC-III electronic health records; synthetic data generation compared to real patient trajectories on distributional and embedding metrics.
papers.scalability_note: The underlying algorithm could scale, but it solves a narrow methodological problem (synthetic EHR generation) rather than a health outcome.
papers.cost_note: Once developed, synthetic data generation is cheap to run; development cost is moderate but one-time.
papers.nyc_note: No direct policy or program lever for NYC. Could potentially support future research infrastructure, but requires subsequent applied work to yield any public health benefit.

papers.title: Skill vs Education Types of Labour Mismatch and Their Association with Earnings
papers.authors: Vsevolod Iakovlev
papers.abstract: This paper analyses the distinction between educational and skill types of labour mismatch and their association with earnings. Drawing on cross-sectional data for 26 countries from the 1st Cycle of the OECD (2012) Survey of Adult Skills (PIAAC), I examine educational and skill mismatch using a comprehensive set of education- and skill-based indicators, explore heterogeneity across worker characteristics, and investigate the sources of conflicting country-level correlations with earnings through an error components model. The results show that country-level unobserved heterogeneity induces endogeneity bias, with both its direction and magnitude varying across mismatch measures. Once unobserved heterogeneity is controlled for, over-education and over-skilling are associated with wage penalties, whereas under-education and under-skilling are linked to wage premiums. These findings highlight both conceptual and empirical distinctions between educational and skill mismatch and demonstrate the importance of indicator choice in the analysis.
papers.summary: The study distinguishes between educational mismatch (credential level vs. job requirement) and skill mismatch (actual ability vs. job need) using OECD data from 26 countries, finding that over-education and over-skilling correlate with wage penalties once country-level unobserved factors are controlled for, while under-education and under-skilling show wage premiums.
papers.method: Cross-sectional analysis of OECD PIAAC Survey of Adult Skills (2012) across 26 countries using error components modeling to address endogeneity bias from unobserved heterogeneity.
papers.scalability_note: International comparative study; findings are descriptive correlations without identified policy levers.
papers.cost_note: Minimal direct cost to implement findings, but study offers no concrete intervention to cost.
papers.nyc_note: Study is retrospective measurement of wage correlations with no program design, implementation pathway, or NYC-specific context.

papers.title: Structuring Transparency: Developing Domain-Specific Generative AI Declaration Frameworks in Higher Education
papers.authors: Nicholas Micallef, Olga Petrovska
papers.abstract: As Generative AI (GenAI) disrupts higher education, institutions increasingly require students to declare AI use. However, generic, binary declarations (e.g., "I used GenAI") fail to capture the nuanced application of these tools in different academic tasks. Establishing transparency is key to protecting academic integrity, promoting AI literacy, and shifting the focus from policing to professional practice. In response, this paper contributes a design artefact and an accompanying position: a framework of two task-specific declaration structures, one for writing-focused activities and one for coding assessments, developed for a Computer Science department on the basis of an existing taxonomy of GenAI usage, together with an argument that task-specific disclosure is needed to move beyond binary declarations. By categorising AI usage across specific cognitive and developmental stages, such as structural planning vs. Textual Content Generation, or code improvement vs. code generation, the framework encourages students to reflect on their own learning process and clarifies the boundary between acceptable assistance and academic misconduct. We propose this domain-specific approach as a foundation for fostering more honest assessment in Computer Science and other disciplines, aiming to better prepare students for professional environments where documenting GenAI workflows might be an essential job requirement.
papers.idea: Higher education institutions could adopt task-specific AI declaration frameworks to improve academic integrity and AI literacy among students.
papers.summary: The paper proposes a framework for domain-specific AI disclosure statements in higher education, tailored to writing and coding tasks, that move beyond binary declarations to capture nuanced AI usage across cognitive stages. The authors argue this approach encourages student reflection on learning and clarifies boundaries between acceptable assistance and misconduct. The framework was developed for a Computer Science department based on an existing taxonomy of GenAI usage.
papers.method: Design artefact development within a Computer Science department, informed by existing GenAI usage taxonomy; no empirical validation or outcome measurement reported in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Framework is education-specific and requires institutional adoption; minimal civic/municipal lever.
papers.cost_note: Primarily administrative redesign of existing declaration processes; low direct cost to implement.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could require CUNY/SUNY systems to adopt, but this is higher-ed policy not core municipal governance or public-facing service; indirect city benefit.

papers.title: Unequal Residential Gains and Losses Under Rising Housing Prices: Evidence from Longitudinal Relocation Behavior
papers.authors: Ling Li, Ping Huang, Yusi Luo, Shujin Yu
papers.idea: N/A — no intervention identified
papers.summary: This appears to be a longitudinal study examining how rising housing prices affect residents' relocation decisions and wealth outcomes across different socioeconomic groups. Without the abstract, the specific findings are unclear, but the title suggests distributional analysis of housing-price effects.
papers.method: Longitudinal relocation behavior analysis (design, sample, and setting cannot be determined from title alone)
papers.scalability_note: Descriptive/analytical study; no intervention to scale
papers.cost_note: Research only; no program cost identified
papers.nyc_note: Findings may inform NYC housing policy but study presents no concrete actionable lever or program design

papers.title: New Evidence Shows the FHA Can Make Sustainable Zero-Down Payment Mortgages
papers.authors: Alexei Alexandrov, Laurie Goodman, Ted Tozer, Sam Valverde
papers.abstract: In this report, we make the case that Congress should permit the Federal Housing Administration (FHA) to insure zero–down payment mortgages for first-time homebuyers. The down payment is the largest single barrier to homeownership. Many first-time homebuyers rely on family assistance for a down payment, and allowing zero–down payment mortgages would level the playing field for potential homeowners without family wealth. If Congress were to allow the FHA to insure zero–down payment loans, it could result in millions of renter households transitioning to homeownership earlier, giving homeowners more time to build housing wealth. Moreover, FHA zero–down payment loans would also replace the inconsistent patchwork of down payment assistance programs. What We Found Buying a home requires a sufficient amount of income, a high enough credit score, and enough savings for a down payment. We quantified that millions of borrowers have the income and credit score to meet underwriting criteria but
papers.idea: Congress should authorize the FHA to insure zero-down-payment mortgages for first-time homebuyers to reduce the down-payment barrier to homeownership.
papers.summary: The authors argue that down payments are the largest barrier to homeownership and propose that FHA-insured zero-down mortgages could enable millions of renters to transition to homeownership earlier and build housing wealth. They quantify that millions of borrowers meet income and credit criteria but lack sufficient savings.
papers.method: Non-empirical policy brief; abstract indicates quantification of borrower populations meeting underwriting criteria but does not describe an empirical study design or data source.
papers.scalability_note: Federal policy lever, but requires Congressional action; not a city-level intervention.
papers.cost_note: No per-unit cost stated; cost depends on FHA loss rates under zero-down scenario.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has no independent authority over FHA underwriting standards; relies on federal policy change. NYC could advocate for it but cannot implement unilaterally.

papers.title: Pinpointing When Economically Valuable Skill Gaps Emerge and Where Intervention Can Make a Difference
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: This project builds on an ongoing SUMI project that identifies which skill groupings from item-level standardized test data drive adult wages at age 25. Early findings have revealed a striking pattern: Specific item-anchored skills that drive economic mobility are largely similar between white and Hispanic students in third grade, but the gap widens sharply at middle school entry and is never recovered, even as high school narrows gaps in the skills needed to get a high school diploma. Yet critical questions remain regarding why this divergence in mobility-relevant skills occurs at the middle school transition, what skills are being emphasized in high school to reduce the diploma gap but not the wage gap, and whether the picture changes when tracking wages through age 30 rather than age 25. This project will directly address these remaining questions by leveraging existing Texas administrative data to identify precisely when and where disparities in
papers.idea: Identify the specific skills that diverge between white and Hispanic students at middle school transition and design targeted interventions to close wage-relevant skill gaps.
papers.summary: This ongoing research uses Texas administrative data linked to standardized test item responses and adult wages to pinpoint when economically valuable skill gaps emerge between racial/ethnic groups. Early findings show that wage-relevant skills are similar between white and Hispanic third graders but diverge sharply at middle school entry, a gap never recovered despite high school efforts to close diploma-gap skills.
papers.method: Longitudinal analysis of Texas administrative data linking item-level standardized test scores to adult wages at ages 25 and 30, identifying which specific tested skills predict later earnings.
papers.scalability_note: Study is diagnostically focused on Texas data; findings may not generalize to NYC's demographics, curricula, and labor markets without local replication.
papers.cost_note: Research cost moderate; interventions derived from findings would depend on implementation type and scale—costs unknown.
papers.nyc_note: No NYC-specific data or interventions proposed; would require separate NYC analysis before any local policy adoption.

papers.title: Asian immigrants, school quality, and the U.S. housing market
papers.authors: Amanda Ang, Eunjee Kwon, Siqi Zheng
papers.idea: N/A — study is descriptive/correlational with no identified intervention lever.
papers.summary: This appears to be an observational study examining correlations between Asian immigrant settlement patterns, school quality, and housing market outcomes. Without an abstract, the specific findings cannot be determined, but the title suggests analysis of how educational and demographic factors relate to housing decisions.
papers.method: Unknown; likely regression analysis of housing market data linked to school quality and immigrant demographic data, but design cannot be confirmed without abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Descriptive/correlational finding with no clear policy intervention identified.
papers.cost_note: Unknown; appears to be academic research rather than a program requiring implementation cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has large Asian immigrant populations and school-choice dynamics, but paper does not identify actionable policy or program city could adopt.

papers.title: PROTOCOL: New‐Onset Diabetes Mellitus Post COVID‐19 Infection: A Systematic Review and Meta‐Analysis
papers.authors:
Emma Cocking, 
Joseph Daher, 
Majid Alabbood
papers.abstract: Campbell Systematic Reviews, Volume 21, Issue 4, December 2025.
papers.idea: Post-COVID diabetes screening and management protocols for healthcare systems.
papers.summary: This is a systematic review and meta-analysis examining the association between COVID-19 infection and new-onset diabetes mellitus. It synthesizes evidence on prevalence and risk factors but does not describe an intervention or program that cities could implement.
papers.method: Systematic review and meta-analysis of existing studies on post-COVID diabetes outcomes.
papers.scalability_note: Clinical protocols can be scaled, but the study itself is a literature synthesis, not an actionable intervention.
papers.cost_note: Screening and management costs vary; abstract provides no cost data.
papers.nyc_note: NYC health department could use findings to inform screening guidance, but study offers no new intervention model or city-specific lever.

papers.title: Blindly Discriminating: The GI Bill and Racial Inequality
papers.authors: Lukas Althoff, Christiane Szerman
papers.abstract: The World War II GI Bill was the largest education subsidy in US history and a cornerstone of the postwar US transition to a knowledge economy. Although formally race-blind, the program's decentralized administration left implementation to local officials and segregated institutions, with sharply different consequences for Black and white veterans. This paper quantifies the GI Bill's impact on Black and white Americans' economic outcomes across two generations, using a regression discontinuity around WWII service eligibility cutoffs and a new data linkage from veterans in the 1940 and 1950 censuses to their sons' neighborhood outcomes between 1990 and 2025. The GI Bill widened racial inequality, doubling white veterans' college completion while steering Black veterans into often-fraudulent vocational programs with no earnings returns. The disparities persisted across generations, increasing the white-Black gap in sons' adult-neighborhood outcomes, including a 5-percentage-point (47 percent) widening of the racial college gap. Unequal returns to the same eligibility account for the intergenerational gap, with no contribution from prewar differences in socioeconomic status or geography. In sum, access to the GI Bill was not nearly the same economic opportunity for Black Americans as it was for white Americans, highlighting that race-blind policy does not guarantee racial equality.
papers.idea: None—this is a historical analysis with no actionable policy lever identified.
papers.summary: This paper quantifies how the formally race-blind GI Bill produced sharply unequal outcomes for Black and white WWII veterans due to decentralized, segregated implementation, and how those disparities persisted intergenerationally in sons' outcomes through 1990–2025. The program doubled white veterans' college completion while steering Black veterans into fraudulent vocational programs with no earnings returns, widening the racial college attainment and neighborhood-outcome gaps across generations.
papers.method: Regression discontinuity around WWII service eligibility cutoffs, linked to 1940 and 1950 census data on veterans and matched to their sons' neighborhood outcomes 1990–2025.
papers.scalability_note: Historical policy analysis; GI Bill no longer exists in this form and cannot be redesigned by cities.
papers.cost_note: No intervention; no cost analysis applicable.
papers.nyc_note: Historical analysis of a defunct federal program; no municipal policy lever or implementation pathway.

papers.title: IUU+DB: Tracking Illegal, Unreported, and Unregulated Fishing, Seafood Fraud, and Labor Abuse through LLM-driven Information Extraction
papers.authors: Henry Bodwell, Hong Yang, John C. Simeone, Kelvin Gorospe, Bella Sullivan, Lana Huang, Jessica Gephart, Sandy Aylesworth, Molly Masterton, Naren Ramakrishnan
papers.abstract: Illegal, unreported, and unregulated fishing (IUU) traditionally refers to fishing activities that violate applicable laws or occur in areas that lack applicable laws. We propose the term IUU+ to capture a broader suite of fisheries sector environmental and associated supply chain trade-related crimes and behaviors. Although IUU+ activity is widely recognized as a serious threat to marine ecosystems, markets, and livelihoods, a quantitative understanding of these incidents, e.g., their frequency, geography, species, actors, and patterns in the type of illicit activity, remains difficult to obtain. We propose IUU+DB, a large language model driven system for building a global incident database of IUU+ activity. The system ingests heterogeneous documents, classifies whether they describe relevant incidents, extracts key data elements such as actors, locations, species, vessels, violations, and enforcement outcomes, and supports deduplication and trend analysis. Case studies and validation results show that IUU+DB can help organize fragmented evidence, surface geographic and behavioral hotspots, support fisheries-domain specific research in academia and non-government organizations, assist source and species risk assessments for industry, and provide support for policy implementation and targeted enforcement efforts to government agencies.
papers.idea: N/A — methods paper for global seafood supply-chain tracking; no city-policy lever.
papers.summary: The paper describes IUU+DB, a large language model system for extracting and organizing data on illegal fishing, seafood fraud, and labor abuses from heterogeneous documents into a global incident database. The authors demonstrate that the system can classify incidents, extract key attributes (actors, locations, species, vessels, violations), and support trend analysis and risk assessment.
papers.method: LLM-driven information extraction from fragmented documents; case studies and validation results reported but no controlled experiment or city-scale pilot described.
papers.scalability_note: Global applicability in principle, but requires domain expertise to maintain and operationalize; not a plug-and-play city tool.
papers.cost_note: Likely moderate upfront development cost for LLM tuning and validation; ongoing marginal cost low if infrastructure exists.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has seafood markets and restaurants subject to fraud, but IUU fishing is not a municipal enforcement lever; DSNY/DOH food safety roles do not typically target supply-chain IUU incidents. Relevant to state/federal/international agencies, not city government.

papers.title: Children Are Not the Enemy: Child-Fit Security as an Alternative to Bans and Surveillance
papers.authors: Kopo M. Ramokapane, Rui Huan, Zaina Dkaidek, Awais Rashid
papers.abstract: Digital technologies are now central to children's learning, play, communication, identity formation, and social participation. Yet dominant approaches to children's online safety often rely on containment mechanisms, including bans, age gates, parental controls, monitoring, and screen-time restrictions. These approaches can be useful in specific contexts, but they often frame child protection primarily as a problem of restricting access to systems designed for adults. In this paper, we argue that this framing is inadequate for children's digital lives and insufficient as a security paradigm. We propose Child-fit security, a design paradigm in which technologies likely to be used by children treat a child as legitimate users, not attackers to be excluded, vulnerabilities to be patched, or risks to be managed. In this paradigm, children's wellbeing, development, privacy, safety, agency, and rights become core security requirements. This shifts the focus of protection from apps, accounts, and data to the child-system relationship, which means protecting both the child and their participation. We conceptualise child-fit security, contrast it with containment-oriented approaches, define its core principles, and discuss its implications for security design. We conclude by presenting a research agenda for making child-fit security operational.
papers.idea: Redesign digital platforms and applications used by children to center child wellbeing, privacy, and agency as core security requirements rather than relying on bans, surveillance, and parental controls.
papers.summary: This paper proposes 'Child-fit security,' a design paradigm that treats children as legitimate users of technology rather than threats to be excluded or managed. The authors argue that dominant approaches relying on containment mechanisms (bans, age gates, monitoring) are inadequate and insufficient, and instead advocate for shifting protection focus from data/apps to the child-system relationship.
papers.method: Conceptual paper presenting a design paradigm framework; contrasts child-fit security with containment-oriented approaches and defines core principles with implications for security design.
papers.scalability_note: Design principles are generalizable across platforms and jurisdictions; adoption depends on platform/vendor willingness rather than local implementation constraints.
papers.cost_note: Implementation costs vary widely by platform; requires design/engineering investment but no direct per-user spending implied.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could influence city-contracted platforms or public digital services, but most child-facing technology is privately owned; limited direct city policy lever.

papers.title: The Early Childhood Care and Education Landscape in Ascending Neighborhoods
papers.authors: Agustina Laurito, Jennifer Candipan, Noli Brazil
papers.abstract: This paper descriptively documents the early childhood care and education (ECE) landscape in neighborhoods that experienced socioeconomic (SES) ascent and descent in the Chicago Metropolitan Area (MSA) between 2009 and 2019. Using data from the Illinois Early Childhood Asset Map and the American Community Survey, we analyze changes in ECE seats across provider types. SES ascent was most prevalent in Cook County, where zip code tabulation areas (ZCTAs) that ascended had a greater increase in ECE seats than the rest of the MSA. We do not find statistically significant differences in ECE availability between descending and stable ZCTAs, regardless of their location within the MSA. We also find little evidence that SES neighborhood change is associated with changes in the supply of Head Start or ECE capacity measured as the number of children per seat. Our study contributes to urban and educational research by highlighting how neighborhood SES change intersects with ECE access.
papers.idea: No actionable intervention identified; study is descriptive mapping of ECE supply changes in gentrifying neighborhoods.
papers.summary: The study documents changes in early childhood care and education (ECE) seat availability across Chicago-area neighborhoods experiencing socioeconomic ascent and descent between 2009–2019. Ascending neighborhoods in Cook County saw greater increases in ECE seats than elsewhere, but the authors found no statistically significant differences in ECE availability between descending and stable neighborhoods, and no clear link between neighborhood SES change and changes in Head Start supply or capacity ratios.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis using Illinois Early Childhood Asset Map and American Community Survey data across zip code tabulation areas in the Chicago MSA; no experimental or quasi-experimental design.
papers.scalability_note: Mapping study with no policy lever; would require separate action to translate findings into intervention.
