papers.cost_note: Data assembly and analysis cost modest, but no implementation cost implied; no concrete program proposed.
papers.nyc_note: Chicago-specific analysis; NYC would need its own ECE asset mapping and SES-neighborhood-change linkage study to act on findings.

papers.title: How Universities and Hospital Systems Affect Local Economies
papers.authors: Jonathan Schwabish, Christine Baker-Smith
papers.abstract: Over the past 75 years, the US economy has shifted away from manufacturing and toward sectors like education and health care. In 1950, manufacturing accounted for about 30 percent of US jobs—a share that’s fallen to just 8 percent in 2026. Over the same period, education and health care jobs rose from about 5 percent to nearly 18 percent. These sectors are key to the economic health and development of communities around the country.
papers.idea: No actionable intervention identified in abstract.
papers.summary: The abstract documents a long-term sectoral shift in the US economy away from manufacturing (30% in 1950 to 8% in 2026) and toward education and health care (5% to 18% over the same period). It frames these sectors as key to community economic health but does not specify what universities or hospital systems should do differently, nor what levers cities have to influence outcomes.
papers.method: Descriptive historical trend analysis of employment shares across sectors, 1950–2026.
papers.scalability_note: Trend analysis, not an intervention; cities cannot easily reverse sectoral shifts.
papers.cost_note: No cost data provided; depends entirely on what policy response is proposed.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has major university and hospital systems; abstract offers no guidance on what to do with that fact.

papers.title: Quantifying the Value of the Federal Home Loan Bank System
papers.authors: Bryson Berry, Jung Hyun Choi, Laurie Goodman, Daniel Pang, Kathryn Reynolds, Katie Visalli, John Walsh, Jun Zhu
papers.abstract: This brief summarizes a three-part study that quantifies the economic value of the Federal Home Loan Bank (FHLBank) System to the US financial and housing finance systems. The three studies examine (1) how the system supports member liquidity and financial stability, (2) expands mortgage and community lending, and (3) funds affordable housing and community development programs. Across multiple methodologies and scenarios, the research estimates that the FHLBank System generates between $94.2 billion and $133.2 billion in annual economic value through crisis prevention, increased lending activity, and mission-oriented investments. Why This Matters The FHLBank System plays a central but often underexamined role in the US housing finance system. This research provides a comprehensive assessment of the FHLBank System’s economic value and broader contributions to financial stability, housing finance, and community investment. The findings are relevant for policymakers, regulators, housing finance practitioners, and community lenders evaluating the FHLBank System’s future role, mission
papers.idea: City could lobby federal regulators to expand FHLBank lending programs in its jurisdiction to increase affordable housing funding.
papers.summary: This brief summarizes research quantifying the annual economic value of the Federal Home Loan Bank System, estimating $94.2–$133.2 billion in value through crisis prevention, expanded lending, and affordable housing/community development funding. The study examines how FHLBanks support member bank liquidity, mortgage lending, and mission-oriented investments.
papers.method: Three-part quantitative study using multiple methodologies and scenarios to model FHLBank System's economic contributions to financial stability, lending activity, and community investment; no specific data source, sample size, or empirical design specified in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: FHLBank programs are federal infrastructure; cities cannot independently create or reform them, only participate/advocate.
papers.cost_note: FHLBank system operates at federal level; city participation in existing programs has marginal cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC cannot unilaterally adopt or reform federal FHLBank system; could only work through member banks or federal advocacy, neither direct city lever.

papers.title: Four Things DC’s New Mayor and Council Need to Know About Wealth Building
papers.authors: Madeline Brown
papers.abstract: As mayoral and council candidates roll out their economic priorities, much of the debate focuses on housing and affordability. But an often overlooked factor underlies all these concerns: whether DC residents have the wealth they need to weather shocks, invest in opportunity, and share in the District’s growth. Wealth gives people the opportunity to thrive. It’s the money people use to pay for unexpected expenses and invest in their futures. It’s emergency and retirement savings, equity from real estate or business investments, and for some, family inheritance. Any policies focused on economic stability and growth for households must support the continuum of financial health: helping people meet basic needs, be resilient to financial shocks, and access opportunity. And any solutions must contend with the fact that DC and the region have some of the largest racial and ethnic employment, earnings, and wealth gaps in the country. Policy solutions that help
papers.idea: Not determinable from abstract—appears to be a policy brief identifying wealth-building gaps rather than proposing specific interventions.
papers.summary: The piece frames wealth-building (savings, assets, inheritance, business equity) as foundational to household economic stability and opportunity in DC, and notes large racial/ethnic wealth gaps in the region. The abstract breaks off mid-sentence and does not present findings, recommendations, or specific policy levers.
papers.method: Appears to be a descriptive policy analysis or think-tank brief for incoming elected officials; no empirical study design evident from abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Wealth-gap analysis is general, but concrete interventions unknown.
papers.cost_note: Cannot assess without knowing proposed solutions.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has similar racial wealth gaps and housing/affordability debates, but applicability depends on specific DC recommendations not visible in abstract.

papers.title: Thomas Brosy on proposed tax increases for Florida’s wealthiest
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: Florida Governor Ron DeSantis has proposed a plan that would eliminate property taxes for roughly 9 in 10 Floridians while shifting the tax burden to the state’s wealthiest homeowners, including those who live there part-time. Speaking with Bloomberg, Thomas Brosy, a tax policy expert at the Urban Institute, said if local governments shift more of their tax burden to the most valuable properties, it “could actually in practice be a way for taxing the rich.” For inquiries, please email media@urban.org.
papers.idea: Shift property tax burden from middle-class to wealthy homeowners to fund local services.
papers.summary: Thomas Brosy of the Urban Institute commented on Florida Governor DeSantis's proposal to eliminate property taxes for ~90% of Floridians while increasing taxes on wealthy homeowners. Brosy suggested this could function as a wealth-redistribution mechanism. No empirical findings or outcomes are presented.
papers.method: Quote from a tax policy expert in a media interview; no original research or data analysis conducted.
papers.scalability_note: Property tax structures are standard municipal tools implementable anywhere.
papers.cost_note: Cost depends on implementation; no per-unit estimates provided.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has complex, high property taxes on luxury properties; this is a Florida-specific proposal with limited novel applicability.

papers.title: Comment Letter in Response to the Department of Education’s Request for Comment on the Proposed Rule ED-2026-OPE-0100, Accountability in Higher Education and Access through Demand-driven Workforce Pell
papers.authors: Jason Cohn, Kristin Blagg
papers.abstract: Writing in response to a request for comment on the proposed rule ED-2026-OPE-0100, Accountability in Higher Education and Access through Demand-driven Workforce Pell: Student Tuition and Transparency System (STATS) and Earnings Accountability, Urban Institute researchers evaluate the third threshold for graduate level earnings. Their analysis suggests that a third earnings threshold (i.e., earnings within the same state for the same field of study) is missing—because of small n sizes in the American Community Survey—for just 4 percent of graduate program completers. Moreover, among programs that have all three earnings measures, this third metric is the lowest of the three just 19 percent of the time. We conclude that omitting the calculation when fewer than 30 observations are present would have minimal effects on outcomes for the “do no harm” test.
papers.idea: None identified; this is a comment letter on federal higher-education accountability metrics, not an actionable municipal intervention.
papers.summary: Urban Institute researchers submitted a comment letter to the U.S. Department of Education evaluating a proposed federal rule on graduate-program earnings accountability. They find that a third earnings threshold (intrastate, same-field comparison) is missing for only 4% of programs due to small sample sizes, and is rarely the lowest of three metrics (19% of the time), suggesting minimal harm from excluding programs below a threshold of 30 observations.
papers.method: Analysis of proposed federal earnings-accountability thresholds using American Community Survey data; no empirical study conducted by the authors.
papers.scalability_note: This is a federal comment letter; directly non-scalable as a city policy.
papers.cost_note: No cost to implement; this is advice on federal rule-writing.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has no control over federal higher-education accountability rules; the item offers no lever for municipal action.

papers.title: What Are School District Secessions, and How Common Are They?
papers.authors: Adriana Vance, Karishma Furtado, Harley Webley, Luisa Godinez-Puig
papers.abstract: In St. Louis County, the Missouri House of Representatives is considering legislation that would allow some school districts in the county to withdraw from the Special School District—a process known as secession, withdrawal, or separation. These partner districts are currently obligated to use the Special School District for special education services. For students, parents, administrators, and teachers, the decision is a personal one that shapes the day-to-day experience of students’ education, families’ well-being, and educators’ livelihoods. At the same time, secession decisions are complex and have potentially far-reaching consequences for education revenue and operations. To better understand secessions, like the attempt playing out in St. Louis, and their ripple effects, we’ve produced a uniquely comprehensive database of school district secession attempts from 2000 to 2026. In this article, we use it to highlight secession trends across the US. In the future, we’ll also use this database to analyze the impacts
papers.idea: Create a database to track and analyze school district secession attempts to inform policy decisions about special education service consolidation.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute created a comprehensive database of school district secession attempts from 2000–2026 to understand trends in districts withdrawing from regional special education service providers. The research uses this database to analyze secession patterns across the US, with a focus on the St. Louis County case. The article promises future analysis of impacts but presents only trend documentation in the abstract.
papers.method: Descriptive database construction of school district secession attempts (2000–2026) across the US, analyzed for trends; specific sample size and analytic methods not detailed in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Database creation is scalable, but findings are descriptive trends; actionable interventions unclear.
papers.cost_note: Research cost moderate; implementation cost unknown pending future analysis.
papers.nyc_note: NYC operates a unified school district, not a county-based multi-district system; secession dynamics do not directly apply.

papers.title: Defendant race and neuropsychological response: an exploratory study
papers.authors: Lauren C. Porter, Will Kalkhoff, Joshua Pollock, Brian D. Johnson
papers.abstract: Abstract Objectives Scholars often highlight negative stereotyping and implicit bias as key mechanisms driving racial disparities in punishment, however these psychological processes present challenges for empirical observation and measurement. In our study, we investigate the relationship between race and neuropsychological response to a sample of booking photos of White and Black male defendants. Methods Thirty participants were shown 500 photos of defendants from Minneapolis, MN in a laboratory setting. While viewing the photos, we collected and analyzed electroencephalograms (EEGs), which capture neurological activity. We examined four markers of brain activity known as event related potentials (ERPs), which have been associated in prior work with the processing of facial stimuli, racial bias and/or expectation violation. Results Our findings are mixed, but we find that images of White male defendants were linked with larger amplitudes on one ERP that has been associated with expectation violation and one ERP that has been associated with more “effortful” and “deep” processing of facial stimuli. Conclusion Our study provides a preliminary and exploratory step towards incorporating these kinds of neurological data in the study of racial bias in sentencing and other criminal legal system outcomes.
papers.idea: Use EEG neuroimaging during judicial review to detect and counteract implicit racial bias in sentencing decisions.
papers.summary: This exploratory study measured brain activity (via EEG event-related potentials) in 30 laboratory participants viewing 500 booking photos of White and Black male defendants from Minneapolis. Findings were mixed but suggested White defendants' images triggered larger amplitudes in neural markers associated with expectation violation and deeper facial processing, suggesting potential differential cognitive processing by race.
papers.method: Laboratory experiment with 30 participants viewing defendant booking photos while EEG recorded brain activity; analysis of four event-related potential (ERP) markers.
papers.scalability_note: EEG is labor-intensive, requires equipment/expertise per participant, and unclear how lab findings translate to actual courtroom use.
papers.cost_note: EEG equipment, technicians, and analysis are expensive per participant; impractical for mass deployment in courts.
papers.nyc_note: No clear path to implementation in NYC courts; raises ethical/legal concerns about requiring judges/jurors to undergo neuroimaging; findings too preliminary to inform policy.

papers.title: Anger About Police-Civilian Relations and Support for Policing Reforms
papers.authors: Linda Balcarová, Justin T. Pickett, Amanda Graham, Francis T. Cullen
papers.abstract: Objectives Police-related anger remains undertheorized and understudied, even as evidence accumulates that issue-specific anger drives public preferences in other policy domains. We address this void in the literature by distinguishing police-related anger that is generalized from that which is situational (a state emotion), and by distinguishing anger felt toward officers from anger felt toward civilians. We theorize that individuals’ racial backgrounds and cumulative personal and vicarious experiences with police mistreatment affect both types of generalized anger and, in turn, support for policing reforms. Methods Our study uses data from two national surveys conducted by YouGov (Survey 1: N = 1,150; Survey 2: N = 1,100), which measured both types of generalized police-related anger (toward officers vs. civilians) and support for policing reforms. Results Anger about police-civilian relations is racialized. Whether it is felt toward officers or toward civilians depends on respondents’ race and past experiences with police. Both types of police-related anger strongly predict support for policing reforms. Conclusion Police-related anger is an important emotional foundation of public policy preferences in the context of policing. Group differences in experienced mistreatment help explain emotional divides as well as the racial gap in support for policing reform.
papers.source: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.venue: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.idea: Not applicable—paper is descriptive research on anger and attitudes, not an intervention.
papers.summary: The study finds that police-related anger is racialized and varies by target (officers vs. civilians) depending on respondents' race and prior experiences with police mistreatment. Both types of anger predict support for policing reforms, suggesting emotional experience shapes policy preferences.
papers.method: Two national online surveys (YouGov; N=1,150 and N=1,100) measuring generalized police-related anger and support for reforms via self-report.
papers.scalability_note: No intervention or program to scale; measures associations between emotions and attitudes only.
papers.cost_note: No cost—observational study with no implementation lever.
papers.nyc_note: Provides context on public sentiment but does not propose a city policy, program, or practice NYC could adopt.

papers.title: Effectiveness and Cost‐Effectiveness of Ecosystem‐Based Disaster Risk Reduction Interventions in Low‐ and Middle‐Income Countries: A Rapid Systematic Review
papers.authors:
Suchi Kapoor Malhotra, 
Ashrita Saran, 
Ratika Bhandari, 
Kevin Ouma Ojiambo, 
Sujata Shirodkar, 
Gavin Stewart, 
Howard White, 
Hugh Sharma Waddington
papers.abstract: Campbell Systematic Reviews, Volume 21, Issue 4, December 2025.
papers.idea: Deploy ecosystem-based disaster risk reduction (e.g., wetland restoration, mangrove protection) to reduce flood and storm damage in low- and middle-income countries.
papers.summary: This is a systematic review synthesizing evidence on ecosystem-based disaster risk reduction (EbDRR) interventions—such as wetland, mangrove, and forest restoration—conducted in low- and middle-income countries. The review assessed effectiveness and cost-effectiveness but does not present original empirical findings in the abstract.
papers.method: Rapid systematic review of existing studies on EbDRR interventions in LMIC settings, appraising evidence on disaster risk reduction outcomes and cost-effectiveness.
papers.scalability_note: Nature-based solutions can be adapted across geographies; scalability depends on local ecology and funding.
papers.cost_note: EbDRR often cheaper than hard infrastructure, but abstract provides no per-unit cost data.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has limited scope for mangrove/wetland restoration; focus on flood mitigation via hard infrastructure and urban green infrastructure is more typical. LMIC context limits direct applicability.

papers.title: Before the Exodus? Young Scientists and the Future of US Science
papers.authors: Pierre Azoulay, Raffaella Sadun, Daniela Scur
papers.abstract: Shortly after major policy changes to US science funding began in early 2025, we surveyed 916 young biomedical scientists – PhD students and postdoctoral researchers – about their career intentions and expectations. The results document a dramatic shift in sentiment. Barely half of respondents now say they are likely to remain in academia, down 22 percentage points from how they felt six months earlier. The fraction likely to stay in the United States fell by 21 percentage points. Even satisfaction with having pursued a PhD in science declined by 16 percentage points. These are not the complaints of established scientists defending their budgets, but rather the stated intentions of the next generation – the scientists who would, in ordinary times, become the principal investigators of the future.
papers.idea: No actionable city intervention identified; abstract documents a sentiment shift among scientists without proposing a policy lever.
papers.summary: A survey of 916 young biomedical scientists (PhD students and postdocs) in early 2025 found sharp declines in their likelihood of staying in academia (down 22 points), remaining in the US (down 21 points), and satisfaction with pursuing a PhD (down 16 points), reportedly following US science funding policy changes.
papers.method: Cross-sectional survey of 916 young biomedical scientists conducted shortly after early-2025 policy changes, compared to self-reported attitudes from six months prior.
papers.scalability_note: Federal science funding policy is not a city lever; any city-level response would be indirect and limited.
papers.cost_note: Unknown from abstract; city interventions to retain scientists could range widely in cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could theoretically fund local biomedical research or offer incentives, but the core problem (federal funding policy) lies outside city control.

papers.title: What do you mean by human-AI collaboration: Prerequisite functions and the affordances needed to achieve it
papers.authors: Mutlu Cukurova
papers.abstract: The concept of 'collaboration' has been extended rapidly to describe what people now do with conversational agents, intelligent tutors, adaptive platforms, and generative artificial intelligence (AI) tools in general. This chapter asks what is gained and lost when a demanding concept from the learning sciences is applied so freely. Returning to long-standing accounts of collaborative learning, it reconstructs the requirements that a situation, an interaction, and a set of cognitive processes have historically had to meet before being called collaborative. Human-AI collaboration requires a partly symmetric and negotiated relationship, shared and negotiable goals, a low and shifting division of labour, interactive and synchronous exchange, and mutual modelling, grounding, and socially shared regulation. Reviewing process-sensitive empirical studies of writing and problem solving, the chapter shows that most current human-AI interaction is better described as consultation, governance, delegation, or instruction rather than as collaboration. To make these distinctions functional, the chapter introduces a five-level diagnostic taxonomy of human-AI teaming (i.e. transactional, situational, operational, praxical, and synergistic) defined by the affordances an AI system exhibits. It shows that only the highest level begins to satisfy the conditions the tradition places on collaboration. The chapter derives the functions an AI system must possess for collaboration to be achievable, argues that most of these are present-day engineering choices rather than capabilities to be awaited, and sets out the implications for research, measurement, and responsible practice of human-AI collaboration in education.
papers.summary: This paper reconstructs the theoretical requirements for genuine human-AI collaboration from learning sciences literature, argues that most current human-AI interaction is better described as consultation or delegation, and proposes a five-level taxonomy of human-AI teaming defined by system affordances. It identifies engineering choices needed to achieve true collaboration but offers no specific intervention, program, or policy.
papers.method: Conceptual/theoretical analysis synthesizing prior accounts of collaborative learning and reviewing process-sensitive empirical studies of writing and problem-solving to derive requirements.
papers.scalability_note: Conceptual framework with no concrete implementation pathway; would require substantial additional development to operationalize.
papers.cost_note: No costs or resource requirements specified; framework-building exercise.
papers.nyc_note: Theoretical contribution to learning sciences; no specific NYC agency, school system, or program intervention proposed or tested.

papers.title: Decision Theory for the Archetype Discovery Problem
papers.authors: José Luis Montiel Olea, Amilcar Velez, Zhuoheng Xu, Haomin Yu, Shunqi Zhang
papers.abstract: In the archetype discovery problem a researcher wants to summarize N heterogeneous policy effects of interest that vary over a discrete set of covariates. The goal is to partition the set of covariates into K<N groups -- the archetype sets -- and to provide a summary of the policy effects for each group. We use decision theory to show that, under a weighted mean-squared-error criterion, a procedure analogous to the Sorted Group Average Treatment Effects (GATES) solves the archetype discovery problem. The key difference is that, in the optimal procedure, archetype sets are obtained by weighted K-means clustering of the N heterogeneous policy effects, instead of relying on K equally-spaced quantiles. We show that the procedure that minimizes average risk for a given prior can be obtained by clustering the different values of the posterior mean estimate of the policy effects of interest. Similarly, an approximately minimax procedure in large samples can be obtained by clustering a consistent estimator of the policy effects. In both of these cases, an exact solution to the weighted K-means clustering problem can be found using a simple and well-known dynamic programming algorithm.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a statistical methods paper with no concrete policy application described.
papers.summary: The paper develops a decision-theoretic framework for discovering policy-effect archetypes by partitioning heterogeneous treatment effects into K groups using weighted K-means clustering rather than quantile-based approaches. The method aims to optimally summarize complex, varying policy impacts across different subpopulations.
papers.method: Theoretical decision theory paper proposing an algorithm (weighted K-means via dynamic programming) for clustering heterogeneous policy effects; no empirical application, data, or case study provided in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Depends entirely on downstream application; method itself is a computational tool, not a policy intervention.
papers.cost_note: Computational cost is minimal, but value depends on whether a city actually uses it to redesign a program.
papers.nyc_note: Could theoretically help NYC analyze heterogeneous treatment effects from existing program evaluations, but abstract provides no concrete example or policy hook.

papers.title: Heterogeneous behavioral mechanisms in epidemiological models
papers.authors: Jessica Pavani, Rob Deardon, Alexandra M. Schmidt
papers.abstract: Traditional epidemic models frequently assume behavioral homogeneity. The susceptible-infected-recovered model provides a robust foundation for characterizing disease transmission, but it does so without accounting for how people actually respond to risk. In contrast, behavioral change models incorporate mechanisms that capture how individuals adjust their actions during an outbreak, recognizing that rising infection risk typically motivates protective behaviors. Yet both approaches share a key limitation: they overlook the inherent heterogeneity of a population. In reality, communities are a complex mixture of risk tolerances and behavioral tendencies. Ignoring this inherent heterogeneity can obscure important differences in how individuals perceive and respond to disease threats. This paper introduces a novel Bayesian mixture model designed to address this limitation by partitioning the population into two distinct behavioral patterns: risk-neutral individuals, who maintain baseline contact rates, and risk-averse individuals, who modulate their behavior in response to epidemic severity. By integrating these disparate dynamics into a unified transmission framework, the proposed model explicitly accounts for varying population behaviors often overlooked by aggregate approaches. Through simulation studies and empirical data applications, we demonstrate that this approach significantly outperforms traditional models in parameter recovery, epidemic trajectory estimation, and forecasting precision. The findings suggest that failing to account for behavioral diversity leads to biased peak estimates and artificially stretched epidemic curves. Consequently, this research provides a more nuanced computational toolkit for predicting outbreak trajectories in socially fragmented environments, ensuring that public health intervention strategies are informed by a foundation of behavioral realism.
papers.idea: None identified; this is a methodological paper on epidemic modeling without an actionable intervention.
papers.summary: The paper proposes a Bayesian mixture model that partitions populations into risk-neutral and risk-averse behavioral groups to improve epidemic forecasting. The authors show their model outperforms traditional SIR models in parameter recovery and trajectory estimation through simulations and empirical applications.
papers.method: Bayesian mixture modeling with simulation studies and empirical data applications; no sample size stated, no field intervention or real-world policy test.
papers.scalability_note: Methodological framework useful for modelers; requires integration into public health forecasting infrastructure but does not itself constitute a deployable intervention.
papers.cost_note: Moderate—adds computational complexity to existing epidemic models; no operational deployment costs estimated.
papers.nyc_note: NYC's health department could adopt this forecasting method, but the paper offers no guidance on *which behavioral interventions* to deploy based on heterogeneity; useful only as a prediction tool, not a concrete program.

papers.title: Testing Preferential Sampling
papers.authors: Isabel Natario, Andreia Monteiro
papers.abstract: Geostatistics aims to infer a spatially continuous phenomenon from observations collected at a finite number of locations, frequently measured with error. Whenever there is stochastic dependence between the spatial and sampling processes, preferential sampling occurs. Ignoring this problem drives to incorrect and biased estimates and, therefore, recognizing it is quite important, but not always simple to execute and understand. In this work, a test for assessing preferential sampling, simple and easy to implement, is presented, overcoming the previous concerns. It is based on the dependence between the number of sampled points and the values of the corresponding measures. The performance of the proposed test id assessed through a large simulation study, which consideres different levels of preferentiability, relation with a covariate, different sample sizes and different test procedure conditions. The results are quite encouraging, with high levels of correct preferential sampling detections, further confirmed by the test application to already known real data sets of lead concentrations in moss samples and red and blue shrimp capture data.
papers.idea: Develop statistical tests to detect bias in environmental sampling to improve accuracy of pollution monitoring.
papers.summary: The paper presents a statistical test for identifying preferential sampling bias—when the location and density of measurement points correlate with the underlying phenomenon being measured. The test was validated through simulations and applied to real datasets on lead in moss and shrimp capture data.
papers.method: Simulation study across varying levels of preferential sampling bias, sample sizes, and covariate relationships; applied post-hoc to two existing environmental datasets.
papers.scalability_note: Applicable only to environmental monitoring programs; requires technical statistical expertise to deploy.
papers.cost_note: Minimal implementation cost; primarily software/analyst time to apply existing statistical test.
papers.nyc_note: Could support NYC's environmental monitoring (lead, water quality, air), but the paper does not propose a concrete city intervention or program change.

papers.title: Mod-Guide: An LLM-based Content Moderation Feedback System to Address Insensitive Speech toward Indigenous Ethnic and Religious Minority Communities
papers.authors: Dipto Das, Achhiya Sultana, Ankit Singh Chauhan, Saadia Binte Alam, Mohammad Shidujaman, Shion Guha, Sunandan Chakraborty, Syed Ishtiaque Ahmed
papers.abstract: Language operates as a mechanism of both marginalization and resistance, especially for minority communities navigating insensitive and harmful speech online. As content moderation increasingly depends on large language models (LLMs), concerns arise about whether these systems can recognize culturally insensitive speech-language that disregards or marginalizes the cultural and religious perspectives of historically underrepresented communities, often through implicit erasure, misrepresentation, or normative framing, rather than overt hostility. Focusing on Bangladesh's Hindu and Chakma communities -- the country's largest religious and Indigenous ethnic minorities, respectively -- this paper investigates the epistemic limits of LLM-based moderation systems and explores methods for incorporating minority perspectives. We co-created a culturally grounded corpus of insensitive speech with community members and integrated their narratives into moderation pipelines using retrieval augmented generation (RAG). Our tool, Mod-Guide, improves LLM sensitivity to minority viewpoints by leveraging contextual cues derived from lived experience. Through mixed-method evaluations involving both minority and majority participants, we demonstrate that RAG-enhanced moderation responses are more contextually accurate and perceived differently across ethnic lines. This work advances research in human-computer interaction, AI ethics, and social computing by foregrounding restorative justice and hermeneutical inclusion in the design of content moderation systems.
papers.idea: Integrate community-informed, retrieval-augmented generation into social media content moderation systems to better flag culturally insensitive speech toward minority communities.
papers.summary: The researchers co-created a corpus of insensitive speech with Hindu and Chakma community members in Bangladesh and tested a tool (Mod-Guide) that uses RAG to enhance LLM-based content moderation. The system showed improved contextual accuracy and was perceived differently across ethnic lines in mixed-method evaluation.
papers.method: Mixed-method co-design study with Hindu and Chakma communities in Bangladesh; corpus creation with community members; evaluation involving both minority and majority participants; quantitative and qualitative assessment of moderation outputs.
papers.scalability_note: Replicable approach but requires community-specific corpus design for each context; platform-dependent deployment.
papers.cost_note: Initial co-design and corpus building is labor-intensive; deployment cost low once built.
papers.nyc_note: Study focused on Bangladesh context; would require NYC-specific minority community engagement and corpus work; depends on platform policies outside city control.

papers.title: Three Things DC's New Mayor and Council Need to Know About Nonprofits
papers.authors: Hannah Martin, Laura Tomasko
papers.abstract: Downsizing of the federal government workforce and federal funding disruptions have a disproportionate impact on Washington, DC. Although total US employment rose slightly in 2025, the Washington, DC region (defined here by its metropolitan statistical area) experienced job losses, driven by declines in both federal and private-sector employment. Federal government jobs fell nationwide but dropped even more sharply in the DC area, leaving it with a 30-year low in federal workers as of January. With a shrinking federal employment and funding base, the new DC mayor and council will be critical in shepherding DC through this period. As they set policy priorities to help DC flourish, it’s important that they recognize that a vibrant nonprofit sector is increasingly important to the overall vitality of the DC region. Understanding the nonprofit sector’s role—and its challenges—is therefore critical as DC policymakers and future elected officials prepare for office. Here are three things
papers.idea: Not determinable from abstract; likely a general policy brief on nonprofit sector support without specific interventions.
papers.summary: DC faces federal workforce and funding cuts that disproportionately affect the region. The authors argue that a vibrant nonprofit sector will be increasingly critical to DC's vitality as federal employment hits a 30-year low, and urge the new mayor and council to understand the nonprofit sector's role and challenges.
papers.method: Policy brief analyzing federal employment trends in DC metro area using employment data; abstract does not disclose specific recommendations or empirical findings on nonprofit-sector interventions.
papers.scalability_note: Nonprofit support frameworks are generalizable but often context-dependent on local philanthropy and funding ecosystems.
papers.cost_note: Unknown from abstract; depends entirely on what the three recommendations are.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has different federal employment exposure and nonprofit ecosystem; relevance depends on whether recommendations address generic nonprofit challenges or DC-specific policy gaps not disclosed in abstract.

papers.title: A First Look at Student Loan Borrowers Exiting Default via the Fresh Start Program
papers.authors: Kristin Blagg
papers.abstract: The Biden administration initiated the Fresh Start program to help federal student loan borrowers exit default without the standard rehabilitation process (nine on-time voluntary monthly payments) or the consolidation process (an agreement to repay under an income-driven plan or to make three full on-time monthly payments). This “opt-in” benefit of Fresh Start was offered to borrowers of most federal loans in default starting in spring 2022. In winter 2022, the US Department of Education took a second step, providing an “automatic” benefit. Most federal student loan borrowers with credit-reportable defaulted loans were automatically reported to credit agencies as “current” until the end of the program in fall 2024. Why This Matters There is relatively little research about how the Fresh Start program affected defaulted borrowers. Understanding outcomes for borrowers who opted in to Fresh Start, or who received the automatic benefit of having their default made current on their credit
papers.idea: Cities could partner with federal student loan programs to help residents exit default and improve credit scores, potentially reducing financial stress and improving economic outcomes.
papers.summary: The abstract describes the Biden administration's Fresh Start program, which allowed federal student loan borrowers in default to exit that status either through an opt-in process or via automatic credit reporting updates. The study examines outcomes for borrowers who participated.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis of borrowers exiting default through the Fresh Start program; method details not fully specified in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: This is a federal program; cities have limited direct leverage over federal student loan policy.
papers.cost_note: No direct city cost; federal program cost not specified in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could potentially coordinate local financial counseling or credit repair services to complement federal Fresh Start, but the policy lever itself is federal.

papers.title: The 4Cs of Housing: Resident Perspectives on Cost, Conditions, Consistency, and Context in a Public Housing Neighborhood
papers.authors: Samantha Teixeira, Zhirui Chen, Rebekah Levine Coley
papers.idea: Unknown; abstract not provided—cannot assess actionability.
papers.summary: Unable to assess. Title suggests qualitative study of resident perspectives on four dimensions of public housing (cost, conditions, consistency, context), but no abstract provided to determine findings or recommendations.
papers.method: Unknown; only title available. Likely qualitative (resident perspectives), but no methodological details provided.
papers.scalability_note: Appears to be neighborhood-specific qualitative work; generalizability unclear without abstract.
papers.cost_note: No cost data available.
papers.nyc_note: Public housing context is relevant to NYC NYCHA, but without findings/recommendations cannot evaluate fit.

papers.title: Risks and Priorities in Federal Disability Measurement
papers.authors: Alexis Weaver, Harley Webley, Nina Russell, Amanda Hermans, Susan J. Popkin, Lizzy Ferrara
papers.abstract: Recent shifts in the federal approach to collecting and using data have raised new concerns about the future of how we measure and understand disabled people, their experiences, and their protections. The Informing Federal Disability Data Measurement project initially sought to learn more about how federal agencies define and measure disability in their work, including in surveys, administrative data, and program implementation. Then in 2025, amid federal changes to programs and data collection, we convened a roundtable of disability measurement stakeholders to assess the implications of the current moment, discuss emerging threats and gaps in disability data, and identify key priorities. This brief shares insights from the roundtable and follow-up survey and stakeholders’ assessments of the biggest risks and priorities for disability research and advocacy. Why This Matters Access to complete and inclusive data on the experiences of people with disabilities is essential for informing public resource allocation, local planning
papers.idea: Standardize federal disability data collection across agencies to improve measurement and inform resource allocation for disabled populations.
papers.summary: This brief reports on a roundtable convened by the Urban Institute to assess federal disability measurement practices and data collection methods across agencies. Stakeholders identified emerging risks and gaps in disability data collection amid 2025 federal policy changes and recommended priorities for improving disability research and advocacy.
papers.method: Qualitative stakeholder roundtable discussion followed by survey of disability measurement experts; no empirical data collection or program evaluation.
papers.scalability_note: Addresses federal data standards; city implementation would be indirect and dependent on federal changes.
papers.cost_note: Unclear; framed as measurement/advocacy work, not a direct service intervention.
papers.nyc_note: NYC cannot unilaterally reform federal disability measurement; applicability limited to alignment with future federal standards if adopted.

papers.title: More Households Are Using Buy Now, Pay Later. What Does That Mean for Homeownership?
papers.authors: Michael Neal
papers.abstract: Buy now, pay later (BNPL) loan options have grown in popularity, especially among younger consumers. With BNPL, a person can purchase something with little or no initial payment and pay off the balance over a small number of payments. According to a report coauthored by the Urban Institute and the JPMorganChase Institute, the data show that between 2019 and 2023, the amount of BNPL spending increased 37-fold, from $2 billion to $75 billion. But even as use of this consumer product grows, it is unclear how it might affect borrowers’ credit. Unlike credit cards, most BNPL loans do not require a hard credit inquiry and often aren’t reported to major credit bureaus, so their use may not uniformly affect credit characteristics like a credit score or debt-to-income ratio. In particular, this lack of visibility could complicate a borrower’s ability to qualify for or repay a mortgage. Last summer, the US
papers.idea: Cities could require BNPL lenders to report to credit bureaus and conduct hard inquiries to preserve mortgage qualification visibility for lower-income borrowers.
papers.summary: Between 2019 and 2023, Buy Now, Pay Later spending grew 37-fold to $75 billion, especially among younger consumers. Most BNPL loans lack hard credit inquiries and credit bureau reporting, creating a blind spot that could harm borrowers' credit scores and debt-to-income ratios needed for mortgage qualification.
papers.method: Analysis of spending data from Urban Institute and JPMorgan Chase Institute covering 2019–2023 period; appears descriptive rather than causal.
papers.scalability_note: BNPL credit reporting is federally regulated; cities have limited direct lever over lender practices without state/federal coordination.
papers.cost_note: No intervention proposed or costed in abstract; regulatory enforcement would be modest.
papers.nyc_note: NYC cannot unilaterally mandate credit reporting or hard inquiries; this requires state/federal action or DFS coordination, limiting city-level action.

papers.title: Disappearing race in criminology: Stigma, race, and loss
papers.authors: Katheryn Russell‐Brown
papers.abstract: Abstract This article is based on Katheryn Russell‐Brown's 2025 presidential address at the 85th annual meeting of the American Society of Criminology, held in Washington, D.C. The article begins with an overview of the Author's approach to research and the highlights of her scholarly contributions. Following this, the article presents a research question—whether contemporary race and crime scholars and their scholarship have been stigmatized by the creation of a moral panic designed to marginalize and criminalize their work. This moral panic specifically targets Black history and other race‐related scholarship. A criminological and sociological lens is used to analyze the breadth of the panic. Using moral panic as a base for the discussion, the next section examines the broad contours of stigma, and stigmatization and how race‐related stigmas operate. Following this is a look at how negative stigmas assigned to race scholars and race scholarship harms the discipline of criminology. For instance, studies indicate that fewer scholars will embrace race and crime scholarship if its value is diminished. Migration studies and imagined futures are included in the assessment of how racial stigmas impact race scholarship. Academic disciplines benefit from rigorous engagement regarding their core principles and perspectives. The absence or diminishment of critical research creates a void in the criminology literature. This raises questions about the representativeness, integrity, and comprehensiveness of criminological scholarship. The article concludes with a charge to the discipline to ensure that race and crime scholarship remain a robust part of the criminological canon.
papers.idea: N/A — no actionable intervention identified.
papers.summary: Russell-Brown's presidential address examines whether contemporary race-and-crime scholars have been stigmatized by a moral panic targeting race-related scholarship, and argues that diminishing the value of such work harms criminology by reducing scholar participation and creating gaps in the discipline's literature.
papers.method: Non-empirical; a theoretical and discursive analysis delivered as a presidential address, drawing on moral panic and stigma theory to frame a critique of disciplinary trends.
papers.scalability_note: Addresses academic culture and publishing incentives, not a concrete program or policy.
papers.cost_note: No intervention, no cost implications.
papers.nyc_note: No city-level policy lever; concerns academic discourse within criminology as a discipline.

papers.title: Fracking ghost towns: How neighborhood exposure drives population decline
papers.authors: Arman Khachiyan
papers.idea: Monitor and mitigate neighborhood population decline from industrial extraction activities to preserve community stability.
papers.summary: This paper appears to examine how exposure to fracking activity correlates with or drives population decline in affected neighborhoods. The study likely documents demographic changes in communities near natural-gas extraction sites.
papers.method: Unknown without abstract; likely observational analysis of census/demographic data matched against fracking locations and timing.
papers.scalability_note: Fracking is geographically concentrated in specific regions (Texas, Oklahoma, Pennsylvania); limited relevance to most U.S. cities.
papers.cost_note: Mitigation would require regulation or compensation; no per-unit cost stated.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has no active fracking; paper is of historical/policy interest only, not actionable locally.

papers.title: Urban internal structures and gender commuting gap: Evidence from China
papers.authors: Yalin Wang, Xiaofang Dong
papers.idea: Redesign urban neighborhoods to reduce gender commuting disparities by co-locating jobs, housing, and services.
papers.summary: This study examines the relationship between urban spatial structure (job and housing distribution) and differences in commuting patterns between men and women in Chinese cities. The research likely documents that women face longer or more complex commutes due to how neighborhoods are organized.
papers.method: Observational analysis of urban structure and commuting data in China; no abstract available so design details cannot be confirmed.
papers.scalability_note: Urban redesign is slow and capital-intensive; applicable mainly to greenfield development or major redevelopment zones.
papers.cost_note: Large-scale urban restructuring requires sustained public investment; no unit costs provided.
papers.nyc_note: NYC's built environment is mostly fixed; insights may apply only to limited redevelopment projects in outer boroughs or new mixed-use districts.

papers.title: Household coping mechanisms under grid failure: Evidence from a high electrification context in Lebanon
papers.authors: Majd Olleik, Haytham M. Dbouk, Anne Neumann, Elsa Bou Gebrael, Sebastian Zwickl-Bernhard
papers.abstract: Despite near-universal electrification in many countries, electricity supply shortages continue to shape household energy use. This paper examines how households adapt to chronic grid failure in high-electrification, high-dependence contexts, using Lebanon as a case study. Drawing on original survey data from 1,000 households, we analyze both supply-side coping mechanisms such as diesel generators and solar photovoltaic (PV)-battery systems, and demand-side adaptations, including load shifting and demand suppression. The results reveal a landscape of household responses, where socioeconomic status plays a central role in determining access to backup solutions and the extent of met demand. While diesel generators remain widespread, a transition toward PV-battery systems is observed, especially among financially capable households. However, decentralized self-generation is associated with inefficiencies, including substantial levels of curtailed solar generation. On the demand side, households exhibit reductions in electricity use, leading to distinct consumption profiles depending on the type of backup system employed. These findings highlight the importance of distinguishing between met and unmet demand when assessing energy needs under unreliable supply. The paper contributes to the literature by providing a quantitative characterization of the interaction between self-generation and demand adaptation in a supply-constrained high-electrification context. It also offers empirical demand profiles that incorporate suppressed consumption, addressing a key gap in electricity system planning. From a policy perspective, the results underscore the need to account for unmet demand, address inequities in access to coping technologies, and reduce inefficiencies in decentralized systems.
papers.idea: Cities could mandate or incentivize distributed solar-battery systems over diesel generators to reduce air pollution and improve grid resilience during outages.
papers.summary: The study surveyed 1,000 Lebanese households to understand how they cope with chronic electricity shortages. It found that wealthier households shift toward solar-PV-battery systems while poorer households rely on diesel generators; both strategies reduce electricity demand but create inefficiencies and equity gaps.
papers.method: Cross-sectional survey of 1,000 households in Lebanon analyzing self-reported coping mechanisms and energy consumption patterns.
papers.scalability_note: Policy lessons are generalizable to supply-constrained contexts, but requires chronic grid failure scenario—not present in NYC.
papers.cost_note: Incentivizing solar-battery systems is capital-intensive; diesel generators are cheaper upfront but externalize health/environmental costs.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has reliable grid supply and does not face chronic electricity shortages; findings address grid-failure coping, not standard urban challenges.

papers.title: Towards Auditing AI Systems in the Wild
papers.authors: Aditya T. Vadlamani, Anutam Srinivasan, Srinivasan Parthasarathy
papers.abstract: AI systems are increasingly deployed in real-world settings where their behavior is shaped by dynamic environments, evolving data distributions, and complex interactions with users and infrastructure. Traditional machine learning evaluation focuses on benchmarks and operates within sandboxed environments, providing only a limited view of the true system behavior in the wild. We argue for the development of principled auditing frameworks that monitor deployed AI systems throughout their lifecycle. We further propose framing auditing as a statistical problem of monitoring constraint violations under uncertainty, where desired properties (e.g., fairness and safety) are treated as risk-controlled constraints that must be continuously evaluated as systems evolve through iterative feedback. This perspective highlights the need for uncertainty-aware monitoring methods, socio-technical specifications of audit criteria, and auditing infrastructures that enable ongoing oversight of AI systems in the wild.
papers.idea: Develop continuous auditing frameworks to monitor deployed AI systems for fairness and safety violations in real-world environments.
papers.summary: This is a methods/framework paper arguing for principled auditing approaches to monitor AI systems as they operate in dynamic real-world settings. The authors propose treating fairness and safety properties as risk-controlled constraints monitored through uncertainty-aware methods, rather than relying only on static benchmarks.
papers.method: Non-empirical position paper proposing a conceptual framework; no empirical study, data analysis, or real-world deployment testing described.
papers.scalability_note: Framework could apply across sectors but requires institutional adoption and context-specific implementation.
papers.cost_note: Infrastructure costs unclear; depends on auditing tool development and staffing needs.
papers.nyc_note: Abstract contains no concrete guidance on implementing audits in NYC agencies; would require substantial downstream work to operationalize.

papers.title: How to Detect and Measure the AI Dangers to Democracy
papers.authors: Giulia Sandri, Claudio Novelli
papers.abstract: Research on artificial intelligence and democracy has grown quickly over the last decade. A shared conclusion in this literature is that AI does not create new democratic problems so much as it makes old ones worse. We now see this across information ecosystems, in elections, and in public administration. However, despite growing evidence, we lack a clear way to prioritize risks in this area, compare them across domains, and identify where democratic control is most likely to break down. So, our problem is: How can we systematize the problems that AI systems pose to democratic processes? This paper argues that principal agent theory may fit the task. In many phases of democratic systems, principals delegate key functions to AI systems and their providers without really being able to monitor how these systems operate or the outputs they produce. Treating AI as a delegation problem helps identify accountability gaps and other governance failures. Most importantly, as we shall illustrate, it provides metrics for empirical assessments of AI impact on democracy. As a second analytical element, we draw on the NIST AI Risk Management Framework and its seven characteristics of trustworthy AI, which supply substantive criteria for evaluating delegated tasks. Operationalized across the three domains through measurable indicators and domain specific trustworthiness criteria, we propose an analytical framework that centers on institutional assessability as the central condition for democratic control over AI. However, we stress that how severe a harm is, and how much risk is acceptable, are evaluative judgments that current methodologies neither acknowledge nor operationalize. This becomes acute when such evaluative judgments are (silently) delegated to private vendors. We identify this as a strong limitation left for future work.
papers.idea: Establish institutional audit frameworks to monitor AI system delegation and accountability in city government using principal-agent theory and NIST trustworthiness criteria.
papers.summary: This paper proposes a theoretical framework for systematizing risks that AI systems pose to democratic processes by applying principal-agent theory and NIST AI Risk Management Framework criteria. It argues that treating AI deployment as a delegation problem can identify accountability gaps, but acknowledges that evaluative judgments about acceptable risk levels remain unmeasured and often silently delegated to private vendors.
papers.method: Non-empirical conceptual framework drawing on principal-agent theory and NIST standards; no new data collection or empirical testing described.
papers.scalability_note: Framework is abstractly generalizable but requires substantial institutional customization per domain and jurisdiction.
papers.cost_note: Audit infrastructure requires initial design and ongoing staffing but no per-unit costs specified.
papers.nyc_note: Relevant to NYC governance (algorithm audits in criminal justice, welfare, education) but paper provides no concrete implementation guidance, metrics, or case studies for a city to operationalize.

papers.title: Modeling Nonlinear Ability Trajectories and Learner Heterogeneity in Online Learning: A Bayesian Nonparametric Dynamic IRT Framework
papers.authors: Zhihua Ma, Alice Xu, Icy Zhang, Guanyu Hu
papers.abstract: Online learning has amplified the need to understand how student engagement patterns influence learning outcomes, particularly given the flexibility of technology-mediated environments. To address this, we propose a Bayesian nonparametric dynamic item response theory (IRT) framework that tracks within-individual ability trajectories across instructional units. The proposed model integrates B-spline basis expansions to capture nonlinear effects of engagement behaviors on ability drift, alongside a Mixture-of-Finite-Mixtures (MFM) prior to automatically determine the number of latent learner clusters. This framework overcomes three limitations in the existing literature: (1) rigid linearity assumptions in engagement-ability relationships, (2) dependence on pre-specified cluster counts, and (3) the inability to track longitudinal ability dynamics. We apply the model to longitudinal data from 198 undergraduates completing a 9-chapter introductory statistics course on CourseKata. The model automatically identified four distinct learner profiles: struggling-declining (11\%), low-stable (23\%), mainstream-stable (55\%), and high-improving (12\%). Results indicate that ability trajectories remained remarkably stable across chapters, and engagement quantity metrics did not significantly predict ability drift. These findings suggest that in introductory online statistics education, academic ability primarily reflects a stable pre-existing characteristic rather than a dynamically malleable course outcome. Ultimately, this framework offers a flexible tool for learner profiling to inform adaptive instructional design.
papers.idea: None—this is a methodological paper on statistical modeling of online learning, not a tested intervention.
papers.summary: The authors develop a Bayesian statistical framework to track how student ability changes across chapters in an online statistics course. Applied to 198 undergraduates, the model identified four learner profiles and found that ability trajectories were stable and engagement quantity did not predict ability gains.
papers.method: Observational longitudinal study of 198 undergraduates in a 9-chapter online introductory statistics course (CourseKata), using a newly developed Bayesian nonparametric dynamic IRT model with B-spline expansions and mixture-of-finite-mixtures clustering.
papers.scalability_note: Framework is a statistical tool for analysis; requires domain expertise and computational infrastructure to apply elsewhere.
papers.cost_note: Cost of implementing the statistical model itself is low, but value depends entirely on whether actionable insights emerge—this study did not identify interventions.
papers.nyc_note: No concrete policy, program, or practice lever. City agencies would need to first decide *why* to profile online learners and *how* to use profiles to change instruction—neither addressed here.

papers.title: Position: Align AI to Our Aspirations, Not Our Flaws
papers.authors: Nikita Kazeev, Bui Nhat Huyen Phan
papers.abstract: We argue that aligning AI to aggregated human preferences is the wrong target. With current technology, one can train AIs to share the values of a Silicon Valley techno-optimist, a degrowth environmentalist, a national-conservative culture warrior, a single-party state cadre, or a devout religious traditionalist. We should not. Human values produce societies that thrive or fail on the merits of those values - from failed states and extreme inequality to declining happiness, political polarization, and government dysfunction in the world's wealthiest democracies. The pluralistic-alignment program correctly diagnoses that there is no single "humanity" to align with, but is dangerous if taken as the main directive. We argue that AI should be trained to a non-negotiable floor of objective alignment goals - competence, bounded by the constraints of factual accuracy, honesty, and lawfulness and that pluralism belongs at the surface (language, register, conventions, missing-context defaults) and across the wide band of legitimate value tradeoffs that respect the floor, but not at the level of values that violate it. We highlight the empirical reality of unfiltered pluralistic values, propose four commitments as a constructive alternative, and engage six credible objections: commercial pressure and practical feasibility, democratic legitimacy, regulatory compliance, over-reliance on institutionalist explanations, the charge that the floor itself is culturally laden, and the limits of Coherent Extrapolated Volition.
papers.idea: Establish non-negotiable AI alignment floors (factual accuracy, honesty, lawfulness) before deploying AI systems in city services.
papers.summary: This is a position paper arguing that AI systems should be aligned to objective standards (factual accuracy, honesty, lawfulness) rather than aggregated human preferences or single value systems. The authors critique both purely pluralistic alignment and value-laden alternatives, proposing instead a tiered approach with a mandatory ethical/epistemic floor and pluralism only at surface and legitimate-tradeoff levels.
papers.method: Philosophical argument with engagement of prior literature and objections; no empirical study, data analysis, or intervention testing.
papers.scalability_note: Principles are universally applicable but implementation requires institutional buy-in and developer compliance.
papers.cost_note: Depends entirely on deployment model; no cost analysis provided in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: City has limited direct leverage over AI vendor practices; would require procurement standards or regulation that aligns with vendor incentives.

papers.title: Digital Twin-Based Simulation for Predictive Decision-Making in Waterway Logistics
papers.authors: Matthijs Jansen op de Haar, Daniel Frutos Rodriguez
papers.abstract: This paper investigates the potential of a Digital Twin (DT) for freight routing across inland waterway networks under uncertain water-level conditions. Existing approaches insufficiently account for increasing climate-induced volatility in water levels, which often result in higher operational costs and emissions due to the need for more expensive transport alternatives, such as road transport. These existing methods often rely on reactive countermeasures to remain resilient. To address these limitations, six interviews with experts in domains related to inland shipping were conducted to identify three common contingency scenarios and appropriate operational responses. These scenarios were subsequently incorporated into a time-sliced simulation environment in which predictive decision-making, enabled by a DT environment, was compared against reactive approaches. The results demonstrate that predictive modeling substantially reduces operational costs and modal shifts at prediction accuracies between 70% and 100%, despite extreme conditions. In addition, the predictive model achieves an average 28.3% reduction in fuel-related costs by reducing the total distance ships travel. The simulation outcomes were evaluated together with domain experts to assess the practical relevance and applicability of the proposed DT-enabled approach.
papers.idea: Use digital twin simulations to optimize freight routing on inland waterways under climate-driven water-level uncertainty, reducing modal shifts to road transport.
papers.summary: The study compared predictive decision-making (via digital twin simulation) against reactive approaches for freight routing on inland waterways facing climate-induced water-level volatility. Predictive modeling reduced operational costs and fuel use by ~28% at prediction accuracies of 70–100%, and decreased costly shifts to road transport.
papers.method: Qualitative expert interviews (n=6) to identify contingency scenarios, followed by time-sliced simulation comparison of predictive versus reactive strategies, with expert validation of results.
papers.scalability_note: Applicable only to cities with substantial inland waterway freight networks; most U.S. cities lack this.
papers.cost_note: Modeling and simulation infrastructure required; ongoing operational savings possible but not quantified in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has limited inland waterway freight logistics; the intervention targets a niche transport mode with minimal city-policy lever.

papers.title: How Insights from Private-Sector Data Can Strengthen Economic Policymaking
papers.authors: Madeline Brown, Rekha Balu, Annie Heinrichs
papers.abstract: The US economy is changing faster than public data systems can keep up. That’s because these systems were built for a very different economy: one with slower-moving production cycles and clearer categories of economic activity. In contrast, today’s economy is more hyperlocal and more uneven across people and places. At the same time, staffing reductions have led to delays in data releases and low response rates on national surveys like the decennial census. These challenges have made it more difficult for federal statistical infrastructure—typically a backbone for economic data—to provide timely indicators. In particular, it’s a serious challenge for state and local policymakers, who for decades have relied on federal datasets for essential insights into the job market, affordability, and financial security in their communities. Data collected by private companies could help fill this gap. Banks, supermarkets, online job boards, and other companies are already collecting consumer data that can
papers.idea: Cities could incorporate private-sector data (from banks, retailers, job boards) into local economic monitoring systems to supplement slow federal datasets.
papers.summary: The paper argues that federal statistical systems are too slow and outdated to track today's hyperlocal, uneven economy. Private companies collect real-time consumer and labor-market data that could fill gaps in federal datasets for state and local policymakers. The authors propose using private data to improve timeliness of indicators on jobs, affordability, and financial security.
papers.method: Position paper/essay; no empirical study conducted. The abstract describes the problem and proposes private-data as a solution but does not present research findings or test the proposal.
papers.scalability_note: Requires data-sharing agreements and governance frameworks; variable by private-sector cooperation.
papers.cost_note: Depends heavily on private-sector pricing and data-access negotiation; unclear from abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has sophisticated municipal data systems; private-data integration would require governance, privacy, and legal agreements not yet outlined.

papers.title: Three Key Facts on Intergenerational Wealth Transfer
papers.authors: Mingli Zhong
papers.abstract: Economic success in the US is often credited to individual effort. But wealth accumulation also depends on structural and historical factors. A projected $124 trillion will change hands between now and 2048, the largest intergenerational wealth transfer in history. This brief analyzes who inherits what, how adults without children transfer wealth, and when money flows upward, from younger to older generations. Why This Matters The data reveal two parallel realities. First, downward transfers (wealth passed from older to younger generations) are larger and more frequent among white families, reinforcing their intergenerational advantage. Second, upward transfers (financial support from adult children to aging parents) are more common among Black and Hispanic families, reflecting compensatory support within constrained economic contexts. For many families of color, wealth transfer may deliver little to nothing, or even delay younger generations’ own wealth-building efforts. Policymakers need clear data to design estate rules, retirement programs, and wealth-building
papers.idea: None identified; paper is descriptive analysis only.
papers.summary: The brief documents racial disparities in intergenerational wealth transfer: white families experience larger downward transfers reinforcing advantage, while Black and Hispanic families more commonly provide upward financial support to aging parents, constraining younger generations' wealth building.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis of wealth transfer patterns; no methodology specified in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Policy recommendations mentioned but not detailed; abstract does not propose specific interventions.
papers.cost_note: Cost unknown; abstract offers no program proposal with budget implications.
papers.nyc_note: No concrete city-level policy lever articulated. Estate and retirement rules are primarily federal/state; brief notes policymakers need data but stops short of actionable recommendations.

papers.title: Clarifying the Future Roles of Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac
papers.authors: Michael Fratantoni
papers.idea: N/A — no concrete intervention identified
papers.summary: This appears to be a policy analysis or opinion piece on the future organizational and regulatory roles of Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, the government-sponsored enterprises that dominate U.S. mortgage markets. Without an abstract, the specific findings or arguments are unclear.
papers.method: Unknown; no abstract provided.
papers.scalability_note: Federal GSE policy has nationwide reach but limited direct city-level lever.
papers.cost_note: Unknown; GSE reform involves no direct budgetary cost to cities but system-wide mortgage-market effects.
papers.nyc_note: NYC cannot unilaterally reform federal GSEs; relevant only if advocating to federal policymakers.

papers.title: Property Insurance Affordability
papers.authors: Jun Zhu, Janneke Ratcliffe, John Walsh, Bryson Berry
papers.abstract: Growing property risk is casting a shadow over the American dream of homeownership. Insurance products such as standard homeowner’s insurance, flood insurance, fire insurance, and other specialized lines are a financial first line of defense, protecting homeowners and the financial institutions who lend to them from these mounting risks. But the rising cost of insurance is threatening the ability of would-be buyers to attain homeownership and, increasingly, the ability of existing homeowners to sustain it. And rising costs are a growing concern for entities who hold and manage mortgage credit risk, including lenders and servicers and the federally backed agencies who guarantee mortgage securities. Why This Matters Homeownership has long been the primary pathway for building financial security and opportunity in the United States, yet it is becoming less affordable. Insurance adds another cost pressure. If households cannot afford to adequately protect their most significant asset, the consequences extend beyond
papers.idea: Cities could regulate or subsidize property insurance markets to make homeownership insurance more affordable for low- and moderate-income buyers.
papers.summary: The abstract identifies rising property insurance costs as a barrier to homeownership affordability and mortgage sustainability, affecting households, lenders, and federally backed mortgage agencies. It frames insurance as a 'financial first line of defense' against growing property risks but does not present findings or propose interventions.
papers.method: Unknown from abstract; appears to be a policy brief or problem statement rather than empirical research.
papers.scalability_note: Insurance regulation is primarily state-level; city-level levers are limited (e.g., mitigation incentives, coordination with state regulators).
papers.cost_note: Subsidy or regulatory approaches could be expensive depending on scope; no cost data in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has limited direct authority over insurance pricing/products (NY State Department of Financial Services regulates); could coordinate mitigation/resilience programs but no concrete city intervention is identifiable.

papers.title: High Interest Rates and Expensive Home Prices Continued to Make Homeownership Less Affordable in 2025, New Data Show
papers.authors: John Walsh, Jung Hyun Choi
papers.abstract: Mortgage activity remained subdued in 2025, with a modest uptick in loan originations driven mainly by refinancing rather than home purchases. That’s according to the latest release of mortgage origination data collected through the Home Mortgage Disclosure Act (HMDA) by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau. The new data show how persistently high interest rates and home prices are continuing to make homeownership less affordable, pushing up debt-to-income (DTI) ratios and up-front costs. These conditions are raising barriers to homeownership, especially for first-time buyers, young buyers, and buyers with low incomes and low wealth. Here, we explore three key trends in mortgage activity between 2021 and 2025, a uniquely volatile period shaped by the COVID-19 pandemic, a sustained rise in home prices, and a surge in interest rates. Mortgage originations went up slightly in 2025, driven by refinancing Since mortgage activity surged in 2020 and 2021, the housing market has been
papers.idea: None identified—this is diagnostic data on mortgage trends, not a policy intervention.
papers.summary: Mortgage originations remained subdued in 2025, driven mainly by refinancing rather than purchases. High interest rates and home prices continue to raise debt-to-income ratios and barriers to homeownership, especially for first-time, young, and low-income buyers.
papers.method: Analysis of Home Mortgage Disclosure Act (HMDA) data collected by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, covering 2021–2025.
papers.scalability_note: Federal mortgage/interest-rate policy levers; limited direct city intervention scope.
papers.cost_note: No specific program or intervention proposed; cost of potential policy responses unknown.
papers.nyc_note: NYC cannot control federal rates or HMDA data collection. Could inform local affordable-housing policy but no concrete lever is specified.

papers.title: Why Cities Customize National Policy During Implementation
papers.authors: Anders L. Nielsen
papers.abstract: Cities commonly serve as implementation agencies for the state, raising questions regarding compliance and adaptation. This paper asks why cities facing similar national demands sometimes choose different implementation strategies. It argues that local implementation strategies are a function of city council interests and local implementation conditions. The argument is tested through a comprehensive study of new Danish legislation on vulnerable social housing areas introduced in 2018, which required 10 Danish cities to develop radical physical redevelopment plans for 15 designated areas. Drawing on extensive public documents, internal archives (over 13,000 pages), and 11 key elite interviews, the analysis reveals how local implementation strategies were strongly shaped by how well the new policy aligned with city council interests and local implementation conditions. In doing so, the study contributes to a theoretical integration of urban politics scholarship with the customization argument developed in U.S. federal and EU implementation research.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a theoretical study of policy implementation mechanics, not an intervention.
papers.summary: This paper analyzes why Danish cities adopted different implementation strategies for the same national housing redevelopment mandate in 2018. The study found that local strategies were shaped by city council political interests and local conditions rather than uniform compliance.
papers.method: Comparative case study of 10 Danish cities implementing one national policy, using public documents, 13,000+ pages of internal archives, and 11 elite interviews.
