papers.title: Reforming Payment for Clinician-Administered Drugs — Most-Favored-Nation Pricing in Medicare Part B
papers.authors: Thomas Hwang, Lisa Clemans-Cope, Aaron Kesselheim
papers.abstract: The federal government is proposing a new way to lower the amount that Medicare pays for drugs administered in doctors’ offices and clinics. The Global Benchmark for Efficient Drug Pricing (GLOBE) model would tie Medicare payments for certain high-cost drugs (single-source drugs and sole-source biological products in select therapeutic classes) to prices charged in other wealthy countries. This paper examines how the proposal works, what it could accomplish, and where it falls short. Why This Matters Americans pay far more for prescription drugs than patients in other countries. Medicare Part B, which reimburses providers for drugs administered by clinicians, including cancer therapies, spent $46 billion on drugs in 2024, up from $22 billion a decade earlier. Patients bear a share of those costs directly. Reforms that bring prices closer to international levels could save money for Medicare, taxpayers, and patients alike. Policymakers, patient advocates, and health care providers all have
papers.idea: Cities could advocate for or pilot most-favored-nation drug pricing models for municipal employee health plans and safety-net hospitals.
papers.summary: The paper examines the federal GLOBE model, which would tie Medicare Part B payments for high-cost drugs to international prices. It analyzes how the proposal works, its potential savings for Medicare and patients, and its limitations. The study is a policy analysis of a federal Medicare reform, not a city-level intervention.
papers.method: Policy analysis and examination of a federal Medicare proposal; descriptive review of the GLOBE model's design and implications.

papers.cost_note: If adopted, would reduce drug costs system-wide; no implementation cost to cities provided.

papers.title: Brady Meixell on Opportunity Zone goals and outcomes

papers.abstract: The Opportunity Zone (OZ) program was established as part of the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act to revitalize low-income rural and urban communities through incentivized long-term investments. Brady Meixell, a housing and communities expert at the Urban Institute, spoke with the Portland Press Herald about how existing research on the program shows that OZ investments are often driven by private market considerations rather than community needs. “You’re not shaping the market, you’re kind of layering on top of existing markets,” said Meixell. For inquiries, please email media@urban.org.

papers.summary: Brady Meixell of the Urban Institute discusses findings on Opportunity Zones, a federal tax incentive program. The research indicates that OZ investments tend to follow existing market dynamics rather than address community needs, suggesting a misalignment between program intent and outcomes.

papers.title: Attitudes Toward Racial Outgroups During Gentrification
papers.authors: Clayton Becker
papers.abstract: Gentrification is a pressing concern for many lower income neighborhoods around the country. However, despite voluminous evidence of the economic and health impacts of gentrification, we know less about how gentrification impacts attitudes. I investigate whether gentrification of Black neighborhoods is associated with more negative attitudinal evaluations of the racial group of their gentrifiers, and whether these attitudes are the same for gentrifiers of different racial groups. I construct a merged dataset combining individual responses from the Democracy Fund + UCLA Nationscape Survey, ZIP code level contextual data from the American Community Survey, and an original dataset of restaurant prices and reviews. In general, in-migration of non-Black newcomers does not lead to more negative attitudes toward these newcomers’ racial group. White-led gentrification of Black neighborhoods may lead to more negative views toward Whites, but only when accompanied by substantial rent increases. Latino-led and Asian-led gentrification are not associated with similar effects.
papers.summary: The study examines whether gentrification of Black neighborhoods correlates with more negative racial attitudes toward the gentrifiers' group. White-led gentrification accompanied by substantial rent increases was associated with more negative views of Whites, while Latino-led and Asian-led gentrification showed no such effect.
papers.method: Merged observational dataset combining individual survey responses from Democracy Fund + UCLA Nationscape Survey, ZIP code contextual data from American Community Survey, and original restaurant price/review data; correlational analysis by gentrifier race.
papers.scalability_note: Describes attitude patterns only; no intervention proposed for cities to implement.
papers.cost_note: No program or intervention specified; cost not applicable.
papers.nyc_note: Pure descriptive research on attitudes; identifies no actionable policy lever a city could operationalize.

papers.title: Probabilistic Identification of Technology Tipping Points in Deeply Decarbonised Energy Systems
papers.authors: Gian Müller, Thomas Schöb, Jann M. Weinand, Iain Staffell
papers.abstract: Energy policy is often guided by a small set of least-cost pathways to net-zero emissions, despite wide uncertainty in technology performance, fuel prices, demand and weather. To avoid overstating confidence in any single pathway, we quantify the likelihood of alternative technology pathways and identify the assumptions driving divergence, including the conditions under which technologies reach critical tipping points in competitiveness. We couple a sector-linked national optimisation model with Monte Carlo sampling (10,000 runs) across two European power systems (Germany and Great Britain) to generate probability distributions of capacity expansion and robust cost thresholds for key technologies. Results reveal substantial ambiguity in the future roles of wind versus solar, gas with carbon capture, and negative-emissions options. Tipping points vary widely with system conditions, while cross-country differences highlight the role of institutional constraints and resource endowments. Britain exhibits an either-or decision around nuclear power, investing if costs in 2035 fall below EUR 4700/kW, otherwise favouring offshore wind. Germany's uncertainty centres on dispatchable low-carbon options: gas with carbon capture (below EUR 2100/kW), biomass with carbon capture (below EUR 4200/kW), or hydrogen if electrolysis is below EUR 560/kW. We reframe scenario analysis as risk management by linking uncertainty to cost targets and minimum deployment requirements for robust net-zero strategies.
papers.idea: No actionable city-level intervention identified; this is a methodological/forecasting study for national energy planners.
papers.summary: The study uses Monte Carlo simulation across 10,000 scenarios to model uncertainty in technology pathways toward net-zero emissions in Germany and Great Britain, identifying cost thresholds at which wind, solar, nuclear, hydrogen, and carbon-capture technologies become competitively dominant. Results show substantial ambiguity in future technology mix depending on cost assumptions and system conditions.
papers.method: Sector-linked national optimization model coupled with Monte Carlo sampling (10,000 runs) applied to two European power systems; no empirical data collection or intervention testing.
papers.scalability_note: Modeling framework is replicable but requires country-level energy system data and expertise; not a proven practice to 'scale.'
papers.cost_note: Study itself is academic research; no intervention cost quantified. Cost thresholds are for technology deployment, not city adoption.
papers.nyc_note: NYC cannot independently adopt national energy system pathways; results are for UK/EU national grids. City can only influence through procurement and local policy within state/federal constraints.

papers.title: Battery Bidding under Price Uncertainty in Wholesale Electricity Markets
papers.authors: Vincent Yinjun-Wang, Madeleine Udell
papers.abstract: Grid-scale batteries increasingly influence outcomes in wholesale electricity markets, but their observed bid patterns remain difficult to interpret. In particular, bids that appear to reflect strategic withholding may instead arise from rational operations under price uncertainty and risk management. We develop an asset-level model of a price-taking battery that submits stepwise buy and sell bid curves in the day-ahead market under a finite set of price scenarios. The battery chooses quantity--price pairs to maximize a mean--CVaR objective subject to physical and market constraints. A direct formulation is a mixed-integer linear program, but we show that its integer decisions can be removed, yielding an exact linear programming reformulation suitable for empirical analysis. Our empirical results deliver three insights. First, withholding behavior can arise even without market power, because scarce stored energy and uncertain future prices increase the value of holding energy. Second, the effect of uncertainty depends on the state of charge: when stored energy is scarce, greater uncertainty raises sell bid prices, whereas when stored energy is abundant it can lower them. Third, risk management reshapes bid curves into layered structures that secure profitable execution across a broad set of scenarios while preserving some exposure to rare but valuable price spikes.
papers.idea: No actionable intervention identified; this is a theoretical modeling paper on battery bidding behavior.
papers.summary: The paper develops a mathematical model to explain why grid-scale batteries submit certain bid patterns in electricity markets, showing that apparent strategic withholding can arise from rational price uncertainty management rather than market manipulation. The model predicts how bid curves reshape based on stored energy levels and price risk.
papers.method: Mixed-integer linear program formulation with empirical application to wholesale market data; no controlled trial or policy experiment conducted.
papers.scalability_note: Academic modeling framework; no policy tool or operational program to scale.
papers.cost_note: Theoretical contribution with no direct implementation costs or benefits quantified.
papers.nyc_note: NYC does not directly operate wholesale electricity markets or set battery bidding rules; NYISO (operator) does, and this paper offers no concrete policy lever for city agencies.

papers.title: Strategies for preventing and reversing polarized online discourse
papers.authors: Leon Klingborg, Kenneth Mavor, Alexander J. Stewart
papers.abstract: Political polarization poses a variety of challenges for modern democracies. Entrenched disagreements on policy can prevent constructive discourse and compromise, and high levels of affective polarization threaten to undermine social cohesion and support for institutions. Finding ways to promote constructive discourse while maintaining free expression has proved a challenge for social media platforms, media outlets and policy makers alike. Here we develop a computational model -- based in psychology -- of online discourse and opinion dynamics under complex individual identities, which we use to assess the capacity of realistic interventions to prevent or reverse polarization. We show that changes to the range of acceptable opinions in a society -- i.e. the Overton window -- have a limited impact on polarization, and that attempts to ``optimize'' the Overton window can even trigger the onset of polarization. In contrast, interventions that shift attention towards under-discussed topics, or increase the costs of violating existing norms, are often effective at preventing polarization, but are less successful at reversing it. Most strikingly, increasing the salience of influential individuals, who model non-polarized discourse, can be highly effective at both preventing and reversing polarization. However we also find that once polarization has set in, even the most successful interventions result in latent extremism when identities are complex. Our work suggests that restricting speech by shrinking the range of acceptable discourse is an ineffective way to tackle polarization, whereas enforcement of existing norms, attention nudges and the presence of elites who model good behavior can be highly effective.
papers.idea: Use computational models to identify and amplify non-polarized influencers and shift media attention to under-discussed topics as a way to prevent online political polarization.
papers.summary: A computational model based on psychological theory of opinion dynamics shows that restricting speech (narrowing the Overton window) is ineffective at reducing polarization, while enforcement of existing norms, attention shifts to neglected topics, and amplification of non-polarized influential figures are more effective. However, once polarization sets in, even successful interventions leave latent extremism when identities are complex.
papers.method: Computational/agent-based model grounded in psychology; no empirical data, RCT, or real-world implementation described.
papers.scalability_note: Model-based; unclear how to operationalize 'salience of influential individuals' or 'shift attention' at city scale without knowing which platforms/outlets or mechanisms.
papers.cost_note: Costs would depend entirely on implementation approach (media campaigns, platform partnerships, etc.); abstract provides no cost data.
papers.nyc_note: Abstract offers no concrete policy levers a city government could directly adopt; mostly addresses social media platforms and media outlets, not municipal agencies.

papers.title: Federated Fair Trade Energy: Speculative Fabulation for a Planet with Limits
papers.authors: Dawn Nafus, Laura Watts
papers.abstract: What happens to permacomputing when electricity grids shift to decentralised green energy, and local communities and municipalities have increased governance over this vital public service? Electricity and computational networks are more than just separate systems that plug together. Shifts to renewable energy generation in the grid are impacting computational systems, and computational demands on electrical power are impacting the electricity grid; one infrastructure limits the other. Permacomputing research tends to focus on 'off-grid' or 'behind-the-meter' energy. This sacrifices some of the social justice benefits that the public electricity grid, managed and regulated for universal service, was designed to provide. Our paper uses empirically-grounded 'speculative fabulation' to identify research opportunities in permacomputing that open up when energy systems are federated across communities. The speculative fabulation takes the form of an interview between the energy manager of a future energy island in the North Sea and a permacomputing podcaster. This allows us to conceive of computing-grid integration in tractable social and ecological terms, and introduce a notion of 'fair trade energy'.
papers.idea: Federate electricity grid governance across municipalities to integrate renewable energy with computational demands.
papers.summary: The paper uses speculative fabulation (a fictional narrative device) to explore how decentralized renewable energy grids could reshape computational infrastructure. It introduces the concept of 'fair trade energy' through an imagined interview between a future energy manager and a podcaster, arguing that federated energy systems could preserve social-justice benefits of public electricity grids while supporting permacomputing.
papers.method: Speculative fabulation presented as a fictional dialogue; no empirical data, no experimental design, no case studies.
papers.scalability_note: Speculative/theoretical framework with no tested implementation pathway or evidence of feasibility.
papers.cost_note: Unknown; no cost analysis provided. Implementation costs of federated grid governance could be substantial.
papers.nyc_note: NYC operates within an existing regulated utility structure (Con Edison); grid federalization is a long-term systemic change with unclear regulatory levers for city government.

papers.title: SCAN: A Decision-Making Framework for Effective Task Allocation with Generative AI
papers.authors: Fendi Tsim, Alina Gutoreva
papers.abstract: We introduce SCAN -- a human-centric decision-making framework to facilitate learners for effective task allocation with Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) based on Vygotsky's Zone of Proximal Development and Metacognition. In SCAN, we systematize and formalize AI-human interaction by introducing a task-identification approach with four "sub-zones": Substitute, Complement, Aid, and Non-negotiable. After describing the four sub-zones, we demonstrate how SCAN framework can be applied for knowledge workers in the workplace and students in education to metacognitively "scan" their use of Generative AI. We then discuss how such framework can be related to cognitive load theory, cognitive offloading, sycophancy, three decision-making modes in human-AI interactions (automation, augmentation, and collaboration), future of work such as upskilling and deskilling, and how it accounts for both human-human and human-AI learning. We propose that SCAN offers a great starting point before discussing whether GenAI complements or replaces our abilities when completing a task, with a general objective of sustaining lifelong learning, and a specific goal of reaching hybrid intelligence.
papers.idea: N/A — no concrete intervention proposed; purely theoretical framework
papers.summary: The paper introduces SCAN, a conceptual framework for helping knowledge workers and students decide when to use generative AI versus complete tasks independently. It categorizes AI use into four sub-zones (Substitute, Complement, Aid, Non-negotiable) and connects this to learning theory and cognitive load. No empirical study or real-world testing is presented.
papers.method: Non-empirical; theoretical framework synthesis drawing on Vygotsky's Zone of Proximal Development, metacognition theory, and cognitive load theory.
papers.scalability_note: Framework could be taught widely but lacks evidence of impact; transferability to policy contexts unclear.
papers.cost_note: Low cost to disseminate as guidance, but no cost-benefit data provided.
papers.nyc_note: Could inform NYC schools' AI literacy curricula, but paper offers no policy specifics or evidence of efficacy in classroom or municipal settings.

papers.title: Commons-Governed Artificial Intelligence: A Taxonomy of Collective Governance
papers.authors: Eduardo C. Garrido-Merchán
papers.abstract: The governance of artificial intelligence is overwhelmingly theorized through two institutional frames. In the market frame, the data, models, and compute that constitute the AI stack are private goods exchanged under property and contract; in the state frame, a regulator imposes rules from above. A third possibility, the collective and self-organized stewardship of AI-relevant resources by the communities that produce and depend on them, remains comparatively under-theorized, even as it proliferates in practice through data trusts and cooperatives, federated learning consortia, public compute initiatives, open-weight model collaborations, and community data sovereignty regimes. This article argues that these arrangements form a coherent institutional family, which we call commons-governed artificial intelligence, and that the analytic vocabulary developed by Elinor Ostrom and her successors for common-pool and knowledge commons is the right backbone for classifying them. We contribute a two-dimensional taxonomy whose first axis is the resource layer of the AI stack held in common, distinguishing data, compute, models, knowledge and evaluation, and energy, and whose second axis is the governance function performed, derived from Ostrom design principles. We populate the taxonomy by examining the published evidence layer by layer, locate ten recurrent institutional archetypes within it, synthesize their positions through a maturity matrix and a comparative reading against the eight design principles, and treat the energy and sustainability of computation as a first-class commons-governance problem rather than an externality. We close with the tensions that constrain the project, openwashing, the compute bottleneck, free-riding, and the tension between scale and sustainability, and with a research agenda for a polycentric AI commons.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a theoretical taxonomy paper with no concrete intervention
papers.summary: The paper develops a classification framework for commons-governed AI systems (data trusts, federated learning consortia, open-weight model collaborations, etc.) using Elinor Ostrom's design principles for common-pool resources. It identifies ten institutional archetypes and discusses tensions like openwashing, compute bottlenecks, and free-riding.
papers.method: Conceptual taxonomy and synthesis of published evidence on existing commons-governed AI initiatives; no empirical data collection or evaluation of outcomes.
papers.scalability_note: Framework is theoretical; no evidence of implementation effectiveness or scaling pathways.
papers.cost_note: Likely moderate cost to establish governance structures, but no implementation data provided.
papers.nyc_note: Paper offers vocabulary and taxonomy but no tested models for NYC agencies to adopt; would require separate pilot work.

papers.title: Wealth Inequality and Planetary Boundaries in a Stylized Agent-Based Model
papers.authors: Thomas Valade, Michael Benzaquen, Matthieu Cristelli, Stanislao Gualdi, Pierre Lenders
papers.abstract: At the intersection of rising wealth inequality and intensifying environmental pressures, we investigate a reverse causal relationship that has received comparatively little attention: wealth inequality may not only be a consequence of environmental crises, but also act as a structural obstacle to the ecological transition itself. We develop a stylized agent-based model in which heterogeneous agents, whose initial wealth follows a Pareto distribution, allocate their income between either a Brown or a Green sector through a utility function. The function is designed to capture the trade-off between short-term returns and exposure to long-term systemic risks. A central ingredient is that wealthier agents perceive themselves as less vulnerable to environmental shocks, thereby reducing the amount of resources available for the transition. We show that, beyond inequality thresholds compatible with those observed in most developed countries, the economy remains locked in a Brown regime, even when a substantial share of agents is sensitive to externalities. We then assess a set of stylized fiscal policies (basic income, carbon taxation, Green incentives, and a combined scheme) and find that their effectiveness depends strongly on the inequality regime and on the regressivity embedded in the fiscal mechanism, revealing multidimensional trade-offs between transition speed, cumulative environmental destruction, growth, and fiscal pressure.
papers.idea: N/A - no concrete, actionable intervention identified
papers.summary: The study uses an agent-based model to show that high wealth inequality creates a structural barrier to ecological transition, because wealthy agents perceive lower personal risk from environmental shocks and thus underinvest in green sectors. The authors test stylized fiscal policies (basic income, carbon tax, green incentives) and find their effectiveness varies by inequality regime and fiscal regressivity.
papers.method: Stylized agent-based computational model with heterogeneous agents whose wealth follows Pareto distribution; agents choose between Brown and Green sectors based on utility functions; no empirical data or real-world setting.
papers.scalability_note: Theoretical model; findings are regime-dependent and highly context-sensitive, limiting direct policy transfer.
papers.cost_note: If policies (carbon tax, basic income, green incentives) were implemented, costs would vary widely; abstract gives no cost estimates.
papers.nyc_note: Model is abstract and stylized; no grounding in NYC economy, institutions, or empirical baselines. General insights (e.g., inequality matters for climate transition) already known; no NYC-specific intervention emerges.

papers.title: Urban Institute and AEI Launch Bipartisan Commission on Artificial Intelligence and the American Workforce
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: WASHINGTON, DC, June 11, 2026 — The Urban Institute and the American Enterprise Institute today launched the Commission on Artificial Intelligence and the American Workforce, a twelve-month effort to assess how artificial intelligence is reshaping jobs, skills, and earnings, and to develop policy options that help workers, employers, and government entities adapt. The commission brings together 20 commissioners from business, labor, academia, and government. Former commerce secretary and Rhode Island Governor Gina Raimondo and former speaker of the house Paul Ryan will serve as co-chairs. The work is directed by Brent Orrell, senior fellow at AEI, and Elisabeth Jacobs, executive director of WorkRise at the Urban Institute. The commission will organize its work around six research tracks and report findings on a twelve-month timeline. It will examine how AI adoption affects specific occupations and wage levels, what the technology means for upskilling and reskilling workers, and what kinds of education
papers.idea: Commission to develop federal and state workforce policy recommendations on AI adoption and worker reskilling.
papers.summary: A bipartisan commission launched by Urban Institute and AEI will spend twelve months assessing how AI reshapes jobs, skills, and earnings across occupations, and develop policy options for workers, employers, and government. The commission comprises 20 commissioners across sectors and will report findings on a twelve-month timeline examining AI adoption effects on specific occupations, wage levels, and upskilling/reskilling pathways.

papers.title: Redeveloping Athens: State–Municipal Entrepreneurship in Postcrisis City
papers.authors: Dimitris Poulios
papers.abstract: The article examines the interplay of crisis, urban restructuring, and local governance in Athens, focusing on the role of state and municipal entrepreneurship in transforming the cityscape amid financial austerity and postcrisis redevelopment. Utilizing the Greek debt crisis as a contextual framework and based on similar work on austerity urbanism and entrepreneurialism, the article highlights how crises reshape urban policy, local agency, and institutional frameworks. By analyzing Athens as a prime example of crisis-driven urban transitions, it explores the dynamics of pro-market reforms and the emergence of new governance models. Particular attention is given in the role of local actors and particularly the private sector (private investors, developers and philanthropic foundations) in shaping urban development and the interplay between these actors and public institutions. Through this lens, Athens serves as a case study for understanding the broader implications of austerity urbanism and late entrepreneurialism in cities navigating economic, social, and environmental challenges. This work contributes to urban theory by emphasizing place-based approaches and the critical role of localized responses to global crises in urban transformation.

It analyzes the interplay between public institutions, private investors, developers, and philanthropic foundations in driving urban redevelopment under austerity constraints.

papers.title: Optimal Medical Liability for AI
papers.authors: Alex Chan
papers.abstract: I study medical liability when artificial intelligence acts as a doctor rather than as a passive clinical tool. The central object is the legally usable medical record: the inputs, logs, warnings, prescriptions, follow-up instructions, and outcomes on which courts, contracts, insurers, and regulators can condition responsibility. I show that AI medical liability is an institutional design problem under imperfect legal information. If the record separates AI-controllable error from patient nonadherence and natural disease progression, high-powered AI-fault liability implements the standard accident-law ideal. If the record is coarse, the first best may be infeasible: the same transfer that disciplines the AI also insures the patient's hidden action. With joint causation, the relevant object is a marginal-responsibility score rather than a posterior cause label. I characterize the feasible set of liability incentives generated by the record and show when the optimal rule is no liability, strict liability, negligence, a safe harbor, comparative fault, or a continuous warranty. I then study algorithmic defensive design, through which AI developers can design not only medical recommendations but also the record on which future liability depends. Adoption, learning, enterprise liability, insurance, no-fault compensation, and regulation enter as ways to change the record, the liable entity, or the financing of compensation. The framework yields conditional implications rather than a one-size-fits-all rule.
papers.idea: Design medical liability rules and clinical record standards to optimize AI-doctor accountability and patient safety incentives.
papers.summary: This paper presents a theoretical framework analyzing optimal medical liability structures when AI systems function as primary decision-makers rather than passive tools. The author characterizes how different liability regimes (no liability, strict liability, negligence, etc.) can be implemented depending on what information is captured in the medical record, and studies how AI developers can strategically design both medical recommendations and the records used to assign responsibility.
papers.method: Theoretical institutional-design analysis of liability mechanisms under different information structures; no empirical data or experiments.
papers.scalability_note: Requires coordination across medical licensing, insurance, and court systems; state/federal regulatory alignment needed.
papers.cost_note: Implementation costs vary widely by liability model chosen; no cost estimates provided in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has limited direct lever over medical liability law (state/federal domain); could influence through hospital systems or pilot programs.

papers.title: A Deep Zero-Inflated Model of North Atlantic Right Whale Presence To Support Blue Economy Management in the U.S. East Coast
papers.authors: Jiaxiang Ji, Laura Nazzaro, Josh Kohut, Ahmed Aziz Ezzat
papers.abstract: Effective modeling of endangered marine mammal species, such as the North Atlantic Right Whale, is critical for balancing marine conservation with the growing blue economy. Passive acoustic monitoring data collected by autonomous underwater vehicles provide new opportunities for localized marine species detection and oceanographic sensing, but introduce complex statistical challenges such as zero inflation, imperfect detection, and intricate dependence structures. In response, we propose the Deep Zero-Inflated Bernoulli (DeepZIB) model--a deep statistical method which jointly models latent species presence and conditional detection probabilities while learning complex habitat relationships from heterogeneous covariate information. We establish theoretical results on the model's structural properties and conduct simulation experiments to demonstrate its ability to recover underlying parameters and latent presence fields. Application to real-world passive acoustic monitoring data on the North Atlantic Right Whale along the U.S. East Coast demonstrates improved model adequacy and predictive performance in capturing the species' dynamic and spatially varying habitat. A key advantage of DeepZIB is its ability to generate high-resolution, spatially and temporally varying presence maps, providing valuable insights for targeted and risk-aware management of blue economy industries, ranging from offshore and marine energy, to fisheries management and maritime transport.
papers.idea: Use deep learning models of whale presence to manage offshore wind and maritime industries responsibly.
papers.summary: The authors developed a deep statistical method (DeepZIB) to detect North Atlantic Right Whales using acoustic monitoring data and model their habitat presence. The model generates high-resolution spatiotemporal presence maps to inform management of blue economy activities like offshore energy, fisheries, and maritime transport.
papers.method: Methodological paper proposing a novel deep learning model (Deep Zero-Inflated Bernoulli) applied to passive acoustic monitoring data from autonomous underwater vehicles; validation on real East Coast whale presence data.
papers.scalability_note: Limited to marine ecosystem monitoring; requires specialized underwater acoustic infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Autonomous underwater vehicles and acoustic monitoring systems are capital-intensive; cost per deployment not specified.
papers.nyc_note: NYC is not an offshore wind operator; whales are federal/regional concern, not municipal responsibility; application would be to state/federal ocean managers.

papers.title: Incumbent Advantage: Brand Bias and Cognitive Manipulation Dynamics in LLM Recommendation Systems
papers.authors: Xi Chu, Yupeng Hou
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are becoming a major way for consumers to find products, but we do not yet understand how brands compete in this new channel. We study brand dynamics in LLM recommendations using skincare products -- a category where consumers cannot easily judge quality before buying and must rely on brand reputation -- across three commercial LLMs (GPT-4o-mini, Claude Sonnet, Gemini 3 Flash), with a robustness check on search goods. In three experiments, we find: (1) a Conditional Monopoly where well-known brands get recommended 100% of the time (IAI = 10.0) when all products have the same specifications, but this dominance disappears with less than a +0.1-star rating advantage for a competitor; (2) authority-style marketing language, including fabricated clinical-evidence claims, breaks this monopoly at a Bias Surplus Value equal to +0.17 rating points, with each model responding differently; and (3) a social dilemma in multi-brand GEO competition: when all brands adopt the same optimization strategy, individual payoff falls from +0.802 to +0.007 in our payoff proxy, and non-participating brands receive zero recommendations in our tests. Our results suggest that generative engine optimization (GEO) should be studied not only as a security risk, but also as an emerging marketing practice that shapes market competition.
papers.idea: Cities could regulate or audit LLM-generated product recommendations to prevent deceptive marketing claims and ensure fair competition.
papers.summary: The study found that well-known brands receive disproportionate recommendation rates from commercial LLMs (GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini), but this dominance breaks down with minimal rating advantages or authority-style marketing language. The research identifies a potential market competition distortion where brands optimize for LLM recommendation algorithms, risking fabricated claims and reduced diversity of recommendations.
papers.method: Experimental study testing three commercial LLMs with skincare products under different rating and marketing language conditions, plus robustness checks on search goods.
papers.scalability_note: Requires federal/state regulation of AI systems; city-level enforcement limited and fragmented.
papers.cost_note: Auditing and enforcement would require new regulatory infrastructure but no direct subsidy.
papers.nyc_note: Consumer protection falls mainly to state/federal regulators; NYC has limited jurisdiction over AI platforms' recommendation algorithms.

papers.title: Leveraging Physiological Signals to Predict Exam Outcomes with Machine Learning
papers.authors: Lala Yamazaki, Ramchandra Rimal
papers.abstract: This study investigates the application of machine learning models to predict exam outcomes using physiological data collected during examination sessions. Physiological stress indicators, including electrodermal activity, heart rate, and skin temperature, were analyzed to uncover their association with academic performance. A variety of machine learning approaches were employed, ranging from standard models like logistic regression, random forest, and support vector machines to more advanced architectures, including transformers, long short-term memory (LSTM), and gated recurrent unit (GRU) models. This diversity aimed to capture the complex interactions within the data effectively. A key focus was assessing the adaptability of transformers in processing numerical data and evaluating their performance in this novel context. Standard performance metrics, such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, were used to compare model efficacy. The experimental results demonstrate that while deep learning models generally excel at capturing complex relationships in physiological data, simpler models like random forests can sometimes achieve superior performance while offering computational efficiency and interpretability. Furthermore, transformers demonstrated notable versatility, showcasing performances comparable to those of the LSTM and GRU models. This research underscores the importance of experimenting with a broad class of models that align with the objectives of the problem at hand, balancing precision, efficiency, and interpretability. By elucidating the relationships between physiological signals and academic performance, this study contributes to understanding stressors affecting students' mental health. It further promotes leveraging physiological data to enhance student well-being and academic outcomes.
papers.idea: Deploy wearable physiological sensors during school exams to predict student stress and intervene to improve academic outcomes.
papers.summary: The study applied machine learning models (logistic regression, random forests, SVMs, transformers, LSTMs, GRUs) to physiological data (electrodermal activity, heart rate, skin temperature) collected during exams to predict exam performance. Results showed that simpler models like random forests sometimes outperformed deep learning approaches while offering better interpretability and efficiency.
papers.method: Machine learning comparison study analyzing physiological signals collected during examination sessions; no sample size, setting, or experimental design details provided in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Requires real-time wearable sensors on every test-taker; logistical and privacy barriers in mass school settings.

papers.title: Three Things DC’s New Mayor and Council Need to Know About the Job Market
papers.authors: Jonathan Schwabish
papers.abstract: Washington, DC, has long operated under a different set of economic conditions than most American cities. With the federal government as its anchor employer and a dense ecosystem of contractors, consultants, and nonprofits built around it, the region has historically been insulated from the kinds of labor market shocks that rattle other areas of the country. But a confluence of forces—including the lingering effects of the remote work revolution, return-to-office mandates for federal workers, and an aggressive downsizing of the federal workforce—is now testing that insulation in ways the data are starting to capture. This election year, the incoming mayor and council will need to understand the effects of these trends if they want to solidify and expand the DC region’s economy. The timing matters enormously. The incoming policymakers will inherit an economy in transition, and the choices they make will shape DC’s fiscal health for years to come. When
papers.idea: No concrete intervention identified; analysis of federal employment trends affecting DC economy.

No specific policy recommendations or interventions are presented in the abstract.

papers.title: Evaluating the Costs of School District Secessions
papers.authors: Luisa Godinez-Puig, Karishma Furtado, Dashni Sathasivam
papers.abstract: Public schools are at the heart of a community. They are places where children learn, families meet, and neighbors share a common investment in the next generation. Schools are personal to those they serve, which is why their promise—a high-quality education for all—can seem unfulfilled when communities feel their needs aren’t being met. School district secession occurs when a city, town, or neighborhood decides to leave a larger school district and create a new one. Supporters often frame the move as a way to exert more local control over public schools, but the effects can ripple far beyond the new district’s borders, especially if the fragmentation concentrates resources into one district. Public schools rely on an array of mechanisms, including taxes, zoning, and school boundaries, for funding and organizational structure. But as research shows, these mechanisms can be applied in ways that expand or limit a district’s resources. Because district
papers.idea: Regulate or restrict school district secessions to prevent resource fragmentation that harms equity in public education.
papers.summary: This paper evaluates the costs and effects of school district secessions (when communities leave larger districts to form new ones). The abstract notes that while secessions are framed as enhancing local control, they can fragment resources and concentrate advantages in new districts while harming those left behind.
papers.method: Appears to be descriptive/policy analysis of secession mechanisms and their fiscal/organizational effects; no empirical study design specified in abstract.

papers.title: About
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: In this project we investigate the state-level costs of secessions, particularly those that concentrate resources. Secession occurs when a community unilaterally breaks off from a larger school district, creating a new district and a “left behind” district. When the seceded district is more advantaged than the one left behind, secession concentrates resources in the new district, which can lead to lower educational quality in the left behind district and entrenched segregation by class, race, ethnicity, or all of the above between the two. Secessions that concentrate resources may also reinforce existing hierarchies in educational advantage and power. School District Secession Attempts across the United States, by Status, 2000–2026 Source: 2000–2026 School District Secessions dataset, compiled by authors To better understand the consequences of secessions that concentrate resources, we aim to answer the following questions using a mixed-methods approach: At the district level: Does secession affect total and source-specific (i.e., local
papers.idea: Prevent or regulate school district secessions that concentrate resources in advantaged areas to reduce educational segregation and inequity.
papers.summary: This study investigates the state-level costs of school district secessions, particularly when more-advantaged communities break away from larger districts. The research examines whether secessions concentrate resources, worsen educational quality in left-behind districts, and entrench segregation by class, race, and ethnicity. The abstract indicates the project uses a mixed-methods approach and a 2000–2026 dataset but does not report findings.
papers.method: Mixed-methods analysis using a 2000–2026 school district secessions dataset compiled by authors; research is ongoing/incomplete based on abstract.

papers.cost_note: Regulatory/legal intervention; costs depend on state enforcement and litigation.

papers.title: The Measurement Gap in the Automation of EU Law: Benchmarking Doctrinal Legal Reasoning under the EU AI Act
papers.authors: Michèle Finck
papers.abstract: Large language models now produce legal text of at least median quality, yet no existing benchmark can evaluate whether they perform doctrinal legal reasoning, which forms the interpretive core of legal work, rather than the ancillary, paralegal tasks that most current legal-AI evaluations measure. This measurement gap is not only methodological but legal: the EU AI Act makes "appropriate accuracy" a binding requirement for high-risk AI used in the judicial domain, yet that requirement cannot acquire operational content without the very doctrinal-reasoning benchmark the field lacks.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a methodological paper on benchmarking LLM legal reasoning, not an intervention.
papers.summary: The paper identifies a measurement gap: large language models can produce median-quality legal text, but no benchmark exists to evaluate whether they perform true doctrinal legal reasoning (the core interpretive work) versus paralegal tasks. The EU AI Act requires 'appropriate accuracy' for high-risk AI in judicial domains, but operationalizing that requirement demands a benchmark that does not yet exist.
papers.method: Conceptual/methodological position paper; no empirical study, data analysis, or intervention testing reported in the abstract.
papers.scalability_note: This is a measurement problem, not a solution; it would require developing new benchmarks, not scaling an intervention.
papers.cost_note: Benchmark development is moderate cost, but abstract provides no cost estimate.
papers.nyc_note: No actionable city-level intervention; addresses EU regulatory compliance and academic methodology in legal AI, not NYC policy or service delivery.

papers.title: Observing Teachers' Instrumental Pedagogical Orchestration in Synchronous Online Learning: A Multimodal Grid Based on Videoconferencing Traces
papers.authors: Intissar Bamou, Christine Michel, Hassina El Kechai
papers.abstract: Synchronous online teaching environments pose specific challenges for the analysis of pedagogical activity as teaching takes place via videoconferencing platforms and interactions are multimodal. While pedagogical orchestration has been extensively studied in the context of face-to-face courses and at the level of instructional design, the analysis of teaching in videoconferencing environments remains under-explored and insufficiently instrumented in terms of methodology. This article proposes a multimodal observation grid designed to analyze the instrumentalised pedagogical orchestration of teachers during synchronous online classes. Based on theories of pedagogical orchestration, multimodality and professional gestures of teachers, this grid identifies a set of observable indicators related to communicational gestures, posture, gaze and the management of digital tools. These indicators are structured and ranked in order of priority according to their observability and analytical relevance. They are operationalised to consider the constraints associated with data that can be analysed in videoconference class contexts. The proposed grid aims to provide a reproducible methodological framework for the analysis of instrumental pedagogical orchestration, with a view to future empirical validation.
papers.idea: None identifiable; this is a methodological framework paper with no proposed intervention.
papers.summary: The paper proposes an observation grid for analyzing how teachers orchestrate pedagogical activities during synchronous online videoconferencing classes. It is a methods paper that structures observable indicators (gestures, posture, gaze, tool use) to support reproducible analysis of online teaching practice.
papers.method: Non-empirical methodological framework development based on theories of pedagogical orchestration, multimodality, and professional teacher gestures; no data collection or empirical validation reported.
papers.scalability_note: Framework development only; unclear whether the grid improves outcomes or can be operationalized at scale without significant training and validation work.
papers.cost_note: Unclear; framework is proposed but not yet tested or deployed; implementation cost unknown.
papers.nyc_note: Would require downstream validation, training of educators, and demonstration of impact on student outcomes before any city adoption would be justified.

papers.title: How Should We Measure Empirical Risk when Synthesizing Population Data?
papers.authors: Joshua Snoke
papers.abstract: Synthetic data has become a prominent solution for preserving privacy while sharing data, but current empirical risk assessment frameworks fundamentally assume a sample-based context that fails to translate for the evaluation of synthetic population level datasets. This commentary explores the implications when synthesizing entire populations in order to do population-level data science, arguing that traditional metrics, such as Membership Inference Attacks (MIA) and Attribute Inference Attacks (AIA), require re-examination. First, MIA may be rendered irrelevant in contexts where population membership is public knowledge or not considered sensitive information. Second, the risk of singling out is heightened because the confidential data contain full population information. Additionally, the absence of an "out-of-sample" comparison group for attribute inference means we need to define other policies when defining acceptable inferences. Finally, we cannot rely on simply returning to subsampling prior to generating synthetic data if the use case is truly to enable population-level data science. This commentary highlights the necessity for considering context when generating and evaluating synthetic population data.
papers.idea: N/A – this is a methodological paper on privacy-preserving synthetic data evaluation, not an intervention.
papers.summary: This is a commentary arguing that standard privacy-risk metrics (Membership Inference Attacks and Attribute Inference Attacks) designed for sample-based datasets are inadequate when evaluating synthetic population-level data. The author contends that context matters: population membership may be public, the risk of singling out is higher, and traditional out-of-sample comparisons become impossible.
papers.method: Theoretical commentary exploring conceptual gaps in existing empirical risk assessment frameworks; no empirical study, data collection, or intervention testing.
papers.scalability_note: Methodological guidance could influence data-sharing practices, but provides no concrete intervention to scale.
papers.cost_note: Not applicable; no program or intervention proposed.
papers.nyc_note: While NYC agencies do share synthetic data for research, this paper offers only methodological critique, not a deployable policy or practice.

papers.abstract: We are excited to introduce the 13 research teams selected for the Student Upward Mobility Initiative’s (SUMI’s) 2026 cohort. We awarded $4 million in grants to expand insights on high school–based skills that drive economic mobility, how those skills develop from middle to high school, and opportunities to better measure the skills students need to thrive in life. The grantees were selected from a highly competitive pool of more than 215 letters of inquiry. Grantees represent a diverse group of scholars at all career stages. Teams include economists, developmental psychologists, cognitive scientists, and practitioners, reflecting the importance and cross-disciplinary nature of the education-to-economic-mobility research space. This cohort will build on SUMI’s first cohort of grantees (2024–26), who are currently delivering results on which PK–12 skills drive future economic mobility among students and how to better measure these skills. The new cohort will also expand the number of states captured in

The cohort will build on prior work identifying skills linked to future earnings and expand to additional states.

papers.title: From Trade War to Green Transition: Optimal Electric Vehicle Tariffs with Revenue-Funded Subsidies
papers.authors: Panle Jia Barwick, Jack Collison, Pinelopi K. Goldberg, Shanjun Li, Yucheng Wang
papers.abstract: We study the optimal design of trade and industrial policy when governments pursue environmental objectives alongside traditional welfare goals. Motivated by the global transition to electric vehicles (EVs) and growing concerns about competitiveness, energy security, and climate change, we develop a framework in which policymakers choose tariffs and domestic production subsidies to maximize welfare, defined as the sum of consumer surplus, domestic profits, environmental benefits, and tariff revenue. We combine a theoretical model of differentiated-product oligopoly with a structural demand model estimated using vehicle-level data from 13 countries that together account for the vast majority of global EV sales. Our central finding is that the optimal policy combines a moderate tariff on imported EVs with a subsidy to domestic EV production financed through tariff revenue. This policy substantially outperforms both outright protectionism and laissez-faire. Relative to current policies, it preserves consumer access to affordable EVs, accelerates fleet electrification, supports domestic producers, and remains budget-neutral. For the United States, the optimal policy more than doubles EV market share, generates over $45 billion in annual welfare gains, and avoids approximately 95 million tons of lifetime CO2 emissions. A key mechanism underlying these results is the pass-through of tariffs and subsidies to prices, which depends critically on demand curvature, product substitution, and market structure. More broadly, our results suggest that effective industrial policy requires careful attention to market structure and country-specific conditions, balancing consumer, producer, fiscal, and environmental objectives rather than adhering to ideological prescriptions.
papers.idea: Cities could adopt tariff-subsidy policies on imported versus domestically-produced electric vehicles to accelerate EV adoption and reduce emissions.
papers.summary: The study develops a theoretical and empirical framework to design optimal tariffs and subsidies for EV markets across 13 countries. The authors find that a moderate tariff on imported EVs paired with domestic EV production subsidies (revenue-neutral) outperforms both protectionism and laissez-faire, potentially doubling US EV market share and generating $45B in annual welfare gains.
papers.method: Theoretical oligopoly model combined with structural demand estimation using vehicle-level sales data from 13 countries representing most global EV sales.
papers.scalability_note: Tariff and trade policy are federal/national-level instruments, not municipal or city-level levers.
papers.cost_note: Study claims budget-neutral design, but tariff design and subsidy administration carry implementation costs; no per-unit cost provided.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has no authority over tariffs or international trade policy; these are federal/national instruments. EV incentives exist but at state/federal level.

papers.title: Dormitories or grants? Need-based aid and university students’ mobility in Italy
papers.authors: Cristian Usala, Rinaldo Brau
papers.idea: Not actionable from abstract—no concrete intervention identified.
papers.summary: Unable to assess. No abstract provided; title suggests a comparison of dormitory vs. grant aid for Italian university students' geographic mobility, but without findings or mechanism, actionability cannot be determined.

papers.title: Competing firms, competing regulators: The strategic cost of fragmented climate policy
papers.authors: Nicole Adler, Gianmarco Andreana, Gerben de Jong
papers.abstract: Climate policy in global network industries is implemented across fragmented jurisdictions, yet firms respond through integrated operational networks. We develop a two-stage game-theoretic framework to analyze how firm-level responses interact with alternative governance structures. Regulators first choose emissions charges. Firms subsequently compete through pricing, service capacity and capital deployment decisions. The analytical results demonstrate that uniform global regulation maximizes welfare in symmetric markets. However, in sufficiently asymmetric markets, a uniform global charge is dominated by decentralized regimes. Multiple regulatory instruments better accommodate region-specific market externalities. We apply this framework to a calibrated case study of North American, Western European and transatlantic aviation markets. The numerical results establish that a globally coordinated regulator setting region-specific charges achieves the highest aggregate welfare. These aggregate gains nonetheless mask substantial distributional disparities across jurisdictions. Effective climate governance in network industries therefore requires more than determining an efficient emissions charge. Policy instruments ought to accommodate regional heterogeneity and transfer mechanisms will be necessary to ensure efficient, politically stable cooperation.
papers.idea: Cities could adopt region-specific emissions charges for aviation rather than uniform global standards, paired with inter-jurisdictional transfer mechanisms.
papers.summary: This game-theoretic study models how airline firms respond to different climate policy structures across fragmented regulatory jurisdictions. The authors find that while uniform global emissions charges maximize welfare in symmetric markets, decentralized or region-specific regimes perform better in asymmetric markets; a globally coordinated regulator setting region-specific charges achieves highest aggregate welfare but creates distributional disparities requiring transfer mechanisms.
papers.method: Two-stage game-theoretic framework (regulators set emissions charges; firms then compete on pricing, capacity, and capital) calibrated to North American, Western European, and transatlantic aviation markets.
papers.scalability_note: Requires international coordination on aviation emissions; NYC alone cannot implement.
papers.cost_note: Implementation costs depend on transfer mechanism design; abstract provides no cost data.
papers.nyc_note: Aviation policy is federal/international; NYC has minimal regulatory authority over airline emissions charges or inter-jurisdictional transfers.

papers.title: Detecting Bot Detection: Prevalence, Techniques, and Implications for Web Measurement Research
papers.authors: Ralf Gundelach, Michael Mühlhauser, Dominik Herrmann
papers.abstract: Browser automation frameworks are essential tools for security and privacy research on the web, yet bot detection scripts increasingly probe their artifacts, threatening measurement validity as automated browsers may be blocked or served different content. Prior work measures detection deployment, while we measure blocking-induced sample loss. Through a literature survey of top-tier security, privacy, and web measurement venues, we find that 83% of papers omit any discussion of bot detection blocking. To address this gap, we conduct a measurement study of 10,000 websites across four browser configurations (40K page visits in total) to quantify detection prevalence and employed techniques. Using custom instrumentation to detect when sites probe for automation, we develop a taxonomy of bot detection techniques and measure how often they appear in practice. Chromium headless encounters a 15% soft block rate compared to 7% for other configurations. Across all conditions, 82% of blocks are attributable to bot detection (59% vendor-confirmed, 23% inferred from condition-dependent blocking), predominantly by providers with integrated bot detection such as Cloudflare (37% block rate) and Akamai (26%). A header spoofing experiment establishes that 75% of Chromium-headless-only blocks are caused by header-level signals alone, yet JavaScript-based environment probing is more extensive than current blocking rates suggest. These findings demonstrate that bot detection creates systematic, provider-correlated sample loss that the web measurement community neither measures nor reports. The downstream effect on specific measurement outcomes remains future work.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a methods paper on web measurement research, not a policy or program.
papers.summary: The study measures how often websites deploy bot-detection scripts that block automated browsers, finding 82% of blocks are bot-detection-related, with Cloudflare and Akamai responsible for 37% and 26% of blocks respectively. The authors document that most web measurement papers (83%) do not report whether their automated measurements were affected by such blocking, creating potential validity issues.
papers.method: Measurement study visiting 10,000 websites with four browser configurations (40,000 total page visits) to quantify bot-detection prevalence and techniques, supplemented by a literature survey of security/privacy venues.
papers.scalability_note: Findings are technical/methodological; no intervention to scale.
papers.cost_note: Not applicable; no program or intervention proposed.
papers.nyc_note: No city-level policy, service, enforcement, or programmatic lever identified.

papers.title: Nearly Half of Working-Age Adults Had Difficulties Affording Health Care in 2025
papers.authors: Michael Karpman, Lisa Dubay, Jennifer M. Haley, Genevieve M. Kenney, Stephen Zuckerman
papers.abstract: In this brief, we examine difficulties affording health care among families of working-age adults (i.e., those ages 18 to 64) using December 2025 data from the Urban Institute’s Well-Being and Basic Needs Survey (WBNS). We find that difficulties affording care were widespread, particularly affecting adults with fair or poor health, chronic health conditions, or disabilities. Why This Matters US households face growing challenges in affording health care as costs have outpaced earnings growth. Millions of Americans remain without health insurance, and even with insurance, many struggle to pay for care. Faced with high costs, some people jeopardize their health by delaying or skipping needed care and medications, while others incur medical debt. The expiration of enhanced Marketplace premium tax credits in January 2026 and forthcoming changes to Medicaid and the Marketplaces are expected to increase the number of uninsured and raise cost burdens for families. Families may also experience growing
papers.idea: No concrete intervention identified; this is a measurement/forecasting brief on health affordability challenges.
papers.summary: Nearly half of working-age adults (ages 18–64) reported difficulties affording health care in December 2025, with disparities among those with fair/poor health, chronic conditions, or disabilities. The brief documents prevalence and warns that expiration of enhanced Marketplace tax credits and forthcoming Medicaid/Marketplace changes will likely worsen uninsurance and cost burdens.
papers.method: Cross-sectional survey data from the Urban Institute's Well-Being and Basic Needs Survey (WBNS) fielded in December 2025; sample size not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Measurement/forecasting only; no intervention mechanism proposed.
papers.cost_note: No cost data or intervention costed; forecasting piece only.
papers.nyc_note: Documents a problem (health affordability) salient to NYC but offers no actionable policy lever for city to pull; mostly federal insurance policy.

papers.authors: Ketevani Kapanadze
papers.idea: Reunify administratively divided cities to improve coordination and service delivery.

papers.title: How We’re Conducting Our Research
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: The Reproductive Health Experiences and Access (RHEA) study, which runs through 2027, includes a survey of more than 50,000 people, reflecting a national sample and 13 states (Arizona, California, Florida, Georgia, Iowa, Illinois, Maryland, Mississippi, North Carolina, New Jersey, Ohio, South Carolina, and Wisconsin). It also includes follow-up interviews with a small group of survey respondents, as well as interviews with policymakers, advocates, and providers and focus groups with people affected by recent changes in reproductive health and abortion access in each study state. The RHEA study is led by the Urban Institute and REAL in partnership with SisterSong. NORC at the University of Chicago fields the survey and disseminates the data to the RHEA partners for analysis. A community advisory board supports the project to ensure communities’ opinions, insights, and experiences are centered in all aspects of the work. To learn more about the RHEA study and our research
papers.idea: N/A — this is a research methodology paper describing a longitudinal survey on reproductive health access, not an intervention or policy recommendation.
papers.summary: The RHEA study is a multi-year survey of 50,000+ people across 13 states tracking reproductive health experiences and abortion access following recent policy changes. It combines survey data, follow-up interviews, stakeholder interviews, and focus groups with community advisory board input.
papers.method: Descriptive prospective cohort survey design with 50,000+ respondents across 13 states, fielded by NORC, running through 2027, supplemented by qualitative interviews and focus groups.
