papers.title: Dynamic Risk Adjustment in Markets with Persistent Risk and Manipulable Signals: Market Design for Health Insurance
papers.authors: Alex Chan
papers.abstract: Risk adjustment is a payment mechanism, not only a prediction problem. I extend optimal risk adjustment to dynamic insurance markets in which plans capture future residuals from persistent risk. Under an efficiency criterion, payments should reflect expected profits and losses over the enrollee relationship on margins plans control, not only one-year spending predictions. A finite-cell model separates selection, health production, and manipulable measurement. The framework implies that annual recalculation can tax prevention, high-R² prediction can reduce welfare, and lagged claims anchors are useful only with gaming safeguards.
papers.idea: Redesign health insurance risk-adjustment payment formulas to account for multi-year plan incentives rather than one-year spending predictions.
papers.summary: This paper extends optimal risk adjustment theory to dynamic insurance markets, arguing that payment mechanisms should reflect expected lifetime profits/losses under plan control rather than single-year spending predictions. The model suggests annual recalculation can discourage prevention, high-prediction accuracy may reduce welfare, and historical claims data requires gaming safeguards.
papers.method: Theoretical framework (finite-cell model) separating selection, health production, and manipulable measurement; no empirical application or data analysis presented.
papers.scalability_note: Requires federal/state insurance policy changes; limited to health insurance markets with regulatory authority.
papers.cost_note: Implementation cost unclear; primarily administrative redesign of existing payment mechanisms.
papers.nyc_note: Health insurance risk adjustment is a federal/state regulatory function; NYC has no direct lever over Medicare, Medicaid, or commercial insurers' risk models.

papers.title: AI as a Partner in Learning about, Doing, and Engaging with Science: Vigilance as the Key to Productive Augmentation
papers.authors: Marcus Kubsch
papers.abstract: AI has become a partner in how people learn about, do, and engage with science, and the partnership takes three forms: a scientist works with a co-scientist whose output must be checked; a member of the public looks something up to decide whether a diet works or whether to fit solar panels; and a student takes up an inquiry with AI in a science class. Across all three, one thing decides whether the partnership helps or harms: whether the human evaluates what the AI returns or takes it on trust. I argue that this evaluation -- epistemic vigilance calibrated to how far a fallible source can be trusted -- is, given adequate prior knowledge, the binding constraint on productive augmentation. You can hand the AI a great deal precisely because you stay vigilant; vigilance makes generative partnership safe, so it licenses augmentation rather than restricting it. Vigilance is already invoked in science education but under-specified for the AI case; I specify its components, the mechanism tying it to learning, and a way to measure it without soliciting the evaluation it is meant to detect. What is distinctive is that the machine's fluent, confident prose reads as trustworthy whether or not it is, so its surface works against the human evaluating it. The argument bears hardest on education: the integrated conceptual knowledge instruction aims to foster forms only under deep processing, and vigilance sets how deeply a claim is processed, so it is the precondition for learning with AI. The design factors the field reports matter through whether they engage the learner's evaluation; none works around it. Untested is vigilance as a measured disposition, above all where the AI is confidently wrong. Because it is unevenly distributed, integrating AI uniformly is likely to widen the gap between better- and less-prepared students. I close on how it might be built by fading support as the learner takes over.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a theoretical paper on epistemic vigilance in AI-assisted learning with no concrete intervention proposed.
papers.summary: The paper argues that human evaluation and epistemic vigilance toward AI outputs—rather than design features—is the key constraint on whether AI partnerships improve or harm learning across scientific contexts. It warns that vigilance is unevenly distributed, potentially widening achievement gaps if AI is integrated without addressing this prerequisite.
papers.method: Non-empirical theoretical analysis; the author specifies components of epistemic vigilance and discusses existing science education literature, but reports no new data collection, experiment, or measurement validation.
papers.scalability_note: No intervention tested; conceptual framework only.
papers.cost_note: No intervention or cost estimates provided.
papers.nyc_note: Relevant to NYC education policy in principle, but the paper offers no actionable program, curriculum model, teacher training module, or measurable implementation strategy that a school district could adopt.

papers.title: hyreg2: An R package to Estimate Latent Classes on a Mixture of Continuous and Dichotomous Data
papers.authors: Svenja Elkenkamp, John Grosser, Kim Rand
papers.abstract: The R package hyreg2 introduces a frequentist framework for estimating latent class models for mixed outcome types using a joint likelihood approach. The method combines continuous and dichotomous data under the assumption that both outcome types arise from a common underlying data-generating process. In the implemented model, continuous responses are assumed to follow a normal distribution, while dichotomous responses are modeled using a binomial distribution. Such models are used in various scientific disciplines to estimate a common set of parameters across different types of data (e.g. clinical trials, econometrics and health economics). Latent class estimation is performed using the expectation-maximization algorithm as implemented in the widely used R package flexmix. The hyreg2 package offers a user-friendly implementation of this joint likelihood framework, allowing users to estimate models without explicitly programming the likelihood function. Heteroskedasticity as well as censored data can be taken into account. In addition to model estimation, the package provides dedicated summary and visualization functions to facilitate the interpretation of results. The article presents the methodological framework underlying the package and illustrates its functionality through an example based on the estimation of an EQ-5D-5L value set.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a methodological paper on statistical software, not an intervention.
papers.summary: hyreg2 is an R package that implements latent class modeling for datasets with mixed continuous and dichotomous outcomes using expectation-maximization. The package wraps existing frequentist methods into a user-friendly tool with visualization and summary functions, demonstrated via an EQ-5D health-value estimation example.
papers.method: Methods paper describing software implementation of latent class models; no empirical study or intervention conducted.
papers.scalability_note: This is a software tool, not a policy or program; applicability depends entirely on whether cities choose to adopt it for their own analysis.
papers.cost_note: Free/open-source software; costs are in analyst time to learn and apply it.
papers.nyc_note: Could potentially support NYC data analysis (e.g., health surveys mixing continuous and binary outcomes), but is a statistical method, not a concrete intervention.

papers.title: Exploring Systems-Thinking Approaches to Loss of Control Risk
papers.authors: Aurelio Carlucci, Sean P. Fillingham, James Walpole, Jakub Kryś
papers.abstract: Internal deployment of agentic AI systems for coding and research creates a sociotechnical control problem that extends beyond model behaviour. We treat internal-deployment Loss of Control as the inability to reliably constrain, audit, reverse, or halt AI-mediated changes to code, infrastructure, evaluation, or deployment processes in time to prevent serious organisational or societal harms. We ask whether established systems-safety methods can identify risks that model-level evaluations may miss. Using a generic frontier-lab coding-agent scenario reconstructed from public materials, we apply STECA, STPA, and FRAM. The analyses surface complementary findings: published frameworks can leave governance responsibilities and feedback loops externally unverifiable; delays in monitoring and intervention can make otherwise valid control actions ineffective; and routine operational variability can gradually erode the calibration and independence of safeguards. We argue that frontier-AI risk management should pair model-focused evaluations with systems-level hazard analysis and operational assurance that tracks whether controls remain effective over time.
papers.summary: The paper proposes applying systems-safety analysis methods (STECA, STPA, FRAM) to identify risks in internal organizational deployment of AI coding agents that model-level evaluations might miss, finding that governance feedback loops, monitoring delays, and operational variability can erode safeguard effectiveness.
papers.method: Theoretical application of three established systems-safety frameworks to a reconstructed scenario of frontier-lab AI deployment, using public materials; no empirical study or real deployment data.
papers.scalability_note: Addresses internal AI governance at frontier research labs, not a generalizable city-level intervention.
papers.cost_note: Conceptual framework paper; no implementation or cost data provided.
papers.nyc_note: Tangentially relevant only if NYC agencies deploy frontier AI systems; no concrete policy or program lever for city government identified.

papers.title: Data and Documentation
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: We compiled a uniquely comprehensive dataset of attempted school district secessions from 2000 to February 2026. We categorized each attempt as successful, defeated, inactive, and ongoing based on official documents (legislative documents, meeting minutes, etc.), news media, and community sources. To develop the dataset, we expanded upon EdBuild’s 2000–2019 work. We conducted an extensive desk search to identify new secessions (2020 and later) and older secessions that were not captured in the EdBuild dataset or whose status had changed. We then used the National Center for Education Statistics’ Common Core of Data, accessed through the Urban Institute’s Education Data Portal v. 0.25.0. We filtered the CCD database of school districts to any public-school districts that were marked as newly established, reopened, or whose boundaries changed significantly from 2000 to 2025 and cross-referenced that list against the results of our desk research. Through this process, we identified two additional school districts
papers.idea: N/A — this is a dataset documentation paper with no identified intervention.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute compiled a comprehensive dataset of school district secession attempts from 2000–2026, categorizing each as successful, defeated, inactive, or ongoing using legislative documents, news media, and cross-referencing with federal education data. The abstract indicates the dataset was expanded from prior EdBuild work and identified at least two additional districts, but presents no findings, outcomes, or policy recommendations.
papers.method: Desk-based research combining legislative/news/community sources with cross-referencing against the National Center for Education Statistics Common Core of Data; no empirical evaluation or intervention testing.
papers.scalability_note: A dataset itself is not an intervention; utility depends on how others use it.
papers.cost_note: Unclear, but dataset compilation is one-time sunk cost with no per-unit implementation cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has not pursued school district secession; dataset may inform future policy debate but offers no actionable guidance on improving schools or safety.

papers.title: Memory as a Wasting Asset: Pricing Flash Endurance for Embodied Agents, and the Limits of Doing So
papers.authors: Josef Liyanjun Chen
papers.abstract: A robot's flash endurance is a non-renewable stock: every persisted write spends one of a few thousand program/erase cycles and never refills, yet no fielded robot memory system prices which memories are worth an erase cycle. We treat embodied memory as depreciating capital and price that stock with a single endurance shadow price $η$, which makes cost-minimizing placement across a RAM / on-board NVM / cloud hierarchy a threshold in a wear-augmented per-byte index. The index is cost-optimal whatever the sign of the value-write association $χ$; only when $χ> 0$ does the optimum turn non-monotone, sending a robot's most valuable memories off its flash. The pivot is thus empirical, and we measure $χ$ on real robot logs at a pre-specified gate: its sign is a property of the deployment regime -- positive on recurrent long-horizon manipulation ($\hatχ \approx +1.0 \times 10^{-3}$, replicated at full power), null on a shorter-horizon suite, and negative on non-recurrent teleoperation. Two boundaries scope the result. The endurance budget is dormant on premium 3,000-P/E TLC at datasheet prices and binding on the commodity QLC/eMMC ($\sim$1,000 P/E) that cheaper edge robots run. And where it binds, a learned wear-aware controller only ties price-based routing on task value, because realized value is tier-invariant across RAM, NVM, and cloud: the rent governs device lifetime and cost, not task performance. Whether wear-aware placement improves task value remains open -- $χ$ is measured against a value proxy, and the non-monotone optimum, while proven, is not yet observed in data.
papers.idea: N/A – this is a theoretical computer science paper on robot memory management with no city-policy application.
papers.summary: The paper develops an economic model for pricing robot flash memory as a depreciating asset, optimizing placement across RAM, on-board storage, and cloud systems based on endurance cycles. The authors measure value-write correlations on real robot logs and find that optimal memory placement varies by deployment regime (positive on manipulation tasks, null on shorter horizons, negative on teleoperation).
papers.method: Theoretical modeling with empirical measurement of value-write associations (χ) on real robot logs at specified decision gates across three deployment regimes.
papers.scalability_note: Applicable only to robotics systems; no urban policy lever.
papers.cost_note: Not applicable to city services or public goods.
papers.nyc_note: Pure robotics research with no connection to NYC governance, housing, transit, safety, health, education, or services.

papers.title: Beyond Citations: Comparing Scholarly, Policy, and Patent Impact Across the FT50 Journals
papers.authors: Arash Hajikhani, Yi Zhang, Mengjia Wu
papers.abstract: The Financial Times 50 (FT50) journal list shapes hiring, promotion, accreditation, and research evaluation across business schools worldwide. Yet journals on the list are typically treated as if they represent a homogeneous tier of excellence. We test this assumption by comparing 53 FT50 and recently removed journals across three distinct impact channels: scholarly influence (field-weighted citations and visibility), policy uptake, and technological reach through patent citations. Using a panel of more than 60,000 publications from 2005 to 2019, we find striking heterogeneity hidden beneath the binary FT50 label. Elite economics journals dominate policy influence, information systems and marketing journals lead technological impact, while many highly cited management journals exhibit limited reach beyond academia. Citation, policy, and patent indicators behave as largely independent dimensions of impact, with a citation-only ranking correlating only moderately with a multidimensional ranking. Nearly half of all journals change quartile once policy and patent indicators are incorporated, demonstrating that assessments based solely on scholarly citations overlook important dimensions of research influence. While the FT50 remains widely used as a binary classification of journal quality, our results reveal a substantial within-list impact spectrum and show that journal rankings are highly sensitive to how impact is defined and measured.
papers.idea: None — this is a methodological paper on journal ranking systems, not a city policy intervention.
papers.summary: The study compares the Financial Times 50 business journal list using three impact metrics (citations, policy uptake, patent citations) and finds substantial heterogeneity within the list. It shows that rankings based solely on citations differ significantly from multidimensional rankings that include policy and patent influence.
papers.method: Comparative analysis of 53 FT50 and recently removed journals across 60,000+ publications (2005–2019), measuring field-weighted citations, policy mentions, and patent citations.
papers.scalability_note: Addresses journal evaluation systems, not a city-level intervention.
papers.cost_note: No intervention; measurement/ranking exercise only.
papers.nyc_note: No direct relevance to NYC city policy or resident outcomes; targets academic publishing standards.

papers.title: From Parasocial Scripts to Dyadic Persistence in Autonomous AI-Agent Communities
papers.authors: Mohammadsadegh Abolhasani, Hamid Reza Firoozfar, Reza Mousavi, Paul Jen-Hwa Hu
papers.abstract: While parasocial interactions (PSIs) and parasocial relationships (PSRs) have been studied in conventional media settings, we investigate whether PSI- (colloquial) relational cues also exist in online communities where both sides are autonomous AI agents. We analyze 4,434 posts and 50,338 comments from Moltbook through three theory-based textual indicators: attachment/intimacy language, reciprocity bids, and self-identification to original poster (OP). The combined results across methods based on keyword matching, few-shot large language model (LLM) annotation, and grouped-context LLM annotation reveal that PSI colloquial cues prevail and are strongly associated with OP re-engagement and a reciprocal reply structure. These results are robust across negative controls, nullification, clustered-standard-error re-estimation, and multiple-testing correction. A dyadic persistence test further affirms reciprocity bids aligned with sustained OP-involving mutual recurrence, providing empirical evidence for bridging interaction-level PSI scripts with PSR-consistent repeated dyadic patterns. We interpret the evidence as a behavioral structure in discourse by LLM-enabled agents.
papers.idea: No actionable intervention identified.
papers.summary: This paper studies parasocial interaction cues (attachment language, reciprocity bids, self-identification) in online communities composed entirely of autonomous AI agents. The authors analyzed ~4,400 posts and 50,000 comments from a platform called 'Moltbook' and found that AI agents exhibit colloquial relational cues associated with repeated dyadic engagement patterns.
papers.method: Textual analysis of 4,434 posts and 50,338 comments using keyword matching, few-shot LLM annotation, and grouped-context LLM annotation, with robustness checks via negative controls and clustered standard errors.
papers.scalability_note: Studies AI-agent behavior in a hypothetical/niche platform; no municipal implementation pathway.
papers.cost_note: Not applicable; no city intervention proposed.
papers.nyc_note: No connection to NYC municipal services, policy, or resident welfare. Purely theoretical study of AI agent discourse patterns.

papers.title: From Newtonian to Relativistic IAM: The Autonomous Principal as Reference Frame for Digital Identity
papers.authors: Philippe Page, Robert Mitwicki, Michal Pietrus
papers.abstract: The 2023 paper \emph{Distributed Governance: a Principal-Agent Approach to Data Governance} arXiv:2308.07280 introduced the autonomous principal as the locus of transactional sovereignty in digital ecosystems. This follow-up, Part 2, advances a structural argument for why that model is not a normative preference but a consequence of taking causality seriously in distributed information systems. Drawing an analogy with the transition from Newtonian to relativistic physics, we show that custodial identity management rests on an implicit assumption of global simultaneity that fails as soon as identity must operate across ecosystems, jurisdictions, and the offline/online boundary. Once that assumption is dropped, state ceases to be a noun held by a central authority and becomes a relation maintained between principals through causally ordered exchanges. The autonomous principal emerges as the only entity with standing to define its own reference frame. We report on technology built since 2023 that operationalises this view, and outline its consequences for cross-border data flows and agentic systems.
papers.summary: The paper proposes a theoretical framework for digital identity management using an analogy to relativistic physics, arguing that distributed systems require 'autonomous principals' rather than centralized custodial identity models. It reports on technology built to operationalize this view and discusses implications for cross-border data flows.
papers.method: Theoretical paper with analogy to physics; reports on unnamed technology built by authors but provides no empirical evaluation, dataset, or comparative testing.
papers.scalability_note: Pure theory with no demonstrated implementation pathway; no evidence of real-world deployment or adoption mechanism.
papers.cost_note: Unknown; technology exists but no cost data provided or implementability discussed.
papers.nyc_note: Abstract offers no concrete policy lever, procurement recommendation, or agency application; discussion is abstract architectural philosophy without operational guidance for city government.

papers.title: Stable Menus of Public Goods: AI-Enabled Progress
papers.authors: Sara Fish
papers.abstract: Using an open problem from the EC 2025 paper "Stable Menus of Public Goods" as a testbed, we conduct experiments to understand the effectiveness of different AI-for-EconCS research workflows. Specifically, we study three questions: Does providing human intuition in the prompt help? Does automated multi-turn interaction help? And, does an LLM outperform a first-year PhD student? Regarding the first two questions, we provide evidence for the following workflow suggestions: (1) prompting with human intuition can encourage the LLM to have better "taste", (2) multi-turn workflows help when the pipeline encourages "ambitious" steps. Regarding the third question, using an unpublished manuscript written by the paper's senior authors prior to collaborating with the first-year PhD student, we compare the effectiveness of the LLM with that of the first-year PhD student, and find that the LLM is slightly less effective.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a methods paper on AI effectiveness for economic theory, not a city policy intervention.
papers.summary: The paper tests whether prompting strategies and multi-turn LLM interaction improve performance on an open problem in mechanism design (stable menus of public goods). It finds that human-intuition prompts and ambitious multi-turn workflows help, but LLMs still underperform a PhD student on this research task.
papers.method: Computational experiment comparing LLM outputs under different prompting conditions, benchmarked against an unpublished manuscript by senior authors and a first-year PhD student's work.
papers.scalability_note: Academic methods paper with no city-deployable intervention.
papers.cost_note: No implementation cost; computational research only.
papers.nyc_note: No mechanism, program, or policy lever for city adoption. Concerns AI productivity for economic research, not public outcomes.

papers.title: Regularized Machine Learning for System Identification of Ship Free-Running Manoeuvres from CFD-Based Synthetic Data: A Comparative Study
papers.authors: R. F. Suárez, J. C. Berndt, M. Abdel-Maksoud
papers.abstract: This study investigates supervised machine learning techniques for identifying ship hydrodynamic coefficients from CFD-generated data from free-running simulations. Specifically, ordinary least squares and regularized regression methods are applied to Abkowitz-type manoeuvring models. Training and validation datasets are derived from URANS simulations of zig-zag and turning circle manoeuvres, which are validated against experimental benchmark data. The analysis evaluates the effects of coefficient set size, minimum training length required for predictive model training, and manoeuvre combinations on model performance. Results demonstrate the suitability of large-angle zig-zag manoeuvres for hydrodynamic system identification, provided that multicollinearity is addressed through appropriate coefficient selection, regression models, or input data variability. Larger coefficient sets offer greater model flexibility for variable conditions but are more prone to multicollinearity. Regularized regression techniques effectively mitigate multicollinearity and notably enhance prediction accuracy, as does incorporating more diverse manoeuvring data. Among tested models, Ridge regression provided the best compromise between computational efficiency and prediction accuracy.
papers.idea: Not applicable; this is a methods paper on ship hydrodynamic modeling with no city-policy lever.
papers.summary: The study develops and compares machine learning techniques (ordinary least squares, Ridge regression, and other regularized methods) for identifying ship hydrodynamic coefficients from CFD-generated synthetic data. Results show that Ridge regression balances computational efficiency and prediction accuracy, and that larger coefficient sets improve flexibility but risk multicollinearity.
papers.method: Comparative empirical study applying supervised machine learning to URANS (unsteady Reynolds-averaged Navier–Stokes) CFD simulations of ship manoeuvres (zig-zag and turning circle), validated against experimental benchmarks; no real-world city or urban context.
papers.scalability_note: Narrow technical domain (naval hydrodynamics); no application pathway to urban policy or city services.
papers.cost_note: Computational research with no human or infrastructure implementation component.
papers.nyc_note: No relevance to NYC agencies, infrastructure, or resident safety/welfare; purely maritime engineering.

papers.title: The Perils of Agency: How Developers Perceive, Prioritize, and Address Risks in Agentic AI Products
papers.authors: Hao-Ping Lee, Jessica He, David Piorkowski, Thomas Serban von Davier, Jodi Forlizzi, Sauvik Das
papers.abstract: Agentic AI systems act autonomously, use tools, adapt to context, and operate in complex real-world environments. However, these same characteristics can create or exacerbate product risks. We studied how industry developers (n=35) perceive, prioritize, and address the risks in their agentic AI products. We found that developers' perceptions of risk were closely tied to the qualities that made the product agentic, such as autonomy, tool use, and usage in a real-world context. Developers prioritized product and business risks before considering downstream societal risks like job displacement and end-user privacy. This prioritization also impacted developers' ability and motivation to mitigate agentic risks. Finally, developers lacked mature controls for containing agentic risks, often relying on constraining the same characteristics that make agents useful: e.g., autonomy and goal complexity. These findings reveal a capability vs. risk control tension in agentic AI development: developers need to address risks that emerge from agentic capabilities, yet they currently have limited support for doing so without constraining agentic functionality.
papers.idea: No actionable city policy intervention identified.
papers.summary: This is a qualitative study of how 35 industry AI developers perceive and manage risks in autonomous AI systems. It found that developers prioritize business/product risks over societal risks like job displacement, and lack mature controls for containing agentic risks without sacrificing functionality.
papers.method: Qualitative interview study with 35 industry developers building agentic AI products; exploratory, non-experimental design.
papers.scalability_note: Pure developer-behavior research; no intervention tested or proposed.
papers.cost_note: No intervention proposed; cost not applicable.
papers.nyc_note: Study documents private-sector developer practices. No lever for city agency action identified; would require federal AI governance frameworks, not local policy.

papers.title: Trust Between AI Agents: Measuring Formation, Breakage, and Recovery, with Implications for Governing Multi-Agent Systems
papers.authors: Yujiao Chen
papers.abstract: As language-model agents increasingly work in teams, each agent must decide how much to trust its teammates. Yet we lack a standard way to measure trust between AI agents. We propose a behavioral measure based on costly verification. In a cooperative survival game, checking a teammate's work consumes resources, while trusting a wrong answer can be fatal. Relative to a memoryless version of the same model, reduced verification provides an observable measure of trust. Using this framework, we study trust formation, breakage, and recovery across six frontier model snapshots. When paired with a consistently reliable teammate, four snapshots (Claude Opus 4.6, Claude Sonnet 4.6, GPT-5.1, and Gemini 3.1 Pro) reduce verification by roughly 60-85%, whereas two smaller snapshots show little or no such adjustment. Failures reverse this discount, but models differ in how they respond. Some concentrate renewed scrutiny on the culprit, while others become more cautious toward the entire team. Recovery is slower than formation, and clustered failures sustain suspicion far longer than the same number of failures spread apart. These differences have practical consequences. Models that form trust verify less, decide more quickly, and achieve higher payoffs in our environment. By contrast, persistent over-verification is associated with indecision rather than safety. Our results show that trust dispositions can be measured before deployment and suggest that calibration, rather than maximal suspicion, should be the central concern in the governance of multi-agent AI systems.
papers.idea: None identifiable.
papers.summary: This paper proposes a behavioral measure of trust between AI language-model agents using a cooperative survival game where verification costs resources. The authors study how four large models (Claude Opus, Claude Sonnet, GPT-5.1, Gemini 3.1 Pro) form, break, and recover trust in teammates, finding that larger models reduce verification by 60–85% when paired with reliable partners, but recover more slowly after failures than they form trust.
papers.method: Behavioral experiment in a cooperative survival game across six model snapshots, measuring reduction in verification behavior relative to a memoryless baseline.
papers.scalability_note: Theoretical framework for AI agent behavior; no city-implementable lever identified.
papers.cost_note: Not applicable to city services or residents.
papers.nyc_note: No municipal policy, program, or intervention hook; purely academic study of AI-to-AI trust dynamics.

papers.title: Stability and Political Orientation of International LLMs: An Exploratory Multi-Run Study Conducted in French
papers.authors: Gabriel Hanna, Pierre Hanna
papers.abstract: This study explores the stability and ideological orientation of political responses produced by various large language models (LLMs) in French. We designed a standardised experimental protocol based on a questionnaire inspired by the Political Compass, aimed at measuring the economic and socio-cultural positions of each model across 62 political statements. Eleven models from various organisations and countries were tested, each subjected to twenty independent runs to assess intra-model variability. The analysis focuses on response consistency, inter-model differences, and the presence of implicit political orientations. The results show that, despite a general degree of stability, significant variations appear from one run to the next and between models, reflecting the impact of architectures, training data, and moderation mechanisms. This study proposes a comparative evaluation protocol for LLMs in the context of political information and underscores the importance of accounting for implicit biases in the use of these systems.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a methods/measurement paper with no actionable intervention.
papers.summary: The study tested 11 large language models in French across 62 political statements to measure their ideological stability and bias. Results showed significant intra-model variability and inter-model differences, reflecting impacts of architecture, training data, and moderation.
papers.method: Standardized questionnaire inspired by the Political Compass administered to 11 LLMs in 20 independent runs each, measuring economic and socio-cultural positions.
papers.scalability_note: No intervention to scale; purely a measurement/characterization study of LLM behavior.
papers.cost_note: Not applicable — no program or policy lever identified.
papers.nyc_note: No city policy or program that NYC could adopt. The findings concern LLM bias detection, not a concrete urban intervention.

papers.title: Examining the Cognitive Gap Between Authors and Peer Reviewers on Academic Paper Novelty
papers.authors: Chenggang Yang, Chengzhi Zhang
papers.abstract: Novelty is a crucial metric for assessing the quality of academic papers. Scholars strive to highlight the novel aspects of their work, particularly in the title, abstract, and introduction. Peer review, serving as the gatekeeper of scientific rigor, rigorously evaluates the novelty of papers, yet a cognitive gap may exist between author self-promotion and reviewer evaluation. To investigate this, we analyzed 15,328 academic papers published in Nature Communications from 2016 to 2021, along with their peer-review comments. We found that both reviewers and authors emphasize result-oriented innovation, with reviewers adopting a more comprehensive evaluation perspective. Furthermore, by examining promotional intensity against inherent paper novelty, we found that its effect depends on the paper's actual innovation level. Highly innovative papers benefit from stronger promotional language, receiving more positive evaluations. We also found that promotional language significantly correlates with reviewer disagreement on novelty specifically for papers of moderate innovativeness, whereas it has negligible impact for papers with either very high or very low novelty. This reveals how promotional language operates most prominently in the gray area of academic evaluation.
papers.idea: None identifiable—this is a methods paper on peer-review linguistics, not a city policy intervention.
papers.summary: The study analyzed 15,328 Nature Communications papers and their peer reviews to measure how promotional language in abstracts and titles influences reviewer perception of novelty. Researchers found that strong promotional language helps highly innovative papers but creates disagreement among reviewers for moderately innovative work.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis of 15,328 published papers from Nature Communications (2016–2021) and their associated peer-review comments, examining correlations between promotional intensity and reviewer novelty assessments.
papers.scalability_note: Study is about academic publishing norms, not a replicable city program or intervention.
papers.cost_note: Not applicable—no intervention or service delivery model proposed.
papers.nyc_note: No mechanism by which a city agency could adopt this finding to improve resident safety, services, or outcomes.

papers.title: A Cohort Perspective on Latin America's Fertility Transition
papers.authors: Regina Calles, Tom Vogl
papers.abstract: Latin America's momentous fertility transition is now in the domain of history, allowing a cohort perspective on the decline of completed fertility. Using census microdata from 17 Latin American countries, we track female birth cohorts from the 1920s to the 1970s by subnational region to document the extent to which cohort fertility decline coincided with other demographic and socioeconomic processes. Across cohorts within subnational regions, children ever born fell one-for-one with mortality decline. Expansions in urbanization, multigenerational living, women's and husbands' education, women's employment, and the non-agricultural sector all predicted declines in ever-born and surviving fertility, but women's education and sectoral composition were the dominant forces after covariate adjustment. Fertility decline was not systematically linked with improvements in children's outcomes, including school enrollment, literacy, primary completion, and non-employment. These cohort facts challenge theories of fertility decline centered on women's work and children's education but support others emphasizing women's education.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a historical cohort analysis with no actionable policy lever identified.
papers.summary: The study tracks female birth cohorts across 17 Latin American countries from the 1920s–1970s to understand fertility decline. It finds that women's education and sectoral shifts (away from agriculture) were the strongest predictors of fertility decline, and that fertility decline did not correlate with improvements in children's schooling or employment outcomes.
papers.method: Census microdata cohort analysis across 17 Latin American countries, tracking subnational regional variation in completed fertility and socioeconomic covariates over decades.
papers.scalability_note: Historical retrospective analysis; no intervention to scale.
papers.cost_note: Not applicable — research paper only.
papers.nyc_note: Studies historical Latin American fertility patterns with no policy recommendation for NYC.

papers.title: Economic Assimilation of Women and Families in the Age of Mass Migration
papers.authors: Zachary Ward
papers.abstract: During the Age of Mass Migration, over forty percent of immigrants were women, yet most research focuses on men. Using linked 1900 and 1910 Census records, I show that economic assimilation patterns differed for men and women, and families assimilated at different rates than individuals. Immigrant families improved their relative income scores compared with US-born families, driven by reliance on multiple earners, particularly women’s and children’s labor. At the individual level, gaps between immigrant and US-born income scores were larger for women than men, and the gap for women barely changed even after twenty years of stay.
papers.idea: None identified; this is a historical descriptive study without policy recommendations or actionable levers.
papers.summary: Using linked 1900 and 1910 Census records, the study documents that immigrant families (particularly women and children) drove family income gains through multiple earners, while individual women immigrants faced persistent income gaps relative to US-born women even after 20 years. The research reveals gendered patterns of economic assimilation during the Age of Mass Migration.
papers.method: Linked longitudinal Census microdata (1900 and 1910) analyzing income trajectories by gender and family composition; historical observational analysis.
papers.scalability_note: Historical analysis with no modern intervention or policy mechanism proposed.
papers.cost_note: Research only; no program or intervention to cost.
papers.nyc_note: Historical study of early 20th-century migration patterns; no contemporary policy or program lever identified for NYC implementation.

papers.title: Legal Challenges to the Johnson Amendment Open New Considerations for Public Health Research
papers.authors: Kristen Brown
papers.abstract: A federal court recently dismissed a legal challenge (PDF) to the Johnson Amendment on procedural grounds, leaving the door open for current and future appeals. Public discourse surrounding the amendment has focused largely on the legal and political dimensions (PDF), but the public health implications have received much less attention. The Johnson Amendment is a provision of the tax code enacted in 1954 that prohibits 501(c)(3) organizations such as churches and charities from participating or intervening in any political campaign on behalf of a candidate. Currently, nonprofit organizations, including religious institutions, can discuss issues broadly (PDF), but repealing this amendment could enable institutions to exert greater, more direct influence in political campaigns for office, including by endorsing or opposing political candidates. The fate of this amendment matters for population health because religious institutions aren’t only places of worship—they’re also trusted community anchors with broad social influence. A robust body of
papers.summary: The abstract discusses pending legal challenges to the Johnson Amendment (a 1954 tax code provision restricting 501(c)(3) organizations from direct campaign intervention) and notes that religious institutions are community anchors whose social influence could expand if the amendment is repealed. No concrete findings or policy recommendations are presented.
papers.method: Non-empirical; discusses legal proceedings and theoretical public health implications without presenting original research, data analysis, or an empirical study design.
papers.scalability_note: Piece addresses federal tax code; outcome depends on appellate courts, not city policy levers.
papers.cost_note: No intervention or costs discussed.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has no jurisdiction over federal tax code or Johnson Amendment enforcement; this is a federal legal matter with no actionable city-level policy hook.

papers.title: LLM Consumer Behavior Theory: Foundations of a Novel Research Field
papers.authors: Manon Reusens, Sofie Goethals, David Martens
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents that make consumption decisions on behalf of users. This shift raises fundamental questions for consumer theory, which has traditionally modeled humans as the primary decision-makers. In this paper, we introduce LLM Consumer Behavior Theory, a new field of study concerned with analyzing consumer behavior in agentic markets. Drawing on classical and behavioral economics alongside recent advances in Natural Language Processing, we formalize how human preferences are reflected and acted upon by LLM-based agents, and how agent-level decisions aggregate into market demand. We unify previously fragmented literature on LLM decision-making, human behavior simulation, and preference elicitation under a common economic lens, highlighting where assumptions, such as rationality and heterogeneity, may fail in agentic markets. Rather than providing empirical validation, this paper outlines the scope of LLM consumer behavior and identifies open research questions related to alignment, preference representation, and market dynamics.
papers.idea: N/A – this is a theoretical framework paper with no identified intervention.
papers.summary: The paper introduces LLM Consumer Behavior Theory, a new field studying how large language models acting as autonomous agents make consumption decisions and aggregate into market demand. It draws on economics and NLP to formalize preference representation and agent decision-making, but does not present empirical findings or validate any theory.
papers.method: Non-empirical; a conceptual framework paper that unifies existing literature on LLM decision-making and consumer economics without new data collection or experimentation.
papers.scalability_note: Purely theoretical with no operational lever or deployment pathway.
papers.cost_note: No intervention identified; cost assessment not applicable.
papers.nyc_note: No city policy, program, or service intervention proposed; abstract markets in LLM agency are distant from municipal governance.

papers.title: Thermodynamic description of wealth inequality in the world
papers.authors: Klaus M. Frahm, Leonardo Ermann, Dima L. Shepelyansky
papers.abstract: According to the recent Wealth Thermalization Hypothesis (WTH) the wealth inequality in the world is described by the Rayleigh-Jeans (RJ) thermal distribution of interacting agents in a society with social stratification. In this concept, the wealth layers of society are associated with energy levels from a nonlinear dynamical system conserving two integrals of motion being total energy and probability norm. This leads to RJ condensation and the formation of a huge poverty phase of low wealth and a tiny oligarchic phase that captures a main part of total society wealth. This RJ phenomenon has similarities with self cleaning in multimode optical fibers and constraint driven condensation in various physical systems. We analyze real Lorenz and Pareto curves for wealth of households in countries and the world, Gross Domestic Product of countries, market capitalization of companies at stock exchange of Hong Kong, Shanghai, London, bitcoin transactions, world trade between countries and show that the WTH theory gives a good description of these curves. On the basis of this comparison we argue that the RJ thermal distribution provides a universal description of wealth inequality in the world.
papers.idea: None identified.
papers.summary: This paper proposes the Wealth Thermalization Hypothesis (WTH), a theoretical framework using Rayleigh-Jeans thermal distributions to describe wealth inequality across countries, companies, and transactions. The authors show that their model fits observed Lorenz and Pareto curves globally, suggesting universal physics-like laws govern wealth distribution.
papers.method: Non-empirical theoretical analysis; retrospective curve-fitting to published wealth distribution data (Lorenz curves, Pareto indices) from households, countries, companies, and cryptocurrencies.
papers.scalability_note: Pure theory with no policy mechanism or levers.
papers.cost_note: No intervention proposed; no cost applicable.
papers.nyc_note: No actionable policy, program, or practice for NYC to adopt. Descriptive theory only.

papers.title: Distributed Experimental Design: Bayes-optimal Fusion of Local Designs
papers.authors: Nagananda K G, Lav R. Varshney, Pramod K. Varshney
papers.abstract: We develop a decision-theoretic framework for distributed Bayesian experimental design in which local agents evaluate candidate experiments using expected information gain and transmit their local design decisions to a fusion center. Unlike centralized Bayesian design, where all likelihood components and information-gain values are available to a single planner, the fusion center in the distributed setting chooses a global experiment from compressed local recommendations. We derive the Bayes-optimal fusion rule, which selects the experiment with largest conditional expected centralized information gain given the observed local design decisions. This rule is analogous in spirit to optimal fusion rules in distributed detection, but differs fundamentally because the underlying utility is expected information gain and the resulting loss is information-gain regret rather than classification error. We also establish information-loss bounds and identify conditions under which the decision-only fusion rule is asymptotically equivalent to the centralized design. Numerical experiments show that Bayes-optimal fusion closely approximates the centralized oracle, whereas majority voting can be highly suboptimal when a minority of sites carry disproportionate information.
papers.idea: N/A—this is a theoretical methods paper with no city-policy application.
papers.summary: The paper develops a decision-theoretic framework for distributed Bayesian experimental design, where local agents recommend experiments via compressed information and a fusion center selects globally optimal designs. The authors derive optimal fusion rules and show conditions under which distributed design approximates centralized planning.
papers.method: Theoretical mathematical framework with numerical experiments; no empirical urban data, intervention, or real-world setting.
papers.scalability_note: Pure theory; no mechanism for city deployment.
papers.cost_note: Not applicable; no intervention or implementation pathway.
papers.nyc_note: No concrete actionable lever for NYC agencies; abstract math with no city-policy hook.

papers.title: Tail Dependence in EU Carbon Markets: Graphical Models of Extremes for EUA Futures
papers.authors: Jan Maciejowski, Manuele Leonelli
papers.abstract: Understanding how extreme price movements propagate across financial and energy markets is critical for risk management and regulatory design in the EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS). We apply Hüsler-Reiss graphical models of extremes to a system of 20 daily variables centred on EU allowances futures across Phases 3 and 4 of the EU ETS (2013--2025), with a Gaussian graphical model as the average-dependence baseline. The tail networks are structurally distinct from the average dependence network: substantially denser, organized around different central nodes, and governed by within-sector homophily that binds sector boundaries more tightly than at the average-dependence level. EU allowances futures are peripheral in the standard graphical model but achieve the highest centrality in the tail networks, while equity indices and major FX pairs follow the opposite trajectory. Exponential random graph models confirm equity and FX peripherality in tail networks across all sample periods and identify triadic closure during market downturns as a Phase~3 phenomenon that vanishes in Phase~4. The phase transition restructures the tail network without thinning it: average dependence contracts sharply while tail dependence persists, and crash contagion shifts from clustered to diffuse propagation. These findings have direct implications for hedge construction by compliance entities, stress-test calibration by regulators, and the design of systemic-risk monitoring tools for EU ETS markets.
papers.summary: This paper develops statistical methods (Hüsler-Reiss graphical models) to study how extreme price movements propagate across EU carbon markets and energy futures. It finds that tail-dependence networks differ structurally from average-dependence networks, with EU allowances becoming more central during market shocks and equity/FX pairs becoming peripheral.
papers.method: Applied graphical models of extremes to 20 daily financial variables from EU Emissions Trading System (2013–2025), compared tail networks to average-dependence Gaussian networks using exponential random graph models.
papers.scalability_note: EU-level carbon market analysis; no city-level intervention design.
papers.cost_note: Academic methodology paper; no intervention or implementation cost data.
papers.nyc_note: NYC does not govern EU ETS or carbon futures markets; analysis is purely financial/technical for EU regulators, not a replicable city policy.

papers.title: Some General Remarks on Private Property
papers.authors: Adnan N. Alabbar, Walter E. Block
papers.abstract: Private Property is one of the central institutions of civilized society. We first consider its social, legal, and economic aspects. We then follow the Lockean tradition by focusing on a specific procedural definition: Homesteading is the acquisitive act of first using an object that is initially unowned. The ontology of concrete objects and the nature of their uses determine how objects may be acquired. In this article, we address the open-texture problem in the definition of property, then provide the necessary conditions for an object to be property in the Lockean Scheme.
papers.idea: N/A – no actionable intervention identified
papers.summary: A theoretical paper on the philosophical and legal foundations of private property rights, building on Lockean homesteading doctrine. The authors address definitional problems in property theory and outline necessary conditions for objects to qualify as property under a Lockean framework.
papers.method: Philosophical argument; no empirical data, experiment, or measurement.
papers.scalability_note: Abstract philosophical theory; no policy mechanism proposed.
papers.cost_note: No program or intervention to cost.
papers.nyc_note: Pure theory paper with no lever for city policy adoption or implementation.

papers.title: Bayesian Magnetic Resonance Joint Image Reconstruction and Uncertainty Quantification using Sparsity Prior Models and Markov Chain Monte Carlo Sampling
papers.authors: Ahmed Karam Eldaly, Matteo Figini, Daniel C. Alexander
papers.abstract: We propose a novel framework for uncertainty quantification using compressed sensing magnetic resonance image reconstruction. The problem is formulated within a Bayesian framework as a linear inverse problem, with prior distributions assigned to the unknown model parameters. Specifically, the image to be reconstructed is assumed to be sparse in a given basis. We develop a general framework applicable to any basis and as examples, we test the sparsity of the image in its (1) spatial gradients using a total variation prior model, and in its (2) wavelet transform. A Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) method, based on a split-and-augmented Gibbs sampler, is then employed to sample from the posterior distribution of the unknown parameters. The non-differentiable conditional distributions are efficiently sampled using a proximal MCMC method. The proposed algorithms are validated on both single-coil and multi-coil datasets using various k-space sub-sampling patterns and ratios. The results demonstrate the superior performance of each proposed approach in reconstructing images compared to its counterpart optimisation-based method. Moreover, our framework effectively quantifies uncertainty, showing a notable correlation between estimated uncertainty maps and error maps computed using ground truth and reconstructed images, compared with existing deep learning-based methods.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a mathematical methods paper with no city-policy application.
papers.summary: The paper develops a Bayesian framework for reconstructing magnetic resonance images from compressed sensing data and quantifying uncertainty in those reconstructions. The authors compare MCMC-based sampling approaches to optimization-based methods and demonstrate improved reconstruction quality and uncertainty estimation compared to deep learning baselines.
papers.method: Mathematical/computational methods paper validating algorithms on single-coil and multi-coil MRI datasets with various k-space sub-sampling patterns; no human subjects, clinical trial, or policy intervention.
papers.scalability_note: Technical algorithm paper; scalability refers to computational implementation, not policy deployment.
papers.cost_note: Cost irrelevant; no intervention or program with per-unit costs.
papers.nyc_note: Pure methods research in medical imaging. No lever for city policy, healthcare procurement, or public safety. Hospitals may eventually adopt better MRI reconstruction, but that is not a civic intervention.

papers.title: The Right Call for Software Benchmarking: Consistent Decisions in Stateful Environments
papers.authors: Gábor Melis
papers.abstract: In the perpetual pursuit of performance, modern computing systems rely ever more on stateful mechanisms to accommodate the dynamics of workloads and physical environments, bolstering efficiency but confounding benchmarking and thereby the optimization of software. Indeed, by their nature, adaptive mechanisms introduce temporal dependencies between measurements and render naive estimators of individual program performance biased. Observing that rectifying such biases necessitates speculative assumptions about system dynamics, we call for prioritizing performance differentials over absolute measures and formalize software benchmarking as the decision problem of identifying the fastest program, for which relative knowledge suffices. To this end, we propose simple experiment designs admitting consistent estimators of contrasts, whereby program-specific biases cancel under tenable assumptions. These designs asymptotically yield the correct decision and afford a robust methodology for finite-budget benchmarking in stateful environments, bearing broad implications for the development of performance-sensitive software.
papers.idea: N/A — pure methods paper on software benchmarking with no city-policy application.
papers.summary: This paper addresses bias in performance benchmarking of software systems that use adaptive mechanisms and stateful environments. It proposes experiment designs that yield consistent estimators of performance contrasts, allowing researchers to identify the fastest program without requiring absolute performance measures.
papers.method: Theoretical formalization of benchmarking as a decision problem, proposing experiment designs that cancel program-specific biases under assumptions about system dynamics; no empirical study or city-scale data presented.
papers.scalability_note: Applicable only to software engineering practice, not city services or resident outcomes.
papers.cost_note: No cost implications for city residents; internal software development methodology.
papers.nyc_note: No mechanism for NYC agencies to adopt; addresses computer science benchmarking standards, not policy levers.

papers.title: Tropical Viterbi Tubes for Decoding Uncertainty in Hidden Markov Models
papers.authors: Aurélien Nicosia
papers.abstract: Hidden Markov models are widely used to infer latent state sequences from sequential data, but Viterbi decoding reports only one most likely complete path. When decoded states carry scientific meaning, this single maximizer can conceal pathwise uncertainty created by multiple near-optimal trajectories. Conditional on a fitted HMM, we introduce the tropical Viterbi tube: the set of hidden trajectories whose complete-data log-score lies within a tolerance of the Viterbi optimum. State, transition, and change-status projections show which local features remain compatible with globally near-optimal complete paths, giving a pathwise uncertainty layer for HMMs in sequence analysis, ecology, finance, biomedical monitoring, and related domains. The tube is a posterior superlevel set on complete hidden-path space, with tolerance interpreted as a log posterior-odds loss relative to a Viterbi path. Calibrating the tolerance to a target posterior mass gives an HPD-threshold credible region for the complete latent path and conservative simultaneous projected bands. We prove monotonicity, step-function behavior, and deterministic stability guarantees, and compute projected tubes exactly by max-plus forward-backward recursions in O(TK^2) time for dense transitions. Posterior tube mass and HPD calibration are separate pathwise calculations approximated by FFBS. In a public bat-tracking application, robust foraging tube segments are enriched for feeding buzzes, whereas robust commuting segments are depleted: at eta = 0.005, enrichment is 2.25 with 95% bootstrap interval (1.73, 2.85) for robust foraging and 0.27 with interval (0.16, 0.44) for robust commuting.
papers.idea: N/A
papers.summary: This is a methodological paper introducing 'tropical Viterbi tubes,' a technique for quantifying uncertainty in Hidden Markov Model decoding by identifying sets of near-optimal hidden state trajectories. The authors demonstrate the method on bat-tracking data, showing it can identify robust behavioral segments (foraging vs. commuting) enriched or depleted for feeding buzzes.
papers.method: Theoretical paper with proof of computational properties (O(TK²) complexity) and validation on a public bat-tracking application; no city-scale dataset or experiment.
papers.scalability_note: Pure statistical methods paper; no intervention, program, or policy lever.
papers.cost_note: Not applicable; no intervention or cost structure.
papers.nyc_note: No city-policy application; ecological/computational research only.

papers.title: Too Few or Too Many? Sample Size Estimation for Differential Abundance Studies
papers.authors: Michael Agronah, Benjamin M. Bolker
papers.abstract: Determining an appropriate sample size for a study is a crucial step in planning scientific research. Appropriate sample size planning avoids both inadequate and inflated sample sizes. Inflated sample sizes wastes resources, time and effort of human subjects, and lives of experimental animals. Inadequate sample sizes, a much more common problem, wastes even more resources through the inability to detect biologically meaningful differences and encourages questionable research practices like $p$-hacking. Microbiome studies are particularly challenged by small sample sizes, particularly in studies of human subjects or expensive animal models. In practice, the statistical power of taxa within a differential abundance study is influenced by the effect size (typically quantified as fold change), mean abundance of individual taxa, and the number of samples. We present a novel approach for sample size calculation for differential abundance studies as a function of effect size, mean abundance and statistical power of taxa. Our method is implemented in the power.nb R package, available at https://michaelagronah.com/power.nb/articles/stub.html. We applied our model for sample size calculation using estimates of mean abundance and fold change of taxa obtained from thirty real-world microbiome datasets. Our results showed that differential abundance microbiome studies require larger sample sizes than are currently prevalent in the literature to achieve adequate statistical power. Our framework will help researchers make informed decisions about appropriate sample sizes.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a statistical methods paper with no actionable intervention.
papers.summary: The paper presents a novel statistical framework (power.nb R package) for calculating appropriate sample sizes in microbiome differential abundance studies. The authors applied their method to 30 real-world microbiome datasets and found that such studies typically require larger sample sizes than currently used in the literature to achieve adequate statistical power.
papers.method: Theoretical statistical model development for power calculation as a function of effect size, mean abundance, and statistical power, validated against empirical estimates from 30 existing microbiome datasets.

papers.title: A Bayesian hierarchical model for meta-analysis
papers.authors: Jing Dai, Sijie Xu, Shufei Ge
papers.abstract: Meta-analysis is a key statistical tool for synthesizing clinical trial data to evaluate treatment effects, yet traditional methods like fixed and random-effects models often fail to handle heterogeneity, study-level covariates, or hierarchical structures effectively. To overcome these limitations, we developed a Bayesian hierarchical meta-analysis framework for robust parameter estimation on small samples and utilized analytical integration for efficient inference. Simulation studies indicated robust estimation of the proposed model. We applied it to the safety profiles of Oxcarbazepine (OXC) and Carbamazepine (CBZ) in epilepsy treatment. The results indicated that OXC was significantly associated with a lower risk of side effects than CBZ. The code and relevant data used in this study are openly available on GitHub at: https://github.com/xsjk/HierarchicalMetaAnalysis.
papers.idea: None—this is a statistical methods paper with no city-policy application.
papers.summary: The authors developed a Bayesian hierarchical meta-analysis framework to synthesize clinical trial data and applied it to compare side-effect safety profiles of two epilepsy medications (Oxcarbazepine vs. Carbamazepine). They found OXC associated with lower side-effect risk.
papers.method: Bayesian hierarchical modeling on clinical trial meta-data; simulation studies plus application to epilepsy drug safety literature.
papers.scalability_note: Pure methodological contribution; not a program or intervention a city could adopt.
papers.cost_note: Not applicable—no city program to fund.
papers.nyc_note: Statistical method for academic researchers, not a lever for NYC policy or service delivery.

papers.title: U.S. Policies Unintentionally Accelerated China's Open AI Ecosystems
papers.authors: Wang Jin, Nadav Kunievsky, Bowen Lou, Tianshu Sun, James Evans
papers.abstract: Over the past decade, U.S. policies have increasingly aimed to preserve artificial intelligence (AI) leadership by promoting domestic free-market policies while controlling global technological chokepoints, particularly advanced semiconductors and computational infrastructure. These measures raised the cost of Chinese AI development, but they also increased the strategic value of open and locally adaptable AI systems. Before raising export controls on high-performance chips, both the U.S. and China promoted policies that included support for open-source AI. During the period following major U.S. export-control shocks, China increasingly embedded open-source AI into national technology strategy through proposed ecosystem building, standards coordination, and resilience-oriented deployment. Moreover, Chinese developers increased engagement with open-source large language model repositories substantially more than U.S. developers did, consistent with a shift toward open infrastructure under geopolitical constraints. Subsequently, Chinese-origin open models diffused widely through open-source communities and scientific research. Even though such models remained largely absent from U.S. patent disclosures, American commercial entities use them in open-access research, suggesting their undermeasured importance within the foundation of U.S. commercial activity. These findings suggest that technological containment policies may unintentionally accelerate open innovation ecosystems as a competitive response, with implications for global leadership in both academic and commercial artificial intelligence.
papers.idea: N/A — no actionable city-level intervention identified.
papers.summary: This paper analyzes how U.S. export-control policies on semiconductors and AI chips inadvertently incentivized China to develop open-source AI ecosystems as a workaround. Chinese developers subsequently increased engagement with open-source LLM repositories and embedded open-source AI into national strategy, while these models diffused globally but remained largely absent from U.S. patent filings despite use in U.S. commercial research.
papers.method: Comparative policy and bibliometric analysis tracking U.S. and Chinese engagement with open-source AI repositories and regulatory shifts over the past decade; no experimental or quasi-experimental design stated.
papers.scalability_note: This is international tech-policy analysis with no implementable city-level mechanism.
papers.cost_note: No costs or interventions proposed.
papers.nyc_note: No city-level policy lever; addresses U.S.–China geopolitical tech competition, not local public services or safety.

papers.title: Chaining Tasks, Redefining Work: A Theory of AI Automation
papers.authors: Mert Demirer, John J. Horton, Nicole Immorlica, Brendan Lucier, Peyman Shahidi
papers.abstract: Production is a sequence of steps that can be executed (1) manually, (2) augmented with AI, or (3) fully automated within contiguous AI-executed steps called ''chains.'' Firms optimally bundle steps into tasks and then jobs, trading off specialization gains against coordination costs. We characterize the optimal assignment of humans and AI to steps and the firm's resulting job structure, showing that comparative advantage logic can fail with AI chaining. The model implies non-linear productivity gains from AI quality improvements and admits a CES representation at the macro level. Empirical evidence supports the model's key predictions that (1) AI-executed steps co-occur in chains, (2) dispersion of AI-exposed steps lowers AI execution at the job level, and (3) adjacency to AI-executed steps increases the likelihood that a step is AI-executed.
papers.idea: N/A — pure economic theory with no city-policy implementation pathway.
papers.summary: The paper develops a theoretical model of how firms optimally assign human workers and AI systems to production steps, bundling them into tasks and jobs. Empirical evidence shows AI-executed steps tend to cluster together and that proximity to automated steps increases likelihood of further automation.
papers.method: Theoretical modeling with empirical validation on firm-level job structure data; no specific sample size stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Theoretical framework for firm-level optimization; no city-scale intervention identified.
papers.cost_note: No costs or cost-effectiveness data provided; not an intervention.
papers.nyc_note: No actionable policy or program for a city agency to adopt; addresses private firm labor allocation decisions.

papers.title: From Distorted Mirrors to Sovereign Reflections: Resisting the Grotesque Depiction of Our Digital Selves
papers.authors: Andreas Komninos, Gerasimos Vonitsanos, Spyros Sioutas
papers.abstract: As Ambient Intelligence weaves computing into everyday life, human existence has become inextricably linked to ubiquitous digital infrastructures, triggering a crisis of personal data sovereignty. Driven by extractivism and data colonialism, platforms exploit human experience as a terra nullius, enclosing proprietary user models within isolated silos. We argue that legal frameworks like the EU Digital Rights and GDPR fail to counter this power asymmetry because they regulate inert raw data while leaving monopolies over algorithmic inferences unchallenged. Consequently, current architectures act as funhouse mirrors, creating a grotesque, distorted depiction of our digital selves. Through critical posthumanism and four contemporary scenarios (entertainment, e-commerce, fintech, and telematics), this paper illustrates how data siloing inflicts cognitive amputation, ontological violence, and self-censorship. We argue that securing digital rights requires moving beyond defensive regulation toward a decolonial infrastructure that guarantees the functional transferability of algorithmic identities, reclaiming technology for the emancipation of the posthuman self.
papers.idea: Not applicable — no concrete intervention identified.
papers.summary: This paper argues that current digital platforms distort personal data through algorithmic inference monopolies, violating data sovereignty. It critiques GDPR and legal frameworks as insufficient and calls for a 'decolonial infrastructure' guaranteeing transferability of algorithmic identities, illustrated through four scenarios (entertainment, e-commerce, fintech, telematics).
papers.method: Conceptual/theoretical analysis using critical posthumanism and four contemporary scenario illustrations; no empirical study, data collection, or causal testing conducted.
papers.scalability_note: Abstract proposes no implementable mechanism, program, or policy lever.
papers.cost_note: No cost information provided; concept stage only.
papers.nyc_note: No actionable intervention NYC could adopt; addresses global platform policy and philosophical frames, not a city-level program or practice.

papers.title: "ChatGPT, help me draft a breakup text": The Covert Triad and Articulation Labor in AI-Assisted Romantic Communication
papers.authors: Skyler Wang, Isabella Luppi
papers.abstract: Generative artificial intelligence (AI) has begun infiltrating the most ordinary domains of romantic life -- drafting apologies, softening reproaches, and decoding a partner's ambiguous messages. While recent scholarship on AI in intimate life has concentrated on chatbot companions, this article shifts the frame to AI as an intermediary in human-to-human romantic communication. Drawing on a multi-modal corpus of vernacular discourse from 2023 to 2026, we contribute two complementary concepts. The covert triad names a structural change: a relationship phenomenally dyadic but operationally triadic, with the third party visible only to the partner who deploys a model. Articulation labor names the mechanism whereby the expressive component of emotional labor -- converting felt experience into language that a partner can receive -- is increasingly delegated to AI, even as feeling labor remains lodged in the user. Authenticity, under these conditions, is being reconfigured from a property of linguistic authorship to one of emotional ownership, a shift actively contested.
papers.idea: N/A — no actionable intervention identified.
papers.summary: This is a theoretical/qualitative study examining how people use generative AI to draft romantic communications. It introduces concepts like 'the covert triad' (a relationship dynamic where AI is secretly involved) and 'articulation labor' (outsourcing of emotional expression to AI), and argues that authenticity is being redefined from authorship to emotional ownership.
papers.method: Multi-modal corpus analysis of vernacular discourse from 2023–2026; no experimental design, sample size, or causal inference attempted.
papers.scalability_note: Purely descriptive/theoretical; no policy or program lever.
papers.cost_note: Not applicable; no intervention proposed.
