papers.title: On the Equivalence of Instantaneous and Mechanistic Reproduction Numbers
papers.authors: Jeremy Goldwasser, Ryan J. Tibshirani, Alyssa Bilinski
papers.abstract: The effective reproduction number ($R_t$) is widely used to track epidemic dynamics in real time. The standard estimation framework uses "instantaneous $R_t$," defined via the renewal equation, which relates new infections to past infections through a generation interval distribution. Compartmental models like SEIR yield a seemingly distinct quantity, "mechanistic $R_t$," based on the effective contact rate and duration of infectiousness. We prove these two definitions are equivalent under homogeneous mixing, the standard assumption in compartmental modeling. We also derive the generation interval distribution implied by SEIR dynamics. A practical consequence is that generation intervals, often treated as assumption-light inputs to renewal equation estimators, in fact encode specific compartmental structure.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a mathematical methods paper without actionable intervention.
papers.summary: The authors prove mathematical equivalence between two epidemiological definitions of reproduction number (instantaneous vs. mechanistic) and derive the generation interval distribution implied by SEIR compartmental models. This is a theoretical contribution clarifying relationships between epidemiological estimation frameworks.
papers.method: Mathematical proof of equivalence between renewal-equation and compartmental-model formulations of reproduction number; derivation of implied generation interval distributions.
papers.scalability_note: Pure mathematical theory; no intervention to scale.
papers.cost_note: No real-world implementation cost; academic research only.
papers.nyc_note: No policy or program lever; improves internal consistency of existing epidemiological estimation methods but does not propose new public health action.

papers.title: Estimating Sloppy Directions via KDE: The Case of Kirman's Ants
papers.authors: Karl Naumann-Woleske
papers.abstract: Models whose predictions depend on only a handful of well-constrained parameter combinations, termed sloppy models, are ubiquitous in nonlinear stochastic systems. The information-geometric approach to sloppiness advocates using the symmetrized Kullback--Leibler divergence and its associated Hessian, the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM), as the natural loss function. However, prior applications have relied on analytically known or parametrically fitted distributions. In practice, for general agent-based or stochastic models the distribution must be estimated from simulation data. I demonstrate, using Kirman's ant recruitment model as a worked example, that a standard kernel density estimate (KDE) converges to the analytical FIM eigenvectors and eigenvalues with simulation budgets accessible in practice. I derive the analytical Hessian in closed form, show numerical convergence of the KDE-based estimate as a function of simulation data, and demonstrate how the stiff direction enables efficient phase exploration across the model's unimodal and bimodal regimes.
papers.idea: None; this is a mathematical methods paper with no city-policy application.
papers.summary: This paper develops a kernel density estimation (KDE) technique to estimate Fisher Information Matrix eigenvectors in sloppy models, demonstrating convergence on Kirman's ant recruitment model. The method enables efficient exploration of parameter space across different behavioral regimes in stochastic agent-based models.
papers.method: Theoretical derivation of analytical Hessian and numerical convergence testing of KDE-based estimates using simulation data from Kirman's ant recruitment model.
papers.scalability_note: Pure mathematical methods paper; no intervention or policy lever.
papers.cost_note: Not applicable; no implementable program.
papers.nyc_note: No connection to NYC governance, services, or policy domains.

papers.title: 'AI Alignment' Encompasses Competing Technical Priorities
papers.authors: Tushita Jha, Rory Svarc, Mateusz Bagiński
papers.abstract: The ML literature contains many distinct concepts falling under the heading of 'AI alignment'. After noting three concepts of AI alignment in the context of their corresponding research programs, we claim that realistic interventions may promote 'AI alignment' under one conception while being actively counterproductive from the perspective of others. We suggest that tensions between alignment ideals emerge due to differences in background threat-models, alongside differences in normative orientations. In light of our analysis, researchers aiming to further the goal of 'AI alignment' should do five things. First, they should not conflate distinctions of policy and distinctions of scientific scope; second, methodological disagreements should be acknowledged explicitly; third, researchers should distinguish between 'AI alignment' as a high-level ideal and specific 'alignment proxies' used in empirical research; fourth, they should use more granular concepts to identify both the source and nature of possible AI harms/benefits; fifth, they should explicitly acknowledge the diversity of 'alignment' concepts in both empirical work and in communication with non-technical audiences.
papers.idea: N/A — no actionable intervention identified.
papers.summary: This paper is a conceptual analysis distinguishing multiple competing definitions of 'AI alignment' in machine learning research and argues that interventions promoting alignment under one definition may undermine it under another. The authors recommend that researchers use more granular terminology and acknowledge methodological disagreements.
papers.method: Conceptual/theoretical analysis of AI alignment literature; no empirical study, no data collection, no real-world intervention testing.
papers.scalability_note: Pure theory with no implementable intervention.
papers.cost_note: No intervention; not applicable.
papers.nyc_note: No concrete policy or program a city agency could adopt. Relevant only to ML researchers' internal terminology practices.

papers.title: Time Without Death: Finitude, Social Order, and What Machines Lack
papers.authors: Canhui Liu
papers.abstract: Machine collectives increasingly coordinate, reciprocate, and form shared conventions on their own, tempting us to call them societies like ours. We argue that this conflates two registers of social order and misses what the human one is for. Human sociality is the way a finite, natal, generational form of life organises its own finitude: members die with their tacit knowledge, newcomers start ignorant, and cohorts must hand on what they cannot keep. Kinship, inheritance, teaching, and much of obligation and trust are the forms this takes, and machine collectives reproduce only the residue once finitude is subtracted. We meet the objection that machines already have death with two distinctions. Autonomy versus heteronomy: a death an operator can reset, roll back, or copy around is not constitutive finitude; the test is resettability. Representation versus binding force: humans learn finitude from others' deaths, so its representation is machine-learnable, but its grip on motivation needs the learner's own end to be inescapable. Treating machines as a model organism, a controlled experiment varying only whether death means loss shows that cumulative, transmissible culture arises only under irreversible loss; a copyable-immortal population is more capable yet culturally empty. Across a machine population and three real human genealogies, copying over-transmits status relative to every human regime and fragments lineage like blood descent, whereas externalisation lowers transmission into the human range and connects lineage like intellectual descent. In a real frontier model, language-model end-game defection vanishes when the game is de-labelled, a sign of recognition, not a mechanism. The gap between a machine collective and a human society is therefore not one of intelligence but of finitude, and it closes only when finitude is built in.
papers.idea: N/A — no actionable intervention proposed.
papers.summary: A philosophical argument that human society differs from machine collectives in that human social order is organized around irreversible mortality, generational turnover, and knowledge loss, which machines lack. The author uses machine learning experiments and genealogical analysis to show that cumulative culture arises only under genuine finitude.
papers.method: Theoretical essay with illustrative machine-learning experiments and analysis of human genealogies; no empirical study design, no control group, no population sample in the policy-relevant sense.
papers.scalability_note: Pure theoretical work with no policy mechanism or implementation pathway.
papers.cost_note: No cost structure; not an intervention.
papers.nyc_note: No city policy lever or practical application identified.

papers.title: Choric Masking in Ambient Release Systems: A Finite Certificate Calculus for Trace Indistinguishability under Bounded Audiences
papers.authors: Faruk Alpay, Taylan Alpay
papers.abstract: This paper develops a finite certificate calculus for ambient release systems, staged probabilistic environments in which a protected coordinate is not observed directly but can remain statistically readable through visible roles, timing, repeated movement, bounded attention, linked rooms, and post-release state. The security notion, choric masking, requires the trace law induced by a protected locus to lie inside or near the convex hull of admissible cover traces under the tests available to a specified audience. For finite horizons, trace laws form polytopes, audiences induce measurement operators, and masking becomes intersection in the projected measurement space. Exposure is certified by separating hyperplanes, kernel obstructions, hypothesis-testing bounds, Fano-type localization lower bounds, and support separation in downstream rooms. The calculus distinguishes trace residue from carrier localization, full-trace exposure from attention-filtered exposure, first-room masking from delayed post-release exposure, and unresolved system pressure from carrier hazard. It proves measurement-polytope equivalence for exact and approximate masks, dual separation certificates, data-processing laws for attention lenses, aperture identities for gaze-thinned observation, lower bounds for mandatory unique gestures, composition rules for linked releases, and a repeated-room debt theorem showing how unresolved pressure can broaden selection and shift cost onto cover populations without localizing the source. The result is a finite, checkable language for auditing privacy, unlinkability, side-channel leakage, and accountability in public-facing release systems.
papers.idea: N/A
papers.summary: This is a pure mathematics/theoretical computer science paper on formal methods for privacy and indistinguishability in probabilistic systems. It develops a calculus and proof framework for auditing information leakage in abstract 'ambient release systems' using concepts from convex geometry and hypothesis testing.
papers.method: Non-empirical; develops formal mathematical theory and proofs for a certificate calculus in staged probabilistic environments.

papers.title: How Advocacy, Politics, and Implementation Challenges Shaped the Adoption of Artemisinin-Based Combination Therapy as First-Line Therapy for Malaria
papers.authors: Cordelia S. Kenney
papers.abstract: By the 1970s, the world’s premier malaria treatment at the time, chloroquine, was failing in Southeast Asia, with resistance to it documented in Africa shortly thereafter. By 2000, chloroquine had become largely ineffective in malaria-endemic countries, while malaria-spreading parasites were also becoming increasingly resistant to available alternatives, such as sulfadoxine/pyrimethamine (SP). Amid dwindling treatment options, clinical research on derivatives of artemisinin, an extract of A. annua, demonstrated the safety and efficacy of artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT). This promising new class of antimalarial drugs provided an effective alternative to the failing options on the market and has since become the standard of care in most settings. Although researchers established the efficacy of artemisinin in treating malaria in 1972 and the safety and efficacy of ACTs through clinical trials in the 1980s and 1990s, ACT did not become the first-line antimalarial treatment in most national drug policies until after 2004. In January

papers.title: The Income Channel to Labor-Market Polarization
papers.authors: Diego A. Comin, Ana Danieli, Martí Mestieri
papers.abstract: We study labor-market polarization by developing a framework with heterogeneous consumers, non-homothetic preferences, and endogenous labor supply a la Roy (1951). Because income-elastic sectors are intensive in high- and low-skilled occupations, shocks that raise either the average or the dispersion of income across households shift relative labor demand toward these occupations, generating an income channel to polarization. We quantify this channel by comparing the effect of shocks that affect the distribution of household income in our model and in a version with homothetic preferences in which the income distribution does not affect sectoral composition. The income channel is sizable: it explains 42% and 210% of the increases in the relative wages of high- and low-skill workers observed between 1980 and 2016, and 26% and 51% of the increase in their hours-worked shares. A quarter of its effect operates through income dispersion, highlighting the importance of departing from representative-consumer frameworks in non-homothetic environments.
papers.idea: N/A — no actionable intervention proposed
papers.summary: The paper develops a theoretical framework showing that income distribution shocks can drive labor-market polarization through sectoral demand shifts. The authors find that this 'income channel' explains a substantial share of wage and employment polarization between 1980–2016, with income dispersion playing a key role.
papers.method: Theoretical model with heterogeneous consumers and non-homothetic preferences calibrated to match U.S. labor-market data (1980–2016); counterfactual simulations comparing outcomes with and without the income channel.

papers.title: Decomposing Shifts in the Beveridge Curve: Implications for Labor Market Dynamics and Inflation
papers.authors: Katharine G. Abraham, John C. Haltiwanger, Lea E. Rendell
papers.abstract: This paper explores the relationship between standard labor market tightness (vacancies divided by unemployment) and generalized labor market tightness (vacancies divided by a measure of effective searchers that accounts for potential hires from all sources including those out of the labor force or currently employed). We show that much of what the standard model attributes to variation in matching efficiency reflects changes in the ratio of effective searchers to unemployment rather than changes in “true” matching efficiency. Generalized tightness outperforms standard tightness in Phillips curve equations, both in models with tightness entering linearly and in better-fitting models with tightness entering nonlinearly. In models that distinguish between movements in generalized tightness due to movements along the generalized Beveridge curve versus Beveridge curve shifts (reflecting changes in generalized matching efficiency and other factors), changes in tightness due to Beveridge curve shifts explain more of the variation in inflation.
papers.idea: None identified; this is a theoretical labor economics paper with no actionable city-policy lever.
papers.summary: The paper decomposes shifts in the Beveridge curve (the relationship between job vacancies and unemployment) by distinguishing between standard labor market tightness and a generalized measure that includes potential workers outside the formal labor force. The authors find that changes in the composition of searchers explain more inflation variation than traditional matching-efficiency models suggest.
papers.method: Econometric analysis of Phillips curve and Beveridge curve relationships using labor market tightness measures; no experimental design, RCT, or quasi-experimental identification strategy described.
papers.scalability_note: Theoretical labor economics; no intervention to scale.
papers.cost_note: No intervention proposed; only methodological/conceptual contribution.
papers.nyc_note: No city-level policy, program, or practice can be derived from this macroeconomic theory paper.

papers.title: Data-Driven Automation
papers.authors: Maryam Farboodi, Andrew J. Koh, Anchi Xia
papers.abstract: We build a dynamic model of data-driven automation in which data (i) is heterogeneous and task-specific; (ii) accumulates endogenously as a byproduct of economic activity; and (iii) exhibits spillovers such that data generated by one task can augment the productivity of another. Along the transition path of automation, data plays a dual role in simultaneously augmenting the productivity of already-automated tasks and expanding the automation frontier. We derive tight conditions for the economy to be partially versus fully automated in the long-run. In the latter case, automation exhibits rich short-run dynamics that depend on the pattern of data spillovers but is always slow in the long-run: the share of tasks produced by labor decays asymptotically as a power law in time. We show that the economy is generically inefficient and analyze how a planner optimally tilts the direction of data accumulation. With endogenous capital accumulation, data-driven automation generates explosive growth but stagnant long-run wages.
papers.idea: N/A — no actionable intervention identified
papers.summary: This is a theoretical dynamic model of how data accumulation drives automation over time, with data exhibiting task-specific spillovers. The authors derive conditions for partial vs. full automation and show that long-run automation is slow (power-law decay) but generates explosive growth alongside wage stagnation. They analyze optimal planner interventions on data accumulation direction.
papers.method: Mathematical dynamic model with theoretical analysis; no empirical data, natural experiment, or policy trial.
papers.scalability_note: Pure theory with no tested intervention; no implementation pathway.
papers.cost_note: Not applicable; no program or initiative described.
papers.nyc_note: Theory paper offers no concrete policy tool, pilot, or program NYC could adopt. Wage stagnation insight is noted but no remedy is proposed or tested.

papers.title: How Do Financial and Longevity Literacy Affect Annuity Demand?
papers.authors: Jeffrey R. Brown
papers.abstract: Deciding whether to use an annuity to draw down accumulated retirement wealth is among the most complex financial decisions individuals face. It requires reasoning about longevity risk, stochastic income streams, and largely irreversible tradeoffs, often at older ages and with limited opportunities to learn from experience. This paper reviews theoretical, empirical, and experimental research to examine how financial literacy and longevity literacy shape annuitization decisions. While some extensions of the standard lifecycle model can rationalize low annuity demand, their explanatory power depends on individuals’ ability to optimize over a set of complex risks and tradeoffs. Evidence shows that, although financial and longevity literacy are only weakly and inconsistently related to annuity take-up, they are strongly associated with retirement planning, the coherence of annuity valuations, and sensitivity to framing and choice architecture. These patterns suggest that literacy should be understood not as a direct determinant of annuity demand, but as a constraint on the extent to which observed annuitization behavior can be interpreted as revealing true preferences.
papers.idea: N/A—this is a theoretical review without an actionable city-policy intervention.
papers.summary: This paper reviews existing research on how financial and longevity literacy affect retirement annuitization decisions. It finds that literacy is weakly linked to annuity take-up but strongly associated with retirement planning coherence and susceptibility to framing, suggesting literacy constrains rather than directly determines annuity demand.
papers.method: Literature review and synthesis of theoretical, empirical, and experimental research on annuitization behavior and financial/longevity literacy.
papers.scalability_note: Pure theoretical review; no tested intervention to scale.
papers.cost_note: No intervention proposed; no cost data.
papers.nyc_note: Retirement annuity decisions are private financial choices; no direct municipal policy lever or city program implied.

papers.title: Dollar Erosion: Understanding the Loss of Reserve Currency Status
papers.authors: Zhengyang Jiang, Arvind Krishnamurthy, Hanno Lustig, Robert J. Richmond
papers.abstract: We quantify the impact of the loss of reserve currency status in goods and asset markets. In goods markets, the loss of seigniorage (1% of GDP per annum) makes American households spend less, mostly on U.S. goods, leaving an excess supply of U.S. goods to be cleared by a real depreciation of the dollar. The larger the home bias and the lower the elasticity of substitution between home and foreign goods, the larger the required depreciation. Standard parameters imply an 8.8% real depreciation of the dollar. In asset markets, about 50% of GDP in dollar bonds must be reabsorbed by home investors, raising the U.S. real interest rate. Standard parameters imply a 90 basis points rise, leading to an aggregate wealth loss of roughly one year of U.S. GDP.
papers.idea: N/A — this is macroeconomic theory with no city-level policy lever.
papers.summary: This paper models the macroeconomic consequences of the U.S. losing its reserve currency status, predicting an ~8.8% real dollar depreciation and a ~90 basis point rise in interest rates, resulting in aggregate wealth loss equivalent to one year of GDP.
papers.method: Theoretical economic modeling of goods and asset markets; no empirical data, experimental design, or real-world intervention tested.
papers.scalability_note: A city cannot adopt or implement monetary/reserve currency policy; this is federal domain only.
papers.cost_note: Not applicable; no intervention or program proposed.
papers.nyc_note: Reserve currency status is a federal/global macroeconomic question entirely outside NYC's policy scope.

papers.title: Reconciling Micro Elasticities with the Macro Decline in Labor Supply
papers.authors: Loukas Karabarbounis
papers.abstract: Micro estimates of the Marshallian elasticity of labor supply are small and typically positive, whereas cross-country and time-series patterns of hours imply a strong negative relationship between wages and hours. I reconcile these two apparently contradictory observations using a single utility specification and taking into account heterogeneity in non-labor income. Micro estimates condition on non-labor income, while macro variation allows capital income to adjust alongside labor income, which strengthens the income effect. A model with heterogeneous households and exogenous capital income yields closed-form expressions in which the distribution of the labor share shapes the gap between the micro and the macro elasticities. A cross-sectional regression of hours on wages that conditions on the labor share recovers the macro elasticity. A dynamic model with heterogeneous households and incomplete asset markets reproduces both elasticities as outcomes when disciplined by joint moments of wages, hours, consumption, and wealth. The income effects that bridge the gap between the two elasticities imply marginal propensities to earn that lie in the range of estimates of micro studies on lottery winners.
papers.idea: N/A — pure theory paper with no actionable intervention lever.
papers.summary: This paper reconciles a longstanding puzzle in labor economics: why individual-level studies show small labor supply elasticities while aggregate cross-country and time-series data show strong negative relationships between wages and hours worked. The author uses heterogeneous household models and capital-income dynamics to explain the discrepancy.
papers.method: Theoretical modeling with closed-form solutions and dynamic simulations calibrated to aggregate moments of wages, hours, consumption, and wealth; no empirical intervention tested.
papers.scalability_note: Pure theory; no policy or program to scale.
papers.cost_note: No implementation cost; not actionable.
papers.nyc_note: No city-policy mechanism identified; macro labor-supply reconciliation does not translate to concrete NYC program or lever.

papers.title: Monopsony Power and the Transmission of Monetary Policy
papers.authors: Bence Bardóczy, Gideon Bornstein, Sergio Salgado
papers.abstract: This paper studies how labor market power affects the transmission of monetary policy. Using administrative U.S. Census data, we show that firms with high monopsony power—defined as those accounting for over 10 percent of the local wage bill—respond less to monetary policy in terms of their wage bill and employment. We then develop a New Keynesian model with heterogeneous firms and oligopsonistic competition to interpret these findings. Wage stickiness combined with firms’ labor market power is key to generating the heterogeneous responses that we document. Our model highlights two channels through which oligopsony shapes the aggregate effects of monetary policy: partial passthrough and misallocation. Calibrated to U.S. labor markets, the model implies that the decline in labor market power since the 1980s has increased the output response to monetary policy by about 10 percent and accounts for about 15 percent of the estimated flattening of the Phillips curve.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a theoretical macroeconomic paper with no identified actionable city-level intervention.
papers.summary: The paper finds that firms with high monopsony power (controlling >10% of local wage bill) respond less to monetary policy in wages and employment. The authors develop a theoretical model showing that wage stickiness combined with oligopsonistic competition shapes how monetary policy transmits through labor markets, and that declining labor market concentration since the 1980s has increased monetary policy's output effects.
papers.method: Administrative U.S. Census data analyzed with a calibrated New Keynesian model with heterogeneous firms and oligopsonistic competition.
papers.scalability_note: This is monetary policy macroeconomics; city governments have no lever over the Federal Reserve or aggregate monetary transmission.
papers.cost_note: N/A — no intervention proposed.
papers.nyc_note: NYC cannot implement monetary policy or directly alter firm-level monopsony power; the paper offers no local labor-market interventions.

papers.title: Don’t Give Up on Lab Experiments: Why the Field Still Needs the Lab
papers.authors: John A. List
papers.abstract: Recent enthusiasm for field experiments, and especially for natural field experiments (NFEs), in which subjects go about their daily activities unaware that any study is taking place, has sometimes been read as a verdict against the laboratory. I argue that such a verdict is wrong. Through the lens of a simple rational-choice model, I show that the four standard experimental designs (laboratory, artefactual field experiment (AFE), framed field experiment (FFE), and NFE) are comparative-static restrictions of one maximization problem, each identifying a parameter the others cannot. The model reveals that each design type has distinctive strengths and weaknesses across various dimensions of knowledge creation, including the enforcement of the conditions for causal identification, the faithfulness of the experimental environment to the theory being tested, the identification of economic primitives via theoretical structure, and the ethics of studying human subjects. On each of the dimensions, the four design types are complements rather than rivals. Nowhere is this complementarity more evident than between the two extremes. The lab enforces the conditions for causal identification that the NFE must inherit from the market; the NFE recovers the parameter that governs behaviour in the wild, free of the selection, scrutiny, and environmental distortions the lab cannot escape. A research programme using all four designs together demonstrates something no single design can produce. The framework further accommodates the recent rise of online and survey experiments as natural extensions. Our discipline’s recent drift away from laboratory evidence is leaving an important structural gap that natural field experiments, however well conceived, cannot fill.
papers.idea: This is a methods paper on experimental design philosophy with no concrete intervention proposed.
papers.summary: The paper argues that laboratory experiments remain valuable alongside field experiments, presenting a theoretical framework showing that four experimental designs (lab, artefactual field, framed field, natural field) are complementary rather than rival approaches to identifying different causal parameters.
papers.method: Theoretical paper using rational-choice modeling to analyze the comparative strengths and weaknesses of experimental methodologies; no empirical study or data.
papers.scalability_note: This is methodological guidance, not an intervention.
papers.cost_note: Not applicable; no program or policy proposed.
papers.nyc_note: Addresses how to conduct better research generally, not a specific NYC policy or program lever.

papers.title: The Targets of Geoeconomic Coercion
papers.authors: Christopher Clayton, Matteo Maggiori, Jesse Schreger
papers.abstract: Great powers, like the United States and China, use existing trade and financial linkages of their economies to the rest of the world to exert geoeconomic power. The targeted entities include both foreign governments and foreign private firms. We characterize conditions under which a hegemonic country optimally targets private entities or their government in a given targeted foreign country. An advantage of coercing private entities is that they internalize less than their own government the equilibrium consequences of acquiescing to the hegemon's coercion. This allows the hegemon to build its power by exploiting the difference between the private cost to the targeted entity of the costly actions it demands and the social value to the hegemon of those actions. Coercing the government gives up some of this advantage, but offers the ability of having the targeted government potentially influence firms in its domestic economy that the hegemon finds valuable to coerce but for which it had limited direct coercive power. The relative strength of these two channels determines the optimal mix of coercion targeted at private entities and governments.
papers.idea: No actionable city-level intervention identified.
papers.summary: This paper develops a theoretical model of how hegemonic powers (like the US and China) choose between coercing foreign private firms versus foreign governments using trade and financial leverage. The model characterizes when each coercion target is optimal based on which entity internalizes costs versus benefits differently.
papers.method: Non-empirical theoretical model; characterizes conditions for optimal coercion strategy targeting private entities versus governments.
papers.scalability_note: Theory paper with no implementation pathway; addresses great-power geopolitics, not local policy.
papers.cost_note: No implementation costs; no intervention.
papers.nyc_note: Concerns US-China geoeconomic competition at nation-state level; no municipal policy lever.
